### Glamazon — 2012-10-07T20:19:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/food-costs.3948/post-56763>

I am recently on the down turn for incoming finances so I have to seriously budgetize my life where as before I didn't have any worries. It has forced me to go to stores that I would not normally even think about going to.

I was very surprised to find at the Dollar Store cans of seafood for a dollar. I have bought kippers, sardines, clams, oysters all packed in water for a dollar. Sources range from USA to China to Thailand. I have also found decently priced canned salmon at Target and Walmart. It is also worth it to troll thru their frozen seafood. Raw, fresh is a luxury purchase right now. I can buy seafood offal (heads and stuff) at Whole Foods for a 1.99 a pound.

The Dollar store also has frozen veggies from the US of A that cost a dollar a bag. I don't eat much veggies anymore. Since it seems nitrates are off the no-no list I might be changing my sources of bacon.

Budget downsizing has forced me to become a more efficient hunter-gatherer.

### Caroline Cooper — 2012-10-09T00:15:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/food-costs.3948/post-56965>

Here are my best tips for beginners. Of course, focus on the shellfish and seafood. Eat the whole animal if possible. In our mixed up society the "waste" parts are usually the best parts. Grow some of your own food in a garden; you can save a load from eating seasonally from a garden. If you can, get some livestock or start wildcrafting, fishing or hunting. I know that might be a bit much but usually we have to spend our time or our money if we want the best food for our families:

[http://eatkamloops.org/where-to-start-limited-time-and-budget/](http://http://eatkamloops.org/where-to-start-limited-time-and-budget/)

Just ignore the stuff about grains. This post is for the mass consumption. I am just trying to get the average person off industrial food and eating the real thing!

### skline@peak.org — 2012-10-09T03:16:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/food-costs.3948/post-57020>

Pasadena -- one of the loveliest climates in the world. Some plant foods should be extremely easy to grow, practically year round there. If you just choose the plants and herbs which are easiest to grow, that's a start. And you can't beat the convenience of walking into your yard, picking the herbs or veg you want when they are at their best, and never even needing a refrigerator for the greens and fresh produce part of your diet. Of course you have to learn timing. What you can have when, and what you need to wait for.

I remember when I lived in Stockton (not the nicest climate in the world!) and I had no money. I bought a two-in-one nectarine tree on sale because it had started as a three-in-one, and one of the grafts had failed. Maybe $8. Planted it, it thrived, soon I had incredible nectarines. I learned I could freeze them very easily. Cut them off the pits, put in a glass casserole dish with a glass lid. Microwave till sloppy looking. Spoon into freezer jars, leaving some room at the top. Top off with apple juice. They were incredible later in the year, and the cost was nearly nil, just some electricity for the freezer.

Today I picked bunches of blue muscat grapes off a grapevine I started from a donated cutting nine years ago. The vine is covered. They are seedless and totally delicious. Because they spike my blood sugar, I don't have them often, but just gave a bucketful to a friend who will juice and can them. I only watered that vine once this summer!

A careful choice of perennials like trees and bushes suited to the area (could you grow avocados??) will give the most food for the least work, and the most food security, all for pennies. Some herbs like dill can sometimes be planted once and then just volunteer from then on. You'll never beat either the freshness or the price.

I'm still learning how to cook fresh fish. So far my default tactic is to poach all the fin fish (with herbs like rosemary and oregano and some sea salt in the water), eat some right away and keep some in the fridge. I take the poaching water (used several times in one day for several piece of fish), add a few vegetables like onions and carrots, then I add a can or bottle of oysters, and at least a half pound or more of Oregon shrimp. Put into mason jars, freeze if necessary, but usually I can just warm it up and eat it before it goes bad.

"Proper" cooking will probably come later on, but this seems to work. The prime rule for cooking fish (which, sadly, my mother never learned) is not to overcook it. The second rule (should probably be the first) is to buy it as fresh as possible and cook it as soon as you possibly can. The most expensive food is what has turned and needs to be thrown out.

### cuivie@me.com — 2012-10-09T07:18:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/food-costs.3948/post-57062>

Did somebody mention Pasadna, CA? We should meet up for some Epi-Paleo grocery foraging!

For my single person Epi-Paleo budget of $70/week (been that high ever since going paleo...just the price of good health, I guess), I've worked this out:

1 day/week total fast

3 days/week "surf"

3 days/week "turf"

Surf days:

\- 10 raw oysters from Seafood Paradise in Rosemead for $1/oyster (total 30 oysters/week)

\- maybe some seaweed (going into winter, not so much) from Whole Foods or ordered in bulk online for Mountain Rose Herbs, not factoring this into weekly cost right now

Turf days:

\- 1.5 lbs of grassfed chuck from Sprouts at $6.99/lb (total 4.5 lbs/week)

\- .33 lbs of grassfed or USDA Natural liver from Whole Foods at $6/lb (total 1 lb/ week)

This is a grassfed chuck heavy diet. I could get more fish (like mackerel) for the same amount of money, but I decided to maximize my seafood impact by maxing out on oysters, and I find that my body CRAVES grassfed chuck and does not feel satisfied without lots of it. Fish just does not satisfy in the same way; my body must need the Vit A or something else in it. YMMV.

### cuivie@me.com — 2012-10-17T05:47:13+0300

> **Destiny;59277 wrote:** That sounds like a very good eating plan. And $70.00 a week for good health - $10.00 a day is very cheap in my opinion compare to illness expenses!

### indigogirl — 2012-09-27T05:45:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/knee-surgury-finding-blog-post-help.3949/post-54684>

Mam, get his doctor to prescribe the ice machine that will Velcro around his knee and keep it cold. My insurance paid 100%. I had to ask my doctor for it, though, as they don't usually prescribe it for meniscus tear repairs. I slept with it on my knee the first 2 nights post surgery and used it as often as I could.

I also took Arnica a week pre surgery to help with bruising...I had almost no bruising.

# Thread: odd senations in my ears and diaphragm
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/odd-senations-in-my-ears-and-diaphragm.3956/>
**Subforum:** The Leptin Rx
**Posts:** 4
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### somewhatchubby — 2012-09-27T10:33:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/odd-senations-in-my-ears-and-diaphragm.3956/post-54708>

well, Ive just got to the eight week period of the Leptin reset. Its been a journey of odd things, The latest seems to be a pulsing pressure feeling in my ears with,particularly at night a sound like a distant fog horn ! low and pulsing. I also have this pressure in the diaphragm and still slightly exagerated heart beat despite thinking that the potassium /magnesium supplementing had sorted things out. In fact I feel low level crappiness.

Is this just another passing phase or do I need to address it? If so, how ?

Also I have lost no weight ,in fact I am 2 lbs heavier.I expected that as JK said it would happen ,it did. On the + side my sleep is so much better, I am not so crabby and I have better energy. My skin looks better too. I am thinking that I will just continue and see how things progress but I wish I felt better.

I have recently started brewing and drinking kombucha, that really has settled the constipation problem.

thanks to all who respond

### nonchalant — 2012-09-28T01:02:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/odd-senations-in-my-ears-and-diaphragm.3956/post-54882>

Hi Somewhat, have you had a test for inflammation? HS-CRP?

### somewhatchubby — 2012-09-28T09:31:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/odd-senations-in-my-ears-and-diaphragm.3956/post-54947>

Hi Nonchalant , no I havnt. I'm off to the docs today, as combined with hot flushes from being post menopause ,its not alot of fun.

I'll look into thy HS-CRP.

thanks.

### somewhatchubby — 2012-10-12T23:48:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/odd-senations-in-my-ears-and-diaphragm.3956/post-57878>

thanks to those who replied. I have been having repeat performances on and off since I first posted about it. Last night I slept little ,I kept having to get up from bed, my heart was racing,pounding under my ribs and into my throat and I had this lump of concrete under my ribs. I thought I was having a heart attack except I had an ECG last week and nothing bad came up . Ive been following various threads in connection with the gut and candi da and whilst searcing today came up with this. This lady excatly described my symptoms from last night/ Dhe has Heliobacter Pylori. She has also been unable to lose wieight....DING !

[http://amyking.wordpress.com/2009/02/20/im-so-happy-i-have-h-pylori/](http://http://amyking.wordpress.com/2009/02/20/im-so-happy-i-have-h-pylori/)

I;m going to take another read and implement some of the suggestions.

One question came to mind. I am getting some milk Kefir evvery day . If I use Manuka honey will it negate the benefits of the bacteria in the Kefir? also is CO effective against H. Pylori ?

thanks.

# Thread: Heart Disease
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/heart-disease.3957/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 45
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Michael — 2012-09-27T12:47:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/heart-disease.3957/post-54714>

What a coincidence! This was the topic on Jimmy Moore's podcast today, where he had guest Kent Rieske.

Mr. Rieske said that what the medical profession does is not "science", which as an engineer appalls him. I thought that was slightly unfair, because I don't think that, say, Ancel Keys equals "the medical profession". I'm also inclined to suspect that engineers have their own _dÃ©formation professionnelle - they think everything's about math. So Rieske said that a low-carb diet would halt the deposition of arterial plaque but could not reverse it. I suspect he's looked at some material on this and is probably right. His answer is medication to reduce LDL cholesterol below a certain number. The idea seems to be that you manipulate variables, by any means available, and the answer falls out. All about math. But what if you don't understand the aetiology fully? What if there are dimensions to the problem you haven't even dreamed of? How if, everything else being as it is, the LDL needs to be where it is?

I had an inkling of what was coming in BG13 - alhough it goes without saying no more than that. I'd seen the "French paradox is really an epi-paleo paradox" material and put a few things together.

So Key's line was that heart disease is caused by saturated fat. The low carb people think it's caused by carbohydrate. Paleo's current position seems to be that it's caused by saturated fat _plus_ high-glycemic load carbs.

But what if the problems is that it's not so much what you eat as what you don't_ eat? Where's the DHA in current diets?

Cordain has something about two Inuit bodies from medieval times that had been mummified in the ice. He says they have arterial plaque, and this shows that saturated fat will cause plaque to form (although, he says, that need not be a problem). I wonder if anyone has thought to do static-isotope analysis on the bones. It would be interesting to know whether they were eating predominantly land-based game (such as caribou) or predominantly sea-resources.

I am realising more and more that the truth is a matter of both great simplicity and great complexity. It is what we do eat AND what we don't. It is what we do and what we don't do. It is what we think, what we don't think, and what we fail to even realise. It is our genetic switches, our epigenetic flipping of those switches and our knowledge of our own selves mixed with our current environment. There will never be one silver bullet or magic answer. The more you know, the more you have to learn.

I love that you are able to put all these sources together and question things. It helps me a lot in discovering my own learning edge.

I want you to channel what I said about the heart and brain in BG 13 with respect to how the Na/K ATPase is affected in metabolic terms in all tissues.......then think about the number one killer of all humans today: Heart disease. How does heart disease tie into this theory of whole body physiology?

If you begin to alter your blood glucose because of dietary or any other epigenetics signal (light or temp) and cause inflammation at a cellular level by eating badly or not being mindful of circadian cycles, you start secreting large amounts of insulin from beta cells to deal with it. This alters your Magnesium levels which activate the metabolically efficient Na/K ratio because of DHA as laid out in BG 13. When we have an altered Na/K (low) ratio, this in turn, simultaneously cause brain metabolism to ratcheded down because its function is directly coupled to the efficiency of the Na/K ATPase for our species. As the brain metabolism falters, it begins to shunt things to it at the determent of other organs. It robs Peter to pay Paul. This physiologic bio energetic "stealing" of energy alters functions everywhere in the body to satisfy the brains needs. As other organs fail slowly under this physiologic directive This further alters brain function from the excessive 60% energy boon created by the DHA/ Na/K ATPase, which results in your feeling less well with less energy and you have a chronic cognitive haze. Is this sounding familiar to anyone? It is the modern human condition. As this goes on chronically brain function is eroded (the real source of adrenal fatigue in the PVN) because there is an excess of Na ions which alter our homeostatic brain controls of the renin angiotensin system and this alters our BP. The renin angiotensin system target organ is the kidneys. The kidneys control the electrolyte balance of the serum and ultimate of the CSF (ultrafiltrate of the serum) to further alter the function of our brain. This slowly erosion of our kidney function goes on undetected and slowly undermines our electrical systems balance. This implies that our declining kidney function, directly afffects our electrical system of our heart (conduction system AV node and sinus pacemaker) and become less optimal or "foggy" too. This shows you why the declining brain function always correlates with declining heart function. They are chemically and electrically tied together by DHA and iodine at a foundational level physiologically in humans. This all results in the many types of cardiac dysrythmias we see clinically. This is an early sign of major bio energenic shifts in cells all tied to oxidation. When this goes on further brain function erodes and we can not sense it well. We in essence lose our primal sense of well being and eventually we develop an inability to sense vascular trouble before it happens clinically. This leads to a heart attack. Now think back to Inger.........she cant tell you any of this physiology........but she can tell you that she feels a sense of well being now........because her signaling and sensation in her brain is approaching optimal........this means the Na/K ATPase battery that is driving her Ferrari engine is working at the peak 60% efficiency I mention in BG 13. How did she intuitively find it? Look at what she eats..........MHS and fermented heart in a jar. She has tapped into her brain sensation because this is what it needs to sense its world to avoid illness. That my friend is understanding heart disease from the Quilt's perspective. Inger is a perfect example of where form has met function.

### Michael — 2012-09-28T15:20:49+0300

They are blind to it. One researcher blogger in particular has done more harm in my view than any other and he remains completely ignorant of what he does not know while remianing quite confident all the bull\*\*\*\* he thinks he knows.

IMO, AHS 2011 caused big problems. AFAICT, there is a tiny coterie of people responsible - they could be counted on the hand of a dreamer in a sawmill. (And at bottom it was driven by a single person.) But what they succeeded in doing was driving a wedge between paleo and the low-carb people. That's all the wider ancestral health movement needed. It's damaged its momentum for a year or two, possibly longer. And such was the apparent plausibility, mainly down to force of the rhetoric, I guess - for I think the arguments were bogus - on this that it even took the confidence away from many low carb people. It's taken them about a year to get it back. I don't believe the low carb people are right on everything, but I think they're more right than wrong, and the movement could have done without a split anyway.

Kitava! As I pointed out here, apart from anything else there's no electricity on the island. No-one seems to have thought that important.

Thanks for the tip in post #12.

I shall have to read BG13 multiple times anyway to get a firmer grasp on how everything fits together. _Why_ the brain and heart tend to be threatened - because they're "energy hogs" - and what follows from that is great stuff.

### Michael — 2012-09-28T19:54:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/heart-disease.3957/post-55044>

Well, first I wasn't accusing Mark. I don't think it's his style. I was just using his forum as an example of what people were tending to say on paleo forums pre-AHS 2011.

Yes I think people in paleo will begin to tumble to what Jack is showing. And yes I think they will say things like: "We never told anyone not to eat fish" and "We did recommend omega-3 oil".

Not good enough.

Just not good enough, and the reason why it's not is apparent in many threads all over this board.

They never specifically told anyone to regularly eat fish. A negative is not enough. Stale O-3 oil without antioxidant protection misses the point.

They stated the cold was merely a "hormetic stressor".

Their evolutionary picture is wrong. Their books neglect brain-chemistry. They've never laid out the theory Jack has.

One gentleman with a widely-listened to podcast stated that a presentation at AHS 2012 was "worth a PhD". (Hmmm I never knew they were \*that\* easy to get). The presentation talked about nutrient density, but the presenter did not know WHICH nutrients the brain needs.

It's not minor "semantic" differences. They're not even close.

### Michael — 2012-09-29T17:46:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/heart-disease.3957/post-55224>

I got sidetracked by the politics of the movement.

But I did read this:

> **Jack Kruse;55876 wrote:** I want you to channel what I said about the heart and brain in BG 13 with respect to how the Na/K ATPase is affected in metabolic terms in all tissues.......then think about the number one killer of all humans today: Heart disease. How does heart disease tie into this theory of whole body physiology?
>
>
> You just explained to me why heart disease happens, and it makes perfect sense. You've just told me something that perhaps no-one in paleo and few people outside it know. And it's huge. But that's not all, is it?
>
>
>
>
> > ... eventually we develop an inability to sense vascular trouble before it happens clinically. This leads to a heart attack.
>
>
>
> I'm thinking that with a brain not working as it should it's not only that we'd lose the ability to sense.
>
>
>
> Now think back to Inger.........she cant tell you any of this physiology........but she can tell you that she feels a sense of well being now........because her signaling and sensation in her brain is approaching optimal........this means the Na/K ATPase battery that is driving her Ferrari engine is working at the peak 60% efficiency I mention in BG 13. How did she intuitively find it? Look at what she eats..........MHS and fermented heart in a jar. She has tapped into her brain sensation because this is what it needs to sense its world to avoid illness. That my friend is understanding heart disease from the Quilt's perspective. Inger is a perfect example of where form has met function.

Am I right in thinking that Inger can probably expect to have every autonomic function working pretty much optimally? This would mean, for example, digestion. It could be all kinds of stuff: maybe if she walked across a room she'd be less likely to bump into something.

I'm wondering now why teenagers are clumsy. I was told as a kid it's because your limbs are growing, so that you've lost a sense of where they are. I'm not sure I buy that.

Getting a bit off-topic, but a couple of things.

Different kinds of bodywork are interesting. They seems to be attempts to fix what exercise can't - because you're dealing with pretty deep things not controlled consciously that have gone adrift. (It interests me that few people in paleo have done more than dabble their toes in this area. And these are people who are supposed to be interested in the body and its needs! Some are even openly contemptuous of yoga: it's sometimes implied that it's something women are often interested in - this apparently counts against it.)

Then there's neurofeedback. Interesting that the brain can self-regulate as soon as it can "see itself". (The only person in paleo who seems to have any interest in this is Nora Gedgaudas.) This is used, for example, with ex-military people stuck in a state of hyper-vigilance (gamma-waves up all the time):

[http://www.homecoming4veterans.org/](http://http://www.homecoming4veterans.org/)

[www.eeginfo.com/research/.../PTSD-NeurofeedbackRemedy.pdf](http://http://www.eeginfo.com/research/.../PTSD-NeurofeedbackRemedy.pdf)

Interesting to investigate more how all these things fit together. I'm thinking some of the tools we use to fix things, be it Tai Chi, be it sophisticated EEG machines, are probably needed more than they might be because of what the conditions of modern life have done to our brains.

### prAna303 — 2012-09-29T20:44:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/heart-disease.3957/post-55255>

"I'm wondering now why teenagers are clumsy. I was told as a kid it's because your limbs are growing, so that you've lost a sense of where they are. I'm not sure I buy that."

This is getting very interesting for me... Nowdays i only work a couple of days in sportsretailshop, my main area is runningshoes AND feets. Everyday i see kids and grownups with serious gaitproblems and other physical distortions. What i have come to see and understand is that there is some really big problems with the modern version of humans. Nobody can "see" with their feet, i worked with dancers and they always train their connection brain to feet, hands and other sensors. They live in a 3D world instead of a flat dull one. What i also see is that it is sometimes hard to explain the most fundamental aspect of bodyfunctions, nobody listen and think for their own sake. Connections body, brain, sense, behavior and so on is lost. I get shocked because i see the whole person and can sometime caught their illness just by walking for 10 meters. Some think that i am a doctor but i have no education from school, just life... I think we must look into the brain - body connection more...

### Michael — 2012-09-30T10:45:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/heart-disease.3957/post-55361>

> **prAna303;56281 wrote:** Yeah, dance have a very important aspect to human life.

I don't know of a single hunter-gatherer society that doesn't dance. One could find video on YouTube of Maori or Bushmen or Australian Aborigines pretty easily. There are enough paintings of Native Americans dancing, too.

And actually the same is true of agricultural civilizations. I don't just mean Iron Age ones like the Zulus. Everyone's surely heard of "country dancing" and what 19-century novel would be complete without a ball?

It's just us. For others dance has been something elemental and sacred or at least a pleasing way of moving in a synchronized way with others - enacting your social condition in a way - and of meeting the opposite sex.

At the turn of the century there was a huge move towards collecting folk songs and dances - something inspired by the Romantic Movement. People like Beethoven and Bruch set Scottish dance tunes. Dvorak and Janacek were doing something similar for Czechoslovakia. Cecil Sharp in England started a society for collecting folk songs and dances before they died out.

There was a kind of push among some educationists to incorporate what people like Sharp found in the curriculum. If you went to school in the 1960s in England you'd still have been taught some dances in a desultory manner. All gone now. All that they think matters is "the 3 Rs". And it never even occurs to them that kids who know how to move might learn better.

When the government in the UK set a national curriculum some 20 years ago now guess which were the last two documents to be drawn up and ratified. Music and Physical Education. Those are seen as decorations to the curriculum that fundamentally don't matter. Many schools have sold off their playing fields - I believe the same has happened in the U.S.

The bit about the people coming to buy sports equipment is interesting. Basically, their propioception is shot - even if they haven't got a specific condition.

### prAna303 — 2012-09-30T11:17:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/heart-disease.3957/post-55362>

> **Michael;56371 wrote:** The bit about the people coming to buy sports equipment is interesting. Basically, their propioception is shot - even if they haven't got a specific condition.

Very true... In a wicked sort of way many think that they can buy themself happiness, freedom, health and a working social life. In Sweden i think that the "ordinary" family has to much money and so on. We rely on a system that always takes care of us no matter what. We downgrade the schoolsystem and change the rules so the student can pass an exam even if they cannot read (for example). The part about music and dance you wrote about is so true, my generation was the last to have dedicated hours for this every year. We simply donÂ´t feed our brains with the good stuff, just the Lady Gaga junk...

### Souldanzer — 2012-09-30T11:26:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/heart-disease.3957/post-55363>

Dance for me is primarily a way of connecting with my inner self, my true primal sense, then it is a way of expression, then processing, then communicating. Rarely does it have a social purpose per say, yet often do I meet people of my kind at dance. I love watching people dance expressively.

It's quite amazing what happens when you find that true connection with your Self, when your movement becomes unintentional expression of the things you have been looking for.... when you naturally realize that you just stepped on the brakes for your left hemisphere and the accelerator of your right, when those two are in true balance amazing insights will happen.... I'm not sure we were ever meant to have a dominant hemisphere.

When you dance, you become one with yourself, one with your environment, one with the music and the beat, one with the world around you. Yet you feel the boundaries.... you feel your feet in contact with the floor, you feel your body moving through space, things are real, things are now. Dance is primal sense. If anything, I danced my way out of anorexia.

Try drumming.... drum circles. Similar magic.

# Thread: Article on Fox news about light from e-readers and sleep
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/article-on-fox-news-about-light-from-e-readers-and-sleep.3960/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### melrito — 2012-09-27T15:32:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/article-on-fox-news-about-light-from-e-readers-and-sleep.3960/post-54733>

thought everyone might like seeing this...

[http://www.foxnews.com/tech/2012/09/26/dont-let-your-e-reader-keep-up-at-night/?cmpid=cmty\_other\_Don%27t\_let\_your\_e-Reader\_keep\_you\_up\_at\_night](http://http://www.foxnews.com/tech/2012/09/26/dont-let-your-e-reader-keep-up-at-night/?cmpid=cmty_other_Don%27t_let_your_e-Reader_keep_you_up_at_night)

### Optimalbound — 2012-09-27T21:10:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/article-on-fox-news-about-light-from-e-readers-and-sleep.3960/post-54807>

Thanks for posting. There are some inaccuracies in the article, as I see it.

> Kindles and Nooks that lack a built-in light cannot adjust the screen, but because the device's e-ink displays have lower contrast, these e-readers are less disruptive to sleep.

In this context, the essential thing about the e-ink display is not the contrast, but the fact that it does not emit any light of its own. So one is free to use any light. Whenever I use my Kindle Keyboard in bed, I use a monochrome red LED lamp as my sole light source.

### melrito — 2012-09-27T23:04:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/article-on-fox-news-about-light-from-e-readers-and-sleep.3960/post-54853>

I totally agree the article doesn't cover everything - no mention of TVs or computers at night either, but it does show that some awareness is being made that artificial light does disrupt sleep (there is of course no mention of everything else it disrupts over time).

# Thread: funny moments in Epi-Paleo living
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/funny-moments-in-epi-paleo-living.3965/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 21
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### ealachan — 2012-09-27T19:20:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/funny-moments-in-epi-paleo-living.3965/post-54767>

We need more levity around here. I want to hear about peoples' funny Epi-Paleo moments. I'll go first - naturally, it involves the bathroom...

I was in the bathroom a few minutes ago taking a pee, and the smell of garlic engulfed me! Not just like a vague smell of garlic, either...but a very specific and strong toasted garlic smell. I sat there sniffing and thinking "my pee smells like garlic?! What does THAT mean?! OMG, I gotta go Ask Jack!" As I finished up in the bathroom and washed my hands, the smell kept getting stronger and stronger. I had flushed by this point, so it COULDN'T be my pee. Then I realized: there are bagels downstairs in the kitchen at work, and the kitchen is directly under the bathroom next to my office. Someone was toasting a garlic bagel for lunch, and it had me completely convinced for a minute or two that I'd had some kind of spectacular systemic breakthrough (or breakdown?!).

Well, it made ME laugh, anyway. Who's next?

### Optimalbound — 2012-09-27T20:53:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/funny-moments-in-epi-paleo-living.3965/post-54795>

I don't have a very funny story, but here's a little CT episode:

This Easter, I shared a hotel room for a week with a good friend of mine (who doesn't know about my CTing). One evening, I was in in the 50 degrees bath tub and reading a book when he came back to our room after some shopping.

Me: "I'm in the bathroom, having a soak!"

Him: "What?? Are you having a bath _again_?! You're not using up all the hot water, are you?"

Me: "Nope... Don't worry...."

I think he cleared out some biofilm that night

My food is safe now.

# Thread: Food as medicine and not entertainment
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/food-as-medicine-and-not-entertainment.3967/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 10
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### indigogirl — 2012-09-27T19:40:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/food-as-medicine-and-not-entertainment.3967/post-54771>

I am being more conscious of my food choices and noticing that in the past (recent past!) many of my choices were dictated by the entertainment value of food, not what would fuel my Ferrari.

So I am making myself eat seafood at least once a day, preferably 2x. MHS are out of the question so far, as is any raw seafood. I will build up to raw oysters! I'm eating fermented food with at least 2 meals. Need to eat more seaweed...so far toasted nori.

Curious to know how epi-Paleo is affecting your food choices?

### ealachan — 2012-09-27T19:53:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/food-as-medicine-and-not-entertainment.3967/post-54773>

I think I've definitely had this change lately as well. I'm finding it a lot easier to tell myself "yes, that beer would taste great, but it's just as bad as eating bread, so let's not", or "kelp really isn't the tastiest thing in the world to me, but eating it will do me good, so down the hatch it goes".

### prAna303 — 2012-09-27T20:58:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/food-as-medicine-and-not-entertainment.3967/post-54800>

Since i cleaned up my food choices for about two years ago and started to eat only what would be available in my region (seafoods) i completely lost emotions about eating. I see food as an energychoice to experience other feelings. I really do feel new thing nowdays, like music i can sense subtile details (classical and other instrumental). The light in the woods makes me go crazy, in a good way. The smells on top of a mountain, wow! ItÂ´s amazing when the addiction to feeling concerning food starts to loose itÂ´s grip on your psyche.

### AKMan — 2012-09-27T22:31:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/food-as-medicine-and-not-entertainment.3967/post-54839>

I have been playing around with the FitDay.com nutrient tool and came up with this awesome menu. Could be split up over a day or eaten all at once. Hits a minimum of 100% on every tracked nutrient. If you take anything away you fall below 100% somewhere.

Can anyone else beat this?

It's only 1400 calories:

8 oz (2 cans) Smoked Oysters

8 oz Chicken livers cooked in 1TBS Butter

1/2 yellow bell pepper

2oz Almonds

3oz Swiss Cheese

6 strips dried seaweed

1397 Calories, 87g Fat, 38g tot carbs, 116g Protein

[](http://http://www.jackkruse.com/forum/convert_files/attachments/55666=482-nut.JPG)

### indigogirl — 2012-09-27T22:39:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/food-as-medicine-and-not-entertainment.3967/post-54840>

> **prAna303;55623 wrote:** Since i cleaned up my food choices for about two years ago and started to eat only what would be available in my region (seafoods) i completely lost emotions about eating. I see food as an energychoice to experience other feelings. I really do feel new thing nowdays, like music i can sense subtile details (classical and other instrumental). The light in the woods makes me go crazy, in a good way. The smells on top of a mountain, wow! ItÂ´s amazing when the addiction to feeling concerning food starts to loose itÂ´s grip on your psyche.

You're senses have become much more acute!!

Looking forward to the day when food loses its grip on my psyche.

### indigogirl — 2012-09-27T22:40:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/food-as-medicine-and-not-entertainment.3967/post-54841>

> **AKMan;55666 wrote:** I have been playing around with the FitDay.com nutrient tool and came up with this awesome menu. Could be split up over a day or eaten all at once. Hits a minimum of 100% on every tracked nutrient. If you take anything away you fall below 100% somewhere.

> Can anyone else beat this?
>
>

>
>
>
> 8 oz (2 cans) Smoked Oysters
>
> 8 oz Chicken livers cooked in 1TBS Butter
>
> 1/2 yellow bell pepper
>
> 2oz Almonds
>
> 3oz Swiss Cheese
>
> 6 strips dried seaweed
>
> 1397 Calories, 87g Fat, 38g tot carbs, 116g Protein
>
>
>
>
> [](http://http://www.jackkruse.com/forum/convert_files/attachments/55666=482-nut.JPG)

Way to go Akman!

### nonchalant — 2012-09-27T22:47:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/food-as-medicine-and-not-entertainment.3967/post-54845>

> **prAna303;55623 wrote:** Since i cleaned up my food choices for about two years ago and started to eat only what would be available in my region (seafoods) i completely lost emotions about eating. I see food as an energychoice to experience other feelings. I really do feel new thing nowdays, like music i can sense subtile details (classical and other instrumental). The light in the woods makes me go crazy, in a good way. The smells on top of a mountain, wow! ItÂ´s amazing when the addiction to feeling concerning food starts to loose itÂ´s grip on your psyche.

Beautifully said.

When you're optimal, you lose the fear of being hungry, and the false bliss of the plant opioids and cannabinoids.

Now you can quit being obsessed about food, and go live your life.

### Patty Cakes — 2012-09-27T23:21:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/food-as-medicine-and-not-entertainment.3967/post-54858>

I am a recovering foodie. I am a recovering baker. I used to love the nuances in food. If it was unique, I would try it. New restaurants in town, I'd be there within two weeks of opening. Now, although I am still fascinated by food, it sits in a different place in my heart. I no longer have a love affair w/ food, I eat for brain nutrition and brain fuel. I still try to prepare it in a fun way but usually that only involves spices. But I learned 15 years ago about using spices and fresh herbs to add to the food and what a difference it can make. I have eaten organic most of my life and have always been fairly thin (except for early thyroid days) but I adored food. No more love affair and I am not sad that we broke up.

I can't wait for my senses to heighten!

### SeaHorse — 2012-09-28T00:44:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/food-as-medicine-and-not-entertainment.3967/post-54876>

I agree...the love affair with food is often a sadly abusive addiction...sometimes I still can miss a certain texture or feel, but freedom is intoxicating. It's true too that our other senses are often ignored when we are food junkies. I've been walking in our woods and it's almost peak leaf colour here in Vermont and I'm drinking it in every day. It is almost like being in an altered state of mind, but it's congruent and there is no damage or hangover...nature's last big party before the cold, sleepy winter.

### janagram — 2012-10-03T01:54:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/food-as-medicine-and-not-entertainment.3967/post-55977>

wow! Akman, this is great! But, holy moly, what about berries? what about eggs? spinach? And please, can't I have wild salmon instead of oysters? (I hear a "Noooooooo"!)

what about ....Baaaaaconnnn!!???

# Thread: Comfort food cravings - no desire for seafood!
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/comfort-food-cravings-no-desire-for-seafood.3969/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 12
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### BJK77 — 2012-09-27T20:03:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/comfort-food-cravings-no-desire-for-seafood.3969/post-54777>

I've had a cold for a few days now. Every time I think it's starting to get better I end up feeling even worse. The cold itself isn't all that terrible just a slight runny nose, but I am absolutely exhausted. I feel so drained it's tough to make it through a day.

The worst part is I have zero desire for seafood right now. I'm still forcing it in, but I'm craving my childhood comfort foods like MAD. For me, this is things like my mom's homemade chicken pot pie, pasta alfredo, chicken corn soup, chocolate chip cookies . . . you get the gist - gluten-filled, non AI-protocol friendly JUNK.

I've been on some sort of a grain-free/GAPS/Paleo diet for 3 years now and honestly haven't really had many cravings. Once I cut out grains my cravings dropped right off, but this is almost unbearable!

My teeth are also killing me which makes me want to bite down on something super crunchy . . . maybe a bowl of Cap'n Crunch. UGH! I know I'd never give in and eat SAD crap again, but wow is it tempting right now.

Bizarre! Anyone else have something similar happen when you were sick? I need to fight this off.

### cramernh — 2012-09-27T20:05:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/comfort-food-cravings-no-desire-for-seafood.3969/post-54778>

Honestly, when Im sick as a dog, all I want is homemade chicken soup. I just cooked a 5 gallon batch for my husband who is fighting a very nasty viral respiratory infection. Everything right down to the stock is freshly made.

I would highly suggest making iced cold water as your friend to help 'fill up'... its rare that I have sweet-urges thanks to being a low carber now for almost 18 months. When it does come around, I fill up my 64oz jug with ice water and Im good to go.

### indigogirl — 2012-09-27T20:19:21+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/comfort-food-cravings-no-desire-for-seafood.3969/post-54780>

I, too, crave comfort foods when I'm sick, especially when it's a stomach virus. Then all I want is broth with noodles.

I'm sure you'll be able to fight it off! Fill up on broths and warm teas??

### vlynnb — 2012-09-27T21:16:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/comfort-food-cravings-no-desire-for-seafood.3969/post-54812>

I just had a cold and I really didn't want fish either, just comfort food. And cold water just gives me the chills. Daughter made a big pot of chicken soup for her family. I settled for eaitng all the things that would be in my chicken soup minus the chicken. The raw crushed garlic clove I ate though about killed me, burned all the way down. Garlic capsules combined with hot pepper and zicam took care of the cold in a few days rather than the 10 it used to take me to get over something and then fish came back into the menu. Don't understand the fish aversion thing either.

### Souldanzer — 2012-09-27T22:01:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/comfort-food-cravings-no-desire-for-seafood.3969/post-54834>

> **BJK77;55600 wrote:** I've had a cold for a few days now. Every time I think it's starting to get better I end up feeling even worse. The cold itself isn't all that terrible just a slight runny nose, but I am absolutely exhausted. I feel so drained it's tough to make it through a day.
>
>
>
> The worst part is I have zero desire for seafood right now. I'm still forcing it in, but I'm craving my childhood comfort foods like MAD. For me, this is things like my mom's homemade chicken pot pie, pasta alfredo, chicken corn soup, chocolate chip cookies . . . you get the gist - gluten-filled, non AI-protocol friendly JUNK.
>
>
>
> I've been on some sort of a grain-free/GAPS/Paleo diet for 3 years now and honestly haven't really had many cravings. Once I cut out grains my cravings dropped right off, but this is almost unbearable!
>
>
>
> My teeth are also killing me which makes me want to bite down on something super crunchy . . . maybe a bowl of Cap'n Crunch. UGH! I know I'd never give in and eat SAD crap again, but wow is it tempting right now.
>
>
>
> Bizarre! Anyone else have something similar happen when you were sick? I need to fight this off.

read Dr K's reply to my food poisoning causing AN relapse....... if you're sick you're oxidizing as I understand. You want to reduce reduce reduce...... DHA/iodine, oxytoxin, melatonin, DHEA..... I might be off here.

I'd make some chicken soup that's paleo-friendly and see if that keeps cravings in check and do other comforting things.

### nonchalant — 2012-09-27T22:51:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/comfort-food-cravings-no-desire-for-seafood.3969/post-54846>

> **BJK77;55600 wrote:**
>
> The worst part is I have zero desire for seafood right now. I'm still forcing it in, but I'm craving my childhood comfort foods like MAD. For me, this is things like my mom's homemade chicken pot pie, pasta alfredo, chicken corn soup, chocolate chip cookies . . . you get the gist - gluten-filled, non AI-protocol friendly JUNK.

Your brain is remembering what made you feel good in the past.

Gotta love those opioids and cannabinoids. Yummy!

### BJK77 — 2012-09-28T16:38:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/comfort-food-cravings-no-desire-for-seafood.3969/post-54996>

I made it through the day without caving, but it is just amazing to me how strong these cravings are. The very odd thing is that I really haven't had any typical illnesses (cold flu, etc) in the last 3-4 years since my health started spiraling downward. It's strange that now that I am adopting such a healthy lifestyle I suddenly come down with a minor cold that has just totally landed me on my butt!

I know there are so many different theories out there about how and why we succumb to these minor illnesses, one of which I believe is that it's actually healthy to get sick on occasion. I think the theory is that it's akin to your body cleaning house and finally being able to address some of the viruses you've been exposed to. Someone explained it to me once saying that the immune system is like a housekeeper. It takes care of what it can and when it's already stressed to the max dirt/germs get stored away in corners and crevices unable to be dealt with. As the housekeeper finally starts to catch up on the "work" it can take on more and begin to tackle the tucked away dust and germs.

Anyone ever hear something similar? What do you think?

### jeepifer@gmail.com — 2012-09-28T17:52:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/comfort-food-cravings-no-desire-for-seafood.3969/post-55012>

Have you tried Yarrow tea? It's very cleansing. The key to it is, if it tastes good, you need to drink it! Drink a cup or two or three, until it tastes bitter. Then you're done. I know it sounds weird, but it really does work!

### Destiny — 2012-09-28T22:18:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/comfort-food-cravings-no-desire-for-seafood.3969/post-55074>

I am not crazy about seafood lately either but I get it down somehow. And not only that, I feel like having bread, pea soup, and a lot of potatoes! I did not miss it or think of it for almost a year. Something is changing.

Could this be due to the change in seasons?

### SeaHorse — 2012-09-28T22:26:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/comfort-food-cravings-no-desire-for-seafood.3969/post-55078>

That's what I think...the equinox was a real turning point for me...I'm feeling the cold, I don't want to CT and I'm wanting to be indoors wrapped in a blanket. I jumped in our pond yesterday and was ok while I was in...but holy moses I shivered for over an hour afterwards and had to take a warm shower...that hasn't been my CT experience since spring. I did stop swimming in early Sept. too. It's super hard to get going in the right direction and embrace the cold...particularly because I know my thyroid and hormones are still yuck.

### prAna303 — 2012-09-28T22:55:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/comfort-food-cravings-no-desire-for-seafood.3969/post-55085>

> **BJK77;55600 wrote:** I've had a cold for a few days now. Every time I think it's starting to get better I end up feeling even worse. The cold itself isn't all that terrible just a slight runny nose, but I am absolutely exhausted. I feel so drained it's tough to make it through a day.
>
>
>
> The worst part is I have zero desire for seafood right now. I'm still forcing it in, but I'm craving my childhood comfort foods like MAD. For me, this is things like my mom's homemade chicken pot pie, pasta alfredo, chicken corn soup, chocolate chip cookies . . . you get the gist - gluten-filled, non AI-protocol friendly JUNK.
>
>
>
> I've been on some sort of a grain-free/GAPS/Paleo diet for 3 years now and honestly haven't really had many cravings. Once I cut out grains my cravings dropped right off, but this is almost unbearable!
>
>
>
> My teeth are also killing me which makes me want to bite down on something super crunchy . . . maybe a bowl of Cap'n Crunch. UGH! I know I'd never give in and eat SAD crap again, but wow is it tempting right now.
>
>
>
> Bizarre! Anyone else have something similar happen when you were sick? I need to fight this off.

Perhaps itÂ´s as simple as this: your craving is deep rooted in your psyche because when you where a kid your parents gave you food as an inverted reward when you where home from school. When you now optimizing your life old memories come up to the surface and when you get sick your system goes to the autopilot mode and you find comfort in your past. Think about why you have such emotional connections to food and use it to take a new step. When we get out of the comfortzone of the modern world and get scared, sick and whatever we often find a relief in doing what we used to do... Simply put, we get scared the \*\*\*\* out of our primal senses when we get in touch with them. Use it and start a new behavior!

### Momma28 — 2012-09-29T20:05:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/comfort-food-cravings-no-desire-for-seafood.3969/post-55244>

> **vlynnb;55639 wrote:** I just had a cold and I really didn't want fish either, just comfort food. And cold water just gives me the chills. Daughter made a big pot of chicken soup for her family. I settled for eaitng all the things that would be in my chicken soup minus the chicken. The raw crushed garlic clove I ate though about killed me, burned all the way down. Garlic capsules combined with hot pepper and zicam took care of the cold in a few days rather than the 10 it used to take me to get over something and then fish came back into the menu. Don't understand the fish aversion thing either.

Wow! 10 days with a cold...The best way to beat a cold is with Xlear Nasal Spray made with xylitol and grapefruit seed extract sprayed routinely at onset of twinge symptoms every hour....then 6k to 8k vitamin C and 10k to 50k iu Vit D3 every 2 to 4 hours until symptoms dissappear. Twenty minutes in the hottest water you can stand helps, too. Bam! Knocks it right out. And we're talking hours. Haven't had a full blown miserable cold in 3 or 4 years now and I've got 8 kids. Theirs are few and far between as well. If it starts as a sore throat add Lugol's into the mix. I paint the thyroid and wrists with it. You can also up your intake of it orally as well and of course, Dr. K's fabulous Epi-Paleo diet!

# Thread: Soo, is there are ETA for the recorded QA session to go live?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/soo-is-there-are-eta-for-the-recorded-qa-session-to-go-live.3972/>
**Subforum:** Feedback/Suggestions
**Posts:** 16

### bigknitwit — 2012-09-27T21:23:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/soo-is-there-are-eta-for-the-recorded-qa-session-to-go-live.3972/post-54820>

I was unable to attend the Q&A session, but would still love to hear others questions. As a general rule, will the Q&A sessions be recorded for later listening by (delinquent) members like me who missed the live event? Generally speaking, is the ETA roughly a week after the live event? I'm sure things are crazy this month, with the site change occurring as well...

### Endless — 2012-09-27T22:53:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/soo-is-there-are-eta-for-the-recorded-qa-session-to-go-live.3972/post-54848>

My Member Dashboard says the following:

> Hi! You are currently subscribed to Dr. Kruse’s Silver Klub.
>
>
>
> You may:
>
> Submit a question to “Ask Jack” in the forums and read the library of “Ask Jack” Q&As
>
> Participate in a live monthly webinar with Dr. Kruse
>
> Sign up for an email lab consult for only $99
>
> Sign up for an educational consult: $269 for a half hour, $349 for a full hour
>
> Access the most recent three monthly webinars
>

>
>
>
> a monthly Q&A with Dr. Kruse following the monthly webinar,
>
> a FREE email lab consult,
>
> educational consults can be booked for $199 for a half hour and $299 for a full hour, and
>
> Gold Klub members can access the entire monthly webinar library on JackKruse.com.
>
> Care to upgrade? Click Here

So it sounds like Gold members are the only ones who are able to participate or listen to the Q&A. I hope that we Silvers can at least liten later, but I don't get that impression from this.

### Kiera2 — 2012-09-28T01:44:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/soo-is-there-are-eta-for-the-recorded-qa-session-to-go-live.3972/post-54891>

Does this mean that the September webinar Q&A is not accessible for Silver (ex Karnivore) members?

Even though Karnivore members paid for it, and attended with the understanding that it would be accessible?

### tellmisty — 2012-09-28T02:14:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/soo-is-there-are-eta-for-the-recorded-qa-session-to-go-live.3972/post-54904>

Silver and Gold members will have access to the Q&A that was recorded on Tuesday. I sent the wrong link to Darren to get uploaded, so the delay is my fault. He has the right one now, so I expect him to have it uploaded to the site by tomorrow.

### Endless — 2012-09-28T07:12:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/soo-is-there-are-eta-for-the-recorded-qa-session-to-go-live.3972/post-54940>

Thanks Misty! How about from here on in...are silver members going to be able to access the Q&As?

### tellmisty — 2012-09-29T03:31:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/soo-is-there-are-eta-for-the-recorded-qa-session-to-go-live.3972/post-55135>

Q&As are going to be for Gold members ... we are going to start blocking off longer blocks of time (this last Q&A was 2.5 hours long) for this session and separating it from the webinars. Just too long otherwise (or we have to do shorter Q&As).

Gold members also get an email lab consult included with their membership and the best prices on consults ... a great bundle for those who are going all-in.

# Thread: Nut cravings
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nut-cravings.3974/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 6
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### bigknitwit — 2012-09-27T21:39:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nut-cravings.3974/post-54827>

Why do I crave nuts? Not a day goes by that I don't wish I could eat nuts. Why do I crave them sooo much? I always say if I could just get back nuts and bacon in my diet, I would feel so much better! I can't eat nuts (AI) though. What's creating this unending desire? I eat seafood 1-2X/day...

### ealachan — 2012-09-27T21:54:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nut-cravings.3974/post-54831>

Is there something in the nuts that you're not getting, maybe? Seafood doesn't have EVERYTHING in it that our bodies need. Maybe you're running a deficiency on something else that your body is trying to correct.

### johnnyb — 2012-09-28T07:09:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nut-cravings.3974/post-54938>

I think nuts were a way to get carbs especially on the way tobecame a real fat burner. I think salt need may drive nut craving in me. At this point i get a brazil nut with diner and occasionally some macadamia nuts. Sometimes I wonder if it's not gut flora signaling for them.

### kathiebu@hotmail.com — 2012-09-28T15:21:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nut-cravings.3974/post-54982>

We often are addicted to food we are allergic to... I also crave nuts, but I feel terrible when I have only a few. It wil go by with time I guess.

### nonchalant — 2012-09-28T15:30:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nut-cravings.3974/post-54984>

I think it's the good quality omega 6 fats in nuts.

### prAna303 — 2012-09-28T16:28:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nut-cravings.3974/post-54992>

Is the craving 24/7 and 365? I feel a need to eat them at late autumn when they are available in their natural state where i live (now). In the summer i donÂ´t even have a thought about them. Perhaps your cravings will loosen up the more optimal and seasonal you get.

# Thread: Recipes for Krusing Kids
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/recipes-for-krusing-kids.3978/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Kids
**Posts:** 25
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Shijin13 — 2012-09-27T23:06:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/recipes-for-krusing-kids.3978/post-54854>

Ok. Ladies and Gents - for those of us who have small peoples running our lives. Lets drop in their favorite recipes, dishes, sauces etc.

These recipes can be things you make your kiddos or they can be dishes they've come up with on their own, that's made it into the "rotation"

### Shijin13 — 2012-09-27T23:09:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/recipes-for-krusing-kids.3978/post-54855>

Recipe #1: grilled squidies (squid, octopus etc)

marinate whole squid/octopus (small ones frozen from the international market) in a mixture of avocado oil, lime juice, lime zest, garlic, salt, pepper

put on grill (over top of smoker chips)

keep an eye on them... they cook quick. pull off serve, eat and wish you had more and be prepared for the next seafood meal w/o them - you will have a revolt on your hands.

### Jude — 2012-09-28T01:16:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/recipes-for-krusing-kids.3978/post-54886>

Good thread--hoping there will be lots more recipes-have a dil who is trying to implement epi paleo and needs recipes for resistant kids.

### bigknitwit — 2012-09-28T01:48:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/recipes-for-krusing-kids.3978/post-54893>

Not very imaginative, but well received by all 4 of mine: oven roasted salmon. Place salmon skin up (salted) below a cool broiler (I do ours in the toaster oven which isn't as hot as an oven broil, but still top heated), insert meat thermometer and remove when done. My kids love to pull the skin up, eat it separately, and then add more salt to the flesh itself.

### Shijin13 — 2012-09-28T03:44:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/recipes-for-krusing-kids.3978/post-54921>

[](http://http://www.jackkruse.com/forum/convert_files/attachments/55762=483-ImageUploadedByTapatalk1348792946.792897.jpg)

Not the best pic. But here they are. We buy them frozen. Come 6-8/pack kids go bonkers over then. It's their favorite seafood. I think thy'd eat squidies or octopus at every meal if they could

### Shijin13 — 2012-09-28T16:38:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/recipes-for-krusing-kids.3978/post-54995>

Egg Cups Summer Recipe:

12 eggs beaten

2c Almond Flour (coconut flour doesn't work as well)

1T Baking powder

fat of choice ( I use ghee or coconut oil) ~ 4Tbs sometimes more

vanilla, & cinnamon for flavoring

mix all ingredients, pour into muffin tins - bake at 350 until done

Serve w/ghee These things taste just like french toast! you can add maple syrup or honey if you like - but I don't.

Egg cups (Fall/Winter):

12 eggs beaten

breakfast sausage - you could also sub chopped shrimp or clams or musssels in these too!

chopped onions and garlic

spinach, broccoli, or other winter veggie chopped

cheese (If acceptable)

Fat of Choice

pour into muffin tin and bake at 350. (If you want more of a baked item add 1 cup Almond flour and 1/2T of baking powder)

2 ezpz breakfasts on the go for those crazy mornings and the kids love them!

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-09-28T17:20:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/recipes-for-krusing-kids.3978/post-55003>

ENCHILADAS

When we were transitioning away from grain foods, I made this once a week. It's a great "comfort" type food - and fun to have with company!! Also, if you haven't tried goat cheese cheddar - it's quite awesome. We like it more than the old kind. It's more spendy, but if you just sprinkle on top for the bubbly cheesy brown look, totally worth it and the block will go for a couple of meals.

The other cautionary tale is you must have a damn good non stick pan. The tortillas take a few tries to get you mojo going. You CAN make them in advance, and they freeeze nicely.

I think I got the idea from a Mark Sisson book - as a recovering foodie, I highly advise those for noobs to all of this. When you first start, all this meat/veg can be boring.

Enchiladas!

Wrap

8 egg whites

tbs almond meal if desired, works fine without

1/4 cup of cream or co milk. I have added mct oils all kinds of things to get the texture just right.

Swirl in a small frypan till it is cooked enough to hold together while you flip it. These will get nice brown spots and look awesome in the final product. You just have to mess with this part.

The rest, you can figure out! It's an art not a science!

### BJK77 — 2012-09-29T16:40:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/recipes-for-krusing-kids.3978/post-55204>

Salmon Patties

1 can Wild Alaskan salmon (14.75 oz) - I use one from Trader Joe's that has skin, bones and all!

1 lb fresh Wild Alaskan salmon

2 eggs

1 cooked sweet potato, mashed

Herbs of choice: my kids like dill weed and a little tarragon

1/2 tsp sea salt

Bacon grease or lard

Beat 2 eggs in a large bowl. Cook the fresh salmon and then flake and mix with the canned salmon in the bowl with eggs. Add mashed sweet potato herbs and sea salt. Form into patties and fry in bacon grease or lard.

My kids love these! I've found they also freeze well and they're great to have on hand for quick breakfasts or lunches. Occasionally for a treat or to make them a little different I'll coat the patties in some shredded coconut or chopped macadamia nuts before frying. I use my vitamix to make the macadamias almost into a meal/flour.

### KiwiLauren — 2012-09-30T01:10:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/recipes-for-krusing-kids.3978/post-55303>

> **BJK77;56204 wrote:** Salmon Patties
>
>
>
> 1 can Wild Alaskan salmon (14.75 oz) - I use one from Trader Joe's that has skin, bones and all!
>
> 1 lb fresh Wild Alaskan salmon
>
> 2 eggs
>
> 1 cooked sweet potato, mashed
>
> Herbs of choice: my kids like dill weed and a little tarragon
>
> 1/2 tsp sea salt
>
> Bacon grease or lard
>
>
>
> Beat 2 eggs in a large bowl. Cook the fresh salmon and then flake and mix with the canned salmon in the bowl with eggs. Add mashed sweet potato herbs and sea salt. Form into patties and fry in bacon grease or lard.
>
>
>
> My kids love these! I've found they also freeze well and they're great to have on hand for quick breakfasts or lunches. Occasionally for a treat or to make them a little different I'll coat the patties in some shredded coconut or chopped macadamia nuts before frying. I use my vitamix to make the macadamias almost into a meal/flour.

Love the addition of sweet potato. I'm going to definitely try this.

My kids often don't like seafood that is too fishy, so sauces become really important for us (I do wish Dr K would publish that sauce book!). They like curry sauces (lots of chopped cilantro, mild curry powder, lots of onions, garlic, lemon juice and olive oil) and also olive sauces (I puree olives with garlic, lemon, herbs) as well as pestos (basil pesto being a fave). I find that if I cook the fish in the sauce at a low temp for longer, it absorbs much of the flavour. The sauces work great for shrimp, too.

My son, who is the most sensitive to 'fishy' tastes, prefers his raw. I keep cold smoked salmon on hand and he makes 'sushi bites' almost every day (he invented them and says he should sell them to get rich). He takes a sheet of nori, cuts it into 4 small squares, and puts a piece of cold smoked salmon, a slice of avocado and a slice of pickle in each, along with fresh cilantro or parsley. He then folds and eats. He is 8 and can make this himself, with very little mess or clean up and (best of all for him) no fishy smell.

### BJK77 — 2012-10-01T17:28:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/recipes-for-krusing-kids.3978/post-55577>

Your son sounds like a little budding chef Lauren! I might have to see if my kids want to try out his creation. I have some smoked salmon in the fridge and they're always more likely to try and, more importantly, like something if they're involved in making it.

We made a seafood "cobb" salad for dinner last night that the kids enjoyed, but we're about at the end of the season for some of the ingredients, so we'll have to modify it for winter eating.

I just topped some of our own homegrown lettuce greens with some fresh tomatoes and peppers from our garden along with some sliced mushrooms, avocado, shrimp and crab meat.

I also fried up some bacon to crumble on top and used the grease to make a hot bacon dressing with a little apple cider vinegar, dijon mustard and a touch of honey.

### Shijin13 — 2012-10-01T20:42:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/recipes-for-krusing-kids.3978/post-55628>

Tonight we're making shrimp salad, wrapped in blanched cabbage leaves... haven't decided what we're going to put into it yet... may ask the kids to help season it up!

### Shijin13 — 2012-10-02T02:40:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/recipes-for-krusing-kids.3978/post-55719>

Made these tonight for dinner

[http://www.ibreatheimhungry.com/2012/05/spicy-shrimp-hand-rolls.html](http://http://www.ibreatheimhungry.com/2012/05/spicy-shrimp-hand-rolls.html)

modified w/a "cocktail sauce" the kids requested - as they love horseradish!

topped w/ guacamole, fish roe (we call them fish poppers!), and wrapped them in blanched cabbage leaves!

### Shijin13 — 2012-10-02T20:58:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/recipes-for-krusing-kids.3978/post-55900>

Avocado, seaweed salad, and "fish poppers"

seaweed salad - you can buy pre-made or you can make your own (I've done both)

peel and slice an avocado, shred/crumble seaweed, then scoop out "fish poppers" AKA fish roe (the kids call them fish poppers b/c they pop in their mouths!), top with balsamic vinegar

nom, nom, nom. I've also added this to regular tuna/sardine salad - and Chaos and Havoc love it!

### Shijin13 — 2012-10-03T01:57:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/recipes-for-krusing-kids.3978/post-55979>

Sausage, butternut squash and apples

pre-cook sausage, and butternut squash

throw cooked sausage, butternut squash, and chopped apples into oven proof pan, w/lots of ghee, cinnamon, nutmeg and salt.

put into oven at 350 until it smells awesome, take out and eat.

the more sausage the better!

### Momma28 — 2012-10-03T04:18:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/recipes-for-krusing-kids.3978/post-56001>

Hi Ladies,

This morning's breakfast was sweetpotatoe cassarole with bacon and sunny side up eggs. Sweet potatoe cass. was super easy. Bake pot. and peel. Mash and add eggs and and spices. I used cinn, nutmeg and clove. I added salt, vanilla and stevia glycerite. I blended in a little almond milk as well, topped with walnuts and baked.

Dinner was Mini Meatloaves wrapped in bacon (which were adorable!), broccoli cassarole and left over sweet potatoe. Mini meatloaves was grassfed beef with pureed butternut squash added in for softer texture with eggs and spices. Take about 2 tbls and roll in ball and wrap with 5 in piece of bacon and set on cookie tray. Fill tray and bake at 350 for 45 mins or so. Broccoli cassarole was frozen thawed broccoli with eggs, 1/2 cups mayo and spices of your choice. Bake in same oven with mini meatloaves and serve. The kids loved it!

### Momma28 — 2012-10-03T04:21:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/recipes-for-krusing-kids.3978/post-56003>

Oh, and I forgot last night's fab dinner....Fish Taco Salad... Cod was seasoned with an organic taco seasoning by Frontier and baked till done. Guacamole was made from fresh avocados...a bed of organic mixed greens was placed in a large flat bowl, topped with the fish and some of the juices for dressing and the guac and salsa were added. OMG! Fabulous! Kids loved it and so did hubby! I never missed the cheese or sour cream.

### Momma28 — 2012-10-03T04:32:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/recipes-for-krusing-kids.3978/post-56009>

OK...one more... my kids' and hubby's all-time fav...Meatza! make a "crust" with the ground beef just like you would an italian meatball. Press into bottom of cookie sheet. Bake at 400 for 10 mins. just till light brown. Take out and top with pizza or pasta sauce you make or your choice...add onions, olives, mushrooms...whatever you like...add more seasonings...we LOVE Frontier's Pizza Seasoning...pop back in oven another 30 mins until done and voila! You'll never miss the bread crust and your kids will feel like there is no other pizza in the world. Serve with salad.

### BJK77 — 2012-10-03T17:11:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/recipes-for-krusing-kids.3978/post-56058>

This is a great paleo "chili" for those following the AI protocol and an excellent way to get some good anti-inflammatory spices in the kids! The flavors are so rich that you can also easily hide in some seafood and/or offal to sneak it into squeamish kids

I made it last night and my kids, who generally don’t like anything “spicy,” devoured it in no time! They had it on a bed of homegrown greens and topped it with some homemade raw sour cream.

2 lb ground beef

2 c. beef broth

Â¼ cup tallow

2 c. diced carrots

1 c spinach

2 c. diced zucchini

1/2 c mushroom powder - this was just a powder I blended from mushrooms I dehydrated - you could just add fresh mushrooms

2 T cumin

1.5 T turmeric

1 tsp cloves

2 tsp coriander

2 tsp sea salt

1 T liquid smoke

\*Anything you’d like to sneak in  I added 2 cans of sardines . . . shhh – don’t tell my kids! They’ll eat most kinds of seafood, but I have yet to get my daughter to willingly eat sardines. My son will as long as she isn’t around to make faces and convince him that they are disgusting. Paybacks

\*Note: I’m just estimating the amount of veggies here. I used what I had just gotten fresh out of the garden. I’m sure you could play around with them and still have a delicious meal.

I browned the beef in the tallow while the veggies cooked in the broth. Mixed the two together, added the spices and let it simmer for about an hour to blend the flavors. Easy as can be!

### Shijin13 — 2012-10-03T18:01:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/recipes-for-krusing-kids.3978/post-56064>

> **Momma28;57222 wrote:** OK...one more... my kids' and hubby's all-time fav...Meatza! make a "crust" with the ground beef just like you would an italian meatball. Press into bottom of cookie sheet. Bake at 400 for 10 mins. just till light brown. Take out and top with pizza or pasta sauce you make or your choice...add onions, olives, mushrooms...whatever you like...add more seasonings...we LOVE Frontier's Pizza Seasoning...pop back in oven another 30 mins until done and voila! You'll never miss the bread crust and your kids will feel like there is no other pizza in the world. Serve with salad.

Meatza is always a win. We have it a couple times/month. I've even made it using Sean Conces' recipe from [http://freerange-human.com/2012/03/26/ketogenic-pizza/](http://http://freerange-human.com/2012/03/26/ketogenic-pizza/)

awesome... Just awesome

### Shijin13 — 2012-10-03T18:02:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/recipes-for-krusing-kids.3978/post-56065>

> **BJK77;57284 wrote:** This is a great paleo "chili" for those following the AI protocol and an excellent way to get some good anti-inflammatory spices in the kids! The flavors are so rich that you can also easily hide in some seafood and/or offal to sneak it into squeamish kids
>
>
>
> I made it last night and my kids, who generally don’t like anything “spicy,” devoured it in no time! They had it on a bed of homegrown greens and topped it with some homemade raw sour cream.
>
>
>
> 2 lb ground beef
>
> 2 c. beef broth
>
> Â¼ cup tallow
>
> 2 c. diced carrots
>
> 1 c spinach
>
> 2 c. diced zucchini
>
> 1/2 c mushroom powder - this was just a powder I blended from mushrooms I dehydrated - you could just add fresh mushrooms
>
> 2 T cumin
>
> 1.5 T turmeric
>
> 1 tsp cloves
>
> 2 tsp coriander
>
> 2 tsp sea salt
>
> 1 T liquid smoke
>
> \*Anything you’d like to sneak in  I added 2 cans of sardines . . . shhh – don’t tell my kids! They’ll eat most kinds of seafood, but I have yet to get my daughter to willingly eat sardines. My son will as long as she isn’t around to make faces and convince him that they are disgusting. Paybacks
>
>
>
> \*Note: I’m just estimating the amount of veggies here. I used what I had just gotten fresh out of the garden. I’m sure you could play around with them and still have a delicious meal.
>
>
>
> I browned the beef in the tallow while the veggies cooked in the broth. Mixed the two together, added the spices and let it simmer for about an hour to blend the flavors. Easy as can be!

mmmmmm chilli...
