### maggie — 2012-10-02T03:40:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-5th-most-important-thing-you-need-to-know-about-diet-and-exercise.4022/post-55740>

> **Michael;56794 wrote:**
>
> I suggest that it doesn't, of course, prove that animals are always "wiser" than humans, or that instinct is always superior to reason. It's just an illustration that sometimes our ability to be able to have concepts at all can lead us astray.

Here's a random factoid that I've always found fascinating. I found the info years ago when I was writing a little piece on Dr. Rodriguez for a textbook I worked on and it stuck in my mind--the primal sense of animals! Just had to insert (from Wikipedia):

Zoopharmacognosy refers to the process by which non-Human animals self-medicate, by selecting and using plants, soils, and insects to treat and prevent disease. Coined by Dr. Eloy Rodriguez, a biochemist and professor at Cornell University, the word is derived from roots zoo ("animal"), pharma ("drug"), and gnosy ("knowing").\[1\]

Observers have noticed that some species ingest non-foods, such as toxic plants, clay or charcoal, to ward off parasitic infestation or poisoning.\[2\] Jane Goodall witnessed chimpanzees eating certain bushes to make themselves sick, and substantial evidence indicates that they swallow whole the leaves of certain rough-leaved plants, such as Aneilema aequinoctiale, in order to remove parasitic worms from their intestines.\[3\] Some Brazilian parrots eat kaolin (a form of clay).

Illustrating the medicinal knowledge of some species, apes have been observed selecting a particular part of a medicinal plant by taking off leaves, then breaking the stem to suck out the juice.\[4\] In an interview with Neil Campbell, Rodriguez describes the importance of biodiversity to medicine:

Some of the compounds we've identified by zoopharmacognosy kill parasitic worms, and some of these chemicals may be useful against tumors. There is no question that the templates for most drugs are in the natural world.\[4\]

William Astor has observed self-medicative behavior in captive estrildid finches.\[5\] The finches, of several different species, were observed to correctly target appropriate antibiotics out of a range made available to them, through which they were able to cure themselves of life threatening diseases (as opposed to relatively minor ailments like indigestion). These finches did not learn this self-medicative behavior from their parents or others in the flock through social learning like apes and chimpanzees are suspected of doing. This ability of estrildid finches seems to be innate

### Inger — 2012-10-02T18:55:57+0300

And don't forget to add seaweed to every dish you make! And then try to find a store that sells Macrel heads to make smoothies from. And don't overcook/burn your meat or it will become carcinogenic! Better yet eat it raw or rotten! And what's better - not to eat lunch if i'm not hungry, or eat it because I need to eat as much seafood as I can? What about farm-raised salmon? is it better then grass-fed beef liver? Is almond flour bad for you? Can I use cream in my coffee? Should I give up eggs? what kind of blue-light blockers to buy? how often to CT at what temperature? Put my feet in or keep them out to stay longer? I can go on and on.
>
>
>
> It's becoming too much for me, I think you are right that it produces more stress for me. Everybody needs to find the right balance for themselves. I don't blame dr. Kruse for providing all this information and striving to be optimal, I blame myself for getting lost in it.
>
>
>
> Barry, thanks for your post.

Anna.. how strange how different we can feel...

To me all of this you write above do not even cross my mind really. I read something.. I instinctively know it is better then I did before.. and I do it. I am thinking very little of food actually, and I am very strict one could say. It is not stressing me one bit. I want to feel great. Great!!! Then I know what I have to do. It is so easy, just do.

I must admit I have hard time to see why some great information could be a burden..? Espesially when it makes life easier.. cheap and easy? I never felt that way.. why? Then I would only stay in my way to better health?

If I am not ready to give something up I wait. I do it when i feel like it but it does not stress me. Step by step.. the road is fun.. you never know what comes around the next corner.. it is soo exiting.. Because it is always something better... and better.. wow!

### Barry — 2012-10-02T22:32:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-5th-most-important-thing-you-need-to-know-about-diet-and-exercise.4022/post-55935>

My posts are to encourage people to look further afield and to get people to think about the big picture. They are not here to stimulate bickering and pettiness. That's not part of the big picture.

I'm also not looking to create an "us" and "them". There's no slander or insult intended. Say what you have to say if it has a valid point and if it is constructive. If not, leave it be.

Some get where I'm coming from, some don't, fine. We're all on different paths. The important thing to realise is that there is no "best" path to be on. I'm not saying my path is the best. Just be happy with the one you're on, make the most of it and enjoy the scenery as you go along..

### Optimalbound — 2012-10-02T23:21:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-5th-most-important-thing-you-need-to-know-about-diet-and-exercise.4022/post-55953>

> **shah78;56766 wrote:** Can you find that study with the diabetic rats curing themselves. That is outstanding! That's a "smoking gun" type of evidence, on the level of Nixon's "secret" tapes!

It's on page 430 of Taubes' GCBC (location 8613 in the Kindle edition)

The study was by Carl Richter of John Hopkins way back in the 1930s.

I once saw a documentary about a tribe somewhere in Africa. Pretty much a stone age type culture. The interesting thing was that the pregnant women of that tribe regularly walked quite a long distance to a particular hill where they ate the soil or clay. They had apparently done so for countless generations. One western antropologist or biologist got interested and analyzed samples of this soil and found that it was very high in certain minerals that were lacking in the ordinary diet of the tribe! When asked why they ate the soil, the pregnant women answered something along the lines of "We just crave it". Makes you wonder, doesn't it?

(Unfortunately, I don't remember the names of the tribe, the country or the minerals..)

> **Anna K.;57184 wrote:** Inger, I'm glad that you find it all very easily manageable. I think one reason for that could be that you look at food as pure medicine - it's there to make you the healthiest possible person you can be. And that's great!
>
>
>
> For me, as I'm sure it is for many people, food is also a source of great pleasure. That makes the choices so much more difficult. I want to be the healthiest I can be too, but I also want to enjoy what I'm eating. I want to have fun and Food is FUN!!! And so finding the right balace between health and pleasure at each meal could get overwhelming because the "healthiest" food is often not the "tastiest". They often overlap, but still leave a lot of room for internal debates. It doesn't matter how "healthy" my brain can potentially get, it is very unlikely to ever get to the point of enjoying fish head smoothies or "high" meat.
>
>
>
> I don't mean to sound derogatory to people who eat that, I'm glad you do it and I'm sure it's good for you. But I'm having enough hard time to figure out what to fry my shrimp in to avoid either saturated fats stopping DHA absorption, or overheating my olive oil and making it rancid. And cherry tomatoes work so great with shrimp, but they are nightshades,   I can't even begin to try to figure out where I can find Mackerel heads in Miami and if they are fresh enough for me to drink them raw.
>
>
>
> Here is another example for you. I've purchased pre-shacked oysters today, so should I eat them raw to get all the benefits or fry them (in what?) to make sure I don't get sick? BTW, do they have some preservatives in them to begin with to maintain freshness? Need to look into this, lol. They are also farm-raised, so not the best choice clearly. What's more optimal - 1/2 dozen of freshly shacked or 2 dozen of pre-shacked? Inger, what would you do?
>
>
>
> So I try and fail every day, I'm not 100% optimal, but I'm beginning to think that I should try less. May be 80% optimal +20% fun is where it is 100% optimal for me.
>
>
>
> thanks for listening to my rant.

Anna.. I see you. You ask, what would I do with the oysters? Hmm.. I would search and do the best I can. To me searching is fun. It is like playing games.. or exploring. I learn a bunch. Then I do the best I can. I test too. I test on myself (but only on myself.. never feed anything you are nor sure is safe to anybody else!!! If I make a wormy MHS and my bro wants too, I warn him.. there might be worms in here.. that could be dangerous.. I drink it but maÃ½be you should not). It is fun too. If I get no answer no where I just test it out, then I know for sure. I never test anything deadly dangerous but close..lol. Just kidding. I always research well before I test anything that I make sure there will be no irreversible damage. I read about others experiense.

Anna.. I enjoy my food very much. No, not the mackerel heads.. not yet.. lol and the rotten meat is just a little and occasional, it is not a meal or anything close.

Taste is very much conditioning too. You will start to like oysters after you ate them a few times, but maybe not the first time. At least that was how it went for me. You just need to have a open mind and let your body adjust.. in the beginning it do need some dicipline and will to outstand some uncomfortableness. If I eat something yucky I always give myself something I very much enjoy after. Like salmon tartar.

If I feel not so well.. I know to eat more yucky food..lol that is when I go very strict and forget about enjoyment totally. I need to feel well that is my first priority. When I do that I feel well again very fast! And I need less yucky stuff..lol.

What enjoyment is a life feeling unwell? Wasted to me. It is a wasted life.

Anna.. think about it not like you were failing.. you are learning! That is fun! Learning is fun! And to learning mistakes are tied. That is how it is..lol. Nothing to stress about. Baby steps. The direction matters.. not how fast it goes. I go very slow!

Oh.. and about the frying.. I do not fry anything actually.. maybe some mushrooms in coconut oil.. I cook stuff in a little water or fishbroth and pour olive oil on it after ready. Tastes great! It is just about to learn some new cooking skills. Frying creates AGEs too and cooking always is better. Or eating plain.. or raw.. I can tell you one can make increadible delicious food with just raw seafood too!

I look at Jacks protocol like a goal I am coming closer, like the direction where I am hiding. I am so very thankful to him because he opened my eyes big time. Without him I would never have realized it is bad to sit in front of the computer late at night.. or having all these fake lights on. Or how bad stress actually is! Or that it is good to have it on the cool side.. to embrace the cold. I always thought.. the warmer the better!!! How wrong was I! I am not sitting one hour in a ice bath. Not even close. I just dip into the river instead of showering and do some minor other stuff like facedunks. Just a few. I am not pushing me. But I am not scared of the cold anymore at all.. and that makes all the difference. I have it cooler now in my appartement. A little less clothes. I take baby steps and I enjoy it so much. I concentrate on seafood now instead of meat. I like it.

I like ALL about this trip. I am hiding in the right direction and that is what counts, not how fast I walk! I know where to go. That itself is beautiful. And the trip needs to be joyful because every day is so much worth. What if I were to die tomorrow? We never know. I need to embrace life fully, to enjoy the moment. I do not want to spend one single day in negative moods.. self pity.. feeling bad.. If detox goes too bad that I suffer I cut back. That is how I am. I need to enjoy every day. Others takes the suffering and go faster. I am not like that..lol..

### Dali Dula — 2012-10-03T16:01:05+0300

Inger, we have a very similar approach. I am on this path, not running, but walking steadily, not doing anything dangerous, but always moving forward. Not caring what others think, they have no idea how empowering it is to treat yourself right. I feel better everyday and I am enjoying the journey even with all it's trials. As one thing changes it opens the door for another change. It doesn't happen all at once. Adaptation does not have to be an emergency. I measure stress in my life. Is a certain thing important enough to stress about? More and more I say no and I leave it behind. Everytime I do this I increase my ability to let go. Letting go is freedom. You continue to be an inspiration.

### Chrysanthemama — 2012-10-08T19:36:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-5th-most-important-thing-you-need-to-know-about-diet-and-exercise.4022/post-56908>

> **shah78;56798 wrote:** I've avoided reading GC,BC because he's preaching to the choir(ME) and it is 500 pages.

I just read Why We Get Fat, and I have to say that each and every one of these books that provide research-based evidence about what we, the choir already know, the more solid my own understanding gets, and the better I get at being able to understand and explain what I am doing, and why.

And Barry, this conversation is just awesome. I love it. I love what people are saying, and I love the discussion. I have found that as I first started navigating the paleo world, I was overwhelmed with information, and I found myself questioning which choice to make and worrying over some details that didn't need to be worried about. I was not broken when I found this site, but I was certainly on a road to being there only it was micro-brewed beer that was my downfall, not much else. I have eaten "well" my whole life thanks to my location and early influences. I am an athlete and an herbalist. But beer was breaking me and it took some serious soul searching to wake up and stop.

Thankfully, as a result of this site, and The Monk Who Sold His Ferrari, and Wheat Belly, and The Art and Science of Low-Carb Performance, I am back on track, and feeling really good. I still have some work to do, mentally, emotionally, physically, but I am finding my own balance based on my intuition, which is what I've always trusted.

Thank you all for this conversation, I really do think it's worthwhile.

### Michael — 2012-10-09T16:00:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-5th-most-important-thing-you-need-to-know-about-diet-and-exercise.4022/post-57095>

Sometimes I feel that what the mind can do might surprise us, and that much of what it can do may have little to do with conscious chains of reasoning.

Some of the WAPF people - like Dr. Cowan - seem more open to that kind of thing than, say, paleo.

And I can probably say the following here without provoking outrage.

I got and read a couple of books on the "sleeping prophet" because Cowan mentioned him and came to the conclusion that something pretty interesting was going on when Cayce was out of his normal waking state. What impressed me was that his "prophecies" were so bad (many refuted now by the passage of time) yet the "cures" do seem to have worked in many cases. And again his esoteric notions of what the world was like and how his unusual gift fitted in seem like bunkum to me, but the \*empirical\* side of the "readings" ...

If someone in a trance says, "The tin you want is on the top shelf" in a building he doesn't know in a town he's never visited, and it is ... You can only explain stuff like that in a manner that would satisfy "skeptics" by positing collusion in deceit by multiple people.

If people must, I guess, and sure deceit does happen where unusual things seem to be occurring (c.f. Houdini's investigations.) But just maybe our ideas of what the mind can do are off.

### janagram — 2012-10-09T22:45:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-5th-most-important-thing-you-need-to-know-about-diet-and-exercise.4022/post-57156>

interesting....it seems to me that prophecy differs from the other ("knowing" where a tin in a building is) by not having happened yet! I imagine that "prophets" tune into threads of possibility and can see how they would spin out, but then they don't always unfold that way, for so many reasons...i don't think Cayce was bunkum.

### chocolate — 2012-10-10T03:08:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-5th-most-important-thing-you-need-to-know-about-diet-and-exercise.4022/post-57216>

The sleeping prophet is smarter than my wakeful husband. He called me after I had been trapped in the house for a week with him post spinal fusion and asked me where I kept his ice. I think anyone would know that had never been to my house.

But I do believe the brains in some people contain infinite and amazing things. If you want to see how vast a mind is, watch someone brilliant trapped inside and trying to fight their way out. It's huge and we really do carry things with us for an eternity and relive them. Protein is very powerful.

### Optimalbound — 2012-10-10T19:41:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-5th-most-important-thing-you-need-to-know-about-diet-and-exercise.4022/post-57368>

Dr. Alexander writes:

> There is no scientific explanation for the fact that while my body lay in coma, my mindâ€”my conscious, inner selfâ€”was alive and well.
>
>
> That is _not a fact, as I see it. What _is_ a fact is only that he remembers it that way_. We just don't know where those memories came from. I think a better phrasing would be "There is no scientific explanation why I have these memories of being concious during my coma".

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-10-10T20:10:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-5th-most-important-thing-you-need-to-know-about-diet-and-exercise.4022/post-57377>

It's a little interesting - but I know I've read long ago about actually mapping the neuronal activity of people having both near death and alien abduction feelings. I think this was in an ethics class of all things. Interpretation of the event depends on external and genetic factors (do you have spiritual gene or not, plus upbringing).

It's a brain fart, interpreted with each individual's bias. The person with the experience essentially HAS the experience. Their body reacts for all intents and purposes as if it truely happened. It was just all in their head in the life of their mind, not our physical world.

Now, if "divine intervention" caused the brain fart - that would merit the existence of God conversation. . . but having the experience is a repeatable and explainable phenomena.

It goes to an expanded discussion of the age old questions - if it happens in your mind and body (most spiritual things are in the beholders mind, not in my reality) does that make it more or less true.

### Michael — 2012-10-10T20:31:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-5th-most-important-thing-you-need-to-know-about-diet-and-exercise.4022/post-57384>

But the interesting thing about this case is that he didn't have a brain that would seem to be capable of "farting":

> Within hours, my entire cortex—the part of the brain that controls thought and emotion and that in essence makes us human—had shut down.

In any case, I think the trouble with the kind of explanation that you were given in the course is that it assumes a certain picture of the world and rules out anything that doesn't fit in with that. One might feel more comfortable with the world by doing that - it's predictable and understandable and obeys such and such a ruleset - but whether it gets you to the truth is another matter.

Why cannot we say something along the lines of "What is going on here is interesting, and moreover seems to contradict some pictures people have of what the world is like, but we don't really understand what occurred"?

### Michael — 2012-10-11T10:32:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-5th-most-important-thing-you-need-to-know-about-diet-and-exercise.4022/post-57538>

I suspect the problem remains where William James left it in his Gifford Lectures (later published as the _Varieties of Religious Experience_). And I would think that is always going to be the case. No-one can prove that the man's interpretation of his own experience is false, but his account of it can't possibly be coercive for anyone else.

### Linz — 2012-10-11T14:46:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-5th-most-important-thing-you-need-to-know-about-diet-and-exercise.4022/post-57551>

The ability of our senses certainly improves but can it expand beyond our normal conciousness with ketosis, improved membrane structure/function and a full complement of DHA for electrical sensitivity?

How do we sense geopathic stress? I certainly think my ears give me some warnings.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-10-11T14:55:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-5th-most-important-thing-you-need-to-know-about-diet-and-exercise.4022/post-57553>

Your brain does this.......and if you look at levee number 30 it is built into the Quilt. I just have not spoken about it yet.

### Michael — 2012-10-11T15:17:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-5th-most-important-thing-you-need-to-know-about-diet-and-exercise.4022/post-57562>

> **Linz;58868 wrote:** The ability of our senses certainly improves but can it expand beyond our normal conciousness with ketosis, improved membrane structure/function and a full complement of DHA for electrical sensitivity?
>
> How do we sense geopathic stress? I certainly think my ears give me some warnings.

That's something I've wondered about, too.

I have no very certain religious beliefs (and some very sceptical moods). And, like many people, I don't think about it much of the time, but yet ...

The other thing that interests me is that some of the people who do neurofeedback say that when the different areas of the brain important for normal waking consciousness are in better balance and other areas are strengthened some people begin to report "spiritual" type experiences.

Also I'm not entirely happy at dismissing what many generations of people in the past have experienced as just illusion or the beliefs of primitives. Much of it may be, but how can I know it _all_ is? Maybe, as with food, they know things and have experienced things that I haven't. There's an old argument to the effect that if your dog sees you reading a newspaper he probably concludes that you're smelling it. In fact, there's a whole world of experience there that he's unaware of, because it's outside his experience. We can always apply reductive explanations to phenomena; doesn't mean we're right to do so.

### Dali Dula — 2012-10-11T15:37:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-5th-most-important-thing-you-need-to-know-about-diet-and-exercise.4022/post-57564>

Here's a fun postulate: Nothing outside your sensory experience exists and you can't prove it does. For example: As you leave for work in the morning as soon as your house is out of your sensory experience it does not exist. Well, I'll call home and speak to my wife and ask her if the house is there. My sensory experience is talking to my wife on the phone, but that doesn't mean she or my house exists. Nothing exists if you are not experiencing it and you can't prove that it does. Walk around with that in your head today.

### Michael — 2012-10-12T08:38:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-5th-most-important-thing-you-need-to-know-about-diet-and-exercise.4022/post-57713>

> **Linz;58868 wrote:** The ability of our senses certainly improves but can it expand beyond our normal conciousness with ketosis ...

That tripped something off for me later.

I suddenly remembered the way people have used fasting - it would be involved in a Native American vision quest, for example - and isn't fasting ketosis?

Like many people interested in prehistory I've got David Lewis-Willisms' book _The Mind in the Cave_.

[http://www.amazon.com/The-Mind-Cave-Consciousness-Origins/dp/0500284652/](http://http://www.amazon.com/The-Mind-Cave-Consciousness-Origins/dp/0500284652/)

Lewis-Williams, Professor Emeritus at the University of the Witwatersrand, believes that the cave paintings were done in a state of trance. This is partly based upon features of the paintings - for example the superimposed geometric designs, which are what one would see going into trance (phosphenes) - but also draws on accounts of the few people yet making these in last few centuries, specifically Native Americans and Bushmen.

Lewis-Williams gives a list of ways people have been known to use to get into altered states of consciousness: auditory driving, dancing, sensory deprivation, fasting, pain, cold, use of plant poisons, and on and on. I suppose I had been vaguely thinking of all these simply as disrupters of normal functioning, but it's interesting that fasting, when you think about it, has this connection with a quite special metabolic state.

Lewis-Williams believes that hominids other than Homo sapiens, such as Neanderthals, may not have been able to get into altered states of consciousness, and this may be one of the reasons why they seem to have left no art.

I think the Bradshaw Foundation for rock art now has some articles by him:

[http://www.bradshawfoundation.com](http://http://www.bradshawfoundation.com)

### Linz — 2012-10-12T17:17:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-5th-most-important-thing-you-need-to-know-about-diet-and-exercise.4022/post-57756>

Perhaps these extremes are disrupters of normal function but in optimal health what is normal function of the senses?

Amazing artistic or musical abilities are obvious to everyone and seen as special but a super sense of smell like our dogs would probably be interpreted as eccentric, and sensing unseen electrical/magnetic forces as deeply suspicious.

Is a cat's purr involuntary like shivering when it needs to protect itself from danger, stress or pain, does it make a conscious effort to purr because it needs to or both? Strange that Western medical science (or even vet science) pays little attention to the self healing ability of cats.

### Michael — 2012-10-12T17:50:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-5th-most-important-thing-you-need-to-know-about-diet-and-exercise.4022/post-57778>

IOW what is optimal normal function of the senses? Interesting question.

I think the point about the art is that people imagined an artist with a brush making some sort of artistic statement - like someone might in our society. But that's anachronistic.

If you look at any art - go wander around the Tate or the Prado or anywhere - almost everything pre about 1500 is likely to be "religious" in some sense of the word. Secular art took centuries to emerge.

Add to that the further back into the past you go, the less doctrinal "religion" is likely to be and the more concerned with direct experience (real or illusory as you like). Someone like Aquinas couldn't have existed in a hunter-gatherer society.

### Michael — 2012-10-12T20:04:24+0300

### cookworm — 2012-09-30T05:23:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/co-and-epipaleo.4023/post-55344>

Is it still a good idea to include coconut oil while going more epipaleo?

### indigogirl — 2012-09-30T05:35:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/co-and-epipaleo.4023/post-55346>

Cookworm, it all depends upon your context as Jack would say.

To better absorb the DHA from seafood one should eat seafood with MUFAS.

As we go into fall and winter we should not use coconut oil as it is a tropical oil.

However, if you are really "broken" or obese I think coconut oil should still be used.

It really depends upon your needs.

# Thread: Crispy Fish Skin...the new bacon!
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/crispy-fish-skin-the-new-bacon.4025/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 19
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### kathylu — 2012-09-30T15:42:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/crispy-fish-skin-the-new-bacon.4025/post-55376>

I remember having crispy fish skin rolls in sushi restaurants and thinking how yummy they were, but I had never tried to make one before. So I thought, "Why not?"

Went to the asian fish market, had them filet some fish for dinner for me and asked them to package up the heads, bones (of course!) and the skin. They didn't even blink!

Rinse the fish skin and dry it on paper towels, then fry it in bacon fat until crisp. Sprinkle with a little salt, eat and enjoy! It was crisp and delicious, very close to bacon in flavor, not "fishy" at all. I'm sure you could use coconut oil too.

The entire family liked it. Try it.

### nonchalant — 2012-09-30T16:30:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/crispy-fish-skin-the-new-bacon.4025/post-55381>

Thanks for the idea, kathylu. I haven't tried it in bacon fat. Mmmm.

One of the online fish stores...I think it might have been the one Jack suggested sells crispy fish skins for PETS!!! They may not scale the skin, but I think there is a goldmine in the concept..."Fish Crisps"

Salmon skin is particularly yummy in bacon fat.

**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet

### Raiken3712 — 2012-09-30T17:25:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cod-liver-oil-vtamin-butter-cod-liver-oil-blend-or-emulsified-cod-liver-oil.4027/post-55390>

[http://www.greenpasture.org/public/Products/EmulsifiedCodLiverOil/index.cfm](http://http://www.greenpasture.org/public/Products/EmulsifiedCodLiverOil/index.cfm)

[http://www.greenpasture.org/public/Products/CodLiverOil/index.cfm](http://http://www.greenpasture.org/public/Products/CodLiverOil/index.cfm)

[http://www.greenpasture.org/public/Products/ButterCodLiverBlend/index.cfm](http://http://www.greenpasture.org/public/Products/ButterCodLiverBlend/index.cfm)

I've been thinking about getting some Cod Liver oil....but I'm not sure which one is best. I figure its a cheaper way than buying enough wild caught fish. If I had the money I would buy my fill of wild caught fish and other seafood. I just don't have it right now. Extra costs at the moment don't help.

Which of these supplements to help raise levels is the best option? I'm guessing the Butter oil blend isn't good because of the saturated fat blocking absorption of the PUFA?

I looked up emulsification because I didn't remember exactly what it was....and I'm not at all clear which one is better. Also I was thinking of the Mint supplement....but would it be better to get Cinnamon or unflavored?

Or perhaps I would be better off getting another supplement? I'm mostly looking for Iodine because of Thyroid issues. The Selenium, Vitamin D....and others is an added plus. Also I'm wondering how nasty this stuff is...any tips on taking it...I've heard it can be pretty bad. Should I take the standard dose or twice that?

I guess something else that might help is my [Omega 3/6 Assay](http://http://www.keepandshare.com/doc/4638949/redacted-bloodspot-fatty-acid-profile-pdf-183k?da=y)

I'm also curious about the Vitamin D/A antagonism. This works in a similar way as the PUFA/SAFA antagonism? Would the body not absorb some of it based on what it needs? Or do you absorb very little of the A and D because they fight for absorption...

It makes sense to me that you would absorb something....as just because two things are fighting for receptors shouldn't stop either one from being used at all.....also what prevents them from being used afterwards.

If say the Vitamin D is absorbed and the body has no room to absorb anymore Vitamin D or A I guess the body gets rid of the excess before it can be used?

### hope2conceive — 2012-09-30T18:16:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cod-liver-oil-vtamin-butter-cod-liver-oil-blend-or-emulsified-cod-liver-oil.4027/post-55404>

Hi, have you checked out Weston A Price? You can find more info on a lot of your questions in these linked articles:

[http://www.westonaprice.org/cod-liver-oil](http://http://www.westonaprice.org/cod-liver-oil)

CLO is an amazing superfood based on the research of Weston A Price. I'm not sure how much Dr K thinks of it but it can do some really good things. There are testimonials all over these pages.

As far as the butter oil, Price found that a Vitamin K source, such as butter oil, was needed for absorption of Vitamin A and D in the CLO which is the main goal for using CLO though the benefits are so varied. From the site:

Because it is so difficult to obtain adequate fat-soluble activators in the modern diet, Dr. Price recommended cod liver oil to provide vitamins A and D, along with a source of vitamin K \[which Dr Price used to call Activator x\], such as butter from grass-fed animals or what he called high-vitamin butter oil, made by low-temperature centrifuging of butter from cows eating rapidly growing grass. Consumed in liberal amounts during pregnancy, lactation and the period of growth, these nutrients ensure the optimal physical and mental development of children; consumed by adults, these nutrients protect against acute and chronic disease.

> I've been thinking about getting some Cod Liver oil....but I'm not sure which one is best. I figure its a cheaper way than buying enough wild caught fish. If I had the money I would buy my fill of wild caught fish and other seafood. I just don't have it right now. Extra costs at the moment don't help.
>
>
>
> Which of these supplements to help raise levels is the best option?

I think Jack's preference would be to eat the whole cod liver. That's what he has linked from his site:

[http://jackkruse.com/recommended-products/](http://http://jackkruse.com/recommended-products/)

You can see "Rugenfisch Cod Liver in Own Juice and Oil" there.

I'd guess that's because it's coming as the full package that way and you get iodine, etc with it that way.

The green pastures CLO might be a reasonable option, though, since it is fermented not processed with heat. I don't know if he's commented on it specifically. I would stay away from the one that's emulsified with mint oil - I had a bottle of that and when I wasn't very far down the bottle it stopped coming out. It turned out it had set solid. I don't know if it was the emulsification process that had caused that, but once bitten twice shy. I have had the one called Oslo Orange and that was OK.

### Raiken3712 — 2012-09-30T21:05:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cod-liver-oil-vtamin-butter-cod-liver-oil-blend-or-emulsified-cod-liver-oil.4027/post-55451>

Have any clue what the cost comparison is? How much would eating that Cod Liver cost to equal the same number of servings as the Cod Liver Oil. My decision of the Cod Liver oil is based on cost per serving and ease of use.

Non emulsified is 118 servings is 37 cents a serving at 44.00. 118 servings if taken one a day is 4 months or so. Emulsified seems to have less servings...not sure of the advantage or disadvantage. I don't even see anything on their page for why you would want one over the other.

As far as the Butter oil...I use Kerrygold Irish Butter so figure I can get my K from that. I'm just not sure about the whole issue with absorption of PUFA with SAFA. Jack mentioned that we shouldn't eat saturated fats with fish as I think he said PUFA and SAFA compete for absorption....similar to the Vitamin D/A...not sure.

Thanks for the input.

### prAna303 — 2012-09-30T18:14:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/swedish-chef-serves-food-on-the-edge-of-rotting.4030/post-55403>

Info about their thoughts about food:

REKTÃšN FOOD

What is rektÃºn food?

I’m sitting on the wooden kitchen sofa, it is brown and has a rag rug on top where grandpa usually rests in the afternoons. The cat, Tudor, is stretched out beside me in the heat from the radiator beneath the big window that towers up and frames the picturesque Lake KrÃ¥ksjÃ¶n. It is autumn, probably moose hunting season, and perhaps I am waiting for my father to come home from the forest. I don’t really remember. The colours in the trees are almost supernaturally bright in the crisp air, as usual in that season. I hear grandpa’s heavy orange steel-capped boots coming up the steps and into the porch, the sound changes to that of feet in woollen socks and water splashing in a wash basin.

The air in the kitchen is hot and slightly damp, full of the scents rising from the stove and what grandma is cooking at the other end of the kitchen. I’m four, possibly five years old, and I really like being there, right at that moment. I don’t remember what was being cooked, perhaps stuffed cabbage or brown beans, it’s really not important; one of the scents that I still remember is that of melting brown sugar, the old-fashioned sort – white sugar mixed with sugar cane syrup, kept in a brown plastic tub. However, what is important is what grandpa says when he comes into the kitchen from an afternoon working on the farm. He sees what’s being cooked and exclaims with approval; “De dÃ¤nne de e rektÃºn mat de, mat at n kaer”. Real food, food for a man. The literal translation is quite bland and insipid, but it’s not the words’ literal meaning that is most important, rather the feeling that they convey. That entire moment condensed into one phrase, rektÃºn food, is what I have afterwards spent so much of my time trying to create for others – often in very different and considerably more sophisticated places and contexts. Sometimes the phrase has almost been forgotten, making room for more exciting and transient things, but somehow it has always been a guide, one that has now developed into the basic premise for what we do at FÃ¤viken.

We respect our raw ingredients for what they are, what they look like and where they come from. We strive to monitor the production of each ingredient from seed to plate. We accept nature’s own choices as the primary factor, and apply our own knowledge in order to maximise every product’s potential. We concentrate on harvesting, preparing, cooking and then serving it. We present every single ingredient in a manner that conveys the feelings that arise in the process to create rektÃºn food.

Our ingredients are primarily from the FÃ¤viken Estate, grown and raised in conditions that we control. After this, they come from people we know in the local area, JÃ¤mtland, and, lastly, they come from our Norwegian neighbours in TrÃ¶ndelag.

We do not follow trends. We serve what we want, when we want.

Respect, control, selection, concentration, presentation.

RektÃºn food

### Michael — 2012-10-01T08:47:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/swedish-chef-serves-food-on-the-edge-of-rotting.4030/post-55540>

> **prAna303;56509 wrote:** Today itÂ´s luxury because only a few try it out, to the "gaga" generation this is so extreme that it makes them gag. They think he is a freak.
>
>
> Nice pun. I never heard the singer, actually ... maybe I'm lucky.
>
>
>
> I don't think what he does could come cheap, unless you did it yourself at home, because it's time-consuming and labour-intensive. At one end of the market there are people who would pay for that. Most others wouldn't - and most other eating-places would keep their costs down by saving on the time and the space and the level of knowledge that the chef would need to do it.
>
>
>
> But you'll get no dissent from me on the idea that everyone should be moving in that direction. At least let's all laugh at any so-called restaurant that buys in frozen ready-meals and warms them over in a microwave. A friend of mine met a guy he knew in the pub who'd just walked out of a job interview. After seeing what they did in the kitchens, the man said to the interviewer, "I'm a chef: I don't warm things up," and walked out.
>
>
>
> I often blend with a wide range of people and the trend is that the things i do is weird, weird like the food he is doing but it has only gone 20 years from this was the basic "poor mans food". Today i had smoked salmon fins for dinner and they are almost free of charge.

Yeah, isn't it interesting how food goes up and down the social scale - and up and down the price scale?

Apprentices used to complain that they got salmon too often; now it's a prestige food. Oysters were the food of the poor in the 19th century; now they're viewed as a luxury item.

You can buy lamb's liver and kidneys ridiculously cheap in England nowadays - because people don't like the idea of eating anything that looks like it actually came from an animal. They're kind of halfway to vegetarianism. However, although they're cheap, I'd lay you money it's not the poor buying them. It'll mainly be Middle Class housewives with an interest in cooking, who reckon on still making and eating all the traditional recipes.

Tim Noakes, the sports science guy from South Africa that six "leading doctors" were hounding in the press, says that he's talked to people actually in government in South Africa who are worried about what the poor specifically are eating. They say it's all starch and sugar and very little protein and fat, and that's why they're so obese and so sick. Over there Noakes says kidneys are so cheap you could get them for virtually nothing, and most are simply thrown away because they can't sell them. I guess either people just don't value them or perhaps they've been so urbanized and de-skilled that they just don't know what to do with them. Noakes said that someone in government there - not sure, but I think it was Buthelezi - told him he'd been on a low-carb diet for thirty years, having been put on it because of diabetes.

### Shijin13 — 2012-10-01T14:46:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/swedish-chef-serves-food-on-the-edge-of-rotting.4030/post-55549>

We've become such a culture/clan of commercialism that most can't get past the idea of eating the whole animal

It's crazy that less than 60 yrs ago we ate the WHOLE animal. My grandmother made brains, kidneys & liver on a regular basis when my dad was growing up. Things changed in the late 60's and into the 70's w/the large managed farms. Those nutritious cuts went to dog food

Today if you want to eat tip to tail you have to either buy the whole animal or go to an international mkt. every week we're the only non Asian or Hispanic ppl shopping at our international market

This weekend they had live conch going to get some next time have to figure out how to shell it and cook it.

Next step is to get a hold of some fresh not frozen grass fed/finished heart and make high meat out of it.

### Michael — 2012-10-01T15:27:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/swedish-chef-serves-food-on-the-edge-of-rotting.4030/post-55555>

> **Shijin13;56702 wrote:** We've become such a culture/clan of commercialism that most can't get past the idea of eating the whole animal
>
>
>
> It's crazy that less than 60 yrs ago we ate the WHOLE animal. My grandmother made brains, kidneys & liver on a regular basis when my dad was growing up.

Much the same in the US and Britain I'd guess. I think partly that people can't "get past the idea" as you say. Then there's social change - people simply losing the skills or opting for "convenience".

But what's going on in S Africa? These will be Xhosa and Zulu. Why are they ignoring cheap meat in favour of junk carbs?

I'd like to hear a lot more from Noakes, because things could play out differently there. Shame only Jimmy Moore has had the gumption to interview him as yet. I think he could get traction if the medical faculty at the University of Cape Town are largely behind him - which is what I gathered - and even government is taking notice.

I did wonder if the infamous open letter was a pharmaceutical company calling in favours, but that's probably a suspicion too far. Just busybodies shooting their mouths off rather than go through the work of re-assessing their position I guess

### Inger — 2012-10-01T15:31:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/swedish-chef-serves-food-on-the-edge-of-rotting.4030/post-55557>

This is my breakfast quite often.. raw reindeer meat with bones and marrow. It is so crazy delicious. I leave the meat in my dehydrator just over night at body temp and in the morning they are so very delish.. It needs no spices, no salt.. nothing. The taste is fantastic just by itself! Very tender too.

Please try again later.", "lightbox\_start\_slideshow": "Start slideshow", "lightbox\_stop\_slideshow": "Stop slideshow", "lightbox\_full\_screen": "Full screen", "lightbox\_thumbnails": "Thumbnails", "lightbox\_download": "Download", "lightbox\_share": "Share", "lightbox\_zoom": "Zoom", "lightbox\_new\_window": "New window", "lightbox\_toggle\_sidebar": "Toggle sidebar" }

I got Lambhearts too!!! I will get them tomorrow.. thanks God! I needed them so badly. And I will get a grassfed beef heart too saturday. And some raw beef fat. I am thinking it is christmas time..lol soon I will fly to the moon...

### Shijin13 — 2012-10-01T15:41:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/swedish-chef-serves-food-on-the-edge-of-rotting.4030/post-55558>

Agree that Noakes in on the right track.

as to why are people choosing junk carbs over cheap meat sources - how much is related to programming in the form of commercials etc, being pushed by the major food mfrs? and how much is related to the fact - that those same individuals - haven't seen the meat prepared b/c their grandparents prepared it, and never taught their children how to make it (b/c they were boys and didn't have girls etc - a lot of what's passed down generationally regarding foods is passed down grandmother - mother - daughter, I think only recently have you seen that branch out to include sons)

look at the US. if you're on Food stamps - the foods you can buy are limited mostly to "Junk" healthy whole grains, rice, pasta, & beans, only recently have veggies/fruits been included, and you don't get a whole lot of meat options, that are reasonably priced. also most people who use food stamps don't have vehicles to get to the nearest grocery stores and end up using convenience stores such as 7-11 - which just offers crap.

could the same thing be occurring in S.Africa - my cousin who was in Lesotho for close to 2yrs, said good quality foods were not available. And my other cousin who's been in Kenya for the past 6mths working w/the CDC and UNICEF studying AIDS prevention - has said meat is very limited, and there's very few people who eat the traditional foods any more. most of the stuff in the stores - are US made products.

those of us who eat out side the box - will be better off in the future should a food shortage occur - b/c we're not opposed to eating those things considered not fit for consumption, or its considered inhumane (horse meat) either way, those of us who eat outside the box, are better off in the future.

### Michael — 2012-10-01T16:13:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/swedish-chef-serves-food-on-the-edge-of-rotting.4030/post-55560>

All that sounds very likely.

I guess if you uproot and move to a city for work there may at the time be little other than "mealies". After a couple of generations if there are these kidneys available for pennies - possibly because the whites no longer will buy them - well, by then you've forgotten they were ever in the diet. You eat what you eat out of habit.

Foreign aid is another interesting one. Joel Salatin seems to think that the current purpose of much of it is to undermine local food-producers. Not an unintended side-effect but a purpose. Again, this would be big business having the ear of government in the US and Europe - or dropping something in the pockets of people in government.

### SeaHorse — 2012-10-01T16:31:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/swedish-chef-serves-food-on-the-edge-of-rotting.4030/post-55564>

> > **Jack Kruse;56704 wrote:** soon you will find out why

I read the article you posted "How to stop following the Herd"...interesting in light of this discussion about food norms. I feel there are maybe two elements to my tastes changing from cooked to raw...one comes from watching, listening and reading on the forum. I'm seeing pictures, hearing stories, reading Inger's posts (and many other pioneers) and all of this is patterning and normalizing something that wasn't part of my thinking before. So repetition and modelling both are changing my brain. And then there are the physiological changes that have happened due to changes in how I've been living....so my own brain is now CAPABLE of making different choices and is starting to crave different nutrition.

The "Herd" article makes the point that decisions people make can come from "public" or "private" information. The first human to break open a lobster on the rocks was pursuing a private experiment...once they had done that, there was public information about what happens when you bash a lobster. Other factors start to play a part in someone's decision making (following, modelling,etc). Suddenly cracking open lobsters is a movement and you've got a social cascade.

Flash forward to today. We are living in a world where many of the social movements and norms are profoundly damaging to us as individuals and as a species. We have to get back to our N=1 experiments and the Forum is providing the "public" information to help us in our private explorations to health....we've got to get back to the lobsters, each of us on our own.

### kathylu — 2012-10-01T19:21:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/swedish-chef-serves-food-on-the-edge-of-rotting.4030/post-55602>

People may find this forum interesting; [www.rawpaleodietforum.com](http://http://www.rawpaleodietforum.com)

There's a lot of wisdom, personal experimentation and interesting discussions there. Of course, a lot of crap too. You have to use your common sense.

### SeaHorse — 2012-10-02T03:13:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/swedish-chef-serves-food-on-the-edge-of-rotting.4030/post-55731>

I am so glad you post photos, Inger. My first reaction is always 'OMG, I can't believe she eats that.' Then I stare at the photo until I feel neutral. Then I keep looking until I'm curious. Then I stop once I get to the point of saying 'Sure, I could eat that.' I'm not suggesting I'll be eating raw reindeer tomorrow (what would Santa do?), but I feel like you help open the door to any possibility. Which means being completely healthy, too  Thanks.

# Thread: I need help with Eczema

**Subforum:** Beginners Area
**Posts:** 20
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### x_danny_x — 2012-09-30T21:56:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-need-help-with-eczema.4035/post-55466>

My Eczema left me with some scars at my feet and lower leg.

my hand and face got affected but they seem to cleared. does anyone know any remedies or anything that can help me get a handle at this. these meds for eczema is not cheap and laser treatment was mentioned by my dermotologist to get rid of them which is expensive. laser treatment though as well doesnt get rid of eczema permantly.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-09-30T22:39:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-need-help-with-eczema.4035/post-55473>

Selenium and several of the other Brain specific nutrients are likely off........read BG 5 again, use some coconut oil on your skin and consider some magnesium oil in the skin too.

### x_danny_x — 2012-09-30T22:48:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-need-help-with-eczema.4035/post-55476>

been using coconut oil and magnesium oil. will read BG 5 again for info that i missed.

will check out Selenium and avoid it.

### BJK77 — 2012-09-30T23:27:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-need-help-with-eczema.4035/post-55480>

> **x\_danny\_x;56510 wrote:** been using coconut oil and magnesium oil. will read BG 5 again for info that i missed.
>
>
>
> will check out Selenium and avoid it.

Mg and coconut oil worked well on DH's eczema as did increasing seafood. I don't think you want to avoid selenium. I think Dr K would say getting selenium and other brain specific nutrients from the epi-paleo diet is what you need to focus on. As I said, increasing seafood has made a huge difference for DH with his eczema/psoriasis issues. I didn't have skin problems per se, BUT my skin has definitely improved on the epi-paleo diet.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-10-01T00:02:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-need-help-with-eczema.4035/post-55481>

Selenium underpins IBD especially crohn's This is why Crohn's highest incidence and prevalence are found in NZ and Aussie because the continent is devoid of Se in the ground. They need seafood to get it.

### x_danny_x — 2012-10-01T01:36:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-need-help-with-eczema.4035/post-55502>

holy cow i see. but what is this that Fish Oil in supplement form is not optimal? i hate seafood but i will force myself to eat it until i get used to it.

i take in Carlson' Fish Oil or Vitamin Shopp's Fish oil, they are not in gel caps or anything, just liquid in a bottle. That still is not good enough.

### Adria — 2012-10-02T18:48:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-need-help-with-eczema.4035/post-55849>

Natural sunlight will help to heal it. Another symptom of food allergy, especially common with milk allergy.. is eczema.

### x_danny_x — 2012-10-02T19:08:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-need-help-with-eczema.4035/post-55856>

you think it is the BCM7 from A1 type milk? i drink coffee alot and i dont have the luxury to add A2 type milk all the time.

i will take away all dairy for good while and see what happens. also do more Cold Thermogenesis.

### Caroline Cooper — 2012-10-09T00:58:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-need-help-with-eczema.4035/post-56982>

Hi x\_danny\_x,

My dad had the worst case of eczema I have even seen in my life. He looked like a boiled lobster. Over 90% of his body was covered with the eczema. He tried everything. Spent a fortune on creams. It just made it worst. In the end, he did some sort of light treatment. It took only 2-3 minutes per treatment and he did about 15 treatments. It completely went away. He has a tiny spot on one hand. I don't know if you have tried this type of treatment but it worked for my dad.

### x_danny_x — 2012-10-09T01:16:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-need-help-with-eczema.4035/post-56989>

is that laser therapy you are talking about caroline?

### Caroline Cooper — 2012-10-09T02:22:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-need-help-with-eczema.4035/post-56996>

> **x\_danny\_x;58291 wrote:** is that laser therapy you are talking about caroline?

No. It was some sort of "super" standing tanning room by doctor's orders. I have to assume the UV light used was very intense because the treatment was 2-3 minutes a session for 15 sessions. He had a great tan after treatment.

### x_danny_x — 2012-10-10T03:50:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-need-help-with-eczema.4035/post-57233>

How did he get this treatment? Did he buy some light treating equipment for this? If so, where can I buy such a device?

### Caroline Cooper — 2012-10-12T03:09:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-need-help-with-eczema.4035/post-57682>

No, it was done by doctor's prescription. I just talked my dad and he said it was UVB treatment. The doctors thought he had eczema for years. Finally they did this treatment for psoriasis. (By the way, his "psoriasis" looked like "eczema".) The treatment worked great. Of course, it might not work for you:

[http://www.psoriasis.org/about-psoriasis/treatments/phototherapy](http://http://www.psoriasis.org/about-psoriasis/treatments/phototherapy)

One other treatment I have found that works great for skin problems that will not heal over the long term is shea butter. (My husband had a skin irritation on the bottom of one foot that would not heal. Likely a chemical sensitivity to epoxy resin. He'd spend a fortune on prescription cortico-steriod creams. Finally, he tried my shea butter and the wound finally healed up.) Shea is very thick and soothing for sore skin. Here is where I get mine:

[http://www.mountainroseherbs.com/learn/butter/shea.php](http://http://www.mountainroseherbs.com/learn/butter/shea.php)

### Gracelind — 2012-10-12T06:34:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-need-help-with-eczema.4035/post-57702>

I can second that Mountain Rose Herbs is a wonderful online source for herbs, essential oils, teas, and healing oils and butters.

There are some testimonials at Chagrin Valley Soap that folks with eczema found healing with some of the different hand-made soaps. A search for eczema on [this page](http://http://www.chagrinvalleysoapandsalve.com/ingredients/baseoils.aspx) will highlight specific oils and other ingredients that have been found to improve eczema.

You can buy sample sizes to try. The whole bars last a very long time if you just wet your hands to soap up and don't keep the bar in the shower. No financial interest, just nearly 10 years of using Ida's soaps and salves.

### fitness@home — 2012-10-12T12:49:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-need-help-with-eczema.4035/post-57721>

I really love the Chagrin Valley soaps and shampoo bars. Don't have to deal with eczema but like Gracelind said, go read the customer testimonials on the website. Great customer service...And I love that you can order sample sizes of the soaps to try.

My favorite shampoo bar is Summer Sunshine - need to place an order soon! Also use their Whipped Squalane Face & Eye Mousse at bedtime (just added one pump of Twist DHEA cream to it as my own little experiment).

### indigogirl — 2012-10-12T16:28:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-need-help-with-eczema.4035/post-57745>

I have had psoriasis on my elbow on and off ( flares, calms down) for years. I've used a myriad of prescription cortisone creams to treat the flares. I've just started treating it with topical iodine and it is drying out and is not itchy. I've only been doing this for 2 days, but so far so good. I was using the Lugols, but it stains my skin until I absorb it. I am now using clear iodine.

I haven't read this whole thread so someone might have mentioned this: Dr Kruse told me about a year ago (I think in response to my question on one of his blogs) to take NAC and 1000 mg vitamin C 3X a day for psoriasis...that's what he told ME, not sure if its general info, but it was not in a consult so he didn't have my labs, background, etc.

# Thread: Eating Fish 3 Meals A Day for A While ... Good Idea?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/eating-fish-3-meals-a-day-for-a-while-good-idea.4037/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 5
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### RebelLibertarian — 2012-10-01T01:22:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/eating-fish-3-meals-a-day-for-a-while-good-idea.4037/post-55495>

Howdy all, anyone see any problems with consuming 90%+ of my animal protein in the form of whole small fish (sardines and the like) and mussels? (Looking to buy frozen, in bulk, but don't want any iodine or selenium toxicities! I should probably add a source of O-6 fats) The rest would be from pastured eggs and GF beef organ meats.

### nonchalant — 2012-10-01T04:55:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/eating-fish-3-meals-a-day-for-a-while-good-idea.4037/post-55522>

Welcome, Rebel!

Occasionally I eat seafood for all 3 meals. More often I eat it for 2 meals. And sometimes I'll just get one epi-meal per day. I do often eat bacon and eggs.

I'm not sure what you mean by iodine or selenium toxicities. Those are both excellent minerals to stock up on.

### Michael — 2012-10-01T09:30:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/eating-fish-3-meals-a-day-for-a-while-good-idea.4037/post-55541>

> **RebelLibertarian;56566 wrote:** ... but don't want any iodine or selenium toxicities!
>
>
> Might happen if you were popping pills I guess. If you're eating seafood so that these nutrients come "in their evolutionary packages" as Jack would put it, it's hard to see how they'd be likely to cause problems. Fishermen aren't known to run into any specific problems from eating largely of their nearest available food-source that I know of.
>
>
>
> Last I heard WHO was saying that two _billion_ people worldwide were iodine-deficient. Currently, it says on their site:
>
>
>
> Iodine deficiency is the world’s most prevalent, yet easily preventable, cause of brain damage.

[http://www.who.int/nutrition/topics/idd/en/](http://http://www.who.int/nutrition/topics/idd/en/)

Their strategy is to iodinate salt - and that's helping even though it's not delivering iodine in the "evolutionary package".

I find it difficult to believe that some shrimp and sole and whatnot are going to cause anyone a problem. The onus of proof would be on anyone who should claim that: it would need to be shown - and with cast-iron data and huge data sets to be believable, because it just doesn't make sense.

### EllieHarrison — 2012-10-01T15:09:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/eating-fish-3-meals-a-day-for-a-while-good-idea.4037/post-55552>

When my cousin started doing the last 4 weeks before a comp on fish he came in a lot better, and he chose a reasonably oily fish as well, as he had dropped his carbs right down by that stage. When he did this, he won my last 4 comps in a row, as compared to using chicken etc, which seemed hit and miss for him. Always ate red meat and whole eggs in the off season though.

Ellie

\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_

Lifespanmeds.com

### RebelLibertarian — 2012-10-02T02:40:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/eating-fish-3-meals-a-day-for-a-while-good-idea.4037/post-55720>

Thanks, guys! I'll report back in 10 weeks, whereupon I should have exhausted my supply of sardines

# Thread: How much sea veggies?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-much-sea-veggies.4039/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 12
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### BJK77 — 2012-10-01T02:13:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-much-sea-veggies.4039/post-55505>

I am finding myself really drawn to sea veggies lately. I've been incorporating more of them into my diet since the epi-paleo blog came out, but lately I'm wanting TONS of them and they're really the only veggie I want other than a bit of avocado or mushrooms.

I follow Terry Wahls on FB and lately she's been putting up quite a few posts about being careful to not overdo seaweed, so I'm wondering how much is too much? How much have you all been eating? I don't measure my food out anymore, but I'm sure I'm eating about 50-100 grams of dried sea veg/day.

### ealachan — 2012-10-01T14:57:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-much-sea-veggies.4039/post-55550>

I think it might depend a lot on your individual situation, like so many other things. I've started eating kelp every day - I toast a roughly 5X5 square of it and the break it up into chips to have with my tuna at lunch time. It's tasty, and the crunch makes a nice counterpoint to the soft tuna. I find it super salty, though...I don't know if I could eat as much as you're eating without feeling like I was going to explode.

### nonchalant — 2012-10-01T17:24:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-much-sea-veggies.4039/post-55573>

I have yet to get into sea veggies much. But I've started drinking Inger's water, and adding a bit of seaweed here and there to my food. I hope I become like you, BJK77, and really start to crave them. They don't taste bad, I just don't think about using them.

### x_danny_x — 2012-10-02T08:27:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-much-sea-veggies.4039/post-55775>

last time i checked avocado is a fruit, not a veggie.

### Michael — 2012-10-02T09:29:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-much-sea-veggies.4039/post-55778>

> **BJK77;56576 wrote:
>
> I follow Terry Wahls on FB and lately she's been putting up quite a few posts about being careful to not overdo seaweed, so I'm wondering how much is too much?
>
>
> Does she say why, BJK77?
>
>
>
> I don't do Facebook, but I tried googling "terry wahls seaweed" out of interest and found this:
>
>
>
> [http://www.terrywahls.com/ForumRetrieve.aspx?ForumID=3749&TopicID=12314](http://http://www.terrywahls.com/ForumRetrieve.aspx?ForumID=3749&TopicID=12314)
>
>
>
> There all she says is:
>
>
>
>
> > Easiest way to begin using seaweed is to buy dulse flakes (health food store, Asian store) and add as a spice to what ever you are cooking. Start small. Try a 1/4 teaspoon at first. It is lovely addition to soups, stews, roasts, vegetables or even sprinkled on a salad. Do not exceed 1/4 tsp per person per day however. If you are taking thyroid medicine - adding seaweed to your diet can impact the amount of thyroid medication that you need.
>
>
>
> There's an apparent contradiction there, since she says start with a 1/4 of a teaspoon, then says "Do not exceed 1/4 tsp". I guess she meant don't exceed that at first.
>
>
>
> There the notion of "too much" seems to be tied to a "too soon". But she gives no rationale for that.
>
>
>
> The final sentence there is a bombshell**:
>
>
>
> If you are taking thyroid medicine - adding seaweed to your diet can impact the amount of thyroid medication that you need.

It's said almost offhand, but what it seems to amount to is putting a pretty large question mark against current medical practice. At least that's how things seem to me. I wonder what others think.

So someone goes to the doctor's surgery complaining of tiredness. The doctor runs a battery of tests, checking he's not diabetic and so on. When he goes back, the doctor say that what showed up was hypothyroidism, and he prescribes some levothyroxine.

Now here's the thing, as I see it. If eating seaweed can impact the amount of that one needs, isn't it doing something of the same? Might not telling a patient to eat seaweed do instead? And even if it wouldn't, why wouldn't the doctor warn hypothyroid patients that eating seaweed could affect their dosage, like Dr. Wahls does?

It's kind of concerning the way some doctors just reach for a pharmaceutical that solves the immediate problem _and just leave it at that_.

And even if it wouldn't, why wouldn't the doctor warn hypothyroid patients that eating seaweed could affect their dosage, like Dr. Wahls does?

Agreed. My gut tells me this is precisely WHY I'm craving seaweed so much these days. My T3 is totally tanked. I just saw a new doc who ordered compounded T3 for me, but obviously if I can provide my body with what it needs to naturally make the hormone for me I'd much rather go that route.

### Michael — 2012-10-02T21:11:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-much-sea-veggies.4039/post-55904>

Thanks.

> **BJK77;57107 wrote:**
>
> Agreed. My gut tells me this is precisely WHY I'm craving seaweed so much these days. My T3 is totally tanked. I just saw a new doc who ordered compounded T3 for me, but obviously if I can provide my body with what it needs to naturally make the hormone for me I'd much rather go that route.
>
>
> Ho, hum ... how many of us? I was put on levothyroxine a few years ago. There seem to be ever so many people on it.
>
>
>
> That's interesting in itself. I think that guy who says "The message of this show is that human beings are not broken by default" has it right. So why do so many need thyroid medication? Why are all these thyroids breaking? Does your G.P. ever wonder as he fills out the prescription?
>
>
>
> The partners at my local practice seem quite good, prepared to take time, ready to talk to you, ready to actually think about some problem you bring them. And they're hard-working and have a high level of education ... and yet ...
>
>
>
> They just seem to look in their toolbox and rummage for a pharmaceutical. I'm inclined to wonder if doctors have an almost "magical" view of things. Probably not; probably that's just how it seems. But it's as if they think, "Ah, that's the problem! Here's the spell (pill) for that. Next please!" In reality much of the time they're handing out stuff that doesn't have a specific effect (as a spell would be supposed by primitive people to have), but a _handful of effects, one desirable (perhaps), others probably not. There's really no such thing as a "side-effect" -- that's_ really a "magical" way of thinking. There are only effects. And at the end of the day -- unoriginal thought here -- much of the time what they're offering only targets symptoms.
>
>
>
> My doctor, when putting me on levothyroxine, never mentioned diet to me. I wonder now: "Shouldn't he have warned me about goitrogens?" I also thought when I happened to learn that some people with thyroid conditions have problems converting beta-carotene to vitamin A -- they can even go orangey -- "Shouldn't he have told me to make sure I ate some foods with pre-formed vitamin A in them, like liver?"
>
>
>
> And, as I say, I think my doctors are pretty good. How about the one that had charge of this lady?
>
>
>
> He was still gathering information. But he did look at the list of medications, and as he did, his face grew hard and angry. He spoke crisply though, and kept his voice neutral: Okay. So she’s on these and these? Two pages of drugs?

One drug that gives someone a nasty side-effect, then another to target the side-effect, and so on in a cascade.

And at the bottom the problem is dietary anyway. Horrific and shocking!

[http://www.meandmydiabetes.com/2011/02/24/medicine-for-magdalena-checkup-with-ron-rosedale/](http://http://www.meandmydiabetes.com/2011/02/24/medicine-for-magdalena-checkup-with-ron-rosedale/)

How many prescribed drugs was Ozzie Osbourne on when he fell off that motorized quadricycle thing? Doctors said afterwards it was surprising he could function at all, even sit upright.

### KiwiLauren — 2012-10-02T23:55:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-much-sea-veggies.4039/post-55962>

But it's as if they think, "Ah, that's the problem! Here's the spell (pill) for that. Next please!" I also thought when I happened to learn that some people with thyroid conditions have problems converting beta-carotene to vitamin A -- they can even go orangey -- "Shouldn't he have told me to make sure I ate some foods with pre-formed vitamin A in them, like liver?"

> [http://www.meandmydiabetes.com/2011/02/24/medicine-for-magdalena-checkup-with-ron-rosedale/](http://http://www.meandmydiabetes.com/2011/02/24/medicine-for-magdalena-checkup-with-ron-rosedale/)
>
>
>
> How many prescribed drugs was Ozzie Osbourne on when he fell off that motorized quadricycle thing? Doctors said afterwards it was surprising he could function at all, even sit upright.

This has been my experience with doctors, too. Even when seeking out the most holistic and open-minded, they still think in terms of symptoms. But it's all Indra's Net. And we are left to sort out the complexities. You can't treat it like falling dominos.

The T3-iodine/seaweed-meds issue is one that shows this. From what I gather, Terry Wahls isn't going for an epi-paleo template. So for that reason alone, adding seaweed without the seafood could be problematic. It's hard to imagine that humans living by the sea would have eaten a lot of seaweed and not a lot of seafood, too. Seafood contains the DHA as well as the selenium that Dr K suggests we 'pre-load' with before increasing iodine consumption. And of course, each of us has the individual issue of sorting out where in this chain of events we are sitting.

It seems pretty clear that one of the main drivers of all these 'broken thyroids' is the move away from an epi-paleo template of eating. Add to that not following circadian cycles and my guess is we all get a thyroid that struggles to some degree. If we boost our T3 with carbs, then other bits of us crash... which eventually impacts our thyroids anyway.

### Michael — 2012-10-03T08:34:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-much-sea-veggies.4039/post-56030>

> **KiwiLauren;57175 wrote:** It's hard to imagine that humans living by the sea would have eaten a lot of seaweed and not a lot of seafood, too. Seafood contains the DHA as well as the selenium that Dr K suggests we 'pre-load' with before increasing iodine consumption.
>
>
> Now you mention it, I think I do recall the pre-loading with selenium, although that's not mentioned [here](http://http://jackkruse.com/brain-gut-6-epi-paleo-rx/) where seaweed is extensively discussed. Perhaps possible low selenium in people is why Dr. Wahls has this caution. Who knows, if she won't say?
>
>
>
>
> > It seems pretty clear that one of the main drivers of all these 'broken thyroids' is the move away from an epi-paleo template of eating.
> >
> >
> > One would suppose so.
> >
> >
> >
> > Add to that not following circadian cycles and my guess is we all get a thyroid that struggles to some degree.
>
>
>
> That sounds all very likely to me, too
>
>
>
> If we boost our T3 with carbs, then other bits of us crash... which eventually impacts our thyroids anyway.

I expect that's true, too. Hang on ... how could "safe starches" hurt us? LOL

I do like fish and have always eaten it fairly regularly. Now I suspect nowhere near regularly enough - and shellfish less often. It seems to me that the French are instructive in more ways than one. I was flicking through an old book of mine on Norman cookery and noticed that after soups and appetizers and eggs it moved onto fish and shellfish ... page after page after page ... before a chapter on "meat and game" and then poultry (much of which was duck recipes). Back in the eggs section, some of the egg dishes contained shellfish and/or fish.

I may also have damaged mine with off circadian cycles. The problems light can cause out of time is something I had little notion of until about five years ago. And likely with excessive carbohydrate and (another common cultural failing) too much alcohol.

I guess there's a possibility of autoimmunity, which seems to be the most common cause of thyroid problems in the U.S. from what I gather. Not something the NHS in the UK is going to test for. Here's one drawback of "socialized medicine": you don't pay at the point of purchase but you get what you're given.

The U.S. seems to have a bewildering array of tests on offer for different things. I don't think some of that exists anywhere else in the world.

### Michael — 2012-10-03T11:58:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-much-sea-veggies.4039/post-56041>

> **KiwiLauren;57175 wrote:** This has been my experience with doctors, too. Even when seeking out the most holistic and open-minded, they still think in terms of symptoms.

It's interesting how the more you hear, the more you see patterns emerging.

So this morning I've been listening to Jimmy Moore's podcast, and he's talking to a midwife. It said the episode was on women's health, but I listened anyway. I came away with the impression that women are perhaps getting an even rawer deal out of the current medical set-up than people in general. She says many women suffer PCOS, because of high-carbohydrate diets. They then go to their doctor, who prescribes the birth-control pill, which kind of addresses the symptom, but does nothing for the cause. Moreover, that can often worsen their insulin-resistance. She says that babies are born too big, because of gestational diabetes, owing to high-carb diets (actually recommended for them, of course), and that's driving caesarean sections, but the doctors don't ask why C-sections are going up, they just gaily go ahead and book them.

Here's what I think. I think they could find themselves in a sticky place, because of the internet - which spreads a lot of information very fast, much of it junk to be sure but some that isn't. I think doctors have got to raise their game or risk people losing all confidence in them.

### nonchalant — 2012-10-03T18:19:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-much-sea-veggies.4039/post-56069>

> **Michael;57252 wrote:** The U.S. seems to have a bewildering array of tests on offer for different things. I don't think some of that exists anywhere else in the world.

Don't worry, we're working hard to rectify that situation here in the States.
