# Thread: Nobel Winners Unlocked Cells' Unlimited Potential
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nobel-winners-unlocked-cells-unlimited-potential.4190/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 5
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### chocolate — 2012-10-08T19:01:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nobel-winners-unlocked-cells-unlimited-potential.4190/post-56905>

[http://www.npr.org/blogs/health/2012/10/08/162496684/nobel-winners-unlocked-cells-unlimited-potential](http://http://www.npr.org/blogs/health/2012/10/08/162496684/nobel-winners-unlocked-cells-unlimited-potential)

> by Michaeleen Doucleff
>
>
>
> 09:05 am
>
>
>
> October 8, 2012
>
>
>
> Twitter (25)
>
> Facebook (104)
>
> E-mail
>
> Share
>
> Print
>
> Comments (11)
>
>
>
>
>
> John B. Gurdon (left) and Shinya Yamanaka will share the prize, worth about $1.2 million.
>
> AFP/Getty Images
>
>
>
> John B. Gurdon (left) and Shinya Yamanaka will share the prize, worth about $1.2 million.
>
>
>
> The two scientists who won this year's Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine discovered that cells in our body have the remarkable ability to reinvent themselves. They found that every cell in the human body, from our skin and bones to our heart and brain, can be coaxed into forming any other cell.
>
>
>
> The process is called reprogramming, and its potential for new drugs and therapies is vast. If neurons or heart cells are damaged by disease or aging, then cells from the skin or blood potentially could be induced to reprogram themselves and repair the damaged tissue.
>
>
>
> The winners — John Gurdon of the Gurdon Institute in Cambridge, England, and Shinya Yamanaka of Kyoto University in Japan and the Gladstone Institute in San Francisco — made their discoveries more than 40 years apart.
>
>
>
>
>
> In 1962, Gurdon proved that a cell from a frog's stomach contained the entire blueprint to make a whole frog. When he took the cell's nucleus and popped it into a frog egg, the egg developed normally into a whole frog.
>
>
>
> This method eventually was used to clone all sorts of animals, including cats, dogs, horses and, most famously, Dolly the sheep — the first mammal cloned from an adult cell. Gurdon, 79, continues to study reprogramming and was working in his lab when he received the call from the Nobel committee.
>
>
>
> But a major obstacle stood in the way of further development of these stem cells: Getting the frog's stomach cell to strip away its specialization and turn into one of the 200 or so cell types known to exist in animals always required the use of an egg.
>
> A question hung over the field for decades: Could a specialized cell reprogram itself all on its own?
>
> Shinya Yamanaka discovered a simple recipe for converting skin from a patient into many different cell types, including nerve and heart cells.
>
> Mattias Karlen/Courtesy of The Nobel Committee for Physiology or Medicine
>
>
>
> Shinya Yamanaka discovered a simple recipe for converting skin from a patient into many different cell types, including nerve and heart cells.
>
>
>
> **In 2006, Yamanaka and graduate student Kazutoshi Takahashi found the answer, and it sent shockwaves through biology and medicine. They demonstrated that any cell could be reset and induced to develop into another cell type. And, even more remarkably, that it took little to get the job done.
>
>
>
> Yamanaka and Takahashi took a few cells from the skin of a mouse and then switched on four specific genes. After a few days, the cells stopped looking like skin cells and starting looking more like embryonic stem cells. That meant the cells could then be manipulated to develop into just about any cell Yamanaka wanted. He could even get the cells to form heart muscle that beat inside a Petri dish.
>
>
>
> Yamanaka, 50, had essentially unlocked a cellular genie in the bottle. The discovery meant that a cell wasn't necessarily trapped in its fate to be, say, a skin, liver or heart cell. Rather, with the right instructions, a cell had the ability to strip away its specialization and become whatever cell the scientists chose. They called these new cells "induced pluripotent stem cells.**"

### Jack Kruse — 2012-10-09T04:59:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nobel-winners-unlocked-cells-unlimited-potential.4190/post-57042>

IPSC's are a big part of my surgical practice.

### indigogirl — 2012-10-09T05:40:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nobel-winners-unlocked-cells-unlimited-potential.4190/post-57051>

Really fascinating.

How commonplace is it for other surgeons to be using IPSC's in their practices?

When will we be growing new organs and using them in surgery?

# Thread: Sharks Threatened
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sharks-threatened.4193/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 4
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Michael — 2012-10-08T21:01:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sharks-threatened.4193/post-56932>

> DEEP-sea sharks, among the slowest growing and breeding of marine species, face being wiped out to meet demand for fish oil dietary supplements.
>
>
>
> Species such as the leafscale gulper shark, which can take 35 years to reach breeding age, and the kitefin shark, which has a similar life cycle, are being hunted down because their livers contain large quantities of the valuable oils. ...
>
>
> Not good news.
>
>
>
> Xavier Pastor, executive director of Oceana Europe, said ... "Vulnerable deep-sea sharks have become the new gold. They are targeted by fishing fleets for their liver oil for use in omega 3 dietary supplements, in cosmetics, and as industrial lubricants, and also for their fins and meat," he said. "These species are extremely vulnerable."

[http://www.theaustralian.com.au/news/world/diet-demands-threaten-sharks/story-fnb64oi6-1226490186469](http://http://www.theaustralian.com.au/news/world/diet-demands-threaten-sharks/story-fnb64oi6-1226490186469)

The story seems to have been syndicated from the _Sunday Times_, which, I think, is behind a paywall.

Here is Oceana's site:

[http://oceana.org/en/eu/home](http://http://oceana.org/en/eu/home)

I'm thinking that perhaps this is another reason to prefer fish over fish-oil - the conservation point of view. People are preferring to supplement with the oil, because they don't like the taste of fish or whatever. They need to man-up (or woman-up) and eat the whole fish, because the result of the demand for fish-oil is that these rare species, because their livers can account for a quarter of their bodyweight, have become targets for over-exploitation.

### JoeBranca — 2012-10-19T21:45:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sharks-threatened.4193/post-59224>

Check out this trailer, turning the Jaws meme on its head. Plus I'm biased towards anything Malick/Lubezki are involved with.

[http://www.devilsteethmovie.com/index.htm](http://http://www.devilsteethmovie.com/index.htm)

### KiwiLauren — 2012-10-20T00:37:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sharks-threatened.4193/post-59243>

I listened to a podcast interview with Sylvia Earle, oceanographer and aquanaut (often called Her Deepness). She talked lovingly about sharks. She said, from her point of view, when you see sharks in the ocean it means the ocean is in good health. But she says she rarely sees them anymore.

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-10-21T01:34:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sharks-threatened.4193/post-59407>

> **KiwiLauren;60784 wrote:** I listened to a podcast interview with Sylvia Earle, oceanographer and aquanaut (often called Her Deepness). She talked lovingly about sharks. # Thread: lemon balm
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lemon-balm.4195/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### chocolate — 2012-10-08T22:21:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lemon-balm.4195/post-56939>

> [http://blog.lef.org/2012/10/manage-hyperthyroidism-naturally.html](http://http://blog.lef.org/2012/10/manage-hyperthyroidism-naturally.html)
>
> Lemon Balm Supports a Calm Mood
>
> One of the most troubling symptoms of hyperthyroidism is anxiety, which can make you feel irritable and restless. **Fortunately, research shows that lemon balm can help support a calm mood by inhibiting the breakdown of gamma-aminobutyric acid (GABA), a neurotransmitter which calms the nervous system.4
>
>
>
> In a clinical trial involving people with mild-to-moderate anxiety, participants were given lemon balm extract for 15 days. They were interviewed with the Free Rating Scale for Anxiety (FRSA), a tool which measures anxiety symptoms.
>
>
>
> The results of the study were significant. Participants felt less anxious and even slept better at night. Remarkably, over 95% of the participants responded to the treatment.4
>
>
>
> For this reason, you may want to consider supplementing with lemon balm to help with hyperthyroidism-inspired anxiety.
>
>
> [http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Melissa\_officinalis](http://http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Melissa_officinalis)
>
>
>
>
> > It is also used as an anxiolytic, mild sedative or calming agent. At least one study has found it to be effective at reducing stress, although the study's authors call for further research.\[10\] Lemon balm extract was identified as a potent inhibitor of GABA transaminase, which explains anxiolytic effects. The major compound responsible for GABA transaminase inhibition activity in lemon balm is rosmarinic acid.\[11\]
> >
> >
> >
> > Lemon balm and preparations thereof also have been shown to improve mood and mental performance. These effects are believed to involve muscarinic and nicotinic acetylcholine receptors.\[12\] Positive results have been achieved in a small clinical trial involving Alzheimer patients with mild to moderate symptoms.\[13\] Essential oils obtained from Melissa officinalis leaf showed high acetylcholinesterase and butyrylcholinesterase co-inhibitory activities.\[14\]
> >
> >
> >
> > Its antibacterial properties have also been demonstrated scientifically, although they are markedly weaker than those from a number of other plants studied.\[15\] The extract of lemon balm was also found to have exceptionally high antioxidant activity.\[16\]
> >
> >
> >
> > Lemon balm is mentioned in the scientific journal Endocrinology, where it is explained that Melissa officinalis exhibits antithyrotropic activity, inhibiting TSH from attaching to TSH receptors, hence making it of possible use in the treatment of Graves' disease or hyperthyroidism.\[17\]
>
>
>
> Ob-X, a mixture of three herbs, Morus alba, M. officinalis, and Artemisia capillaris, may help regulate obesity. Ob-X reduces body weight gain and visceral adipose tissue mass in genetically obese mice.\[22\]
>
>
>
> Recent research found a daily dose of the tea reduced oxidative stress status in radiology staff that were exposed to persistent low-dose radiation during work. After only 30 days of taking the tea daily researchers found Lemon balm tea resulted in a significant improvement in plasma levels of catalase, superoxide dismutase, and glutathione peroxidase and a marked reduction in plasma DNA damage, myeloperoxidase, and lipid peroxidation.\[23\]
>
> **
>
> Lemon balm was found to be effective in the amelioration of laboratory-induced stress in human subjects, producing "significantly increased self-ratings of calmness and reduced self-ratings of alertness." The authors further report a "significant increase in the speed of mathematical processing, with no reduction in accuracy" following the administration of a 300 mg dose.\[24\]
>
>
>
> Lemon balm is the main ingredient of Carmelite Water, which is still for sale in German pharmacies.\[25\]

### hope2conceive — 2012-10-10T23:16:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lemon-balm.4195/post-57426>

Lemon balm is the main ingredient of Carmelite Water, which is still for sale in German pharmacies.\[25\]

\-- Love it.....

# Thread: Sept Labs are BACK! Well... some of them

**Subforum:** Optimal Labs

### Shijin13 — 2012-10-08T22:44:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sept-labs-are-back-well-some-of-them.4196/post-56946>

Got my Sept labs back - well most of them... the hormone labs from Genova haven't come in yet... so I'm running blood work for the hormones on Thursday

I'm on a 1/2 grain (30mg) of armour thyroid....

Thyroid (RANGE)

T4 1.01 ng/dL (.82-1.77)

TSH 4.53 uIU/mL (.450-4.5) (I knew this was happing...

Free T3 3.4pg/mL (2.0-4.4)

rT3 18.1 ng/dL (13.5-34.2)

T3:rT3 ratio 18.8

### KiwiLauren — 2012-10-08T23:12:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sept-labs-are-back-well-some-of-them.4196/post-56952>

Gretchen, with such high T3 but also elevated TSH, what does this mean? You've obviously been feeling hypo since you said you knew it was happening, but your T3 is over the range. What do you make of this?

### Shijin13 — 2012-10-08T23:32:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sept-labs-are-back-well-some-of-them.4196/post-56955>

My PCP thinks its the fact that the rT3 is getting low finally. But she's worried about the fact my TSH is off the charts... and the fact the T4 was low even w/the armour (which is a NTD T4/T3 combo)

a part of me wonders if the high T3 isn't just a result of me responding to summer carbs??? and we'll see it drop in the winter again... guess we'll see... I'd love to hear Patty's or Colleen's take on this...

I've known all along I've had thyroid issues... we're just now peeling the layers back so we can see what's actually going on... good thing is if I'm making T3 - that means I'm making it in the gut...so my gut must be on the road to optimal....and if I'm making T3...then I should be converting LDL -> pregnenolone -> progesterone (fingers crossed!)

### maggie — 2012-10-09T00:06:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sept-labs-are-back-well-some-of-them.4196/post-56961>

> **Shijin13;58257 wrote:** if I'm making T3 - that means I'm making it in the gut...so my gut must be on the road to optimal....and if I'm making T3...then I should be converting LDL -> pregnenolone -> progesterone (fingers crossed!)

Shijin, congrats on the improved labs! Must be good to see things moving in the right direction.

I wondered about your statement I quoted above. Could you unpack that a little? I don't understand why it means you're making T3 in the gut. Would like to understand. Thanks

### Shijin13 — 2012-10-09T00:15:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sept-labs-are-back-well-some-of-them.4196/post-56966>

T4 is converted in the Gut to T3... Jack mentioned this in the EPCOTx webinar.... so if your gut is bad - you're not going to have optimal hormone conversion... he also covers it in BG12 & CT7 (If I remember correctly)

you need K2 to help convert T4->T3, K2 is made in the gut. and its easiest to convert T4->T3 where K2 is most prevelant.

you need T3 + Vitamin A to convert LDL ->pregnenolone

If you in a stressed pathway your LDL->pregnenolone -> cortisol

If you in the optimal pathway your LDL->pregnenolone->progesterone and all other hormones...

I think I understand this - but I could have missed a piece and Jack will correct me if I'm off base... but these are all things he and I talked about during my consult (remember N=1, so this may not all apply to you)

### Patty Cakes — 2012-10-09T00:16:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sept-labs-are-back-well-some-of-them.4196/post-56968>

You are definitely getting T3 from somewhere. Also the range for T3 seems very narrow. It looks like no FT3 test, right? ou could finally be getting the T3 you need due to the clearing of the rT3. You are almost on the other side of that! Congrats!

Anyway my thoughts are not totally clear on this. The high TSH is your pituitary sending the signal that more thyroid hormone is needed. That usually corresponds to low readings on T3/4 labs. With your T3 being high and T4 at least being in range I am wondering if something is off in the pituitary. Does Cycloset do anything to that gland?

### Shijin13 — 2012-10-09T00:51:08+0300

I do know my pitutary is a bit "out of sorts" my prolactin levels are below range, and I'm nursing (not that I haven't been trying to wean Havoc) still. and those levels explain why I needed domperidone to induce/support lactation w/both of my kids. W/ds I actually was able to stop taking it last December and still make milk while only having him nursing 3x/day - its kinda like my pitutary just isn't firing on all cylindars

I guess my question to you you is what are symptoms of hypo?

No weight loss (just stagnated) of recent I've been hot as hades, with crazy "hot flashes" but they're not peri-menopausal hot flashes, and my temp will be 97 when these hot flashes occur.

Today I'm actually cold - but then again its the first real cool day we've had - highs are in the mid 60s and its wet... I'm actually enjoying the cold feeling... been shivering all day...in a light cotton tshirt....

### KiwiLauren — 2012-10-09T00:55:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sept-labs-are-back-well-some-of-them.4196/post-56981>

Hypo would be feeling cold, cold hands and feet, sluggishness, weight gain/no weight loss. But with such a high T3 it's hard to imagine you being traditionally hypo. That TSH was what I didn't understand either. I have the same question: why would your pituitary be signalling the need for more T3 when it's already high? ### Patty Cakes — 2012-10-09T01:12:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sept-labs-are-back-well-some-of-them.4196/post-56988>

I know rT3 is made when adrenals are dysfunctioning or iron is very low and maybe some other things but those are the biggies. Also FT4 is converted to FT3 in the liver and a small amount in the gut. So I think rT3 is made in either one of those two places. Not a very scientific explanation. :-/

Took the armour at 5:30 am. Blood work was at 8:30

Felt the thyroid kick in in July after my 1st consult w/Jack and he told me to push the seafood, the longer I've been epipaleo tge more I've felt like it was off.

### bigknitwit — 2012-10-09T18:01:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sept-labs-are-back-well-some-of-them.4196/post-57104>

> **Shijin13;58404 wrote:** Took the armour at 5:30 am. Blood work was at 8:30
>
>
>
> Felt the thyroid kick in in July after my 1st consult w/Jack and he told me to push the seafood, the longer I've been epipaleo tge more I've felt like it was off.

Well, I'm no expert, but I'd say the T3 from your Armour dose at 5:30am was at about maximum concentration at the time of your blood draw. And then it would slowly recede throughout the next several hours. Is the T4 value you gave a free, or a total? Either way, after the am boost from the Armour, your body is now stuck with creating T3 from your (low) T4 levels. I'm guessing you need more Armour, dosed mid-afternoon.

Also, isn't 80% of the conversion of T4 to T3 done in the liver? I thought only 20% happened in the gut?

### Patty Cakes — 2012-10-09T18:02:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sept-labs-are-back-well-some-of-them.4196/post-57105>

I don't like to take the NDT before the blood test in the morning. I always wait until after the test. NOW that the range has been adjusted your FT3 is still a little low and show be at least 4.0. I felt like a rockstar when mine was at 4.2. Not hyper at all.

So you have a high TSH, slightly low FT3 and low FT4. This all seems to fit. You are still exhibiting being a little hypo in my eyes. But if things are kicking in you may want to wait another 3 months and see how things go. The 30mg Armour you are taking is really a tiny dose. I bet you could up it to 45 or 60 mg and be just fine. But, and I could be completely off base here, you want your thyroid to kick in and not rely on drugs so you don't want to take so much that you suppress your body's own ability to pump out that FT4 and FT3. Someone correct me if that last statement is completely off base.

whats funny is my prolactin levels have come up on cycloset (which its supposed to suppress and my milk supply hasn't dried up either!) so something's working at the pituitary level I think...

### Patty Cakes — 2012-10-10T02:00:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sept-labs-are-back-well-some-of-them.4196/post-57204>

> **Shijin13;58483 wrote:** what do your recent labs look like???
>
>
>
> I know my HOT isn' a typical peri/menopausal hot flash... I'm just freaking hot... I can't stand it if the temp is above 70, pretty much right now... ughhh.

My labs when I was on NDT were practically perfect (no meds taken that morning):

FT3-4.2 (2.0-4.4)

FT4-1.44 (.82-1.77)

TSH-.006

but.....I had a rT3 ratio of 16.8.

So I went on T3 to bring that ratio above 20. Really like this T3 only.

August labs (no meds taken that morning):

FT3 - 2.2 (2.0-4.4)

FT4 - 1.44 (.82-1.77)

TSH - didn't test

rT3 ratio - 44 whoo-hoo!!

Since t3 only is a fast acting and doesn't stay in the system much longer than 8 hours there was nothing left the morning of that testas is shown with the low reading. Compared to NDT which has the T4 (which is converting into the next day) and T3. Thinking maybe there is a seasonal connection but hard to tell with the T3 only. This is when I think it would be good to take the med about 2 hours before testing as I think that is when T3 peaks.

Because those NDT labs were so good I think my body likes NDT better. Contemplating going back...

### KiwiLauren — 2012-10-15T23:41:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sept-labs-are-back-well-some-of-them.4196/post-58404>

> **bigknitwit;59855 wrote:** My house is an hour of insanity before school each morning, and I'm not going anywhere (sometimes I drive them in my PJs!). You have my complete respect for being able to pull off what you do, daily!

BKW, I am a solo parent Monday-Friday of every week (my husband lost his local work after the earthquakes) and have been for over a year. You CAN do this. I find two things required to keep my morning going to task... one is to get up before the children. Even if that means having to set a non-optimal alarm clock  while my ASI panel crawls its way to optimal. The other is to keep a sense of humour. I tell my kids "I don't want to be a stressed-out-crazy-tantruming-mummy anymore" so here's what we all have to do. Their jobs are small, mine are huge. Once they are awake, I put on fun music, keep an eye on the clock and begin multi tasking. (Getting up early means I've already had my thyroid meds, been outside to sun-gaze and stretch, got dressed and even checked the forums.... thankfully with this lifestyle showering is no longer a necessity, which is a great time-saver!). Oh, and I literally say to myself every morning before I get out of bed, 'thanks for another day to be alive.' It helps.

### AKMan — 2012-10-16T00:47:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sept-labs-are-back-well-some-of-them.4196/post-58429>

> **bigknitwit;58396 wrote:** How long before the labs did you take your dose of Armour? That would impact the FT3 on the labs...
>
>
>
> Another thought: TSH takes some time to shift. So if you've changed something recently that's impacting your thyroid, your TSH may not reflect that just yet.

This makes a big difference! I pulled a fast one recently, I had two docs each put in a lab request for TSH, T4 and FT3. I took Armour 30mg at 6am, had first test at 8am and second at 2:30pm. Results were:

8am:

TSH - 3.05 (ref .34 - 4.82)

FT4 - 1.01 (.59 - 1.61NG/DL))

FT3 - 8.1 (3.1 - 7.7pmol/L)

2:30pm:

TSH - 3.00

FT4 - 1.00

FT3 - 5.1

I was told that all of T3 from Armour is out of your system in 8 hours. This kind of backs that up. It seems it would be better to skip your morning dose of NDT if you want to see what your own thyroid is producing and take your morning dose if you want to see what the NDT is providing.

Thanks! I am on my own in the mornings (my husband got a great new job in a not-so-great new location, so he leaves at 5:30am). We have it pretty well organized now. I help my younger 2 make their lunches/snacks for school the night before. The older 2 make their own lunch in the mornings, and seem to pull it off pretty well. I have some stuff available in the fridge (cut fruit, etc), that is easy for them to use. The boys empty the dishwasher, I get the meds out for thyroid kids, and I get started on cooking breakfast - fish for everyone! I can't believe that somehow they've all started eating it, everyday!

It's always the last minute stuff that gets crazy. But the insanity of getting everyone in the car and out the door is usually in good humour. In a few weeks we will "fall back" and gain an hour. This is the first year this will really help my household, because my kids are really tuned at getting up with the sun and are sleeping later now. I have a difficult time waking my 6 year old (something I've never really had to do with any of them, or even myself) to get ready for school.

# Thread: Garden help needed?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/garden-help-needed.4200/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 10
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-10-09T00:08:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/garden-help-needed.4200/post-56963>

Ok good people - this is my first time to grow anything. It's froze the last couple of nights. My basil is all black and droopy.

The parsley, stevia, tyme, oregeno and mints look ok as does the kale. The tomato plants look all wilted and blackish too.

What are you supposed to do in winter? I planted some cauliflower, kale and cabbages cause I thought they were winter stuff. . .but the sprinklers are turned off. . . do you water in the winter? What can I do with all my herbs that are still alive, anything I can do with the blackened basil? Is this a delicacy anywhere?

Thankfully I've harvested about 5 quarts of pesto already so I don't feel too cheated.

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-10-09T00:16:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/garden-help-needed.4200/post-56967>

### Patty Cakes — 2012-10-09T00:19:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/garden-help-needed.4200/post-56970>

Tomato plants look so sad. When I lived in the northwest I never attempted to garden in the winter due to the dips in weather. Always had a summer garden. Now in So Cal we can grow year round. I know I have heard of "glazing" or putting a layer of water over oranges when it freezes so they are ice coated and the ice protects them a little. I think some people even cover the plants with plastic to help shield out the cold. But I have a brown thumb so not sure you should listen to me at all!!!

### Caroline Cooper — 2012-10-09T00:35:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/garden-help-needed.4200/post-56975>

1\. Harvest you tomatoes right now. Put them in a box in the house and allow them to ripen. Eat right away or freeze for later. If you think they just can't ripen you could make fermented green tomatoes:

[http://eatkamloops.org/winter-storage-kimchi-and-lacto-fermented-green-tomatoes/](http://http://eatkamloops.org/winter-storage-kimchi-and-lacto-fermented-green-tomatoes/)

2\. Mulch and cover your kale and chard with straw. You will be able to eat these for weeks or months, depending on your micro-climate, zone and elevation.

3\. You should have dried your herbs during the summer. If you still have some herbs that are green and in good condition, harvest now and tie into bunches and hang in your kitchen to dry. When dry put into bags for later use. Remember to label. You can also freeze too.

4\. It's too late to plant a winter garden at this point. OR you could set up a hoop-house. These are super easy green houses. Here is a picture of mine. Look around the internet for a "plan":

[http://eatkamloops.org/learning-about-garden-weeds/](http://http://eatkamloops.org/learning-about-garden-weeds/)

5\. Do some container gardening. OR dig up some herbs and bring them indoors. The peppers in these post are now in my living room for the rest of the season. You can see the inside of our big hoop-house:

[http://eatkamloops.org/garden-harvest-photo-essay/](http://http://eatkamloops.org/garden-harvest-photo-essay/)

6\. You have a great "heat-sink" with your paving stone area assuming the area get any winter sun. If you have containers you could have them on the paving stones protected by a hoop-house. Have a large barrel of water for watering and thermal lag.

7\. Sorry, friend. Don't automatic water if you are in an area that freezes. Otherwise you could do thousands of dollars of damage to your automatic watering system. (Around here you have to go six feet down for livestock watering.) Use a bucket for winter watering. The plants wont need much. You will develop great shoulder muscles! It's almost as good as carrying milk pails!

Here's a great book: How to Grow Food in Your Polytunnel All Year Round by Mark Gatter.

### skline@peak.org — 2012-10-09T02:31:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/garden-help-needed.4200/post-56997>

There's a great book by a market gardener in Maine. He harvests greens 12 months a year WITHOUT ARTIFICIAL HEAT.

Something of a genius at it.

[http://www.amazon.com/Winter-Harvest-Handbook-Production-Greenhouses/dp/1603580816/ref=sr\_1\_2?s=books&ie=UTF8&qid=1349738783&sr=1-2&keywords=4+season+gardening](http://http://www.amazon.com/Winter-Harvest-Handbook-Production-Greenhouses/dp/1603580816/ref=sr_1_2?s=books&ie=UTF8&qid=1349738783&sr=1-2&keywords=4+season+gardening)

Some reading and some experience will show you which plants are strictly warm weather, like tomatoes, basil, corn, and green beans, and which others can tolerate some frost, like kale (very tough, kale), other cabbage family plants, peas, some herbs, and so on. Some root plants like carrots you can keep after frost by laying down a very thick mulch. Then you get down into it to harvest.

In Colorado you can have some very warm spells separated by bitter frost and snow. The deep frost without snow cover is the most damaging. Wetting things down first can help. When you have a warm spell, always water at once. The plants are very vulnerable to drying out at a time like that. It's amazing what can winter over under a good snow cover, and show up happy as can be come spring.

### hope2conceive — 2012-10-09T02:45:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/garden-help-needed.4200/post-57001>

I do kale throughout the winter with mulch/protection unless it's way way cold. I had great cilantro last year too....it took a while though. My favorite was garlic - we got lots and it came out amazing, and I planted it way late for my climate (7).

I lost my basil the first year too...next year you'll know what the blackish leaves mean. So exciting you are doing this!

How neat you have so much space too!!

### hope2conceive — 2012-10-09T02:52:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/garden-help-needed.4200/post-57003>

PS Have you heard of this blog? I love it...tells you what to do each month. Mother Earth News also has a great garden planner.

[http://www.agardenforthehouse.com/](http://http://www.agardenforthehouse.com/)

This year, I also really got into gardening with the moon, the way people used to garden. Totally fascinating....and I love what I'm learning about the seasonality and prime time for each of the things I try to grow, and also how they too are dependent on the right light (and temperatures)....

[https://www.biodynamics.com/content/2012-north-american-biodynamic-sowing-and-planting-calendar-maria-matthias-thun](http://https://www.biodynamics.com/content/2012-north-american-biodynamic-sowing-and-planting-calendar-maria-matthias-thun)

for next year:

[http://www.bookdepository.com/book/9780863159183?redirected=true&gclid=CMq8zL\_O8rICFZSd4Aod\_3YARA](http://http://www.bookdepository.com/book/9780863159183?redirected=true&gclid=CMq8zL_O8rICFZSd4Aod_3YARA)

Just something to check out if it floats your boat.

### Martha Ray — 2022-06-19T23:57:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/garden-help-needed.4200/post-313683>

> [skline@peak.org said:](/goto/post?id=56997)
>
> There's a great book by a market gardener in Maine. He harvests greens 12 months a year WITHOUT ARTIFICIAL HEAT.
> Something of a genius at it.

> [skline@peak.org said:](/goto/post?id=56997)
>
> [http://www.amazon.com/Winter-Harvest-Handbook-Production-Greenhouses/dp/1603580816/ref=sr\_1\_2?s=books&ie=UTF8&qid=1349738783&sr=1-2&keywords=4+season+gardening](http://http://www.amazon.com/Winter-Harvest-Handbook-Production-Greenhouses/dp/1603580816/ref=sr_1_2?s=books&ie=UTF8&qid=1349738783&sr=1-2&keywords=4+season+gardening)
> Some reading and some experience will show you which plants are strictly warm weather, like tomatoes, basil, corn, and green beans, and which others can tolerate some frost, like kale (very tough, kale), other cabbage family plants, peas, some herbs, and so on. Some root plants like carrots you can keep after frost by laying down a very thick mulch. Then you get down into it to harvest.
> In Colorado you can have some very warm spells separated by bitter frost and snow. The deep frost without snow cover is the most damaging. Wetting things down first can help. When you have a warm spell, always water at once. The plants are very vulnerable to drying out at a time like that. It's amazing what can winter over under a good snow cover, and show up happy as can be come spring.

Thank you for this book reference:
This is the link that worked for me [https://www.amazon.com/winter-harvest-handbook-deep-organic-greenhouses/dp/1603580816](https://www.amazon.com/winter-harvest-handbook-deep-organic-greenhouses/dp/1603580816)

# Thread: buying whole fish from the supermarket?  is that a bad idea?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/buying-whole-fish-from-the-supermarket-is-that-a-bad-idea.4201/>
**Subforum:** Beginners Area
**Posts:** 4
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### x_danny_x — 2012-10-09T01:19:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/buying-whole-fish-from-the-supermarket-is-that-a-bad-idea.4201/post-56990>

i was wondering if buying fish from the supermarket lets say shop rite or something, is a bad idea? i generally like to buy fish already boneless and skinless.

i know it is not good to buy anything else from the supermarket such as meat, eggs, etc. since they are probably from steroid/antibiotics animals.

but are fish a different story? buying from the fish market, the prices are sky high

### Integral_D — 2012-10-09T02:36:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/buying-whole-fish-from-the-supermarket-is-that-a-bad-idea.4201/post-56999>

Whole fish are a great idea. You can use every bit of it if you can figure out what it's good for. In some markets -- there's an asian market near me -- with a big fish counter, you can get them to clean it for you, and even bone/fillet it. You're not likely to be eating fish entrails, so don't bother with them. However, keep everything else and learn what to do with it. One start -- salmon skin, nice and crispy, just like in a good sushi restaurant.

### x_danny_x — 2012-10-09T05:34:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/buying-whole-fish-from-the-supermarket-is-that-a-bad-idea.4201/post-57050>

ah, so it is okay to eat the skin. was wondering about that. thanks for that.

but i also wanted to know if its okay to buy fish from the local supermarket? such as walmart and shop rite.

### Gracelind — 2012-10-12T06:11:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/buying-whole-fish-from-the-supermarket-is-that-a-bad-idea.4201/post-57699>

Hi danny,

You might ask the butcher/fishmonger at the market when the fish was caught and when it was delivered. If it's fresh, wild-caught, and has been kept under proper refrigeration I'd think that the fish would be fine.

# Thread: Truly the brain does HEAL in Ketosis!  Testimony of a Two Year old!
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/truly-the-brain-does-heal-in-ketosis-testimony-of-a-two-year-old.4202/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 15
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Momma28 — 2012-10-09T02:50:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/truly-the-brain-does-heal-in-ketosis-testimony-of-a-two-year-old.4202/post-57002>

The story begins in January of this year when I am given custody of a 16 month old nephew lost to the system by a drug abusing, alcoholic mother. I'd only seen him once before this. He made no eye contact or any attempts at physical connection. He had jerky, palsy-like movements and seizures intermittently throughout the day. He weighed 18 pounds and was 28 inches tall; just 46th percentile on the growth charts. He would only eat bananas, white bread toast and commercial milk that was being microwaved and put in a bottle, oh, and of course, soda. He had by this time been given 22 vaccines.

His first week here I let him choose what he would eat to ease him in with the other 6 kids and let him acclimate. The second week it was on....nothing was offered but what I wanted him to eat. Three days later and lots of tantrums and shed tears and he was eating meats for the first time in his life. He was given watered down Welch's with D3, Iodine, a multivit liquid and probiotics in it throughout the day. He was also given a B12 transdermal patch. His diet was fresh veggies all loaded with organic butter or bacon fat. Meats were offered with each meal and he soon grew to love them. All seizures halted within the first week most likely due to lack of dietary fats. His palsy-like movements have gradually ceased as well and have become much more normal. In fact, his balance has more than improved, its incredible.

It took him 6 weeks to learn his name. Since then, he has learned so much more...body parts, animals, fruits and veggie names and songs. His cognitive function, that was about an 8 month old, is now just slightly behind his 2 years that he turned last week. He has been on a ketogenic diet for 8 full months now and today is 30 (77th%) pounds and 34 (87th%)inches tall. He has grown 6 inches and gained 12 pounds in 8 months! Unheard of! He makes eye contact, responds to facial expressions, simple commands and best of all, gives and receives LOVE! It is true...a ketogenic diet can heal the brain, even from something most docs would call AUTISM.

### kathylu — 2012-10-09T02:56:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/truly-the-brain-does-heal-in-ketosis-testimony-of-a-two-year-old.4202/post-57006>

I can only bow my head in respect and awe...thank you!

### indigogirl — 2012-10-09T03:17:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/truly-the-brain-does-heal-in-ketosis-testimony-of-a-two-year-old.4202/post-57021>

Inspiring!!

Made my heart feel full.

How awesome of you to love a vulnerable human!

This is so heartwarming. Nobody is a lost cause.

I'd only seen him once before this. He made no eye contact or any attempts at physical connection. He had jerky, palsy-like movements and seizures intermittently throughout the day. He weighed 18 pounds and was 28 inches tall; just 46th percentile on the growth charts. He would only eat bananas, white bread toast and commercial milk that was being microwaved and put in a bottle, oh, and of course, soda. He had by this time been given 22 vaccines.
>
>

> His first week here I let him choose what he would eat to ease him in with the other 6 kids and let him acclimate. The second week it was on....nothing was offered but what I wanted him to eat. Three days later and lots of tantrums and shed tears and he was eating meats for the first time in his life. He was given watered down Welch's with D3, Iodine, a multivit liquid and probiotics in it throughout the day. He was also given a B12 transdermal patch. His diet was fresh veggies all loaded with organic butter or bacon fat. Meats were offered with each meal and he soon grew to love them. All seizures halted within the first week most likely due to lack of dietary fats. His palsy-like movements have gradually ceased as well and have become much more normal. In fact, his balance has more than improved, its incredible.
>
>
>
> It took him 6 weeks to learn his name. Since then, he has learned so much more...body parts, animals, fruits and veggie names and songs. His cognitive function, that was about an 8 month old, is now just slightly behind his 2 years that he turned last week. He has been on a ketogenic diet for 8 full months now and today is 30 (77th%) pounds and 34 (87th%)inches tall. He has grown 6 inches and gained 12 pounds in 8 months! Unheard of! ### Adria — 2012-10-09T19:07:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/truly-the-brain-does-heal-in-ketosis-testimony-of-a-two-year-old.4202/post-57117>

Momma28 what a smart and loving mother you are. Now your nephew has a priceless gift.. of a new life..so wonderful! Please keep us updated on his progress.

### Momma28 — 2012-10-10T00:12:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/truly-the-brain-does-heal-in-ketosis-testimony-of-a-two-year-old.4202/post-57174>

Thank you all for your words of encouragement...they mean so much. This little fella has had a hard first months of life. He continues to improve on the irrational fears that must have been part of his trauma. Ceiling fans and anything else that's spinning, in particular. We took the kids to the Dentzel Carousel last summer and he went bizerk. This was the first time we had seen him react in absolute terror. We use the desensitization technique with him to get him to recognize things he is afraid of as everyday objects or occurances. I turned the ceiling fans on all summer after that to get him used to the spinning above him. His other fear was the trash can. LOL He would get hysterical if you asked him to throw away his diaper. I was working with him on simple commands. We had to walk him to it, open the lid and have him release the diaper all with him in hysteria. After two months of screaming, he can now open the can and deposit it himself in triumphant victory! And he is so proud of himself when he does. He is really a very smart little boy. Most of these children are.

You all will get a kick out of this. Last night while reading him his little word dictionary that is full of colorful pictures in a to z order, we hit the B's. The word "breakfast" had pics of a bowl of cereal, pancakes and doughnuts...yeah. Then there was a pic of broccoli. I said, "oooh, I see something yummy to eat". At which he immediately reached for the broccoli and pretended to hungrily stuff it in his mouth. It was so cute. He didn't even know what the cereal or pancakes was. He has surprised me daily with what he knows even though he cannot talk yet. He is just now starting his first words and all of his wonderful baby babble!

### indigogirl — 2012-10-10T00:17:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/truly-the-brain-does-heal-in-ketosis-testimony-of-a-two-year-old.4202/post-57177>

Please keep us updated. This is so heartwarming and inspiring. Just makes me smile!

### Momma28 — 2012-10-10T00:27:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/truly-the-brain-does-heal-in-ketosis-testimony-of-a-two-year-old.4202/post-57180>

> **Adria;58419 wrote:** Momma28 what a smart and loving mother you are. Now your nephew has a priceless gift.. of a new life..so wonderful! Please keep us updated on his progress.

Adria,

What a sweetie you are. You would fit right in with the first seven kids....their names all start with "A". This new little fella's name is Jeramyah. I will certainly remember to update you all on his progress from time to time. He is a handful, but he brings great joy. I guess i learned to mother by what I lacked in one. I married and started a family, have homeschooled 22 years and in the process healed my own inner child by becoming the mother to her that I always wanted. I was very blessed to have married a man that allowed me that freedom and time to heal. My soul just delights in loving, healing and blessing children. Patty Cakes, I wish that I could take them all, but my house and my husband's wallet aren't big enough. For now, I am more than willing to continue to help other mothers in loving and healing theirs. I feel so blessed to have come across Dr. K's info and the wonderful people on this forum and to have a place to send these mothers so they can learn more and that there is now validity to what I'm telling them. This is the south and a Doctor's word is gold here, but without a credential they just look at me and walk away, though you have the answers to help their children and themselves.

### JoeBranca — 2012-10-11T00:49:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/truly-the-brain-does-heal-in-ketosis-testimony-of-a-two-year-old.4202/post-57458>

Wow, such an unforgettable lesson reinforcing so much of what this site is about. A lot of daily challenges and hard work you are doing, but it's a simple equation isn't it? Confident knowledge + Perseverance = Amazing Results

### Jack Kruse — 2012-10-11T00:51:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/truly-the-brain-does-heal-in-ketosis-testimony-of-a-two-year-old.4202/post-57460>

# Thread: Gut microfilms
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gut-microfilms.4204/>
**Subforum:** Adrenal Rx and Leaky Gut Rx
**Posts:** 13
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### indigogirl — 2012-10-09T03:29:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gut-microfilms.4204/post-57023>

Can someone please explain gut microfilms?

When I google it I come up with stuff about German films!

### Jack Kruse — 2012-10-09T03:57:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gut-microfilms.4204/post-57030>

think plaque on your teeth........it is the film that allows bacteria grow on it.......you got it in your gut too.

### bigknitwit — 2012-10-09T16:11:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gut-microfilms.4204/post-57096>

[http://coolinginflammation.blogspot.com/2009/09/cure-for-inflammatory-diseases.html](http://http://coolinginflammation.blogspot.com/2009/09/cure-for-inflammatory-diseases.html)

I liked reading this site. I still don't have a good solid grasp of the protocol though. Like how long to live with the diarrhea (I'm sure it's unique to each person). What signs to look for to tell you your films might be cleared out? And then there's the repopulation bit, which I think it pretty hormone dependant to achieve a good result - pesky hormones! I'll copy/paste what I wrote about it in the Hashimoto's thread:

_Jack talked in the last webinar about re-engineering the gut, and it seems to me, the starting place is getting a decent hormone profile, and then stripping out gut biofilms, and then re-seeding using the epcotx. He mentioned in passing about using Mg and/or coconut oil for "2-3 days" to remove existing biofilms. However, when I have done more reading about these biofilms, it seems they need far more treatment than just "a few days" of diarrhea. The bacteria create a polyssacharide (hard shell, I think) exterior and live happily underneath it. Antibiotics don't reach them in any measurable quantity, and they are very difficult to erradicate. I was reading the other night on another site about them (here: [http://coolinginflammation.blogspot....-diseases.html](http://http://coolinginflammation.blogspot....-diseases.html)). This author has a lot of similar ideas to Jack's, so of course I like a little consistency. But other places I've read about biofilm removal taking months (protease enzymes between meals can apparently do some decent work at cracking their shells).

The thing is, I think caution is needed, because without the right initial conditions after the clean-out (and this involves hormones, like the Pg/E2 ratio, for reasons I can't articulate, and would love if someone else could!, good cortisol profile, probably others), I would think you would cultivate something less than desireable.

So, these tidbits have been dropped, but without any real measurables (like how longshould you continue with diarrhea Mg loading? How do you know that they're cleared out and you're ready for the next step? Would CO be better, if once cleared you know it because the diarrhea stops - or does it stop?). BUt it seems imperative that hormones are step 1..._

### indigogirl — 2012-10-09T16:29:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gut-microfilms.4204/post-57098>

Bigknitwit, I don't have any answers for you. I, too, would like more, specific info on how to do this. I do remember Jack saying ( during a Webinar or Q&A) that he had diarrhea for 3 weeks when clearing his gut microfilms.

### freesia — 2012-10-09T19:48:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gut-microfilms.4204/post-57122>

If you google **Biofilms** you're likely to come up with a lot more. That's the most commonly used term to describe them.

I've done biofilm protocols over the past 4 years - destroying them can release a lot of stuff so a gentle approach (like Jacks) is highly recommended by me! Companies like Kirkmans and many others make formulas used by parent's treating their autistic children with a biomedical approach. Treating Biofilms can be very effective.

### indigogirl — 2012-10-09T23:44:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gut-microfilms.4204/post-57164>

[http://m.youtube.com/#/watch?v=lpI4WCM\_9pM&desktop\_uri=%2Fwatch%3Fv%3DlpI4WCM\_9pM](http://http://m.youtube.com/#/watch?v=lpI4WCM_9pM&desktop_uri=%2Fwatch%3Fv%3DlpI4WCM_9pM)

### bigknitwit — 2012-10-10T02:20:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gut-microfilms.4204/post-57205>

[http://bacteriality.com/2008/05/26/biofilm/](http://http://bacteriality.com/2008/05/26/biofilm/)

### Momma28 — 2012-10-10T03:00:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gut-microfilms.4204/post-57215>

The question to me is....do these bacteria enter the body through the teeth? Such as through the infection of root canals or decay under mercury amalgam? I have so many friends with serious health issues that have had one or more root canals and lots of AI issues.

Dr. Jerry Tennant says that these bacteria are dormant in the body until ph level drops low enough for the oxygen to fall within the tissues. They then wake up and begin to feed.....on us. They liquify nutrients in the cells and "drink" them up. Their waste products are actually what cause us to respond with inflammation. How the Body Works Part 2 on youtube.

Also, one of my friends who has suffered with fibromyalgia for years and has it well under control with a ketogenic diet underwent treatment with a Dr. Reezor out of Chicago by using something called Bellugi. This is supposedly an enzyme produced by a worm of this name. I will question her as to the results. It was supposed to clear biofilm. This was a year or so ago and had no clue what she was talking about back then. I'll keep every posted.

### indigogirl — 2012-10-10T03:29:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gut-microfilms.4204/post-57221>

Momma28, you might be interested in this book: [http://books.google.com/books/about/Uninformed\_Consent.html?id=nNVpAAAAMAAJ](http://http://books.google.com/books/about/Uninformed_Consent.html?id=nNVpAAAAMAAJ)

"Uniformed Consent: The Hidden Dangers in Dental Care"

Dr. Huggins and Dr. Levy assert that a large number of disorders are, though often incurable, easily preventable. He proposes that multiple sclerosis, lupus, leukemia, chronic fatigue syndrome, Parkinson's disease, many mental disorders including Alzheimer's, and even major diseases like breast cancer are caused, in part, by the toxins we place in our bodies. Where do these toxins come from and how do they get into our bodies? You my be surprised to find that you have actually paid to have them put there. These dangerous materials--mercury, cadmium, beryllium, nickel, and others--are used in "everyday dentistry" to make up the fillings, root canals, and bridgework in our mouths, and are supposed to be "safe." But are they?

"Uninformed Consent" presents cases of toxic poisoning--of depressed immune systems and inexplicable illnesses--to toxins entering the bloodstream from the heavy metals in dental materials. The authors also discuss the hidden truths that the dental industry in America doesn't want to talk about, and the real reasons the dangers of these materials have been suppressed and ignored.

Drs. Huggins and Levy implore the reader: "Don't leave your health in your dentist's hands and assume that all will be fine. Become informed and take an active role in your health. Know what will be implanted in your mouth. You must decide at the outset what is more important to you--the life of a filling or "your" life." "Uninformed Consent" will give you the facts so that you may take responsibility for your dental--and complete--health and wellness.

### freesia — 2012-10-10T05:42:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gut-microfilms.4204/post-57244>

> **indigogirl;58489 wrote:** Freesia, what kind of protocols have you done? How did you assess the results?

Hi Indigogirl.

I started a protocol using Kirkman's Biofilm defense (plus Vit C, bioflavinoids, activated charcoal and a few other things)...then I switched to something called Interfase Plus (Klaire Labs). The protocol crippled me! My understanding is that destroying biofilms too fast can lead to bacteria, their toxic byproducts and metals they're holding onto all spilling out into the gut. How well your gut is functioning, and the state of your general health will determine how well you cope with this. I had to step back the doses of everything but still really struggled with the protocol and I wouldn't know if it was a success. I guess what I determined was that I had a problem with biofilms?!

A year later I started with a more gentle approach using apple cider vinegar, coconut oil, occasional activated charcoal, and edta suppositories. I was doing the GAPS diet at the time so gut was in better shape I expect and I didn't have the same negative results, though fatigue and headaches increased temporarily. I followed the protocol for six months and I did improve over that time but it's impossible for me to assess how much any improvement was down to the biofilm treatment vs starting the GAPS diet.

Wish I could be more helpful!

PS should have clarified in my previous post that I have seen biofilm treatment mentioned by parents in my local biomed group as having had a very positive effect on their autistic children.

### Patty Cakes — 2012-10-18T22:16:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/enterolabs-compare-educate-thread.4206/post-59014>

> **vkiernan;60523 wrote:** I love cashews too! Thanks for the reminder. I believe it only tests those things listed. Perhaps they are the most reactive in the general population??? I don't know how to interpret the meat thing. How can I not eat meat? What about bacon (pork)?

This has a lot of info in it for you. It looks to me like the results are very valuable.

So you cannot eat dairy, eggs, grains (most if not all but I think you are off all), and no chicken (no loss there) and pork. We eat turkey bacon but maybe that's like chicken. But there is also lamb bacon that is wonderful and similar but not totally alike.

I'm curious how the beef result would be interpreted too. I had an allergy test and almonds came up as a very mild allergy. They told me I could have them every four days. I just eliminated them. But I wonder if you can have beef just not all the time.

Also cashews are good for soups, smoothies, and other items for a creamy thickening ingredient. DH has a problem w/cashews so we don't eat them but I've heard good results.

I'm trying to figure out are you Celiac or Celiac Sprue with these results or is that inconclusive on that front?

Thanks Patty and yes I have dropped eggs, dairy and grains except the little slip of butter here or there but mostly ghee. Pork is REALLY tough for me as I work for a pork mfg company and I live off the stuff. We make awesome bacon, braunsweiger, hams, etc. I would think that turkey falls in the same class as chicken. I think I need to tell my son to get me a deer. He's bow hunting at present and that would probably be a safe bet.

As for celiac vs. celiac sprue, I don't think there is a difference and I believe it is inconclusive as you have to have a biopsy to confirm. All I know is I have the gene and my daughter actually has confirmed celiac by biopsy. Guessing if I'm not there already, I'm well on my way if I don't change things around.

### Patty Cakes — 2012-10-19T01:38:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/enterolabs-compare-educate-thread.4206/post-59067>

Good thing you caught it now before it manifested into something worse! I think a lot of people maybe scared to have this done because of the fear of the results. I'm excited for you because you now know what you cannot do. I can't wait to get my testing done after the first of the year.

Working for a pork manufacturer just puts another kink in the wheel of life and you will just work through that. Like I said DH doesn't eat pork so I have gotten used to other meats cured similar to bacon and it's not even my issue! I've have enough foods on my own that must be eliminated!!

My understanding, very limited at best, was that celiac was just ABs produced from the wheat and Celiac Sprue was ABs from both dairy and wheat. Like I said I don't know that much about it and I'm sure you know waaaay more with your daughter being Celiac.

Also I think Cyrex Labs does a pretty conclusive blood test for Celiac. I had their cross-reactive for gluten test done and it was good info. I was told things can change once you get the body healthier so I am hoping to get that test redone again next year sometime. But first is EnteroLab A+C.

I called her today just to get a better explanation of my results. She made it sound it was accurate. She also told me I shouldn't eat salmon right now as sometimes there is a cross reaction to tuna. I have a lot in the freezer. Kinda wondering what I am supposed to eat? Can't eat chicken, pork, tuna and even beef is on the list. Can't eat grains. Veggies are carbs. No eggs. No dairy. Hmmm, what else is left, water! I wonder how shellfish play into this - totally different then tuna. I realize I am exaggerating a bit but still I didn't expect to react to any meats.

She did tell me to start with the top of the list and gradually not eat those things and if I do only eat them every 4 days like Patty said. She said right now things are pretty flared up and by not eating from the top would help. I asked her if I will ever be able to eat some of those things and she said I might if I get my system settled down. She also said to not eat a lot of the things that I didn't react to as I might start. She also mentioned to keep a food journal and note everything I can think of to see what I might react to.

### Lyndra — 2012-10-25T02:53:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/enterolabs-compare-educate-thread.4206/post-60062>

Got my results back today and mostly very good news. There's one confusing & contradictory finding perhaps someone can help explain:

> Anti-tissue Transglutaminase IgA: 4 units, normal range is < 10. The level of intestinal IgA antibodies to the human enzyme tissue transglutaminase was below the upper limit of normal. Hence, there is no evidence of a gluten-induced autoimmune reaction to this enzyme.
>
>
>
> Anti-gliadin IgA: 14 units, normal range is < 10. The level of intestinal anti-gliadin IgA antibody was elevated, indicative of active dietary gluten sensitivity. For optimal health; resolution or improvement of gluten-induced syndromes it is recommended that you follow a strict and permanent gluten free diet. As gluten sensitivity is a genetic syndrome, you may want to have your relatives screened as well.

Based on a recent reaction when I inadvertently ate gluten, I'm convinced I am gluten sensitive. I would like to understand the transglutaminase result and why that indicates I'm not gluten sensitive.

The other quandary is that I had low values for casein & eggs (both 4) but I wonder whether it makes sense to eat them or not since I show some level of antibodies...

### MamaGrok — 2012-10-25T16:07:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/enterolabs-compare-educate-thread.4206/post-60129>

I totally don't know, but would guess that you're sensitive to the gliadin protein, but not the transglutaminase enzyme that your body produces to help you digest gluten. Thus, still wheat sensitive, just not autoimmune to this specific enzyme at this point.

### bigknitwit — 2012-10-25T17:12:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/enterolabs-compare-educate-thread.4206/post-60137>

I don't totally know either. However I do know that tissue transglutaminase antibodies are associated with celiac disease (autoimmune). The body even tries to destroy it's own gluten enzyme. In your case, it looks like this is not happening at a significant level, like MamaGrok said. I wonder if JK would say that any level of ttg antibodies is a marker for celiac (much like he says with low levels of thyroid antibodies and Hashi's). That I really couldn't say.

> **MamaGrok;61731 wrote:** I totally don't know, but would guess that you're sensitive to the gliadin protein, but not the transglutaminase enzyme that your body produces to help you digest gluten. Thus, still wheat sensitive, just not autoimmune to this specific enzyme at this point.

I got the gene test for my daughters and they each have some form of the gene.Â  Means that they are or can become gluten intolerant.Â  One was already showing signs of it so that is why we tested.Â  She would get a runny nose about an hour after eating gluten.Â  Anything can turn the gene on.Â  So if you are already not eating any gluten ----it would just be wanting to know.Â  I am wondering if these genes are in the 23 and me.Â  I have not seen them yet.Â  I know the 23 and me checks for celiac.Â  Since testing the girls and they have 2 copies we know my husband and I each have to have one.Â

### Lanie — 2012-12-25T00:27:41+0200

<<<Okay, Got my almost 4yo daughter's enterolab back this morning.
Most of it makes complete sense. I'm a bit surprised by the eggs and rice though, and I thought almonds were an issue.
Luckily no meats were on her reaction list.
This was an expensive test but I wish I had this information for her 2 years ago.... I can't get that crucial development time back. But, now I know better and can do better moving forward with her....
[DDENTEROLABRESULTS.txt](http://http://www.jackkruse.com/forum/convert_files/attachments/65513=559-DDENTEROLABRESULTS.txt)\>>>

RiverNeighbor - Thank you so much for writing this.Â  I've been lurking for a long time, and seeing this post of yours inspired me to get this test done for my 2-year old daughter (almost 3).Â  I was balking at the price, but your post put me over the edge.Â  Got her results today.Â  She's having an immune reaction to pretty much everything - especially gluten.Â  Now we have the information we needed to really justify a huge lifestyle change.Â  We were half-assing the no-dairy before, and allowing wheat on special occasions.Â  No more of that.Â  I'll be cooking for her a lot more now.Â  Thank you - you never know who you're going to help, and I really appreciate you sharing your story.

### RiverNeighbor — 2012-12-27T19:52:48+0200

Lanie, Â  I'm glad it was helpful. Â For us, Â giving up the milk wasn't as hard as I thought it was going to be. Â We said "bye" to it one day and no one has said a peep about it since. Â I never really gave them juice so they just started drinking more water. Â  The harder thing for us has been variety in meat and getting in veggies. Â At least she likes SeaSnax. Â In fact, Â my daughter is doing better than my son (I didn't have him tested but at least for now I've eliminated the same foods for both). Â I'm very pleased to say that we have been 100% compliant with our elimination of the foods she responded to! Â But, Â I was hoping that by now we would be able to try egg yolks but unfortunately she still is having some gut problems. Â I'm thinking that the Metametrix GI test (and maybe some other tests) will be our next step. Â  Â We've got an appointment with a Naturopath next week who I interviewed and think will be helpful - so fingers crossed. Â 

I think we've got some vaccines coming up at 4 years. Â I've decided that we're not doing any vaccines until we get all her gut and immune issues under control - and even then we will take a good hard look at what we really think is necessary. Â

Yep, just cuz they are carbs and I was running thru the list of no no's in general. Nothing to do with the lab result itself. Just so many things off the "list." I will say though, since I have had a heavy metals test and then a consult with Jack, there is a possibility that my problem might be due to the amount of metals I have and not a "true" autoimmune issue. Now, I will not know until I clear the metals and retest but I wanted to let others know that there might be hope. This is why testing is so critical and to apply the results to your own n=1 and not anyone else's. We are all different even though some things "appear" to be the same.

### hellojtm — 2013-01-04T20:18:59+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/enterolabs-compare-educate-thread.4206/post-69379>

Sounds great V.

I don't think you should avoid vegetables in any case. You can have a very rich diet of vegetables and leafy greens!! Then add in those shellfish and other items people mentioned to round it out. I think avoiding fresh veggies because they are carbs is wild. I can see the value of keeping a diet low-glycemic, etc... or seasonally glycemic... but no veggies seems massively restrictive and I don't know the logic supporting it. We're not Inuits after all

I'm not saying you can't do it, just saying that you should question whether you need to, especially in light of all the new restrictions you are about to make.

I was reading some stuff by Charles Poliquin in which he was testing his body builders regularly for food allergies and they were pretty much all allergic to the things they ate the most and if they stopped eating them they antibodies went away. His response was to have his builders cycle their foods, i.e. eat eggs for a couple months and then drop them for a couple months (hey! it's almost seasonal!). Which is kind of a hack, maybe the common thread amongst the foods was a history or current leaky gut. Maybe the reason you're not showing antibodies to cashews is because you haven't had many of them lately?

Point is, I'm not that worried about your labs (admittedly I'm a total newb) because I don't think you have a real allergy to some of those foods and you'll be back eating them when your other issues are resolved. I mean, imagine you have poor digestion and a leaky gut, you have the opportunity to develop an immune response to any food at that point because the food is getting into your intestines undigested.

BEST OF LUCK.

jesse

Thanks Jesse. You are spot on on all accounts. I did add more veggies in and then Jack reminded me that there is a time and place for them and winter is not it. Although, I do still eat low carb veggies but was trying to really slow down on them. I did have leaky gut for sure and I know I am healing it or things wouldn't be getting better. And yes, I did have a problem with those foods as they were the ones I was eating all the time. Enterolabs told me not to eat things I was ok with very much or I would end up having the same reaction. My take away is to rotate. Sometimes not so easy but I am sure trying. I am going to get my metals cleared somehow and then see where things are at. I will know if I start clearing the metals and feel better and then add foods in and I start tanking again.

### MamaGrok — 2013-03-05T20:59:38+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/enterolabs-compare-educate-thread.4206/post-79168>

I forgot to update this when I did get my results.

Enterolabs (range, then my results)
Fecal Fat 00 299
Anti-Tissue Transglutaminase IgA <10 14

Most Antigenic Foods Sensitivity Stool Panel (500 max possible)
Anti-gliadin IgA <10 66
Anti-casein IgA <10 19
Anti-ovalbumin IgA <10 36
Anti-soy IgA <10 16

Comprehensive Food Sensitivity Stool Panel - Measuring immunologic reactivity
Mean Value 11 Antigenic Foods <10 14
Tuna Most
Rice Most
Beef Most
Oat Most
Chicken Intermediate
Pork Intermediate
Corn Intermediate
Cashew Intermediate
White potato No significant
Almond No significant
Walnut No significant

Gluten Sensitivity Gene Test
HLA-DQB1 Molecular analysis, Allele 1 O202 (DQ2.2)
HLA-DQB1 Molecular analysis, Allele 2 O602 (DQ6.2)
Serologic equivalent: HLA-DQ 2,1 (Subtype 2,6)

At the time of the test, I had been off gluten for one year, 100% off dairy 95% of the previous year, but had had some recently, same with eggs, same with soy. No oats in two years or more. Everything but tuna, beef, & pork are rare or absent in my diet.

Fat malabsorption was way over positive a year ago, so that is a big improvement.
I understand that ovalbumin is only in the egg white, so I'm not sure this means I have to avoid egg yolk.
I'm not at all sure what these results mean I should do, because, for instance, it didn't test salmon or cod. If I eliminate tuna, it will increase the amount of salmon, cod, herrings, etc., and perhaps I'm sensitive to any or all of those more than tuna. It seems it could do more harm than good. Same goes for beef & pork.

I'm also not sure why they don't give absolute values for the 11 most common. After all, they give values for wheat, even though the relative amount is still most important. Let's say I'm a 66 for wheat & 3 on beef. Let's say everything else of the 11 most common is under 3, and sure, beef is "most" reactive, but it doesn't really matter.

Analysis welcomed.

# Thread: CT caused rapid weight gain?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-caused-rapid-weight-gain.4214/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 5
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### FarmersDaughter — 2012-10-09T13:36:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-caused-rapid-weight-gain.4214/post-57077>

To start I am 32, female, and been paleo for +/- two years. Strictly so oct 2011-April 2012. 5'6", and at the start of CT, 160. Started leptin reset that all, about the time of the big ass thread at mad. BAB, etc. no weight loss seen, so I added CT.

I started with the face bath, the high fat/meal and ice water prior to, etc. When i moved into cold baths-45 min at about 58\*, things changed - gained 22 pounds of fat in two menstrual cycles. I stopped CT, suspecting that was the cause, as even while on 100% paleo, my weight was stable. (Another frustrating issue, but an aside, at this point.)

Fast forward to current, October 2013. The weight as not budged, though activity as increased dramatically (going from desk work to building fence and farm work-climbing bins, etc,) and per food journals intake is virtually identical.

I'm hovering at 182, which is far too heavy at my height. Walking an hour a day beyond what I do at work isn't changing anything, and its been 3.5 months since adding that.

Currently on 1.5 gr naturethroid (recently down from 4 grains) iodine, se, k2, a,d, bcomp, natural calm, DIM, 100 mg progesterone 12-25, and 10 mg DHEA. Suspect low estrogen and testosterone too, but had to start with a new doc who must reinvent the wheel since prev doc left practice.

In the interest of keeping this brief, I'm confused. I've followed instructions to a t, aside from quarterly labs, as I'm currently unemployed, with a mortgage AND a rent payment, (and oddly enough, the "recovery" hasn't hit my neck of the woods yet).

Suggestions on things to look at? I'm stumped, stress is lower than any point in th last 10 years even with the financial drought, and it doesn't appear to matter whether I eat 100% SAD or 100% paleo, the jeans fit the same. I feel better on paleo, which is th sole reason I'm sticking it out. I'm about ready to say to hell with it, however. Any suggestions of things to review/do differently would be most appreciated.

Laurel

### Jack Kruse — 2012-10-09T14:30:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-caused-rapid-weight-gain.4214/post-57088>

CT helps weight loss........if youre gaining you need a work up.

### Patty Cakes — 2012-10-09T18:19:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-caused-rapid-weight-gain.4214/post-57108>

It sounds to me like you need a hormone panel, hs-CRP, full thyroid panel (TSH, FT3 and FT4 + antibody tests), and ASI at the very least. It sounds like you have a lot going on inside that only lab tests will reveal.

I'll tell you a little of my story as an example of weird loss/gain/loss that was easily explained in my labs. I lost 10# upon starting paleo. Started CT and BAM! 8 of those came right back on. I succumbed to the suggestions of getting labs. My hormone panel showed I was estrogen dominant after years of being low. I was quite surprised. I continued to CT to try and flush out the estrogen all the while doing other things to help reduce the high estrogen and supplementing my progesterone. I stopped CTing for three months in the summer and recently started back up. In the time period of stopping CT I lost about 5 of the eight I had gained back and it was S-L-O-W. After starting CT back up I am now dropping weight and within a couple of lbs. of my goal. This shows the weight gain and loss is not linear and can be attributed to more than CT. For me, CT was the catalyst that caused the weight gain while my body was trying to release that excess estrogen. Your labs will tell your story for you!

### FarmersDaughter — 2012-10-10T19:17:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-caused-rapid-weight-gain.4214/post-57362>

All I have at this point are 24-hour urine labs, would be more than happy to throw them up for a second opinion if anyone is familiar with them. I have high 5alpha reductase due to a progesterone deficiency (as I understand it) so among other things, that is working against me.

Blood tests will have to wait until I can either find employment that pays, or sell my house, as right now I'm juggling bills, unfortunately. (My deep freezer died last week on top of everything, lost close to 250 pounds or more of good homegrown beef, pork, and elk, darn it. Thank heavens it's hunting season!)

Maybe in my next life i will get a body that reacts appropriately to what I do  in the meantime, however, maybe Ill add as much shellfish as I can, in the interest of nutrient density, until I can swing bloods.

Would it be prudent to restart CT as well, and tough it out, or just wait? Any thoughts? Thank you both!

### Patty Cakes — 2012-10-10T19:30:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-caused-rapid-weight-gain.4214/post-57367>

Doing CT now or waiting is a contextual thing. Depends on your situation. Based on progesterone deficiency I would venture a guess that you are estrogen dominant, a club many of us belong to. This could be a partial reason for the weight gain. For me personally, I just went ahead and kept CTing. I'm not saying you do that but with my situation it seemed right at that time. Eating as much shellfish can only help. Do what you can because anything helps!

Sorry to hear about your lost meat. I would be so sad to loose the contents of our freezer....

# Thread: Gut Bacteria Show Intelligence?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gut-bacteria-show-intelligence.4217/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### AKMan — 2012-10-09T19:46:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gut-bacteria-show-intelligence.4217/post-57121>

[http://phys.org/news/2012-10-intestinal-bacteria-phages-weapons.html](http://http://phys.org/news/2012-10-intestinal-bacteria-phages-weapons.html)

Phages, short for bacteriophages, are viruses created by bacteria that target and destroy other bacteria. Unfortunately, despite a lot of research since their discovery nearly century ago, very little is known about how they function, particularly, in the human gut. In this new research, the team discovered something that had never been seen before, a strain of bacteria that create a phage for the express purpose of killing off other bacteria that are competing for the same resources.

The gut, as most know, is home to trillions of bacteria; some provide benefits to the host, such as helping to digest certain foods, while others are not so good, causing digestive problems. One in particular, the plentiful Enterococcus faecalis, appears to live without creating problems in the gut, but causes a lot of problems when it gets in the bloodstream, accounting for many hospital acquired infections. In looking at a particular strain of E. faecalis known as V583, the researchers found that when it was introduced alone into a germ-free mouse gut, it began churning out phages, which seemed counter-productive as it takes a lot of energy to do so.

In looking closer, the team discovered that V583 did have a purpose, and that was to kill any other strains of E. faecalis that might show up, gobbling resources. Thus, the gut bacteria were creating phages to use as a weapon against closely related bacteria that might consume shared resources. The researchers call it a form of bacterial warfare, but also suggest that the discovery might offer some insight into how bacteria in general might be used to create phages in ways that can be controlled, allowing for the development of targeted anti-bacterial agents that kill offending bacteria without harming those that are beneficial.

### chocolate — 2012-10-10T03:12:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gut-bacteria-show-intelligence.4217/post-57218>

I keep thinking that I got nuttin' after years of antibiotics. This stuff is amazing to me.
