Ask your doc to arrange for this tests:

Spectracell Comprehensive Nutritional Panel

[http://www.healthremedies.com/intracellular\_vitamin\_analysis\_spectracell.html](http://http://www.healthremedies.com/intracellular_vitamin_analysis_spectracell.html)

[http://www.healthremedies.com/essential\_metabolic\_fatty\_acids\_analysis\_emfa\_genova\_diagnostics.html](http://http://www.healthremedies.com/essential_metabolic_fatty_acids_analysis_emfa_genova_diagnostics.html)

\-------------------

Also, on day (3-4) of your cycle get at LabCorp:

(It would help also to get same list on day 19-21.)

LONG LIST for LabCorp

Send copy to patient [janszulc@optonline.net](mailto:janszulc@optonline.net)

1 ------- Pregnenolone, Mass Spectrometry--Test Number: 140707 CPT Code: 84140

2 ------- 17α-Hydroxypregnenolone by HPLC-MS/MS

4 ------- Progesterone--Test Number: 004317 CPT Code: 84144

5 ------- Cortisol, Free, Serum (Free Cortisol, Serum Cortisol, CBG)

6 ------- Cortisol, AM & PM Test Number: 104000 CPT Code: 82533(x2) (7:30AM, 12PM)

7 ------- Corticosteroid-binding Globulin (CBG), Transcortin

8 ------- Corticotropin Releasing Hormone (CRH)

9 ------- Dehydroepiandrosterone (DHEA) Sulfate--Test Number: 004020 CPT Code: 82627

10 ------- Dehydroepiandrosterone (DHEA)--Test Number: 004100 CPT Code: 82626

11 ------- Androstenedione, LCMS--Test Number: 004705 CPT Code: 82157

12 ------- Testosterone, Total, Serum Test Number: 004226 CPT Code: 84403

13 ------- Testosterone, Free and Weakly Bound--Test Number: 143255 CPT Code: 84402; 84403

14 ------- SHBG

15 ------- Dihydrotestosterone (DHT)--Test Number: 500142 CPT Code: 82651

16 ------- Androstanediol Glucuronide (Endocrine Sciences) Test Number: 500881 CPT Code: 82154

17 ------- Estrogens, Total; Test Number: 004549 CPT Code: 82672

18 ------- Estradiol, Sensitive Test Number: 140244 CPT Code: 82670

19 ------- Estrone, Serum Test Number: 004564 CPT Code: 82679

# Thread: Researchers-discover-mechanism-by-which-magnesium reduces cytokine production
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/researchers-discover-mechanism-by-which-magnesium-reduces-cytokine-production.4237/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 10
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### chocolate — 2012-10-10T19:45:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/researchers-discover-mechanism-by-which-magnesium-reduces-cytokine-production.4237/post-57369>

[http://www.news-medical.net/news/20121010/Researchers-discover-mechanism-by-which-magnesium-reduces-cytokine-production.aspx](http://http://www.news-medical.net/news/20121010/Researchers-discover-mechanism-by-which-magnesium-reduces-cytokine-production.aspx)

> Magnesium sulfate is given to many pregnant women to treat preterm labor and preeclampsia and was recently shown to prevent cerebral palsy; however little is known about how it works. Researchers at Case Western Reserve University School of Medicine recently discovered the mechanism by which magnesium reduces the production of cytokines. Cytokines are molecules responsible for regulating inflammation; they play a key role conditions, such as diabetes, obesity, atherosclerosis, asthma, and alcoholic liver disease and cirrhosis. Although the study related to pregnancy, inflammation is the culprit of many conditions and learning more about individual's magnesium levels may help a much broader patient population.
>
>
>
> In a study published in The Journal of Immunology, the laboratories of Helene Bernstein, MD, PhD, and Andrea Romani, MD, PhD, reported that magnesium decreases inflammation by reducing the activity of cells' primary protein, Nuclear Factor Kappa Beta (NF-kB), and the subsequent production of cytokines. This new insight offers a promising new immunotherapeutic strategy by which a simple nutrient, known to be safe based on its extensive usage in obstetric settings, can decrease inflammation in diseases other than pregnancy, including in other sepsis, respiratory distress syndrome, asthma, atherosclerosis, diabetes and cancer. The cost of all of these diseases in the United States exceeds $200 billion annually.

Carbs are great in long light cycles when they grow in nature. They are not ideal in times where they do not belong biologically. This creates and epi genetic mismatch and that mismatch alters cellular signaling to cause inflammation and this inflammation ruins your brain's ability to think well. When you cant think well you make poor choices and you begin to become a dog chasing its tail, while never quite perceiving why you are failing over and over again.

Change is a hard barrier for humans to break. It require fluidity of thought and that requires both a physical and mental motion to be used to get on in the environment. The new relationship helps you learn new ways of thinking about your situation and your life. Ultimately, you look at the world in a way that would have been so foreign to you that it wouldn’t have made any sense before you changed. I practice thinking this way and it opens doors to my brain that were previously chained shut.

The voice of culture is so loud though, and the systems so ingrained, that even when people are aware it's awfully hard to find the ways out of that "system." As Jack alludes, we first unchain the doors in our minds, and before long we can step through with our bodies as well.

**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nobel-prize-for-cell-signalling-work.4241/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Michael — 2012-10-10T21:16:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nobel-prize-for-cell-signalling-work.4241/post-57402>

> Two molecular biologists who have spent decades uncovering the workings of proteins central to cell communication have won this yearâ€™s Nobel Prize in Chemistry, to general acclaim from others in the field.

[http://www.nature.com/news/cell-signalling-work-nets-chemistry-nobel-1.11557](http://http://www.nature.com/news/cell-signalling-work-nets-chemistry-nobel-1.11557)

### Jack Kruse — 2012-10-11T00:15:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nobel-prize-for-cell-signalling-work.4241/post-57450>

I am glad you linked this here. BG 13 just was posted and I want to link this again to show you the significance yet again:

TRUTH BOMB ALERT: Read this again carefully: This is significant as the sodium-potassium pump accounts for some 20% of the basal metabolic rate but approximately 60% of the energy utilization in the brain. This expands all cellular performance like no other physiologic attribute in any mammal on this planet. This is precisely why humans have special abilities that no other mammal does. This physiologic dynamic is what really powers the human brain so efficiently.

HOW ABOUT THE EYE? This is where the circadian signals first begin to register in the brain’s SCN. (Think CT 4 and 6)

NON GEEKS: I told you the suprachiasmatic nucleus was a big deal to aging and biology in the massively important CT-6. When your ability to signal light is altered through your eye you die quicker.

GEEKS: What does make a difference in paper is the biochemical reactivity of **G-protein coupled receptors and DHA because of the addition of that last double bond in DHA. When neural lipids have that last cis double bond unique things begin to happen when proteins are embedded in the lipid bilayer loaded with DHA. For example in the eye, the G-protein called rhodopsin, a light sensing protein, markedly alters its biochemistry and metabolism in its interaction with DHA. DHA actually changes the abilities of rhodopsin to allow it to transduce light quickly because it is able to solubilize rhodopsin more strongly than the saturated fat , palmitate in the cell membrane. When DHA is removed from the cell membrane we lose the ability to transduce light. This has been experiementally shown in infant retina who were deficient in DHA. This effect is mediated by the pi electron cloud I spoke about in Brain Gut 5. _It is a quantum effect directly on the biochemistry of the neural lipid’s interactions with these G coupled proteins._**

BG 13 was published before the Nobel was given for this cellular signaling issue. Anyone beginning to see when you read the basic science and couple it to the brain physiology how incredibly important DHA and G- coupled proteins are? Well guess what folks? This marriage of G proteins and DHA is ridiculously important to circadian signaling. If your signaling sucks.......you get sick and you die earlier of all neolithic diseases.

Seeing any connections yet at all?

### JoeBranca — 2012-10-15T05:27:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cod-liver-any-us-sources.4243/post-58248>

### Michael — 2012-10-15T11:46:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cod-liver-any-us-sources.4243/post-58273>

I think what metal has been used for the can should tip you off as to whether BPA has been used to line it.

Fish tins are often aluminium - I don't know why. Al may not be ideal but is probably safe enough unless the tin contents are very salty or very acidic and subjected to high heat. The point here is that they're probably not lined.

Steel cans, however, will need to be coated - inside and out - otherwise they'd rust. In the old days they were plated with tin (Sn). Hence the alternative name for a can a "tin". Presumably, they are still plated with Sn but some are also lined with a thin layer of plastic. I don't know why - I'd guess that the plastic resists some foods better.

So I think you'll find there's only a plastic lining (the BPA is in that: it's a hardener) if the can is steel. If the can is aluminium, no need. You should be able to tell from the appearance of the metal what it is. Or test with a magnet.

I think you can pretty much tell if there's a plastic lining to a tin, anyway. You can see it. Open something like a can of tomatoes, and you can see it there - like a thin layer of paint.

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-it-possible-to-tell-anything-from-this.4247/post-57479>

My doc is unbelievably stingy with labs...I told her I suspected Hashis and she would not order both the antibody tests, just one of them and the TSH. Nothing else.

TSH 1.25 (ref 0.35-5.50 mU/L)

Thyroid PeroxAB 30 (ref 0-60 U/mL)

In April my TSH was 2.93 and that's all I know. I'm workiong on getting a full thyroid panel from LE but I have to go to the US...hoping to do that next month. In the meantime I have been having new/different symptoms that I feel might be related to my thyroid....pressure/tightness in throat area, pounding heart, palpitations (always have, but more lately). I've lost 12 pounds and while I'd like to think it's from the leptin reset and CT I wonder.

I'd be grateful for any advice, insight, or helpful hints to help convince my doc to get me more tests!

Your doc is sounds totally clueless. My first doc tested me for Hashis that later confirmed the diagnosis but said "You aren't BAD enough for meds". I would eat AI for the next 18 mo. I would suggest to wait for your other results after you get them next month and then you will know more about the story of your thyroid.

### Patty Cakes — 2012-10-11T18:31:24+0300

# Thread: Just started HCG - Have a few questions.
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/just-started-hcg-have-a-few-questions.4255/>
**Subforum:** HCG Protocol
**Posts:** 29
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### differentstory — 2012-10-11T17:15:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/just-started-hcg-have-a-few-questions.4255/post-57597>

I was so focused on the diet part of this that now it seems the hard part was really the mixing and injection part. I just did my first injection and worried that I didn't do it right.

1\. Mixing Questions

I got the HuCOG HCG and mixed it last night and started today. It is a 2000 iu vial (I have 6) and I mixed it with 1 cc of fluid and then put it into a different vial with 3cc of fluid (the bacto stuff). I know there are various opinions on mixing. I was going to do it the Colleen method, but found that part of the instructions on Colleen's website a bit confusing (probably for only newbies), so I just did the 4cc as I found another site with more specific step by step instructions.

I know (based on a calculator I found and reading Colleen's website) that I will need 3 2000iu vials for 43 days. So that means the one vial I mixed last night should last me appox. 13 days?? I'm doing 150iu for the dosage right now.

I just see that tiny amount of liquid in the vial (after mixing) and I freak out a little and think I'm not doing it right. Is that really going to last 13 days??? I am using insulin needles and did my first dose this morning to the "30" line.

Also, will there be a bit leftover after 13 days that I will just throw away (assuming 150iu was the perfect dose for me)?

Just feeling a bit overwhelmed at the moment.

Hoping someone can answer me and avoid the terms "iu", "cc" and "ml" as it is making me a bit crazy at this point - pretty confused.

2\. What should I do with the needles?? Do I just put the cap on and throw them in the garbage? I worry our trashman is going to see and think I'm a junkie.

3\. For the vials I have left (5 of the 6), I don't mix until I need them and DO NOT have to refrigerate those, right?? Until mixed? Just nervous I'm going to screw something up.

3\. So confused about the 40/43/46 day thing. I want to do 43 days based on Colleen's website. Seems like the right thing to do. I thought it was 40 injections, skip one day a week which will get you to 46 days (skip 1 day a week for 6 weeks). Where did the 43 number come from??

4\. I am skipping one fruit and doing 300g of protein like Cavemam. Is that right? Just checking.

5\. I read different opinions via Google on intramuscular injections vs. subcutaneous injections. Didn't see how Colleen felt about one or the other on her website. I was doing the subcutaneous for now and working up to intramuscular. Anyone have a strong opinion about one vs. the other?

6\. Anyone have an opinion on how long it's going take for me to get comfortable with doing my own injections? I made my husband do mine this morning, but I can tell he is pretty uncomfortable doing it. Don't get me wrong, he is super supportive. He just hasn't read as much as I have and I think he's a little freaked out (more that me!!). HA!

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-10-11T17:26:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/just-started-hcg-have-a-few-questions.4255/post-57598>

ok! Soooooo, what you just said was exactly what I said when I was at this stage. Thank god I bought extra vials. I dropped one and paniced that I jostled it too much, then I messed one up tryin to finess the whole pull liquid in and out of a syringe. IT SO DOESN"T "just work".

Ok, the way you mixed sounds exactly the way I did it, I did the HUCOG 2K vials, too. HEre is the good news, the injections are only hard the first 2 times. . .I got real lax after that and just ran to the bathroom after sneaking my vial out of the fridge and stuck it in my tummy. . .no drama with alcohol pads etc. That really is the easy part!!!! I stuck it in the fat in my tummy, changing sides every injection.

I did not do skip days at the start, and wish I would have! It helps you dial in your dose. For me 150 too much.. .I ramped it down until I found my sweet spot, and I mixed it up a bit from day to day depending on how I was feeling. I started with 175 - WAY TOO MUCH.

The 13 injections sounds right. I made all mine up at once so all I had to do was grab one from the fridge. Yea, just hide them in the trash when done. You will feel like a skanky junky for a few days, then you won't.

Post here when you are adjusting to the diet, I'll be happy to discuss! Did you read my post to someone else about fruit? I happen to think the 1 fruit a day (I say strawberry or apple) is a good idea. I also like it for the dinner, I think the little bit of carb helps with sleep - which can be sketchy when in such deep calorie dep/ketosis.

I would put everything in fridge just to be sure. I think with the dry vials it shouldn't make a diff (it was shipped un fridged right?) but I was not going to fail and worry it was because of some technicality. Keep the technicalities to a minimum so you can focus on your behaviors.

I could tell before I got to 40/43/46 days I was "done", so I quit a little early. I had quit losing and it seemed like a bit of a waste of effort!

The deal is 40 injection max, no matter how you do it. . .skip days are up to you, but seem like a good idea. I ignored TOM and powered through. In fact, my TOM started right when my VLC day started oooops!

Skip having hubby do it, just grab a prepared needle and run to the bathroom and shoot up in private like a junkie should!!!!

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-10-11T17:28:21+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/just-started-hcg-have-a-few-questions.4255/post-57600>

Also, I found chicken and beef to be most satisfying - the fishes left me thinking about the next meal.

OMG, Cavemam. Okay, feeling so much better about all of this. I think it was the whole emotional part of all of this that I wasn't prepared for. ### differentstory — 2012-10-11T17:53:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/just-started-hcg-have-a-few-questions.4255/post-57607>

### colleencoble — 2012-10-17T22:16:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/just-started-hcg-have-a-few-questions.4255/post-58781>

Sorry, I've been traveling and just saw this. You may need to lower your dose, just monitor your hunger. You should NOT be hungry on the right dose. Not weak either. And don't monkey with your dose without doing a skip day if you run into hunger or weakness. A skip day will tell you exactly what you need to do. if you feel better by 4 or 5, the dose is too high and you need to take it down. If no better then it's too low. But hopefully 150 iu will work.

And you need to get used to the iu and cc. cc and ml are the same thing. Iu is the amount of hcg in each dose.  I usually pull out that tiny bit that's left over in a 2000 iu vial and go to the next vial to get the rest of the full dose. But then a duller needle doesn't scare me.  Or you can do 2 shots that day. hope things are going well for you!

### differentstory — 2012-10-18T19:49:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/just-started-hcg-have-a-few-questions.4255/post-58959>

Thanks, Colleen! I have a dosing question today. Here's where I'm at:

EASY PEASY: Load Days - Dose 150iu

BAD DAY: Day 1 - Hunger 8; Mood & Energy 3; Dose 150iu

BAD DAY: Day 2 - Hunger 9; Mood & Energy 2; Dose 125iu

JUST A LITTLE BETTER: Day 3 - Hunger 6; Mood & Energy 4; Dose SKIPPED

MUCH BETTER: Day 4 - Hunger 3; Mood & Energy 7; Dose 125iu

BEST DAY YET: Day 5 - Hunger 2; Mood & Energy 8; Dose 125iu

Day 6 (so far) - Hunger 2; Mood & Energy 7; Dose 125iu

So things are FAR BETTER than they were the first 3 days. The first 3 days were miserable for me (I probably should have loaded 3 days). Thank you for your advice (on my blog post that you answered).

My question is that yesterday (Day 5 or Day 7 if you count load days) was \*almost\* perfect. I had great energy. I was down 1.5 this morning. I have lost 6.5 in all. I'm seeing numbers on the scale I haven't seen in 10 years. BUT I still have this annoying hunger in my belly and food thoughts popping in my head every 20-30 minutes (hunger-wise I'm a 2 out of 10 with 0 being no hunger). I have felt that way yesterday and today.

Which way should I go with the dose?? I tried 150iu in the beginning per your advice and now I'm at 125iu. I have learned I adjusted too fast though. Still just confused on whether to go up or down and by how much? Maybe just 10iu? Hunger is mostly constant throughout the day but was easing late last night.

### vkiernan — 2012-10-19T14:48:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/just-started-hcg-have-a-few-questions.4255/post-59144>

I'm curious, who should do this? Are there any guidelines as to why anyone does it? Is this for when everything else fails? Does it matter how much you have to lose?

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-10-19T17:10:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/just-started-hcg-have-a-few-questions.4255/post-59170>

> **vkiernan;60684 wrote:** I'm curious, who should do this? Are there any guidelines as to why anyone does it? Is this for when everything else fails? Does it matter how much you have to lose?

It's not a quick fix without damage kind of thing. I think you have to decide if losing Xpounds of fat will be worth the negatives. Seems that the successes are:

1\. Yoyo dieters who've screwed up so much they can't lose weight anymore no matter what they do.

2\. Those that are 10-20ish lbds overweight and their bodies just won't lose that fat no matter what they do.

I'm sure my perception is totally screwed because I'm coming FROM almost 400 lbds, but I see lots of "skinny" women overly concerned about that last 10 lbds or so, or freaking out because OMG I GAINED 5 LBDS I AM A BEAST! . . .I think you have to weigh the negatives of the extra fat cells compared to overall health.

Doing 500 calorie diet for 4-6 weeks wrecks many things, too. Just for some, it might be a better path to overall health as dropping 25 lbds of visceral fat is such a positive.

The thing is, it seems you MUST do it for a pretty legnthy time period. AND if you are in the obese category, I think it does burn fat from the visceral areas.

What I've not seen other than theory is if it just empties the fat cells, or actually destroys them. If it causes apoptosis - then I'd say it's good might outweigh its bad for more people. So far it's just the orginal docs message of saying it is so. None of the studies I've seen really indicate it's actually happening.

More importantly, you should not do this unless you are completely dialed in on a low carb paleo/epi paleo like a zealot. Without crazy zealotry eating the 6 weeks after the calorie restriction - you just undo all that hard work.

It's about dopamine, reward pathways and resetting metabolism. You will have to restrict to very few foods and sllllloowwwwwwly work them in. LOTS OF FOODS YOU CAN NOW EAT. I think it's a 16 week minimum proccess that you need to be laser focused on. And be prepared to end the calorie deprivation and still need to restrict like you have an ED.

There is lots of evidence that it works WITH your metabolism, and does not slow your metabolisim like calorie/exercise laden restrictions do. For that reason alone, is why you would consider it for weight loss. With out THAT factor, it would definately be easier just to fast and calorie restrict at a reasonable pace.

### vkiernan — 2012-10-19T20:34:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/just-started-hcg-have-a-few-questions.4255/post-59209>

Thanks Mam. That was a lot of great input!!! I have never considered it before but I have gained weight recently. First with the HC this spring and now again which I think my progesterone is pushing my estrogen but not sure. I am not obese and you would probably slap me as I have less than 10 lbs but before I really didn't have any. I cannot stand what I just gained. I feel absolutely miserable in the boob/gut area. It irritates me all day. My pants are tight and I hate that too from a feel standpoint. I know I need to seriously CT again before I do anything. Ct'ing worked right before the HC and then nothing worked; but, since I'm off the HC, it might work and help a lot of other things. Hoping the t3 will kick in soon and warm me up. I have been so cold and just can't get into the cold tub, not to mention our house is around 64.

### differentstory — 2012-10-20T19:45:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/just-started-hcg-have-a-few-questions.4255/post-59361>

Cavemam, you are such a huge inspiration for me! I love your "can do" attitude.

That said, I don't fully agree with your thoughts on HCG. I don't believe those are the only 2 groups of people that HCG can help. I believe there is a large population of people that cannot lose weight, for whatever reason - food in a box I think. I don't believe that people can't lose weight because of yo-yo diets either; I believe it goes much deeper than that. I thought you also felt this way at some point? I haven't really yo-yo dieted much in my past but I have always been pretty chubby and ate pretty unhealthy foods growing up.

It wasn't until this year that I really started to notice that it has been almost impossible for me to lose weight without starving myself (I lost weight on WW, but that was calorie restriction which doesn't work).

I personally believe my weight problems have everything to do with hormones. I have had some pretty screwed up hormones my whole life and recently had a full panel done to check them. I'm amazed I am even functioning with the levels I'm at! No wonder I don't have a menstrual cycle.

For instance, I have always said "I'm allergic to the sun" (since the age of 13) and rarely go out in it because I get this weird heat-flushing reaction that is extremely painful. I am actually deathly afraid of the sun for the pain it has caused me in my life. But, as I've grown up and gotten smarter, I've dialed the issue down to something going wrong with my levels of T3/T4 - which again is a hormone thing. And as I've read more and more, I've found I have a large number of problems that all seem to be related to wonky hormone levels - keratosis, dry skin, being a night owl, depression, anxiety, exhaustion, my inability to play sports. . .things I've struggled with my whole life.

So, for me, there is a 3rd group of people HCG is awesome for and it is those who just can't lose weight - but not because of yo-yo dieting. I think maybe you are insinuating that, through voluntary actions of your own, you got to a level of 400 pounds. I, on the other hand, am on the other end of the spectrum maybe more so where Gary Taubes is at. My belief is that I don't think you had any control over that. I think it is the result of your environment, the crappy boxed foods that were available to you and your parents (from an educational and economical standpoint) and your genes.

That said, I really see no down side to HCG. What wrecking are you referring to? It doesn't mess with your metabolism, it removes those crazy unnecessary fat stores and it forces you to eat hard-core paleo which hopefully will be a habit we stick to long-term. I guess I could see one wrecking point to be that it is so restrictive that it probably has a high rate of failure, but even then I've haven't read where people lose 8 pounds, then fail and go on to gain 25 (like on other diets like WW).

Just my 2 cents.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-10-20T19:50:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/just-started-hcg-have-a-few-questions.4255/post-59364>

### differentstory — 2012-10-20T19:57:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/just-started-hcg-have-a-few-questions.4255/post-59368>

My post? Let's here it. I'm all ears.

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-10-20T20:47:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/just-started-hcg-have-a-few-questions.4255/post-59371>

> **differentstory;60913 wrote:** My post? Let's here it. I'm all ears.

Unless Jack has something to add other than a drive-by passive agressive comment (sitting in traffic and feeling annoyed maybe?). . .I say this:

We actually are not that far off. I threw out "yo yo dieter" as my term, but it was a lazy choice of words. I was operating under the Cavemam world view that of course anybody fat has tried to lose weight. . .at least anybody old enough and wise enough to be here! But of course, there are many people that have more reasons than YO YO diet. . .I guess what I \*meant\* was those that can't seem to lose weight doing 'tried and true' programs that work for other people.

I love you brought up the Taubes book. Mentally, that was a watershed moment for me. Prior, I had been stuck in my very deep rut of self-viewed failure for not being able to stop myself from eating volumes of bad food. For gods sake (my old world view) everyone else I knew was able to control themselves.

When in fact, it was that one teeny-tiny little seed that was planted, that blossomed and probably built a key neural pathway that aided in my re-invention. It was the simple idea of fat storage was my body keeping me alive and protecting me from something else going on. I wasn't getting fat because I was eating too much (which of course I was), but I was eating too much to support my fat which might have a different reason for being there.

I remember exactly what I was doing when I heard that (I was listening to an audio book and painting my sons room). Something clicked with that one idea - and the world has been different since.

I love opposing opinions, they force me to:

1\. Practice my objective muscle

2\. Think through my view

3\. Borrow and incorporate something I hadn't considered before - someone else's view/framework to add to my knowledgebase. Doesn't matter if you agree or disagree, understanding why someone has a particular perspective expands your own wisdom.

I am not sure about the hormone thing. I see it unfolding before my very eyes, and I'm not seeing what we all want to be true. What is unclear to me right now is what effects on your current body it will have. No argument on balancing them and being as healthy as possible. My question to explore is will it undo the harm (overfat) or will you have to make it worse before you make it better?

Will you need to get as healthy as possible. . .THEN take a few steps back on purpose to take care of some of the residual damage (over fat) and struggle a little more to get back to healthy?

I think that may be the answer for me. When I clean out a closet, the entire surrounding area looks like a bomb zone. But, when I'm done after all that hard work - it's container store photo ready. I think I may have to suck it up and face a closet cleaning day.

### differentstory — 2012-10-20T21:39:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/just-started-hcg-have-a-few-questions.4255/post-59379>

Okay. We are on the same page then. I just took "yo yo" dieter to mean maybe extreme starvtion, mini thins and ED and i think hcg is for more than that.

Funny about your apocoleptic moment with Taubes. I had the very same moment and that has changed my whole life (while painting my sons bedroom - just kidding).

I dont know about the hormome thing either. I didnt mean to insinuate that I did. Only that I think my weight and hormone problems are related (for me) and it makes me wonder if it similar for others (my mothers family which has really struggled with weight and mental health issues for years).

i do find it curious based on old journals, that i have a higher sense of well being, joy and positivity when i have been thinner.

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-10-20T21:54:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/just-started-hcg-have-a-few-questions.4255/post-59380>

> **differentstory;60924 wrote:** Okay. We are on the same page then. I just took "yo yo" dieter to mean maybe extreme starvtion, mini thins and ED and i think hcg is for more than that.
>
>
>
> Funny about your apocoleptic moment with Taubes. I had the very same moment and that has changed my whole life (while painting my sons bedroom - just kidding).
>
>
>
> I dont know about the hormome thing either. I didnt mean to insinuate that I did. Only that I think my weight and hormone problems are related (for me) and it makes me wonder if it similar for others (my mothers family which has really struggled with weight and mental health issues for years).
>
>
>
> i do find it curious based on old journals, that i have a higher sense of well being, joy and positivity when i have been thinner.

I think this is a relevant factor - clearly that pharmacy in our heads is somehow effected by the outside world. Think about when you have a crush, and how you feel around them etc. Same thing with seeing a good old friend. Same thing (in a negative way) when you see someone you D R E A D.

One could argue that still, it's all things generated inside yourself and you just need to harness that. Having a child changed that for me. Any parent knows the sheer happy making chemicals just a hug or unsolicited smile from ones offspring can bring. I do think that others can and do influence us. . . thus when you are seen by the world as "lesser" and treated as such, how can that NOT make you feel bad.

The world simply reacts differently to better looking people. Fatter people are seen as less healthy, thus the tribe shuns them. It's really a viscious circle.

I to am genuinely happier when I'm feeling like my looks "fit in". It's seen over and over again in the human condition, like with wealth.

Once you have a baseline of met needs, then going over that doesn't necessarily translate into a happier state of being. I think the same thing for how you look. When you are in a state of being comfortable interacting with the world, then your baseline is met - and there isn't the same happy return on investment with being better looking.

Believe this - super wealthy people are no happier for their wealth than an average person, yet they are happier for their wealth than an impoverished one.

You could also say a super good looking person is no happier than an average looking person, yet they are happier because of their looks than someone very ugly.

### colleencoble — 2012-10-21T23:18:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/just-started-hcg-have-a-few-questions.4255/post-59521>

i agree with you, differentstory. I LOVE hcg. Love it, love it. I tell everyone about it. It's changed my life. I could not lose weight any other way. No weight watchers, not low carb, not paleo, nothing else worked for me. You're right that hormonal problems are underlying all our problems. HCG fixes some stuff there. Then when you go forward and eat a good paleo diet, you can keep the weight off as long as you get your hormones in order.

I tend to see 3 problems when people regain. Either they go back to eating crappy food, or their hormones are messed up or they have a food sensitivity and keep eating that item (usually dairy if they are not eating grains.)

### colleencoble — 2012-10-21T23:22:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/just-started-hcg-have-a-few-questions.4255/post-59522>

differentstory, on your dose, let's talk about it a bit. Have you skipped a day in the last 2 days? If so, how did you feel in the evening? If you haven't, go ahead and do that and examine how you feel after 3-5.

You may need to go up just a tad or down just a bit. And make sure you're not mistaking an empty feeling for hunger. I actually love that empty feeling.

### colleencoble — 2012-10-21T23:27:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/just-started-hcg-have-a-few-questions.4255/post-59525>

vkiernan, I don't recommend hcg if you have less than 10 pounds to lose. It's the bomb if you have anything over 15 pounds to lose though. But you have to stay on it a minimum of 23 days and 30 is better. And you lose so quickly that you have to adjust things after the first week or 10 days.

You might take a look at Nora Gedgaudas diet which is pretty keto. That often works when you just need to lose a bit. Or try some intermittent fasting. If you read Pounds and Inches, the steak day Dr. Simeons recommends is basically intermittent fasting. You skip breakfast and lunch, then eat the biggest steak you can get down with an apple or a tomato. And you drink plenty of fluids that day. It often results in a loss of about 3 pounds. I know some people who have done 1 a week and lost just those few aggravating pounds.

I would get your hormones looked at if I were you though. Since you had to be on HC, you've got some other problems for sure.

### differentstory — 2012-10-23T00:15:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/just-started-hcg-have-a-few-questions.4255/post-59722>

Thanks, Colleen for the advice.

My HCG life has been a bit of a roller coaster the last 3 days. On Friday, I threw the HCG in my purse after injection. Why? Who knows. Went to look for it Saturday and it took me over an hour to finally find it in my purse. Ugh. So I had to mix a new amount and now that I understand your mixing instructions better, I did it your way with more fluid. I also decided to go up 10iu to see if that would help. But. . .that was probably one too many changes. I totally wasn't thinking and injected 175iu instead of 135. Oops. Actually didn't cause much of a difference, but I did drop down to 135iu the next day (Sunday).

Then on Saturday, we made chili (tomatoes, broth, tomoto paste and spices), but something was wrong. After I ate it, I was uncomfortably full and sure enough the next day I was up 3 pounds. ( So on Sunday, no chili, but I used the same meat in a broth soup and the meat had been seasoned pre-chili. I ended up feeling the same full feeling again! So it's got to be the spices (chili powder, garlic salt, onion powder). I'm worried they had MSG. They were from an asian grocery store. All other ingredients were from Whole Foods.

Today, I'm swearing off all spices and eating clean so back to feeling mildly hungry. I've been on 135iu for 2 days. Thinking I should try 150iu and see if it gets better. I am slightly less hungry at 135iu than 125iu. Hungrier in the morning than at night.

### colleencoble — 2012-10-23T00:34:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/just-started-hcg-have-a-few-questions.4255/post-59727>

It would have been okay,honey. It can be out about 4 days and still be okay. It does sound like you maybe should bump your dose a bit.

I always gain on that chili. There's something about tomato and beef together that does me in. And remember tomato paste is loaded with sugar. It's got more than just 1 tomato in it.

### differentstory — 2012-10-23T00:37:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/just-started-hcg-have-a-few-questions.4255/post-59731>

Thanks!! I have been eating cleanly for 8 days and was SO looking forward to that chili, but you are totally right. It's not worth it. Back to salt and pepper, protein and side salads.  Thanks for your support. (Can't help but wonder if you ever have a "retirement party" how many people would attend?? Probably need a ballroom and it would be standing room only!)

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-10-23T00:39:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/just-started-hcg-have-a-few-questions.4255/post-59732>

> **colleencoble;61300 wrote:** It would have been okay,honey. It can be out about 4 days and still be okay. It does sound like you maybe should bump your dose a bit.
>
>
>
> I always gain on that chili. There's something about tomato and beef together that does me in. And remember tomato paste is loaded with sugar. It's got more than just 1 tomato in it.

What she said. It's only a few bites of food - might as well go clean as possible and power through. Let's face it, you don't want it to taste so good you want more right?

I was really kinda done with my chewy bland chicken.

On an interesting note, I have become an apple addict since the hcg. I think there is some magic in them there apples.

### differentstory — 2012-10-23T00:52:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/just-started-hcg-have-a-few-questions.4255/post-59734>

Hmmmmmn. . .need to switch to apples. Very bored with oranges. On another topic, is it normal to see my sex life go from 0 to 100??? I am out of control. My husband doesn't know what's gotten in to me.

### colleencoble — 2012-10-23T01:25:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/just-started-hcg-have-a-few-questions.4255/post-59740>

I LOVE Pink Lady apples. It's dessert all by itself.

Not planning on retiring though. I love helping people find a new thin life.

# Thread: you gotta have heart... ok, but pig heart?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/you-gotta-have-heart-ok-but-pig-heart.4257/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 4
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### granitereno@gmail.com — 2012-10-11T18:10:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/you-gotta-have-heart-ok-but-pig-heart.4257/post-57610>

So what I am reading is:

\*heart is good for us.

\*raw heart is very good for us

This seems easy, as I have some good sources for pastured hearts: beef, lamb and pork.

I have been eating the beef and lamb very rare any way, so transitioning to raw won't be that big a lift.

However... what do we think about raw pig?

It is about as clean as you can get: pastured, raised in the woods with no more than two per acre.

Thoughts?

### ealachan — 2012-10-11T18:14:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/you-gotta-have-heart-ok-but-pig-heart.4257/post-57611>

I think it probably depends on what they were fed when they were alive, just like any other meat. I don't know if pastured pork is as high in Omega-6's as grain-fed...? It would be interesting to find out.

I like grassfed calf heart, personally. I have some defrosting in my fridge right now, in fact.

### Integral_D — 2012-10-11T19:35:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/you-gotta-have-heart-ok-but-pig-heart.4257/post-57622>

I would be wary of eating raw pork-anything. There is a long tradition of eating raw "red" meats (e.g., beef tartare) and seafood, but pretty much every culture stays away from raw pork. I'd take that as an cue.

### granitereno@gmail.com — 2012-10-12T17:07:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/you-gotta-have-heart-ok-but-pig-heart.4257/post-57751>

I hadn't thought of this from the traditional/cultural paradigm.

But that makes a lot of sense to me.

Thanks!

# Thread: Arctic Diet
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/arctic-diet.4259/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Michael — 2012-10-11T20:28:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/arctic-diet.4259/post-57632>

Quote from Geoff Bond:

> The Eskimo diet is overbalanced in favor of the omega-3 oils, the opposite to that of the western diet. This causes overproduction of compounds that abnormally _reduce blood platelet stickiness and blood clotting, which explains the Eskimo's unstoppable nosebleeds and immunity to heart disease.
>
>
>
> However, most people in the west have the opposite problem ... Steven Gundry, medical director of the International Heart Institute, in Palm Springs, relates how American surgeons could not understand the difficulty that Japanese surgeons had in controlling bleeding under the surgeon's knife. Belatedly, they realized that this is the normal condition for healthy people: the Japanese with their diet rich in oily fish, have the omega-3/omega-6 balance about right._

_

[http://www.amazon.com/Deadly-Harvest-Intimate-Relationship-Between/dp/0757001424/](http://http://www.amazon.com/Deadly-Harvest-Intimate-Relationship-Between/dp/0757001424/)

I'm not sure about a number of things in Geoff Bond's book, but this one has the ring of truth.

I think the nosebleeds are mentioned in Kabloona_, which is contemporary with the older life and by an actual anthropologist.

Presumably, on a "shoreline diet" you'd be getting plenty of seafood, but you'd also probably be eating some terrestrial animals and abundantly of vegetable foods (and fruits in-season) and perhaps nuts. This is where omega-6s would be coming from, and this is what you can't do in the arctic.

The Mediterranean diet comes to mind again. I don't want to say "low carb Mediterranean". Maybe better to say grain- and legume-free Mediterranean. That might be pretty close.

### SeaHorse — 2012-10-11T22:37:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/arctic-diet.4259/post-57654>

_Kabloona was one of my favorite books I read when I was studying arctic anthropology and history...also the essays by Barry Lopez Arctic Dreams_

There are lots of berries and plants in the arctic during the summer...the indigenous people were shore based during the summer... there was a big difference between what they ate during the short summers and what they ate during the winter...clearly emphasizing omega 3, but there would have been carbs and sugars and starches to some degree.


══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# Thread: The 6th most important thing you need to know about Diet and Exercise
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-6th-most-important-thing-you-need-to-know-about-diet-and-exercise.4260/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Fitness
**Posts:** 14
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Barry — 2012-10-11T20:50:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-6th-most-important-thing-you-need-to-know-about-diet-and-exercise.4260/post-57634>

If I'm honest, I never expected to write more then the "1st" post. I don't think 5 steps ahead like neurosurgeons. However, I've realised that I've got more to say and hopefully it helps and resonates with some. Again, these are just my opinions of how I see things from where I sit.

Take the Journey to Convince Yourself

I think one of the biggest challenges people face, or more importantly, the biggest challenge that people do not overcome, is to convince themselves. You may have found the best advice and read all the articles and blogs. You may have bought the best coconut oil and the best resveratrol. You may have found the best work-out routine (or what you were told was the â€œbestâ€￾) and you may be following the best CT protocol. But things arenâ€™t working out, weight loss is still a problem and your CRP is still high. Whats going on ? Well before I home in the specific point I am trying to make here, problems are always multi-factorial. Everything counts. My previous 5 posts count. The 30 levees all count. But the point I am trying to make here is that in order for things to truly work, you need to first convince yourself that the decisions you are making and the steps you are taking are right for you. If you do that, the machinery will work better, the process will be more efficient and the end product will be reached when the time is right.

What I find is that people want fast answers and quick solutions. They want to know how to get to the highest point of the mountain without having to climb it. This happens a lot in todayâ€™s modern world with the streams of freely available and easily accessible information. It happens on this forum. It happens in my practice. I get asked about what I think of dairy or what I think of paleo. I get asked lots about my â€œopinionâ€￾ on various health and fitness issues. People want to know what I have learnt. This happens on this forum too. The â€œAsk Jackâ€￾ section. Interest is fine and discussion is welcomed. There is just one big problem that is being overlooked. You should not go on someone elseâ€™s opinion, even if you are asking the most respected professional or referencing the most peer-reviewed literature. They donâ€™t know you. You know you. On top of that, conventional wisdom is outdated and the science doesnâ€™t work. It misinterprets the interpretations. The science of research and studies is the science of research and studies. Itâ€™s not the science of you. We all have to accept that. If you accept that, and believe in yourself and your own monitoring system, everything else is irrelevant. What is relevant is what works for you, this is where the N=1 comes into play and should dominate proceedings.

Iâ€™ll give you a personal example. Many will have read my Fasted State posts. What Iâ€™m doing goes against almost every bit of literature and research about sports nutrition. Running fasted for very long periods, and in my case, on a very low carb diet, is supposed to cause immune system problems, adrenal stress, chronic inflammation, muscle loss and reduced performance. Yet, Iâ€™m never sick, rarely tired or stressed, have a good body comp and win races. Why ? Well, one of the reasons is that what the literature says is not always right and when you look deep into the science you find out the truths. Secondly, and more importantly, Iâ€™ve convinced myself that what Iâ€™m doing is right. I donâ€™t care what some professor has to say about some RCT that says fasted state is bad for the athlete. Heâ€™s not doing it. I am. I know how it feels and it feels good for me. I am getting the results I want and I enjoy doing it.

The same goes for anyone out there regardless of your goal. Learn about what you do and then do it. Through the process of doing it and listening to your body, convince yourself that it is right for you. Donâ€™t do something because you read it somewhere or Dr K said it was the best thing for you to do. I do not doubt Jack here. What I doubt is your lack of trust and belief in the most important part of the whole game, the conductor. I know there is always a caveat to what I say due to the people here who suffer from chronic illness. However, the principle of learning and forming your own opinion still applies. If you learn and then listen to your body as you implement the changes, you will improve your ability to go on â€œfeelâ€￾. If you go on feel, you are then in control. You will know what works for you and what doesnâ€™t so that you can find your own optimal. There is no generic optimal. There is whatâ€™s optimal for you. You need to convince yourself of that in order to achieve it. Find your own way to the top of the mountain. It might not be the same way as everyone else. It may not also be the perfect, safest or quickest route. But the key thing is that you remain convinced that it is the best way for you to go. Knowledge is power for sure. But experience is far more relevant and tangible. Acquire the knowledge but share the experience. Do not go where the path may lead, instead go where there is no path and leave a trail.

â€œBeyond the walls of intelligence, life is definedâ€￾

### Shijin13 — 2012-10-11T21:49:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-6th-most-important-thing-you-need-to-know-about-diet-and-exercise.4260/post-57644>

Barry - I think you've just reiterated BG14 but in different words.

btw I've loved all 6 of your posts....

### Jonathin — 2012-10-12T18:19:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-6th-most-important-thing-you-need-to-know-about-diet-and-exercise.4260/post-57785>

Most people want to have confidence. I think what this means for many is to have no doubt about what they know or why they choose to act. Instead of internally using reason and evidence over time as one experiences life, many look to authorities, politicans or other sorts of communicative people for explainations and roadmaps to the future. Obviously, the validity of knowledge and truthfulness is sometimes questionable. Sometimes it is amazingly absurd.

Evaulation and judgement can be nebulous in the moment. Often times a lack of energy in the form of boredom or distraction wins out. Sometime it is just a lack of conceptual imagination to envision that something is possible. Maybe we have lost too much physical and psychic energy through bad diet, a semi-toxic environment and a dysfunctional culture to connect to that visionary part of ourselfves. On the other hand, if we can define 'it', we can strive to understand it and possibly overcome our present limitatiions. Personally, I graviate to people and networks of people that are attempting to find their way along that path.

### Barry — 2012-10-12T18:27:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-6th-most-important-thing-you-need-to-know-about-diet-and-exercise.4260/post-57787>

> **Shijin13;58964 wrote:** Barry - I think you've just reiterated BG14 but in different words.
>
>
>
> btw I've loved all 6 of your posts....

thanks, maybe myself and Jack think alike after all ;-)

### Linz — 2012-10-12T18:35:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-6th-most-important-thing-you-need-to-know-about-diet-and-exercise.4260/post-57789>

Barry, a great post and so true but there is a huge wall to climb before setting out on a solitary journey. First step, how do we convince ourselves the experts might be wrong (and which ones are right)? When the perception of good health is really far from actual good health most have no idea how to search for something better or even know that they could. And public education tells them they CAN'T. Education teaches us to listen to the experts and professors - they will have the answer and yes, usually it is a quick fix - take your statin and your heart is protected, take your steroid and you won't get worse (and someone else will pick up the pieces in a few years time). If those experts actually said loud and clear, 'I can show you how to make huge improvements in performance or health for yourself or I can give you a quick fix to make improvements for the next year or so but it will also do you some harm,' I believe many more would take the better path. And there would be more and more examples for others to follow.

Continual television advertising for 'no fat' fast food. No fat = hungry all the time = can't follow long term advice = need a quick fix, too ignorant or lazy to take good advice. How often are our schoolchildren told, 'question everything'? More often they are told to do what they are told.

If chronic disease also means a declining brain function how can those affected hope to leave the conventional path. With a decent brain (perhaps not by Jack's standards), scientific background, good reason to suspect there might be a better way and respect for the environment, the last thing I wanted to do was question the quick fix. I was lucky, I recognised the decline and when I took my own path a few amazing changes happened (at first not to the symptoms I expected to help) and I found real science supporting the change, but even so it was several years before I had the confidence to stand in the doctor's office and say these are real changes because of what I have done and everyone should, at the very least, be given the basic information on how to start making real change. If you fear fat (and that includes many of the doctors I have seen and most of my friends) why would you decide that it could actually save you.

With hindsight, experience is relevant and tangible but sustained improvement won't be made by luck and intuition, and how many are taught to continue seeking the knowledge they need for themselves?

### Barry — 2012-10-12T20:02:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-6th-most-important-thing-you-need-to-know-about-diet-and-exercise.4260/post-57809>

Linz,

I think you answered your own question. It doesn't matter how "huge" the wall is to climb, thats my whole point. The key to everything is that you as an individual climb that wall in continuous forward motion, building your own confidence as you go along.

Sustained improvement comes from individual pursuit. Of course learning and self education are needed too but that is almost mandatory. I'm not saying that people need to just go on "luck".... I'm saying people need to form their own opinions and understandings... regardless of what others say or what is "theoretically" correct.

Or as Henry Ford once said "If you think you can do a thing or think you can't do a thing, you are right"

### Zorica Vuletic — 2012-11-05T01:25:02+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-6th-most-important-thing-you-need-to-know-about-diet-and-exercise.4260/post-62104>

Good post, Barry. Very true.

Btw, have you had any recent races? How are those going? You're inspiring because you do what you love, you don't let ideas and/or people get in your way, and you truly believe in what you do. In other words, you live your own life according to Barry and that is 'good enough' for you.

### Inger — 2012-11-05T09:43:13+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-6th-most-important-thing-you-need-to-know-about-diet-and-exercise.4260/post-62169>

>
>
>
> Or as Henry Ford once said "If you think you can do a thing or think you can't do a thing, you are right"

Barry...! I LOVE this!! That is why whining has no purpose whatsoever!!! If you think you can not, don't even try. Do something else, what ever you want. Something you believe in.

Or just do it.

The quote from you above is fundamental IMO. That is about to live 100%. It is beautiful!

### prAna303 — 2012-11-05T10:28:03+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-6th-most-important-thing-you-need-to-know-about-diet-and-exercise.4260/post-62172>

“But you see," said Roark quietly, "I have, let’s say, sixty years to live. Most of that time will be spent working. I’ve chosen the work I want to do. If I find no joy in it, then I’m only condemning myself to sixty years of torture. And I can find the joy only if I do my work in the best way possible to me. But the best is a matter of standards—and I set my own standards. I inherit nothing. I stand at the end of no tradition. I may, perhaps, stand at the beginning of one.”

**Posts:** 8

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-10-12T06:44:12+0300

Any warnings or suggestions or preferences?

### Momma28 — 2012-10-12T07:00:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ox-bile.4264/post-57707>

> **Cavemam;59025 wrote:** Any warnings or suggestions or preferences?

I have recommended Standard Process Cholacol to people with gall bladder removed and they have raved about the improvement in their digestion on it.

### jeepifer@gmail.com — 2012-10-12T10:57:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ox-bile.4264/post-57718>

Start off with lower amount. You can always add, but hard to subtract!

I just got Nutricology Ox Bile, 125mg, and take 1-3 capsules at meals, depending on how much fat. So far, so good!

### FarmersDaughter — 2012-10-12T16:15:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ox-bile.4264/post-57741>

I'm thinking of adding this as well...out of morbid curiosity, what happens with too much?

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-10-12T16:29:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ox-bile.4264/post-57746>

so I was looking around, and saw that there were enzymes that had ox bile along with betaine HCL and bunches of other stuff. . .anybody got any experience? Better to take just the two things seperate or would it make sense to look for a single enzyme package?

### ealachan — 2012-10-12T17:12:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ox-bile.4264/post-57754>

I use ox bile in combination with Rainbow Light brand digestive enzymes. The ox bile I use is Nutricology 125mg and I take two with fatty meals. I've gotten so that I can skip it with no adverse effects if I'm not eating a fat-heavy meal...yay, adaptation?

Ox bile really helped me, where Beatine HCL just sent me running for the bathroom. I've never taken more than 250mg of ox bile at a time because the 250mg did what I needed it to do (broke down the fat I was eating). Didn't see much sense in trying to take more just to see if it would give me a stomach ache.

### vkiernan — 2012-10-12T20:37:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ox-bile.4264/post-57819>

Diahrea, at least from my own n-1. I use Jarrow, pretty potent and I have to be careful so I don't get the D, but things are still floating so I don't know what is up. Titrate slowly is what Jack told me. I also use enzymes, Now brand, that has some in it and I don't believe I get any benefit from the ox bile in it.

### Destiny — 2012-10-12T22:36:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ox-bile.4264/post-57854>

> **FarmersDaughter;59062 wrote:** I'm thinking of adding this as well...out of morbid curiosity, what happens with too much?

You will get a good inside cleaning!

# Thread: Purslane
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/purslane.4267/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 9
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Michael — 2012-10-12T09:50:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/purslane.4267/post-57716>

Does anyone collect and eat purslane? Apparently it grows wild all over the southern US and was known to the 49ers as "miners lettuce". I sometimes wonder about exploiting these wild plants morre, although you probably have to be careful where you pick them these days.

### SeaHorse — 2012-10-12T14:14:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/purslane.4267/post-57724>

It is a common weed in vegatable gardens in our area of Vermont...I ate it a lot when I was eating greens. It's very tasty and a bit meatier than a lot of greens....the taste is on the lemony, sour side ....not bitter.

### Jonathin — 2012-10-12T17:40:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/purslane.4267/post-57772>

It grows commonly on disturbed soil that is largely bare of vegetation. Very common in the West. We probably could have eaten pounds per day this summer! Nice change or compliment from/to other salad green. For a vegetable, relatively high in omega-3s.

### L8F — 2012-10-13T18:55:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/purslane.4267/post-57967>

I like it, and we have it in New England for sure. I have heard it is incredibly good for you nutrient-wise. Plus it tastes nice. I kind of think of it a bit like watercress, even though it doesn't exactly taste similar.

### Endless — 2012-10-14T01:22:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/purslane.4267/post-58018>

I've eaten it in summer along with chickweed, lambsquarters and sorrel growing as weeds in my garden. Come to think of it maybe I should forget the garden and just eat the weeds.

### Glenn A — 2018-11-27T15:31:29+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/purslane.4267/post-256693>

I eat purslane when I find it, but don't find it where I live now in South Carolina. It was a common weed I always found in my garden in Southern California. Here's a very recent recommendation for purslane due to it's plentiful omega-3 levels, and other health benefits like for liver health in the face of Hep.C.

[http://www.greenmedinfo.com/blog/po...66&ct=t()&mc\_cid=ed4932f5b6&mc\_eid=0c62482334](http://www.greenmedinfo.com/blog/powerful-and-proliferative-formidable-purslane?utm_source=Daily+Greenmmedinfo.com+Email+List&utm_campaign=ed4932f5b6-The+Formidable+Purslane&utm_medium=email&utm_term=0_193c8492fb-ed4932f5b6-86753466&ct=t\(\)&mc_cid=ed4932f5b6&mc_eid=0c62482334)

The site, GreenMedInfo, is a great way to search journal abstracts regarding health. You must be a member to accomplish some things though.

### Katie Durham — 2018-11-27T15:58:25+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/purslane.4267/post-256699>

It's a succulent, which is more obvious in the cultivated varieties that have much larger leaves than what you might find in your lawn. I've ordered it before from superseeds.com. Tasty. A little too easy to grow. Well known for omega-3 if you can trust your conversion rate. (I don't rely on it for any DHA.) I think it's main value is as a nutrient dense supplemental green to the crucifers and the high oxalate greens like spinach which might not agree with everyone in large quantities.

### Sheddie — 2018-11-27T19:05:12+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/purslane.4267/post-256740>

Since, in middle Georgia, Purslane volunteers to fill in the lower area of my container garden items, I let it, thinking it shields the soil nicely, might help keep it from drying out. Never had a completely Purslane salad but very often grab some with herbs, green onions, etc., for inclusion in a salad. Agree with above adjectives, "lemony, watercressy, sour, bitter." You can strip off the green 'leaves' that resemble succulents, or eat the whole stems with leaves. I like that it lasts and stays the same size all summer. It's very attractive garnishing a plate of beautiful fish. I've thrown it in soups but never really saw, or tasted it after that! I've bought some before that had flowers, maybe even the volunteers came from those via my soil recycling? However, none of these small, thin 'wild' ones have ever flowered. Mmmm. How nice to muse about this little plant!!

Some day I want to find a way to collect the free and ample pine and other pollens. Heard some are good for hormonal support...

### Glenn A — 2018-11-29T12:49:04+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/purslane.4267/post-256902>

> [Katie Durham said:](/goto/post?id=256699)
>
> It's a succulent, which is more obvious in the cultivated varieties that have much larger leaves than what you might find in your lawn. I've ordered it before from superseeds.com. Tasty. Well known for omega-3 if you can trust your conversion rate. (I don't rely on it for any DHA.) I think it's main value is as a nutrient dense supplemental green to the crucifers and the high oxalate greens like spinach which might not agree with everyone in large quantities.

Thanks! I'll order some for next year's garden and just let it grow on the fringes with other weeds. It may choke out some other things I don't eat! Actually, I believe I will rely on it for DHA! I'm a fan of getting nutrients from food, and of eating almost everything freshly picked. I'm convinced this is as important as the gross quantity of any food in determining what is available once ingested. So, purslane, being a greater carrier of ALA than spinach by a factor of 2-3, is definitely a potential source of DHA. Yes, it depends on one's conversion rate. The fish oil industry quotes low rates, but there's a distortion going on. See my post titled "What's are the conversion rates for omega-3 to DHA?"

# Thread: Allergenicity of rice
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/allergenicity-of-rice.4268/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Michael — 2012-10-12T10:51:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/allergenicity-of-rice.4268/post-57717>

> Japanese researcher Hideaki Tsuji ... says that rice is the foremost allergen in Japan.

That's interesting. It would have to make one wonder whether we don't hear so much about problems with rice not because it is necessarily benign, but simply because it's not consumed as often as wheat is in the west. And what literature there is on this may be mostly in other languages. The more you keep throwing something problematic at your gut the more chance you have to get a reaction. (Thomas O'Bryan, who's usually billed as a gluten expert, says that one of the problems with wheat is that people eat it for breakfast, lunch, and dinner - and for snacks in between.)

Professor Cordain did warn them. I notice in _The Paleo Answer_ he says one of the things about wheat intolerance is that it is the most studied grain intolerance and we should not be blase about other grains. He notes in that book that a relatively common form of anaphylatic shock in the Far East comes on as a result of eating buckwheat.

And yet in the paleosphere a typical post might read,"\_\_\_ says buckwheat is OK, where \_\_\_ turns out to be a celebrity paelo blogger who may not have any relevant qualifications (medicine, biochemistry, anthropology, etc.) at all. In fact, it may be that we just don't know about the problems, because we don't commonly consume it in the west.

Quinoa is another. Yet Geoff Bond claims that Ameridians have concentrated the saponins from quinoa and used them on the tips of poison arrows. I assume that's accurate, and it makes you think ... OK these chemicals aren't concentrated in the food, but still do you really want to be eating that?


══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# Thread: Bedtimes?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bedtimes.4269/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Kids
**Posts:** 8
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### TheKid — 2012-10-12T14:32:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bedtimes.4269/post-57728>

I've got a 21 month daughter and we're struggling with determining what is the best bed time. She generally sleeps at about 9:00 - 9:30 pm, but depending on our schedule, that can stretch to 10 pm on occasion.

She sleeps straight through until about 7:30 or 8 am. Sometimes if my wife is still sleepy she'll nurse her and she will fall back asleep until 9:00 am.

She doesn't nap much during the day. Might do an hour, but not much more.

What is the best bedtime? How long should she sleep. Should we be waking her up earlier and not lettin her sleep in?

Thnks.

### Shijin13 — 2012-10-12T14:39:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bedtimes.4269/post-57732>

Our bedtime routine during the week is bath at 8pm, story time at 8:30 and in bed by 9:00pm. I'd live to get them in bed earlier but w/work & commuting some days we don't get home until 6pm and dinner doesn't get on the table before 7

Havoc is up around 5:30-5:45 like clock work every day. Chaos is up on weekdays w/prodding at 6:00 weekends she's up around 6:45-7:00

Havoc regularly takes naps whole Chaos will only take them if we maker her lie down or if she's at daycare and everyone else is sleeping.

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-10-12T17:17:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bedtimes.4269/post-57757>

We had to go with some reverse engineering for this. Mini me got up at the same time every mornin no matter what, so we backed bedtime out from that and just go with the flow.

Since we've really switched to no electronics at night without the glasses and get up first thing and breath to the sun (seriously, I can't belive I make my family do this, so woo woo, so un me.) we just go with the cues, if he starts to be sleepy - he goes to bed.

Of course I rearranged my life so he goes to a school from 12:30-3:30 and has no homework, and we do a "family bed" and sleep all snuggled up in the freezing cold. We do active outdoor things in the a.m. and evening - including watching the sun go down behind the mountains. All that woo woo sheeeit just takes time - and of course not everyone has that luxury.

What I'm saying, is figure out what her natural rhythm is and work around it!

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-10-12T17:20:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bedtimes.4269/post-57759>

> **TheKid;59049 wrote:** I've got a 21 month daughter and we're struggling with determining what is the best bed time. She generally sleeps at about 9:00 - 9:30 pm, but depending on our schedule, that can stretch to 10 pm on occasion.
>
>
>
> She sleeps straight through until about 7:30 or 8 am. Sometimes if my wife is still sleepy she'll nurse her and she will fall back asleep until 9:00 am.
>
>
>
> She doesn't nap much during the day. Might do an hour, but not much more.
>
>
>
> What is the best bedtime? How long should she sleep. Should we be waking her up earlier and not lettin her sleep in?
>
>
>
> Thnks.

ha, I also wanted to let you know you have a blessing to count! Your daughter is sleeping! I'm still scarred tremendously (and probably carrying pounds from) my child that could not sleep or lie down for more than 4 hrs at a time until 18 months, when you think about adding feeding him in there, I rarely got more than 2hrs of sleep in a row for almost 2 yrs. Seriously. Still bitter.

Chrrrrrrist, back to me this is. So sorry! I'm very peppy and energetic today. I think I did too much mct oil. Plus I started back in on the iron. I bet that's it.

### KiwiLauren — 2012-10-12T21:14:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bedtimes.4269/post-57824>

> **TheKid;59049 wrote:** I've got a 21 month daughter and we're struggling with determining what is the best bed time. She generally sleeps at about 9:00 - 9:30 pm, but depending on our schedule, that can stretch to 10 pm on occasion.
>
>
>
> She sleeps straight through until about 7:30 or 8 am. Sometimes if my wife is still sleepy she'll nurse her and she will fall back asleep until 9:00 am.
>
>
>
> She doesn't nap much during the day. Might do an hour, but not much more.
>
>
>
> What is the best bedtime? How long should she sleep. Should we be waking her up earlier and not lettin her sleep in?
>
>
>
> Thnks.

I'd say it's a combination of cues from the child and cues from the environment. In other words, if you are following the circadian cycles and not using lights/screens from the time of natural darkness, then you can follow her cues about when she needs to sleep. You said 'depending on our schedule' which makes me wonder if you have the sort of schedule that makes it impossible to follow her own cues. That being said, as Cavemam said, she IS sleeping and as long as her daytime behaviour doesn't indicate sleep deprivation, I wouldn't worry at all about it. If a human being is sleeping well, aligned with nature's rhythms and has good mood and energy, I can't see why you'd intentionally disturb their sleep. (I am guessing (though we are only 7-8 months into the circadian cycles following) that you will all go to sleep slightly earlier in winter and later in summer.)

### Norbert — 2013-04-11T21:11:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bedtimes.4269/post-83780>

A baby from 2 to 3 year should be sleep fourteen hour per day. Children from about 21 to 36 months of age still need one nap a day, They typically go to bed between 7 and 9 p.m.

### Jason Lutan — 2015-07-03T21:39:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bedtimes.4269/post-170597>

Gota make it work for you, no matter what it is. Main thing is a schedule, same one all the time...makes things a lot easier....

### optimizedprimate — 2015-07-06T01:42:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bedtimes.4269/post-171031>

I second the schedule and routine. red lights on as soon as sun goes down and we all go to bed at 9pm.

True story ...

I was in the USA to visit a friend about 4 years ago. I stopped at an Information Center to get directions to her hotel (I was lost). I walked in and told them where I was going. "Where are you from?" says the guy behind the counter (my accent is slightly strange, a bit American-leftover, a bit Kiwi). "New Zealand." The first guy shakes his head and says, "Huh?" The second guy behind the counter smiles and says, "Oh, I know, you mean like Europe."

### nonchalant — 2012-10-15T04:13:29+0300

Don't get me started. It seems that every great accomplishment in the past has been attributed to aliens. It sure couldn't have been someone's creative genius. Since mankind has been going downhill, people cannot conceive of the notion that people in the past have been able to accomplish great things without all the gee-whiz computers, satellites, lasers, etc. If it were aliens doing all the thinking/work, then we can relax -- because people back then were as dull-witted as they are today.

Maybe the statue of David was actually alien-made? Michelangelo was such a fraud.

### FarmersDaughter — 2012-10-12T18:21:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/timing-ct-with-bhrt.4275/post-57786>

If one has what appears to be estrogen dumping from CT (altered cycle, substantial weight gain, breast tenderness) and is on supplemental cyclical progesterone, would timing CT to coincide with last half of cycle while extra progesterone is present be useful?

Or am I over thinking?

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-10-12T18:52:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/timing-ct-with-bhrt.4275/post-57790>

wow, that sounds like a good idea for managing the negatives. . . I hadn't thought of it in those terms. I think as I add in deeper and deeper CT I may try this!

### FarmersDaughter — 2012-10-12T19:08:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/timing-ct-with-bhrt.4275/post-57794>

Well, i decided I'm trying it, so I'll let you know how it pans out. Going a bit slower with the CT this time...maybe that will keep the damage to a dull roar.

>
> I was kind of curious about how it would effect you, considering your AN history. I know that it doesn't give everyone the "munchies" per se, but most people definitely get them. I wonder if it would, generally speaking, exacerbate an anorexic's compulsion to not eat, or if it would ease it up? Not that I'm saying you should go try it, obviously...it was just something that came to mind after your first post up there talking about your doctor offering it to you.

I honestly have no idea whether or not it would help with AN. I bet it would help to relieve some anxiety yet that would return as soon as the person stops taking it. I'm probably the only person in the world who's gonna say I've never tried it, lol. No interest other than the pain relief. But the "munchies" were part of the reason I decided against. I wouldn't be okay with it later on (weight gain - I don't need to anymore) and I do have a history of binging and purging which was very difficult for me to stop and I'm still working on it. So - not gonna go there. Too much at risk.

### ealachan — 2012-10-12T22:56:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dried-stevia.4276/post-57862>

> **Souldanzer;59176 wrote:** I honestly have no idea whether or not it would help with AN. I bet it would help to relieve some anxiety yet that would return as soon as the person stops taking it. I'm probably the only person in the world who's gonna say I've never tried it, lol. No interest other than the pain relief. But the "munchies" were part of the reason I decided against. I wouldn't be okay with it later on (weight gain - I don't need to anymore) and I do have a history of binging and purging which was very difficult for me to stop and I'm still working on it. So - not gonna go there. Too much at risk.

Sure, makes total sense.
