# Thread: Natural Niacin Beats Prescription Zetia for Cholesterol
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/natural-niacin-beats-prescription-zetia-for-cholesterol.4303/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### chocolate — 2012-10-14T18:31:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/natural-niacin-beats-prescription-zetia-for-cholesterol.4303/post-58120>

[http://www.greenmedinfo.com/blog/natural-niacin-beats-prescription-zetia-cholesterol](http://http://www.greenmedinfo.com/blog/natural-niacin-beats-prescription-zetia-cholesterol)

> What can Merck's prescription drug Zetia do for you that niacin can't? Apparently, nothing at all. In fact, it could worsen your atherosclerosis.
>
>
>
> A study published in the New England Journal of Medicine concluded that niacin, also known as vitamin B3, is better for reducing cholesterol and preventing heart disease than Merck & Co., Inc.'s prescription medication Zetia.
>
>
>
> Researchers found that over a 14 month period niacin was significantly more effective at reducing artery plaque than ezetimibe, the active ingredient in Zetia. In addition, niacin was found more effective than Zetia at decreasing the number of heart attacks.
>
>
>
> Zetia is a medication used in conjunction with statins to try to further lower levels of the so-called bad LDL cholesterol in the blood. It claims to work by blocking the absorption of cholesterol in the digestive track. Annual sales of the drug in 2010 were $2 billion.
>
>
>
> **The study was halted early because the niacin group was doing better. In fact, the researchers concluded that the more LDL cholesterol was reduced in the Zetia group, the greater was the progression of their atherosclerosis. In addition, there were significantly more major cardiovascular events among patients using Zetia than among those in the niacin group.**
>
>
>
> The lead author of the study questioned whether Zetia was effective at all and concluded that "prudent clinical practice currently favors the avoidance of ezetimibe" until further clinical studies are conducted.
>
>
>
> What is niacin?
>
>
>
> Niacin is a water soluble B vitamin also known as "nicotinic acid" used by the body to convert carbohydrates, fats and protein into energy. It also contributes to keeping the nervous system, digestive system, skin, hair and eyes healthy.
>
>
>
> Niacin has been used for over 50 years as an effective method for raising the levels of good HDL cholesterol in the blood and is also known to help reduce bad LDL cholesterol and triglycerides.

# Thread: High fevers
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/high-fevers.4309/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Kids
**Posts:** 15
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### trishalinn@gmail.com — 2012-10-15T09:43:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/high-fevers.4309/post-58271>

My 16 month old twins have been spiking high fevers about once a month for 6+ months now. It lasts anywhere from 2-4 days. The fevers are getting higher and more difficult to bring down with each passing month. I didn't notice the trend until July, at which point I told my husband if it happened again I was taking them to the dr. Until that point I had assumed it was some sort of virus despite the fact that there were no other accompanying symptoms.

In August D spiked a fever, but not E. I took him to the Dr and she ran a bunch of tests on both boys - nothing. Everything came back fine. No sign of infection. CBC was normal. She referred us to a pediatric hematologist/oncologist who we just saw this pat Friday. She is running a bunch of tests, but said if they come back neg she will be referring us to a pediatric infectious disease specialist.

I'm getting tired of not knowing what's wrong with my babies. I'm on night 4 of siting up all night holding D to make sure his fever doesn't spike too high while he sleeps. I have to give him both ibuprofen and Tylenol to get his temp under 102. I hate giving him so many drugs. I'm very worried about them.

Do you think it would be better to take them to a bigger hospital? Maybe they would get to the bottom of things quicker? We live in Michigan. Is there a hospital anyone recommends for pediatrics? U of M is a reasonable distance, Cleveland Clinic?

### Shijin13 — 2012-10-15T12:45:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/high-fevers.4309/post-58277>

Are they teething? It's a fallacy that kids can't spike big fevers when teething Havoc spiked a fever if 105 while bringing in his 1yr molars around 18mths. The average temp was 104 for 6 days - he ended up having an infection in his gums as his teeth broke through.

Have you checked for a bladder infection? They're hard to dx in little ppl

### trishalinn@gmail.com — 2012-10-15T16:24:21+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/high-fevers.4309/post-58299>

Teething, yes. The doctors have said because the fevers are so high and happening on an almost monthly basis for 6+ months they don't think it's that.

They've done a urinalysis and come up with nothing. There CBC looked good, no signs of infection there.

I have read some about periodic fever syndrome. I know it is rare, but my mom has also said that my brother ran high fevers off and on until he was 3 and there was no apparent cause. Now, the pediatrician never sent her to specialists...but, I wonder if it might be something like that. I just get terrified sometimes wondering if its something serious...

### Shijin13 — 2012-10-15T16:34:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/high-fevers.4309/post-58303>

follow your instincts mama.

btw - did any of your other kids have similar occurrences ?

### trishalinn@gmail.com — 2012-10-15T16:39:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/high-fevers.4309/post-58306>

No,nothing like this with my other boys.

### trishalinn@gmail.com — 2012-11-01T22:12:14+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/high-fevers.4309/post-61398>

Just a quick update. We've now seen oncology/hematology and they did not find anything. Then we saw allergy/immunology (for about 3 minutes) who referred us to a pediatric rheumatologist. Rheumatology thinks we are dealing with some sort of periodic fever syndrome, except my boys don't have any of the other symptoms that often go along with it. I really liked the pediatric rheumatologist. He spent two hours with us asking all sorts of questions. In the end he told us we were doing everything right with the boys. He applauded us for having them on a grain free diet and sticking to whole foods. He also said it was great that I was still nursing them. This is in contrast to the oncologist/hematologist who looked aghast when I said I was still nursing them and asked me if it wasn't about time to stop that. And couldn't fathom not giving my kids grains...she asked well, don't they eat sandwiches? Cookies? Crackers? When I said no, she said...well what do you feed them then? Needless to say, ot is the rheumatologist who is giving me hope for the medical profession!

### RiverNeighbor — 2012-11-01T23:29:38+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/high-fevers.4309/post-61413>

So are they thinking it's autoimmune?

(I'll be thinking of you... I know what it's like to be working through issues and be working figuring out stuff with the kids)

### Inger — 2012-11-02T08:07:29+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/high-fevers.4309/post-61497>

One thing come into my mind.. you CTed quite heavily didn't you? According to Jack not a good idea when breastfeeding... Because of the detox... It might have something to do with that? Detox/symptoms could come delayed in the kids? Or are you doing CT still. Maybe you could do only mild CT like cooler air and stuff? I am just thinking out loud here..

I love that you breastfeed the boys still. Kudos to you...! Our world needs more women like you!!

Oh.. and for the fever. I used to give my small siblings a herbal tea when they had really high fever. It helped a lot! It just lowered the fever in a natural way so there was no danger. I use it on myself too when/if I get high fever. It makes one sweat more and then the fever starts to lower slowly. The flowers from the tilia tree are what I have used;

[http:/http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tilia](http://http:/http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tilia)

### trishalinn@gmail.com — 2012-11-03T00:53:27+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/high-fevers.4309/post-61647>

Not autoimmune, its a genetic abnormality. Apparently there are several varieties and all quite rare. I think they are particularly suspecting this because both of my identical twin boys have it. I also have a brother who had high fevers with no other symptoms from the time he was an infant to 3 years old.

### trishalinn@gmail.com — 2012-11-03T00:56:47+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/high-fevers.4309/post-61649>

> **Inger;63247 wrote:** One thing come into my mind.. you CTed quite heavily didn't you? According to Jack not a good idea when breastfeeding... Because of the detox... It might have something to do with that? Detox/symptoms could come delayed in the kids? Or are you doing CT still. Maybe you could do only mild CT like cooler air and stuff? I am just thinking out loud here..
>
> I love that you breastfeed the boys still. Kudos to you...! Our world needs more women like you!!
>
>
>
> Oh.. and for the fever. I used to give my small siblings a herbal tea when they had really high fever. It helped a lot! It just lowered the fever in a natural way so there was no danger. I use it on myself too when/if I get high fever. It makes one sweat more and then the fever starts to lower slowly. The flowers from the tilia tree are what I have used;
>
>
>
> [http:/http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tilia](http://http:/http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tilia)

Thank you for the tea idea Inger! I will check it out.

I don't think what I did could be called heavy CT. Last winter/Spring I did manage to get into a 55 degree tub a couple times a week. I didn't do anything this summer. This fall I've been using the cool fat burner vest, but just haven't had the time to get in the tub.

### FarmersDaughter — 2012-11-03T17:23:44+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/high-fevers.4309/post-61783>

> **Trisha;63408 wrote:** Not autoimmune, its a genetic abnormality. Apparently there are several varieties and all quite rare. I think they are particularly suspecting this because both of my identical twin boys have it. I also have a brother who had high fevers with no other symptoms from the time he was an infant to 3 years old.

I have been following this with interest, as my son would routinely be fine at bed, spike a 104+fever, and be listless and miserable, and then be fine again by morning. He grew out of it by the time he was about two or so, but it did worry me a lot at the time. We did a lot of cool showers and ice bags that he could have at-will. He had FTT as well, was below the third percentile for height and weight until he was about three or so. Still is very low weight for his age, but height is midrange. Doctors just patted me n the head and called me overanxious, but had no answers, so I just did what I could symptomatically.

I wonder if he has a variant of that, or if there is another reason for his fevers. Sometimes things that are "quite rare" are only rare because they aren't looked for, I think...

### RiverNeighbor — 2012-11-04T05:29:26+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/high-fevers.4309/post-61922>

Do they know why the fevers spike? I just keep wondering why it wouldn't cause a fever all the time....

Trisha, I hope you all get some relief/understanding/resolution soon. I know what it's like to have twins without other children so I am sure your days are filled with just normal taking care of the kids stuff without all the extra stuff.

Do you have any help or is it you and hubby?

### trishalinn@gmail.com — 2012-11-05T04:29:05+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/high-fevers.4309/post-62136>

> **RiverNeighbor;63696 wrote:** Do they know why the fevers spike? I just keep wondering why it wouldn't cause a fever all the time....
>
>
>
> Trisha, I hope you all get some relief/understanding/resolution soon. I know what it's like to have twins without other children so I am sure your days are filled with just normal taking care of the kids stuff without all the extra stuff.
>
>
>
> Do you have any help or is it you and hubby?

Its called periodic fever syndrome, because the fevers are periodic, not constant. He has no infection...chest X-ray has came back clear, urinalysis clear, lots and lots and lots of bloodwork came back clear. Sounds like the next step is genetic testing to see if they have one of these genes for periodic fever syndrome.

It is exhausting. They usually spike fevers at different times of the month which I suppose is good. But it feels like much of the month is sleepless nights because I'm up holding a baby and taking temps every 20 minutes and trying to keep the temps down. I do have family around, everyone works full time jobs, but helps as they can. My mom took all four of my boys on Saturday afternoon for three hours so I could sleep. That was amazing. I was so exhausted after several nights with NO sleep.

### RiverNeighbor — 2012-11-07T18:34:02+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/high-fevers.4309/post-62579>

Trisha, hang in there..... look forward to the calm after the storm. I dont think there is any more precious gifts than the time, love, care, and perseverance of a mom taking care of her babies when they need her most. It is hard work to give those gifts on very little sleep, I know. Keep looking for, and taking advantage, of opportunities to keep your body getting what it needs too. Besides what my husband could help with when he was home I didnt have any help.... family ended up being more work/stress at the time I needed them most. I remember driving the kid to physical therapy (they had torticollis) and thinking that my sleep deprived state was worse than having had a couple drinks. In retrospect I should have found a way to find and hire someone to come help me sometimes... but I know this is a tough option due to trust, cost, etc..... Anyway.... i am not trying to make this about me..... just want you to know that someone understands and empathizes....

I'll be keeping you in my thoughts

### Glen PDQ — 2012-11-14T18:54:16+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/high-fevers.4309/post-64282>

What kind of bedding are they sleeping on? There is a belief that mold growing on fire retardent treated mattresses and bedding can cause extremely potent toxins. These toxins have even been linked to SID. Secondhand mattresses or mattresses used by the 2nd, 3rd, 4th etc. babies born to a family are supposed to be even more hazardous. In New Zealand the baby safe plastic mattress covers are popular. It prevents the baby from breathing in the toxic gas layer which clings to the bedding. Supposedly there have not been any SID cases in New Zealand in homes that are properly using the baby safe mattress covers.

If the fevers are cyclical, what is happening in or around your house every month just before the fevers start? Does a neighbor spray something? Does the city or town spray anything or send roadside weed sprayer trucks around? Do you drive a certain route in your local area on a monthly basis? Could the fevers be allergies related to wild plants that bloom?

# Thread: Richard Feinman Quote
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/richard-feinman-quote.4312/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Michael — 2012-10-15T14:10:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/richard-feinman-quote.4312/post-58282>

The quote is:

> "They've substituted arbitrary rules of evidence for common sense."

He's talking about the dietary establishment and how they work to block what many clinicians believe they should be doing.

The "rules of evidence" are "arbitrary" because they're just staked claims and not real rules of evidence such as would be used in a court - where they'd be thrown out.

Quote delivered in context here:

[http://www.askthelowcarbexperts.com/2012/10/30-dr-richard-feinman-getting-low-carb-to-join-the-scientific-mainstream/](http://http://www.askthelowcarbexperts.com/2012/10/30-dr-richard-feinman-getting-low-carb-to-join-the-scientific-mainstream/)

### Michael — 2012-10-15T15:36:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/richard-feinman-quote.4312/post-58292>

Actually, this is sounding like quite a useful interview.

One nice feature towards the end is that Dr. Feinman is starting a scheme whereby you photograph your meals and email them to him, and these are then used as an example of what real people actually doing low carb diets are eating. These may then be cited as evidence in future research. The problem seems to be that people who are opposed to low carb, paleo etc. are getting to mould public perceptions of what is in such a diet. This is what Dr. Oz did when he ambushed Gary Taubes. He'd laid out this vast plate of meat as an example, and Gary said later that actually he'd have preferred a modest portion of salmon and some salad with it.

So how about it? The email address should be at the URL quoted above. Why not send Dr. Feinman some photos of what epi-paleo menus in winter mode look like?


══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# Thread: Yaghan Natives of South America
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/yaghan-natives-of-south-america.4313/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 11
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### TheKid — 2012-10-15T15:25:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/yaghan-natives-of-south-america.4313/post-58291>

We typically tend to look north when we want to examine traditional cultures living cold climates; but the south gets pretty cold too. I don't know how I found this today. I surely wasn't looking for it.

Anyway, before the Europeans wiped them out (there literally was a genocidal campaign), the Yaghan people lived a very interesting life. This from Wikipedia:

[http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Yaghan](http://http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Yaghan)

Despite the extreme cold climate in which they lived, early Yahgan wore little to no clothing until their contact with Europeans.\[5\] They were able to survive the harsh climate because:

1) They kept warm by huddling around small fires when they could, including in their boats to stay warm. In fact, the name of "Tierra del Fuego" (land of fire) is a name given to the island cluster by passing European explorers who witnessed these fires burning.

2) They made use of rock formations to shelter themselves from the elements.

3) They covered themselves in animal grease.\[citation needed\]

4) Over time they had evolved significantly higher metabolisms than average humans, allowing them to generate more internal body heat.\[6\]

5) Their natural resting position was a deep squatting position, which reduced their surface area and so helped to conserve heat.

### Michael — 2012-10-15T18:45:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/yaghan-natives-of-south-america.4313/post-58328>

Yes, certainly cold in Tierra del Fuego! It has been suggested that some of the people on the southern tip of South America may not have come via the Bering Strait and may have been related to Australian Aborigines - mainly on the grounds that old photos show people who look a little like that and that there's a rock painting that shows darker and lighter skinned figues at war. Whether DNA testing could sustain that ... If there are many people left known to be descended from them.

Charles Darwin wrote a little about Fuegians he met in The Voyage of the Beagle.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-10-15T19:16:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/yaghan-natives-of-south-america.4313/post-58337>

notice how polar people are darker than most expect.........CT6 and the alpha MSH link again. Inuits, the Outback natives in aus are Antartica natives and these people.........all have a tie if you look for it.

### Michael — 2012-10-15T19:56:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/yaghan-natives-of-south-america.4313/post-58352>

Tried a google image search and interestingly the first one that came up was on a page that has an extract from Darwin's diary.

Not much in the way of clothing despite the cold:

[http://2.bp.blogspot.com/\_9oTslXzpqec/SaT\_MAFr1bI/AAAAAAAAFGM/t4ekRJUS-iE/s1600-h/Yamana-Family.jpg](http://http://2.bp.blogspot.com/_9oTslXzpqec/SaT_MAFr1bI/AAAAAAAAFGM/t4ekRJUS-iE/s1600-h/Yamana-Family.jpg)

He says they eat shellfish, fish, and "sea eggs" (?)

> To look at the Wigwam; any little depression in the soil is chosen, over this a few rotten trunks of trees are placed & to windward some tufts of grass. Here 5 or 6 human beings, naked & uncovered from the wind, rain & snow in this tempestuous climate sleep on the wet ground, coiled up like animals. In the morning they rise to pick shell fish at low water; & the women winter & summer dive to collect sea eggs; such miserable food is eked out by tasteless berries & Fungi. Jerking out little fish out of the Beds of Kelp

[http://darwinbeagle.blogspot.co.uk/2009/02/25th-february-1834.html](http://http://darwinbeagle.blogspot.co.uk/2009/02/25th-february-1834.html)

### Jack Kruse — 2012-10-15T20:00:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/yaghan-natives-of-south-america.4313/post-58358>

People are afraid of going outside their comfort zone in so many ways.........

Every time I have stepped outside my comfort zone in my life and into vulnerability it is clear that there is a risk in my current world falling apart. The interesting part is that it never seems to occur in the way that you originally feared it might. And sometimes, it reveals your own opportunity costs of your current reality that you were blind too for decades. Change is often better than good.

### Michael — 2012-10-15T20:14:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/yaghan-natives-of-south-america.4313/post-58360>

Can you say more, please?

I think that "afraid of going outside their comfort zone" applies to everyone, but were you suggesting specifically that it applied to Darwin here? He saw them, their lack of clothing, the possible insecurity of their food supply, and experienced fear perhaps? And that would colour his view of them? The truth is they probably just don't need many of things he thinks of as absent.

### villjamur_stevenson — 2012-10-15T20:54:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/yaghan-natives-of-south-america.4313/post-58375>

The father in that picture of Darwin's does not look happy.

### janagram — 2012-10-15T21:04:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/yaghan-natives-of-south-america.4313/post-58383>

> **villjamur\_stevenson;59887 wrote:** The father in that picture of Darwin's does not look happy.

yes but remember the early photos of our forefathers and mothers? They look so stern....because they were unused to the concept of photography and we had to learn to open up to the camera....(I still don't like it)

### ealachan — 2012-10-15T21:10:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/yaghan-natives-of-south-america.4313/post-58384>

> **villjamur\_stevenson;59887 wrote:** The father in that picture of Darwin's does not look happy.

None of them really look particularly stoked to be there, honestly...but then again, when a strange white man is rounding you and your family up and posing you, then telling you to hold still while he points a big box at you in order to capture your image, it's probably not exactly a cause for celebration and mirth.

### Michael — 2012-10-15T21:13:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/yaghan-natives-of-south-america.4313/post-58385>

Bruce Chatwin wrote about the area and its people, too:

[http://www.amazon.com/Patagonia-Penguin-Classics-Bruce-Chatwin/dp/0142437190/](http://http://www.amazon.com/Patagonia-Penguin-Classics-Bruce-Chatwin/dp/0142437190/)

I read it some while back -- not sure I recall much of it now. He's an interesting writer, though. He took a degree in archaeology but drifted into work as an art expert at Sothebys almost by accident, IIRC.

### Entelechy — 2012-10-28T19:22:48+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/yaghan-natives-of-south-america.4313/post-60602>

there was some discussion of the indigenous peoples of Tierra Del Fuego back in April in the What the H-- thread.

Jack has said they would be part of a future blog.

[http://forum.jackkruse.com/showthread.php?43-What-the-H-is-Factor-X-Post-your-guesses&p=19983&viewfull=1#post19983](http://http://forum.jackkruse.com/showthread.php?43-What-the-H-is-Factor-X-Post-your-guesses&p=19983&viewfull=1#post19983)

they are fascinating, as their metabolic processes respond differently than the Inuit, the Kalahari bushmen, and Korean Ama.

their metabolic rate actually falls as the temperature decreases.

Favorite quote from Darwins notes..

“It was marvelous the way they could stand the cold. One woman who was suckling a baby came out to the Beagle in a canoe, and she sat there calmly in the tossing waves while the sleet fell and thawed on her naked breast. On shore these people slept on the wet ground while the rain poured through the roofs of their crude skin huts”. It was obvious that this tribe had adapted physiologically in some manner to the extreme climate with very little in the way of protective garments."

# Thread: How do you treat Attenion Deficit Disorder
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-do-you-treat-attenion-deficit-disorder.4319/>
**Subforum:** Beginners Area
**Posts:** 7
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### x_danny_x — 2012-10-15T19:45:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-do-you-treat-attenion-deficit-disorder.4319/post-58349>

Man, I feel like I am not going to get passed 50 years old. I created a lot of threads I just realized.

Anyway on with the show. I have ADT or ADHT. When studying, I cannot stand still for more than 15 minutes. I have to walk around. I heard your brain can study well for 30-45 minutes before needing a break.

I was given Ritalin and Selegiline. Ritalin works and made happy and focused. However, it dried my mouth and made me feel like some sort of robot. It killed my sex drive I think after making it go overdrive.

Selegiline I just dont notice any difference at all with it.

Anybody has any recommendations?

### Souldanzer — 2012-10-15T19:48:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-do-you-treat-attenion-deficit-disorder.4319/post-58350>

try a little bit of l-tyrosine and see if there is an improvement. Attention deficit disorder is often connected with low dopamine.

### indigogirl — 2012-10-15T19:57:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-do-you-treat-attenion-deficit-disorder.4319/post-58354>

Adding ginkgo will help with the sex drive.

Am interested in what you find out...we've been going in circles for years with this with one of our kids, now 20. He takes Vyvanse just to study for finals, day of finals and big papers. Would love a healthier alternative.

### x_danny_x — 2012-10-15T19:58:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-do-you-treat-attenion-deficit-disorder.4319/post-58356>

yeah, Selegiline goes after the MAO-B inhibitor in the brain, so dopamine levels can go higher. Ritalin does some sort of different action to cause higher dopamine levels.

L-tyrosine, isnt that found in energy drinks?

### KiwiLauren — 2012-10-16T00:03:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-do-you-treat-attenion-deficit-disorder.4319/post-58419>

I'd be thinking brain signalling, therefore insufficient DHA, poor O3/O6 ratio, inflammation, too. Do you have labs, I can't remember? Are you pounding the seafood?

### x_danny_x — 2012-10-16T08:14:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-do-you-treat-attenion-deficit-disorder.4319/post-58493>

I just started the sea food. So far I have eaten a bunch of sardines and a large salmon. This is a through one week. I guess I can give it a shot after a couple of months.

### FarmersDaughter — 2012-10-16T18:19:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-do-you-treat-attenion-deficit-disorder.4319/post-58539>

Check thyroid and adrenal status, dump Ritalin, add tyrosine and iodine. DLPA helped me as well, but my focus issues vanished when adrenal fatigue resolved. It took two years of hc and a little medrol, but stimulant drugs actually deepen the hole that caused the problem to begin with, and worsened my adrenal health.

# Thread: Any input here?  Tooth issues / paleo cravings, etc.

### BJK77 — 2012-10-15T20:07:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/any-input-here-tooth-issues-paleo-cravings-etc.4320/post-58359>

No idea where to post this, so hopefully this works

Once again, I need some input here to help figure out whatâ€™s going on. It seems like every couple of months or so I go through some funky phase and I can never figure out if itâ€™s some sort of massive detox, food reaction or who knows what! I canâ€™t wait for the day when I can instinctively know whatâ€™s going on with my body.

Hereâ€™s the deal: Off and on (sometimes months apart, sometimes weeks apart) Iâ€™ll have pain in my teeth. Sometimes itâ€™s on the left, other times the right and once in a while it will just seem to be my whole mouth. I had 7 amalgams removed 2 years ago and ever since then Iâ€™ve had this issue. The amalgams had to come out as they were all 20+ years old and falling apart in my mouth. The removals were all done following the Hugginsâ€™ protocol. Soon after my last 2 were removed though I had a major health crash. This is when I started being able to tolerate hardly anything â€“ food, supplements, etc. I had been on the GAPS diet and continued with it (with little success) until I found Dr. Kruse.

It seemed like I was finally starting to be able to tolerate more foods, at least veggies. I donâ€™t eat any fruits, chocolate, coconut butter, dairy, eggs, nuts/seeds, whatever â€“ my only foods are seafood, meats, fats and veggies. I eat only organic and pastured/grass-fed meats. I was eating a bit of cabbage, cauliflower (in season here now) and seemed to be doing just fine with them and all of the sudden the last two weeks I canâ€™t tolerate them anymore. They are causing MASSIVE tooth pain. I know thereâ€™s a connection between carbs and gut/mouth flora, but seriously this is ridiculous.

I had a consult with Jack over the weekend and he suggested I try a tea bag, clove oil, oil pulling with CO â€“ and Iâ€™ve been doing all of that, but Iâ€™m finding very little relief.

Iâ€™m also finding that Iâ€™m totally craving red meat and even pork. I haven't had cravings for SAD crap for awhile now, so that's not an issue. I love, love, love seafood â€“ I have all my life, but Iâ€™ve got no desire for it. Iâ€™m still shoveling it in twice a day, but my body really wants meat and broth right now.

I recently started compounded T3 (timed release â€“ 5 ncg in AM) and oral Progesterone (50 mg before bed). Other than that I canâ€™t think of any changes Iâ€™ve made.

So, oh smart ones . . . do you see any connection with all of this? Iâ€™m hammering the FCLO as well and it does seem to help some with the pain, but I need to figure out the root cause of all of this. I could go to the dentist, but theyâ€™ll just want to do x-rays and given that this happens off and on a lot and theyâ€™ve yet to find anything I doubt anything is going to show up â€“ plus, Iâ€™m not real big on frying my thyroid anymore than itâ€™s already been done. What to do, what to do?!?!

### ealachan — 2012-10-15T22:44:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/any-input-here-tooth-issues-paleo-cravings-etc.4320/post-58394>

> Oh, I do still eat red meat. I should have mentioned that. My BAB is always red meat (or pork) - 7-8 ounces plus some heart or liver every day. It's my other 2 smaller meals where I've been eating seafood that I'm finding myself wanting meat then too. I suppose I'll try a few days with all meat and see what happens. It just feels wrong to stop the seafood after having the epi-paleo diet drilled into us for the last few months, kwim?

There's no saying you can't eat pork or beef WITH your seafood. Saturated fat competes with DHA for absorbtion, but I don't think it's ever been stated that it completely NEGATES DHA absorbtion. For that matter, there's no saying you can't eat some very lean pork or red meat with your fish and bypass the majority of the saturated fat issue completely?

For what it's worth, when I crave red meat, it's usually iron that I want / need.

### kathylu — 2012-10-15T22:52:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/any-input-here-tooth-issues-paleo-cravings-etc.4320/post-58396>

I have also had all my amalgam fillings replaced and a few of them did hurt for several years afterward. Everything is calm now though.

### Souldanzer — 2012-10-15T22:58:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/any-input-here-tooth-issues-paleo-cravings-etc.4320/post-58397>

BJ, your inbox is full.....

I had exactly that happening.... there were bite issues that the first dentist created and it resulted in a minor disaster. He basically broke one of my molars  The new fillings weren't adjusted correctly, I started chewing in funny ways b/c it hurt so bad, and it resulted in the force being distributed incorrectly, which lead to a crack in one of the cusps. First dentist had to replace the new filling once already b/c it wasn't sealed correctly mad. At that point there was no other way to fix the problem than put a protective cap on the tooth. Did I say I want to still call him names??? I still have issues with that tooth occasionally but not bad enough to pursue anything else right now.

There wasn't anything obvious about the fillings being done badly. I had three of them. We tried adjustments. No go. We replaced one to try it out. It worked. We did another one. It worked again. I cracked the tooth with the third one. Nightmare.

Did I say I want to still call him names??? I still have issues with that tooth occasionally but not bad enough to pursue anything else right now.

Just cleaned out my box Soul. Thanks!

I've wondered about having them replaced again. I actually kind of feel like one of them may have chipped somehow. I can almost feel a very small hole and, if that's the case, I'm sure that could be the culprit here.

When I had the work done they did tell me that 2 of them were extremely deep and they weren't sure how well the fillings would "take" and that I might end up needing a root canal. Honestly I think I'd rather have the teeth pulled than deal with a root canal . . . not sure which would be worse, really.

There is definitely some inflammation behind all of this. I'm just not sure what the root cause is. I can feel that the gums on that side of my mouth are inflamed right now and get quite sore when I floss. I can also tell me bite is off again, especially when I lay on that side of my head. Just a few weeks ago I was finally able to close my mouth and feel like my bite was actually corrected. I need to figure out what happened between then and now and why this continues to happen every so often.

Glad to hear you did get things straightened out for you. To be honest, I'm terrified to go to another dentist. After seeing so many and not getting any decent answers it seems to be a total waste of both time and $$.

Oh - and I hear ya on wanting to call a dentist names. I actually had a tooth pulled last year - 2 days after Christmas because a dentist told me it was infected and that's what was causing all of my issues. Turns out nothing was wrong with the tooth! I lost it and a nice chunk of change for nothing.

# Thread: What is natural/bioidentical Cortisol
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-is-natural-bioidentical-cortisol.4329/>
**Subforum:** Adrenal Rx and Leaky Gut Rx
**Posts:** 10
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### hope2conceive — 2012-10-16T03:24:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-is-natural-bioidentical-cortisol.4329/post-58467>

I am stumped....can anyone tell me what the name for bioidentical cortisol is, and where do you get it? A compounding pharmacy? I need to have a talk with my MD.....

Here is what I read in one of Dr K's blogs:

Cortisol supplementation may provide one of the most rapid improvements of all the hormone replacements. The one thing to pay attention to is that bio-identical replacement is always better choice than synthetic options. This is due to the optimal chemical transduction at the receptor level. **Moreover, the bio-identical preparation gives a more consistent clinical result in my experience.** I see many people on low dose prednisone or hydrocortisone replacement that still struggle. Often changing the med is all that is needed to help the clinical scenario. The higher the initial dose, the quicker the short-term benefit, but it is quite short lived. More often, the requirement for reversal is low doses with over many months to ramp up to effect.

~ Thanks!!

Bio-Hydrocortisone (HC) and yes you get it from a compounding pharmacy. It is fairly expensive. Everything in context, Jack also says that adrenal fatigue can be a brain problem not an adrenal problem.

Following the light rules can probably get you there safer. Had I known that tidbit about it being a brain problem before I went on it this spring, I would NOT have done so and my af was stage 7.

### vkiernan — 2012-10-16T04:15:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-is-natural-bioidentical-cortisol.4329/post-58481>

> **hope2conceive;59987 wrote:** Thanks for sharing...I will be asking for it for sure. I've been on Cortef also since spring, just 2.5, then 5 and now 10mg just once in the am....but not feeling much different at all. Sigh. I have been doing the light since August 29...but also missed a few days at a hotel in September. We will see....hopefully insurance will help cover it.
>

> So may I ask...how do you feel now? Are you off it and on the bio-identical one? Or are you so much better you no longer need it? If you have a blog, I'd love to read it

I hope your ins. covers it. Mine didn't. I was on the bio-identical one. I only used the Isocort for about 2 weeks as it gave me an upset stomach. I attibute it to the lactose in it even though it had lactase too. I stopped for testing purposes and have not gone back on it. There are still days I think of taking small amounts just to see if it takes the edge off. Still fighting to feel better. Haven't tested my adrenals since and really need to. Yes, I have a journal called Better Late than Never. Kinda boring but a way to track what I had been doing as it was hard to know what was affecting what when you do so much at once. When I first started taking it I could tell but then the energy went away quickly iirc and I continued feeling crappy. I took it for 2 months and was taking 35 a day. 12.5, 12.5 and 10. Then I dropped back a bit at noon and in the afternoon as I wasn't sleeping well. It took a bit for me to find a good dose and I don't think I really ever did. My experience with the HC really isn't a good one

The best I have felt in years is now actually. I have started following the AI protocol meaning no eggs, dairy, grains, corn, etc. etc. I gave up dairy first. Then I gave up eggs. 2 weeks after that I gave up coffee and within a few days I was feeling a little happy. My biggest problem besides being tired, losing my hair, etc. was my mood. I always woke up with a black cloud hanging over me. This has been happening for a few years. My days never changed except when it was pms week and then it was worse. If I got upset over something my dh did, I would be mad for days literally. Could never turn it around. I knew I had a problem. I found out I was hypo thyroid last spring 2011 and this spring found the adrenal issue which I knew I have had for a long time but didn't really realize what I was dealing with. Matter-of-fact, I was told in 2009 I had adrenal fatigue. I wish I would have researched it further back then, oh well.

You didn't really say what your problems are. Care to elaborate??? Please tell me what labs you have had done too. I think I read something on you a while back but don't remember the guts.

### hope2conceive — 2012-10-16T19:04:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-is-natural-bioidentical-cortisol.4329/post-58550>

That is great you feel better now!

Been doctoring with my gut (and brain, I guess) since 1998 when I got pneumonia and gut flora zonked by Zithromax. Thyroid unhappy (hyper at the time)....then several longer term issues with mono/elevated EBV and chronic fatigue.....later learned it's Adrenal Fatigue. My functional medicine MD helped me get rid of heavy metal issues but my adrenal issues and thyroid issues are still there. I tried for years to go without any drugs and finally started Armour then NP Thyroid in January 2012. Then in April I caved in to 10mg Cortef, just in the am as that is when it's weakest and some DHEA.

Had SIBO in 2009 and also several parasites since 2001, get the GI Fx about once a year more if an issue...like this year (waiting for results now). My ASI shows a dead flat line the two times I did that (doing a new one today!). My gut has been leaky and I've played with all kinds of diets, SCD, GAPS....no grains in years except in Europe where they have sourdough rye that doesn't bother me (but only eat it when there, as a treat)...but no improvement in energy and especially not HORMONES, my big thing bc I'm still dreaming of having a baby at my advanced age of almost 40. I was doing primal stuff for a while, following Nora Gedgaudas and her book...Primal Body/Mind.....till I found Dr K in August and resonated. Was doing lunaception since February so the darkness at night resonates and I now crave it! Also did Kneipp therapy - hydrotherapy with alternating hot/cold water which is amazing, in Europe too. Was into Ayurveda and acupuncture (still am that) but nothing is going as I want. The hypothalamus is sick and that is what I'm trying to heal...

As far as labs, I will have new data soon. My FT3 is consistent around 2.7-9 way too low but the MD has no answers except to keep going. My Ft4 has been around 0.9-1.1 also in the toilet, and TSH went from 1.2 in 2009 to 1.5 and 2 and 2.5 till January 2012 and has been down to 0.86 in my August test. TPOs have been low since 2010 but i know means little. Vitamin D with supplementation I have managed to get up to the 60s though hope it will go higher.

My big problem is also the luteal phase in my cycle because PG doesn't stay high enough...it goes up really late and it is consistently higher than it should be in the follicular phase....which may be from supplementation in the past but my gyn wonders if it is a cyst or endometriosis (though I have no or very very little cramps with periods..requiring no help usually, or if so, a little essential oils)....so I am totally messed up there. But I guess I'm learning I have pretty classic issues, too....being basically in oxidation, not reduction for years, sigh.

Will do more labs this week, hoping for guidance from Dr Kruse in my very first email consult (yaye! can't wait for his response) and already have an Rx for some of what he suggests generally, like HS CRP, other stuff...so we shall see. I hope to post results from here for sure and would love your input.

Anyway...thanks for asking!

not sure about Hashi's. I had it in the past....AB have been low this year but I know that's not all that counts of course. I cut out eggs too (haven't done dairy in ages, nor grains and never liked corn; coffee I was never into either, and I am free of a sugar addiction - in fact I had my first sweet potato today in almost 4 years...boy was that sweet (with nothing but butter on it))....and maybe my digestion is a little better without it, but not much. I cut out eggs too (haven't done dairy in ages, nor grains and never liked corn; coffee I was never into either, and I am free of a sugar addiction - in fact I had my first sweet potato today in almost 4 years...boy was that sweet (with nothing but butter on it))....and maybe my digestion is a little better without it, but not much. We shall see..the saga continues. I enjoy the learning. I have probably read close to 300 nutrition books in almost 15 years. And STILL I know so little

If you are doing a blood draw for rt3, you need to do a blood draw for ft3 at the same time. Can't do one and later do another and accurately calculate the ratio. There is a ratio calculator on STTM website that will help you figure it out. Or, post out here and we will help.

The t3 protocol is to rid one's self of too much rt3 so t3 can get into the cells and do its thing. I have rt3 probably from an adrenal issue. I have been on a NDT med for a year and still have rt3. Course I was actually diagnosed with af this spring but my rt3 is still high. When you take t3 only, you don't have any t4 to continue converting to rt3. Over a few months of t3 only, your body will start to rid itself of the rt3 and eventually the t3 will be useful once again. A lot of people switch back to some sort of NDT, but some stay on t3 forever as they do well with it. Seems like some people have a conversion problem and perhaps never get to the root cause so t3 works for them.

If you have Hashi's, you still have Hashi's; at least the way I understand it. It is an autoimmune disease and you can never get rid of it - but more like tame it. Sounds like you are following the AI eating protocol. We have a Hashi's thread out here and it has some good stuff in it. A lot of us have recently been told we probably have it. A real blow. I am ok with not eating grains, but take away my dairy and eggs and that's downright depressing; although, I am surviving.

### danniellethatcher@gmail.com — 2012-10-16T05:00:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/labs.4330/post-58486>

hi there... hoping someone here can give me some info or input. have been on Armour for probably 4 months, held at 90mg with little to no effect. Doctor would not raise it. But she did decide to switch me to synthroid 75 mg 1 x day and cytomel 5mcg 2 x a day.(just started them today) here are my labs:

TSH 0.06, RANGE 0.40-4.5

TESTOSTERONE FREE 1.5, range 0.2-5.0

VIT D, 25 hydroxy 38, range 30-100

MAG RBC 4.1, range 4-6.4

T4, FREE 0.9, range 0.8-1.8

T3, FREE 3.0, RANGE 2.3-4.2

T3 REVERSE 19, RANGE 11-32

SEX HORMONE BINDING GLOBULIN 101 , RANGE 17-124

HEMOGLOBIN 11.6, RANGE 11.7-15.5

IRON, TOTAL 146, RANGE 40-175

IRON BIDING CAPACITY 304, RANGE 250-450,

%SATURATION 48, RANGE 15-50

FERRETIN 50, RANGE 10-232

DHEA 58, range 25-220

PROGESTERONE 13.5 (hysterectomy.. was told this was good)

ESTRADIOL 121(NOT SURE WHERE IN PHASE)

IGF I, LC/MS 139 RANGE 52-328.

TAKING 5-10k DAILY VITAMIN D. DEALING WITH LEAKY GUT AND MALABSORBTION ISSUES. TAKING 3 DIFFERENT TYPES OF MAGNESIUM DAILY AS WELL.

any thoughts??? TAKING 25 MG OF IRON, 80MG PROGESTERONE, CARRY THE MTHFR GENE, METHYL PROTECT B VITAMINS

### AKMan — 2012-10-16T18:58:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/labs.4330/post-58548>

Here's what I think is going on:

The Armour wasn't high enough for you to build up a good supply of T4, or you weren't absorbing it for some reason.

Your labs are indicating that you are responding to the exogenous T3. However, T3 is very short lived in the body, after 8 hours it is all gone and since you aren't getting enough T4, you have nothing to convert to T3 after the T3 you took in pill form is gone from your system.

Your TSH is very good because your average T3 levels are good.

I have found through my own labs that it takes about 3-5 weeks to build up a good supply of T4, so I think your doctor made a wise decision to switch you off Armour. He probably could have also upped your Armour to 120mg/day and had you take it 4 times a day.

The trouble with Armour, as seen by most CW doctors is that it contains a higher ratio of T3 to T4 as is found in normal humans.

I was on Synthroid for about 6 years and did fine on it. Hopefully you are scheduled to retest in 3-5 weeks.

### Patty Cakes — 2012-10-16T19:09:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/labs.4330/post-58552>

90mg (1 1/2 grains) of Armour is a very low dose. Most people who are on an NDT feel best between 2 1/2 - 5 grains. Personally I wouldn't take Synthroid or a synthetic T4. If your body has trouble converting the FT4 to FT3 (which could be the case with a leaky gut) the T4 isn't going to do you much good since the FT3 is the bioavailable thyroid hormone.

I felt extremely hypo on 90mg. I ended up at 150mg feeling good with labs that showed my FT3 was in the very top of the range. My TSH was .008, very low. I have Hashis so that TSH is irrelevant not to mention that when taking NDT it is suppressed anyway.

If your adrenals are bad you may not be able to tolerate all the thyroid meds. Those should be treated as well. What are your adrenals doing these days?

I went through 5 total docs before I found one who would treat me the proper way for my thyroid. I think you need a new doc.

### bigknitwit — 2012-10-16T20:14:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/labs.4330/post-58568>

I think I would start shopping for a new doctor. 60mg or Armour = 38mcg T4 plus 9mg T3.

So your Armour dose of T4 was 57mcg T4, and 13.5mg T3

Now you will be taking 75 T4 and 10mg T3

Your T4 labs are pretty low, and I don't expect the move from 57 to 75 will do much for that. Meanwhile your lifeline, the T3, is being reduced. How soon had you taken your Armour (and how much) prior to the blood draw? Are you symptomatic of being hypoT?

# Thread: Iodine again

### Michael — 2012-10-16T13:36:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/iodine-again.4334/post-58499>

I've no idea whether he's right of course, but it's interesting to see iodine coming up in health contexts:

> A lack of iodine in the traditional American diet has a direct link to the high incidences of breast cancer currently seen in western women. That is the observation of Dr. Dave Frahm, a naturopathic doctor ...

http://enumclaw.patch.com/articles/breast-cancer-pattern

### Jack Kruse — 2012-10-16T15:13:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/iodine-again.4334/post-58503>

Mike go back and read BG 5.......notice everything everyone is deficient in is in seafood. Go back and Read Cordains book 108-113. Notice his tables. All these nutrients are in seafood. Paleo is not the answer to neolithic disease.......seafood is.

### Michael — 2012-10-16T15:46:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/iodine-again.4334/post-58514>

So Dr. Frahm is probably right. And to connect prostate cancer? And perhaps osteoporosis, too?

This looks even bigger than I'd thought.

And if so paleo has really missed this boat. They mostly recommend land-based food when iodine (and DHA, etc.) are as big as this? And in the U.S. of all places where there's this broad "Goiter Belt" of iodine-deficient soil.

And as you've said before Cordain's own tables should have tipped him off.

And what's almost the first thing he says? Cut your salt. That's crazy advice unless you're getting iodine from seafood. Government mandates that to be iodinated for a reason.

Professor Cordain is a smart and hardworking researcher. I think the problem is he's too "conservative". I don't mean that in a political sense - he may be on the left for all I know. I mean he goes along with the consensus in the research community - vide his attitude on salt. Taubes has torn that salt-consensus apart. If someone like Jimmy Moore questions Cordain on changing his opinion on something, he says loftily that he goes by research and that the research changes. I don't think he sees that sometimes "the research" is just professional groupthink. Gary does. Gary breaks windows. Cordain doesn't break windows. I think Peter at Hyperlipid is the same: Peter never saw a paper whose assumptions he didn't question. He's not credulous of research.

### FarmersDaughter — 2012-10-16T15:54:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/iodine-again.4334/post-58515>

IMO, Iodine in salt is a bad joke. Most of it evaporates before its even used, and the balance is so miniscule, it just prevents frank goiter. Subclinical goiter is still very common...if your pcp will palpate the thyroid. That "if" is the $64,000 question, at least around here.

but then, I've never been afraid of iodine...got started taking it when I saw Dr.Fitt...made me sicker than crap at first...bromine, but now, we take lugols with relative impunity. First sign of a bug of any kind...congestion...ten drops of 5%.

### nonchalant — 2012-10-16T17:13:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/iodine-again.4334/post-58523>

> **FarmersDaughter;60034 wrote:**
>
> but then, I've never been afraid of iodine...got started taking it when I saw Dr.Fitt...made me sicker than crap at first...bromine, but now, we take lugols with relative impunity. First sign of a bug of any kind...congestion...ten drops of 5%.

Hi FarmersDaughter, can you give some more details about your bromine issue? How did you get past that? Slowly and methodically?

### FarmersDaughter — 2012-10-16T18:10:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/iodine-again.4334/post-58535>

Pulse dosing..ramp up, 1, then two, then three (etc) to ten iodoral every other day, lots of sea salt and lots of water.

I had what felt like a bad flu for about ten days...achy, snotty, congested, felt like hell. Weird acneform rash, don't recall what else. Pushed thru and it ended. My sis was way sicker than I, and had to back off to every third day for several months. I had a lot of yeast too, but that's pretty well gone I think.

I take between 10 and 20 drops of lugols 5% now, with Se and C daily. More if it sounds good.

### nonchalant — 2012-10-16T18:51:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/iodine-again.4334/post-58546>

Thanks, FarmersDaughter. I think it will take me many months, like your sis.

### MamaGrok — 2012-10-22T20:17:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/blue-blocking-shades-that-arent-industrial-looking.4336/post-59677>

I wanna see a pic of the vipers in action! Those are the ones I picked out, too!

So far, I have:

\- the industrial ones from amazon everyone's been getting. For me, they're too big without glasses on and too small with. They do block ALL blue, even from fluorescent light bouncing off CDs and the blue LED on my computer power button.

\- random brand blue-blockers I got at Walgreens, made to be worn over glasses. Look decent, fit fine. Block just as much blue as the others

\- Knockoff Blublockers I got off ebay (search on Blueblocker, note spelling) for wearing over my contacts. They fit fine and look fairly normal, but while they block almost all blue light, I still see a tiny bit of blue from CD's & a lot of the blue from my computer's blue LED power button.

# Thread: Adding thyroid med, need supplements help!
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/adding-thyroid-med-need-supplements-help.4337/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 7
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### jeepifer@gmail.com — 2012-10-16T19:56:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/adding-thyroid-med-need-supplements-help.4337/post-58563>

Hi all, I need some advice on how to fit thyroid med (NDT, 1/2 grain 2x day to start) into my food routine. I know you're supposed to take it on an empty stomach, once in AM and once early afternoon is what's been advised by my doc.

So here is what I'm thinking (and what supplements I'm taking/when):

Roll out of bed and take:

5-HTP (300 mg timed release)

2 blood pressure meds (ugh. hope to get off at least one someday!)

1/2 gr NDT

Cook BAB and eat 1/2 hour later. I usually take with BAB:

250 mg ox bile (I have no gallbladder)

Curcumin C3 Complex 500mg

Pure B-Complex Plus

Life Extension PQQ Cap

Iodoral 50 mg

50 mg DHEA

Then at lunch 5-ish hours later:

Multi (looking for recommendations for good one)

6000 IU D3

375 mg ox bile

Should I try to take the second 1/2 of NDT before I eat lunch? Would the multi at lunch interfere?

I could wait until mid-afternoon, if that wouldn't interfere with getting to sleep.

Any thoughts, suggestions, or critiques of my schedule and supplements is welcome!

### bigknitwit — 2012-10-16T20:04:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/adding-thyroid-med-need-supplements-help.4337/post-58566>

I've never had a problem with Armour interfering with sleep. I would actually recommend you do a mid-afternoon dose, to even out T3 levels across the day. Also, if you are worried about absorption if you've eaten recently, you can try lightly chewing the Armour and then placing it under your tongue for sublingual absorption, bypassing the stomach.

### jeepifer@gmail.com — 2012-10-16T20:18:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/adding-thyroid-med-need-supplements-help.4337/post-58570>

It just dissolves under the tongue? Huh.

### bigknitwit — 2012-10-16T20:25:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/adding-thyroid-med-need-supplements-help.4337/post-58572>

> **jeepifer;60099 wrote:** It just dissolves under the tongue? Huh.

Well, no, not really! And it doesn't taste all that great either - not bad, but definitely not something good! Just leave it under the tongue as long as you can deal with it, and then swallow whatever's leftover.

### FarmersDaughter — 2012-10-16T20:33:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/adding-thyroid-med-need-supplements-help.4337/post-58573>

Some brands taste better and dissolve better than others. Thiroyd does so wonderfully. Naturethroid is awful.

A grain of ndt helps me feel more rested in the am...more T3 for all the nightshift functions.

### Patty Cakes — 2012-10-16T21:43:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/adding-thyroid-med-need-supplements-help.4337/post-58580>

> **bigknitwit;60102 wrote:** Well, no, not really! And it doesn't taste all that great either - not bad, but definitely not something good! Just leave it under the tongue as long as you can deal with it, and then swallow whatever's leftover.

I'd add to this that you want to chew it up a little before letting it rest under your tongue. Some folks have had issues with the fillers creating absorption problems of the actual active medication.

A second dose is best around 1-3pm since it has to last you the entire evening. You may have to play around with the times in that range and maybe even extend it to 4pm. I never could go until 4pm after the first dose and then if taken too early I would crash in the evening by 8pm!

Another rule of thumb to follow with taking the NDT is if taken sublingually wait at least 30 minutes before eating. If swallowing wait at least 1-2 hours to eat.

I believe the other thing to note is no iron and I think calcium within 3-4 hours of meds. I never took either so didn't have to worry about it so someone correct me if I am wrong on this.

# Thread: Were the hominids in Tanzania killing large game?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/were-the-hominids-in-tanzania-killing-large-game.4338/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Michael — 2012-10-16T21:09:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/were-the-hominids-in-tanzania-killing-large-game.4338/post-58576>

Maybe not ...

The story that hominids, probably Homo erectus, were killing big game has been going the rounds. For example here:

> It appears that early hominids werenâ€™t just desperate scavengers picking at bones and fighting over marrow after all.
>
>
> [http://www.marksdailyapple.com/weekend-link-love-209/](http://http://www.marksdailyapple.com/weekend-link-love-209/)
>
>
>
> However:
>
>
>
> ... if hominins were feeding on ribs and breaking open the rib cage, the authors argue, they, not carnivores, got to the carcasses first. That means the hominins must've killed their prey themselves or driven off animal carnivores.
>
>
>
> Not so fast, say other scientists who weren't involved with the paper. "That's a very iffy argument," says paleoanthropologist Peter Andrews, an emeritus researcher at London's Natural History Museum. It's possible, he says, for carnivores to leave the ribs behind for scavengers. So the bones would bear peel marks even though the hominins hadn't procured the carcass themselves.
>
>
>
> Paleoanthropologist Michael Pante of University College London says modern-day lions often leave lots of rib meat on a carcass. So evidence that hominins consumed the ribs doesn't necessarily mean the hominins nabbed the booty themselves.

[http://news.sciencemag.org/sciencenow/2012/10/baby-back-waaaay-back-ribs.html](http://http://news.sciencemag.org/sciencenow/2012/10/baby-back-waaaay-back-ribs.html)

So maybe they made the kills and maybe they didn't.

And in any case, this would have been an easier task for Homo erectus than it would have been for an Australopithecine.

### KiwiLauren — 2012-10-17T00:09:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/were-the-hominids-in-tanzania-killing-large-game.4338/post-58608>

I thought it was interesting in those videos linked to BG 14 that the theory suggested that it was after pre-humans began eating huge amounts of seafood ('smart before they were smart') that they had the ability to go inland and hunt down big game.

### Michael — 2012-10-17T08:48:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/were-the-hominids-in-tanzania-killing-large-game.4338/post-58683>

Yeah, Dr. Broadhurst is very good, isn't she? "Smart before they were smart" makes the point so well.

# Thread: Just started cytomel and have questions
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/just-started-cytomel-and-have-questions.4339/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 51
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### DarleenMB — 2012-10-16T21:59:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/just-started-cytomel-and-have-questions.4339/post-58583>

First off my dosage is 25mcg and like an idiot I followed the instructions and took one whole tab. Now I'm wondering if that was a mistake.

Also, my cortisol (am AND pm) are both low. Any help in fixing that? Should I stop the cytomel and fix adrenals first? I am so confused.

And my T3/RT3 ratio is 3.8

Oh yeah, just got a new digital thermometer and have hit a new low. Temp was 94.8

### bigknitwit — 2012-10-16T23:43:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/just-started-cytomel-and-have-questions.4339/post-58597>

I'm sure others will chime in here, but I'll offer what I can.

If one tab = 25mcg T3, then yes, I would definitely break it up into 2-4 doses throughout the day. T3 has a short life and is high maintenance for taking orally.

Next, I'm not sure what your labs looked like to begin with, but you may really need to ramp up the T3 slowly. Otherwise, after a few days, your adrenals may not be able to handle the sudden increased metabolic rate. Maybe a good starting dose would be a total of half a tab (12.5mcg) broken up throughout the day? You may need to ask for more tabs in a lesser dose, unless the doc gave you time-released T3? You will need to monitor your temps (basal morning), and even throughout the day, to get a sense of how you're responding to the T3. I have a metal (non-mercury) thermometer that I used to use all the time (got it at the pharmacy for $8). I'd hold it under my armpit for 10 minutes. I found 10 minutes would produce a steady reading. Some prefer under the tongue readings, but I found it more doable under the armpit. HTH!!

There's a lot written up at the stopthethyroidmadness website about how to monitor your temperature for adrenal and thyroid management.

I have a metal (non-mercury) thermometer that I used to use all the time (got it at the pharmacy for $8). I'd hold it under my armpit for 10 minutes. I found 10 minutes would produce a steady reading. Some prefer under the tongue readings, but I found it more doable under the armpit. HTH!!

Btw...

BKW, you are on on realthyroidhelp also, arent you? I'm mamabear over there...been quite a while since I checked in, I should pop in.

### bigknitwit — 2012-10-17T02:12:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/just-started-cytomel-and-have-questions.4339/post-58625>

>
> BKW, you are on on realthyroidhelp also, arent you? I'm mamabear over there...been quite a while since I checked in, I should pop in.

Yes! That's me! Haven't been there in a while either though...

### Patty Cakes — 2012-10-17T03:50:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/just-started-cytomel-and-have-questions.4339/post-58647>

Your rT3 ratio should get better in 3-4 months on t3 only. The dose should be divided up into 3 to start. You can ramp up one of those three doses every 5-7 days (maybe sooner just depends on your body). Definitely read stopthethyroidmadness.com for more info on T3. If you have low cortisol you may get to a point where the body cannot take any more T3 and you will appear hyper: HR increasing too much, temp too high, heart palps, weird blood pressure, etc. That may or may not be enough T3 for you but it's all the adrenals can handle. You will know if you still have hypo symptoms as well.

I'm not a fan of the digital thermometers. I have three of them and all can give different readings. I have a Geratherm basal non-mercury thermometer that seems to work well as long as you don't drop it or keep it in a hot car! You should be monitoring the HR, BP, and temp at least two times a day to see what the T3 is doing to your body.

### vkiernan — 2012-10-17T03:56:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/just-started-cytomel-and-have-questions.4339/post-58649>

> **Patty Cakes;60181 wrote:** Your rT3 ratio should get better in 3-4 months on t3 only. The dose should be divided up into 3 to start. You can ramp up one of those three doses every 5-7 days (maybe sooner just depends on your body). Definitely read stopthethyroidmadness.com for more info on T3. If you have low cortisol you may get to a point where the body cannot take any more T3 and you will appear hyper: HR increasing too much, temp too high, heart palps, weird blood pressure, etc. That may or may not be enough T3 for you but it's all the adrenals can handle. You will know if you still have hypo symptoms as well.
>
>
>
> I'm not a fan of the digital thermometers. I have three of them and all can give different readings. I have a Geratherm basal non-mercury thermometer that seems to work well as long as you don't drop it or keep it in a hot car! You should be monitoring the HR, BP, and temp at least two times a day to see what the T3 is doing to your body.

Patty, dumb question but if one is still cold does that mean it isn't enough? Or, is the only way to really know by testing the things you mentioned? I have to buy a thermometer as my doc told me to take it 2 times a day along with my pulse; so thanks for the tips.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-10-17T04:39:23+0300

### DarleenMB — 2012-10-17T05:31:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/just-started-cytomel-and-have-questions.4339/post-58666>

thanks. I asked for the time release but she may not have heard me. I'll call tomorrow and ask her to redo the scrip because it didn't say anything about being TR.

I will say I felt great today. Like I woke up and suddenly felt more alive. Missed that.

### vkiernan — 2012-10-17T14:20:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/just-started-cytomel-and-have-questions.4339/post-58693>

I am doing a hybrid mix of sr and reg. I take my sr t3 40 mcg at 6 am and I am adding reg. t3 6.25 mcg every 4 hours and slowly titrating up from there. Doing the protocol on STTM. So far, all is ok. I'm thinking that by having the sr, if I forget for an hour, I am not completely running out and it should help smooth things over.

### fitness@home — 2012-10-17T18:20:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/just-started-cytomel-and-have-questions.4339/post-58722>

Iâ€™ll be watching this thread, as I started the STTM T3 protocol in October.

In June, my FT3/RT3 ratio was 11.3. Went through two doctors and my endocrinologist in an attempt to have just one of them understand and then treat. Seriously shook up my endo with the request â€“ he did not react well to it (firmly entrenched in the TSH only viewpoint). I did not succeed with the docs, so I ordered T3 myself.

About a six weeks ago, I began preparing by adding supplemental iron as my Ferritin and IBC levels leave a lot to be desired (very low). Also tracked my temps and BP for a â€œbaselineâ€￾. Currently adding and increasing dosages slowly as I work my way through the protocol and still tracking temps/BP. This isnâ€™t something you should do in a haphazard manner and I respect that fact.

Will get thyroid + iron labs in early January and post results in the Labs section. If successful in raising the FT3/RT3 ratio and I am feeling **good**, the next decision will be whether to stay on T3 only or transition back to NDT. Crossing that bridge laterâ€¦

### Patty Cakes — 2012-10-17T18:51:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/just-started-cytomel-and-have-questions.4339/post-58725>

> **vkiernan;60183 wrote:** Patty, dumb question but if one is still cold does that mean it isn't enough? Or, is the only way to really know by testing the things you mentioned? I have to buy a thermometer as my doc told me to take it 2 times a day along with my pulse; so thanks for the tips.

Here is my rule of thumb for someone with thyroid disease, although I'm sure there are "other" factors that may play into this. Simply put if your temp is low you need more T3. If you temp is erratic (averaged daily) from day to day it's most likely the adrenals.

As far as testing goes, with T3 the lab result will depend on when you took (or didn't take) the meds. My last T3 lab was at the bottom of the range but I hadn't had any T3 for over 14 hours...I'm sure the T3 is long gone. I'm not sure what the best timing is.

### BJK77 — 2012-10-17T20:07:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/just-started-cytomel-and-have-questions.4339/post-58739>

> **Patty Cakes;60261 wrote:** Here is my rule of thumb for someone with thyroid disease, although I'm sure there are "other" factors that may play into this. Simply put if your temp is low you need more T3. If you temp is erratic (averaged daily) from day to day it's most likely the adrenals.
>
>
>
> As far as testing goes, with T3 the lab result will depend on when you took (or didn't take) the meds. My last T3 lab was at the bottom of the range but I hadn't had any T3 for over 14 hours...I'm sure the T3 is long gone. I'm not sure what the best timing is.

So how long do y'all wait before testing to see if the T3 is effective? How soon do you increase your dose if you don't feel like you're getting results?

### bigknitwit — 2012-10-17T20:19:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/just-started-cytomel-and-have-questions.4339/post-58742>

I wonder about timing taking the T3 and measuring in the lab. Seems we can produce dramatically different results depending upon when we last took the T3. We say we want the T3 in the "top quartile," in the labs, but for those of us supplementing it, what does this mean? Top quartile when our T3 dose is at it's highest concentration (and then we try to maintain that level steady state throughout the day). My guess is that ideally, this is what we're after. A reading close to 4.2 for free T3 where the range max is 4.2, given that I just took T3 roughly 3 hours before the draw, and that my regimen allows me to maintain that level throughout the day. This allows us some "wiggle room" when dealing with a worried doctor. We can always take the T3 much farther away from the blood draw, to minimize the chance of the doctor seeing levels that are "too high." I don't like playing these kinds of games though...

### bigknitwit — 2012-10-17T20:21:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/just-started-cytomel-and-have-questions.4339/post-58744>

I would think your T3 labs would be reflecting your T3 dosages almost right away. But the TSH takes much longer to respond fully - like 6 weeks. Most doctors are so entrenched with the TSH that they usually make you wait that long.

Stopthethyroidmadness has excellent info on increasing T3 (or Armour, which has T3) dosages, based on tracking your temperatures in the mornings, and throughout the day. It makes it look so easy!! In reality, I found it a little trickier, but at least it's something to work with.

> **BJK77;60275 wrote:** So how long do y'all wait before testing to see if the T3 is effective? How soon do you increase your dose if you don't feel like you're getting results?

### bigknitwit — 2012-10-17T20:26:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/just-started-cytomel-and-have-questions.4339/post-58748>

Last winter when I switched from synthroid to ARmour, I was tracking my progress by monitoring my temperature. What I found was that my temps stayed nice and even throughout the day, even while I was becoming more and more hypoT (as the T4 levels dropped, and weren't replaced adequately). I was on 2 grains of Armour, and figuring it was more than enough to replacing the 137mcg synthroid plus 1/2 grain Armour I had been taking prior. I was wrong. And my temps didn't really reflect it. I was still averaging 98.5ish on my metal thermometer during the day. Anyway, my take-away lesson from that experiment is that temps are not solely influenced by thyroid hormone levels. I think the hypothalamus also has it's say here in other ways.

> **vkiernan;60229 wrote:** I am doing a hybrid mix of sr and reg. I take my sr t3 40 mcg at 6 am and I am adding reg. t3 6.25 mcg every 4 hours and slowly titrating up from there. Doing the protocol on STTM. So far, all is ok. I'm thinking that by having the sr, if I forget for an hour, I am not completely running out and it should help smooth things over.

### Patty Cakes — 2012-10-18T01:47:35+0300

I increased every 5 days-ish in the beginning but it is all dependent on how much you started on, were you on meds before this T3, which kind of meds if you were, if you are dealing with rT3, and how healthy your adrenals and iron are. I feel great on the dose I am on now. But it took months to get here.

Testing again depends on many factors. If you are clearing rT3, I've read where it takes about 8-12 weeks to clear all the rT3. I only wanted to test once so I waited 6 months and my ratio went from 16 to 40.

Yes, Kiwi I do that! Except I am now taking T3 but I used to do it with my NDT. I used the circadian method T3 protocol (CMT3) for healing the adrenals while also clearing the rT3. It took about 6 months for me. Now I like the T3 so am staying on it for a little while. There is a lot to the timing of when you take the early dose. You start at 1.5 hrs prior to your normal wake-up time and go backwards from there depending on HR, temp, BP. Those must be monitored 2-3 times a day to see what is happening. It was a success for me and my adrenals are good except the morning is a little high now. But I have adjusted the time of day I take the morning dose and the amount. The adrenals were so bad I started out in the very bottom of the range on all four test times.

### Patty Cakes — 2012-10-19T17:14:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/just-started-cytomel-and-have-questions.4339/post-59172>

> **Jennifer;60699 wrote:** Yes kiwi. I am trying it. Starting to think rT3 is my major problem with years of low iron and thyroid destruction (from eating gluten). I had low iron for 20 some years. That is one cause of high rT3. Getting some advice from a yahoo group on this. It's most interesting but confusing as taking T4 can cause high rT3. Just chewed my Armour this morning. I might even start a night dose. It was suggested I stabilize Adrenals and get iron higher before trying just T3 no NDT. I wAs surprised to find that the Armour tasted good - sweet even. Kind of scary. Wonder whats in it. I will be interested to see my labs on Monday.

Forest Labs who makes Armour, added a sweetener and changed the filler in the tablet a few years ago. I think most of the tabs, unless compounded have some type of "sugar" added to them.

Thyroid Hormone in Armour Thyroid

The active ingredient in Armour Thyroid is derived from pig thyroid glands. It is a combination of the thyroid hormones T4 (levothyroxine) and T3 (liothyronine). According to Forest Laboratories, the maker of Armour Thyroid, these hormones are in a fixed ratio in each tablet of 4.22 parts T4 to 1 part T3. The prescribing information for Armour Thyroid states each 60 mg tablet of Armour contains 38 mcg of T4 and 9 mcg of T3.

Lubricant in Armour Thyroid Tablets

Calcium stearate is an inactive ingredient used as a lubricant in Armour Thyroid tablets. According to Pharmaceutical Formulation Databases and Resources, lubricants in medication tablets help keep the medication powder from sticking to the equipment used to make the tablet.

Fillers in Armour Thyroid Tablets

Dextrose and microcrystalline cellulose are inactive ingredients used in Armour Thyroid tablets. According to Pharmaceutical Formulation Databases and Resources, these act as fillers and compression aids to improve the overall characteristics of the tablet powder.

Other Inactive Ingredients in Armour Thyroid Tablets

According to Pharmaceutical Formulation Databases and Resources, sodium starch glycolate is used to help the Armour Thyroid tablets dissolve once a patient takes the pill. Opadry white is a coloring agent that helps keep the color consistent in each batch of tablets.

[http://www.livestrong.com/article/43912-armour-thyroid-list-ingredients/#ixzz29kpFWRpy](http://http://www.livestrong.com/article/43912-armour-thyroid-list-ingredients/#ixzz29kpFWRpy)
