# Thread: drinking milk all year round and are we supposed to be drinking milk at all?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/drinking-milk-all-year-round-and-are-we-supposed-to-be-drinking-milk-at-all.4375/>
**Subforum:** Beginners Area
**Posts:** 9
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### x_danny_x — 2012-10-18T08:59:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/drinking-milk-all-year-round-and-are-we-supposed-to-be-drinking-milk-at-all.4375/post-58884>

Okay, I got to heated discussion with a medical student who still believes in the traditional way of life (eating grains, cholesterol bad, saturated bad for you). first i should of avoided since i couldn't stop thinking about fighting the dude. unwanted high levels of anger, cortisol, etc.

i got to the point about A1 and A2 type milk. he didnt know what i was talking about, go figure.

however, people keep bringing this up. can milk be consumed all year round? i see raw cream as a fat to consume during the fall and winter. so i assume milk can be consume as well.

however, the medical student mentioned we should not be drinking milk at all. i decided to keep my mouth shut since i wasn't sure of the answer either.

i see at my local farmer's market that raw yogurt is available. isn't raw yogurt the same as raw cream? i tasted it and has no sugar whats so ever, lol. talk about being more sour than salt, lol.

### ealachan — 2012-10-18T17:08:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/drinking-milk-all-year-round-and-are-we-supposed-to-be-drinking-milk-at-all.4375/post-58928>

No, yogurt is different than cream. Yogurt is populated with bacteria that break down a lot of the milk sugars as they colonize and grow. Yogurt is technically fermented. Cream is just cream. Also, just because dairy doesn't taste sweet doesn't mean there aren't sugars in it.

Some people do ok with dairy products, some do not. You have to test yourself to find out. There is no black-and-white right-or-wrong answer for everyone.

### PaleoCarol — 2012-10-18T17:18:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/drinking-milk-all-year-round-and-are-we-supposed-to-be-drinking-milk-at-all.4375/post-58932>

Thanks, Ealachan, for your response.

When you say "test" do you mean allergy test or genetic testing? I was tested to see if I am allergic to dairy, wheat, rye, corn, oats, barley and they all came out negative. Yet when I eliminate them from my diet I feel better. I must be sensitive to the chemicals, preservatives and other things in them.

BTW, I love, love love the photo of you in your hat!

PaleoCarol

Some people do ok with dairy products, some do not. You have to test yourself to find out. There is no black-and-white right-or-wrong answer for everyone.\[/QUOTE\]

Thanks...it's my husband's hat. I stole it for that picture...

re: testing - I meant in an N=1 sense, not so much a clinical sense. If you feel better not eating dairy, then don't eat it. Listen to your body. I love, love, love me some raw heavy whipping cream in my coffee in the mornings, but I've noticed that when I cut it out, my sinuses are WAY happier. So...I now view HWC as a once-in-a-while treat, rather than a daily staple.

# Thread: CT, vitamin C and hibernation
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-vitamin-c-and-hibernation.4378/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 7
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### AndrÃ©s — 2012-10-18T14:17:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-vitamin-c-and-hibernation.4378/post-58896>

First, the science.

It seems our eutherian cousins still able to hibernate increase their brains' ascorbic acid reserves before entering hibernation, [http://www.sciencenews.org/sn\_arc97/12\_6\_97/bob1.htm](http://http://www.sciencenews.org/sn_arc97/12_6_97/bob1.htm):

> Merging their interests, Rice and Drew decided to investigate antioxidant concentrations in hibernating arctic ground squirrels. They've now found that the amount of ascorbic acid in the blood of hibernating animals rockets to four times that measured during nonhibernating periods. Moreover, the amount of ascorbic acid in the cerebrospinal fluid that bathes the central nervous system doubles during hibernation. "It seems an important strategy to build up this extracellular store of ascorbic acid," says Rice.
>
>
>
> The researchers also noticed that when a squirrel roused temporarily from its torpor, a periodic occurrence for most hibernators, its vitamin C supply returned to normal within hours.

I think it is plausible that the mechanism for increasing concentrations of ascorbic acid on humans' brains may be still active on us and that it is activated upon cold exposure, since it could be a mechanism at play on brain protection under low temperature conditions, but I can't find any concrete evidence about it. Anyone measuring his/her spinal fluid content of vitamin C before and after CT?

Now, the personal anecdote. No, I haven't entered hibernation . I simply went and check the possible interaction between cold and vitamin C before merging them to treat a little common cold. I know I said that I would try CT upon any illness to see how it fares, but I had to be operative and just decided to smash it with all of the hammers at hand.

We were at Disneyland Paris this last rainy weekend, me, my daughters, my girlfriend and her daughter. Maximum temperature was 63Â°F (17Â°C) on Friday, 57Â°F (14Â°C) on Saturday and lower on Sunday. On Friday I wore my jacket but I got my feet wet, my weakness point. I got a little common cold. I brought there only a little vitamin C supply. From there on, I wore just my T-shirt waist upwards all the weekend (I think I was the only kruser there) and ate a generous share of butter to keep BAT on. I have to say that I improved a lot till Sunday that I got my feet highly wet again. So, upon arrival to Spain on Sunday I took a high dose of vitamin C. On Monday afternoon I [titrated on vitamin C](http://http://www.orthomed.com/titrate.htm) (1g each 5 minutes; 34g total) while wearing my [DIY-ice-vest](http://http://forum.jackkruse.com/showthread.php?1652-Effective-Efficient-CT&p=30971#post30971) (actually ice-vest v.2 with 6 panels of ice, 1 pound each): cold crushed.

I think about [Dr. Kruse position about vitamin C](http://http://forum.jackkruse.com/showthread.php?3996-Brain-Gut-12-is-now-Live&p=53150&viewfull=1#post53150) as that of a purist (I hope it doesn't sound disrespectful since I don't try it to): we have evolved with enough adaptations to not having to rely on it, and we should turn on all of those adaptations instead of relying on high doses of exogenous vitamin C. Correct me if I have misinterpreted.

I think that I see the beauty of this argument/approach, but I think that we should use vitamin C whenever it is convenient, since all of the machinery to its use is still active on us.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-10-18T14:47:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-vitamin-c-and-hibernation.4378/post-58899>

I agree with this concept fully

### nonchalant — 2012-10-18T17:01:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-vitamin-c-and-hibernation.4378/post-58926>

> **AndrÃ©s;60436 wrote:** On Monday afternoon I titrated on vitamin C (1g each 5 minutes; 34g total) while wearing my DIY-ice-vest (actually ice-vest v.2 with 6 panels of ice, 1 pound each): cold crushed.

Interesting approach. I've been waiting for illness, eager to try CT with it, but I keep resisting the cold, darnit.

Sometimes my throat starts to get sore, and I get hopeful, but it all clears up quickly.

Patience, Grasshopper...

### AndrÃ©s — 2012-10-19T11:28:19+0300

>
>
>
> Nonchalant;60466 wrote:** Interesting approach. I've been waiting for illness, eager to try CT with it, but I keep resisting the cold, darnit.
>
> Sometimes my throat starts to get sore, and I get hopeful, but it all clears up quickly.
>
> Patience, Grasshopper...

LOL! I suppose we are going to get sick a lot less often now...

### freesia — 2012-10-19T21:04:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-vitamin-c-and-hibernation.4378/post-59219>

Most intersting!!! Thanks Andres!

I've just come through winter and I had the usual couple of cold viruses hit me via my children bringing them home from school...

I didn't try CT right when I had a high temperature or a pounding head, but getting back in a cold bath (sub 50F) at the tail end of my virus completely obliterated it.....much like using high dose Vit C (I use 4 - 5g per hour) has treated me in the past...but faster.

I'd be really interested in measuring spinal fluid before and after CT.

Thanks again for your observations...and the link.

### AndrÃ©s — 2012-10-21T13:32:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-vitamin-c-and-hibernation.4378/post-59447>

> **freesia;60760 wrote: Most intersting!!! Thanks Andres!You're welcome!
>
>
>
>
> > **freesia;60760 wrote:** I didn't try CT right when I had a high temperature or a pounding head, but getting back in a cold bath (sub 50F) at the tail end of my virus completely obliterated it.....much like using high dose Vit C (I use 4 - 5g per hour) has treated me in the past...but faster.
>
> Precisely as me. If I hadn't got my feet wet again on Sunday, only air CTing would have put in remission this little common cold of mine.
>
>
>
> freesia;60760 wrote:** I'd be really interested in measuring spinal fluid before and after CT.

He, he! With enough _patients_ volunteering, perhaps Dr. Kruse would get in position of measuring it...

### AndrÃ©s — 2012-10-24T20:29:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-vitamin-c-and-hibernation.4378/post-60011>

> **Anna K.;61590 wrote: I'm glad to hear that there is an evolutionary reason for taking mega dose of C.Actually, I don't think Dr. Kruse would agree on the "mega" part for humans.
>
>
>
> Anna K.;61590 wrote:** Are there any dangerous of mega dosing C for a long time?

Of all of those supposed evils of ascorbic acid from conventional wisdom (some discussed [here](http://http://www.vitamincfoundation.org/faq.shtml) or [here](http://http://www.vitamincfoundation.org/faqdoc.shtml)), I only think plausible for doses larger than 9g/day that of possible copper (ceruloplasmin) deregulation: see [here](http://http://www.vitamincfoundation.org/forum/viewtopic.php?f=14&t=2145) and [here](http://http://www.drrisley.com/html/VitaminC.html). I like my daily intake around that of a carnivorous mammal production just in case, [around 3 grams scaled from dogs and cats](http://http://www.seanet.com/~alexs/ascorbate/198x/stone-i-orthomol_psych-1984-v13-n4-p280.htm). With this dose my acne is completely held at bay. Of course I go much higher when ill, for five days at most.

# Thread: Coconutoil and ghee

### Jasmin — 2012-10-18T16:49:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/coconutoil-and-ghee.4381/post-58921>

I have doubts about coconutoil... I know that it is best to use the oil in summer but when I'm far from optimal is it then ok to use all year? I have low metabolism, osteoporosis, Reanuds, and stress.

I am also in doubt about ghee when I have an AI.

### shilohman — 2012-10-18T17:05:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/coconutoil-and-ghee.4381/post-58927>

If you are fixing something, then coconut oil and ghee can be used year round. Dr. K has said this many times on the forum.

### x_danny_x — 2012-10-18T18:51:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/coconutoil-and-ghee.4381/post-58948>

we can use coconut oil all year round? from reading BG 6, it mentioned for coconut oil to be used during the spring and summer.

also that we cannot take it with seafood.

### ealachan — 2012-10-18T19:45:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/coconutoil-and-ghee.4381/post-58953>

> **x\_danny\_x;60488 wrote: we can use coconut oil all year round? from reading BG 6, it mentioned for coconut oil to be used during the spring and summer.
>
>
>
> also that we cannot take it with seafood.**

**

1\. They're just suggestions, not hard-and-fast rules. Everyone is different, depending on their specific situation**.

2\. Dr. Kruse has said repeatedly in the forums, blogs and comments that coconut oil can be used year-round if your level of metabolic dysfunction is such that the coconut oil would benefit you.

3\. You can eat coconut oil with seafood, you just won't absorb as much DHA from said seafood, which is one of the chief reasons for eating it to begin with.

### Michael — 2012-10-18T19:47:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/coconutoil-and-ghee.4381/post-58957>

> **x\_danny\_x;60488 wrote:** we can use coconut oil all year round? from reading BG 6, it mentioned for coconut oil to be used during the spring and summer.
>
>
>
> also that we cannot take it with seafood.
>
>
> Sure, but that's general advice for most people. Jasmin says she has autoimmune problems. Jack has specific advice for those people which is not quite the same. Here it is:
>
>
>
> [http://jackkruse.com/the-leaky-gut-prescription/](http://http://jackkruse.com/the-leaky-gut-prescription/)
>
>
>
> Part-way down that page, under the subheading "HOW DO WE TREAT IT? ..." he says:
>
>
>
> B. Consider use of coconut oil as the main fat in the diet until the syndrome is reversed.

I suppose that coconuts are out of season in the winter, and that might make coconuts, and coconut products, less than ideal in some ways. However, presumably their benefits with regard to AI problems are so great that, if you have those, that outweighs any small downside to consuming them out of season.

EDIT

While I'm typing that, the answer goes up. Ealachan must have quicker fingers, or a quicker mind, than me.

### Michael — 2012-10-19T16:51:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/coconutoil-and-ghee.4381/post-59163>

> **x\_danny\_x;60559 wrote:** so mainly if you have some healing to do or if it benefits you in some way you can actually eat it out of season.

I think you have it right.

Jack rarely says things without some pretty deep reasons. (And people out there in the blogosphere ought to realize that, if they don't want to end up with egg on their faces.)

See his latest post on why ladies are different from men in "Ask Jack" where he talks about the ability of our brains to signal our world to us.

I don't know, but maybe we could pick up natural chemicals in the food that "told" us it was ripe - and hence what time of the year it was. Something like that. So there'd be a signalling mismatch, if it wasn't.

But frankly the healing properties of coconut oil are so impressive that if you've got problems it just outweighs anything else.

### Lyndra — 2012-10-19T19:04:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/coconutoil-and-ghee.4381/post-59195>

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-10-21T17:26:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/coconutoil-and-ghee.4381/post-59466>

> **hope2conceive;60962 wrote:** Ugh! I can't remember reading that in your point number 2. And I even did a search. Oh well.
>
>
>
> Eachalan, do you have a favorite fat to use with seafood? I am struggling with giving up butter (and I should, I think). I have used macadamia nut oil...but it changes the flavor a lot. I'm a bit afraid of bacon. Not the fat, the potential parasites...I have been told by my functional MD to avoid any pork stuff.

I think when you look at it in a logical fashion, that's a very very very minor concern. Also goes to sourcing your product. Cured bacon from sun raised pork (piggies in the sun for the vit-d win) is safe. Also, almost any parasites still around we destroy in digestions. The old school worry of parasites in pigs was a good cautionary tale with all the wild pigs (pig ban is found in most religions). . but frankly, you just are not goin to be able to go find a "wild" pig anymore.

The brain tapeworm that has been the scare of this century (trichinosis) is killed with heat - and wouldn't be left around in the rendered fat.

BUT, of course we all do the little steps we can, and if avoidin pork is yours - more power to you. No reason you have to have additional fats with your seafood, either. Seems like we get so caught up in 'MOAR FAT MOAR FAT" that sometimes we forget to step back and go the easy route. Shrimp coctail! Poached Salmon!

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-10-18T19:43:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lyndra-rolls.4383/post-58952>

Lyndra suggested, and I ran with it!

1 can sardines

1/2 avocado

tbs rice vinegar (to taste)

Orange bell pepper for crunch

Nori Paper

Stop Drop and Roll!

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-10-18T19:56:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lyndra-rolls.4383/post-58963>

quite good. tasted like old school tuna-salad that you would eat on wonder bread. The vinegar I used had a little sugar, 5 g, but you could use some that didn't. I personally figure if it's the bridge I need getting in more cheap seafood then I will go with this recipe now and then.

I realized when thinking about carb grams. . I usually do about 40 a day now with changing weather. All in the form of vinegar and 99% dark chocolate with a few veggie carbs thrown in. Livin like a rockstar!

### Lyndra — 2012-10-19T02:06:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lyndra-rolls.4383/post-59073>

All fancied-up! Looks tasty except I don't do nightshades :-(. I had a dream last night about going out for sushi and my mom (long departed) was in awe of how I eat. I bet chopped mushrooms, black olives, or capers would be good, too.

Oh, and guess what I got at the seafood market the other day - yes, a fish head (actually head plus reamed out body). Supposedly came with roe but I couldn't find it so I just dumped it all in a pot. I didn't eat it as-is but did make stock. Baby steps on this one. Tomorrow I pick up oysters (they are special order these days) along with salmon roe (also available by order). I may have to ask for lessons on cleaning whole fish which he does carry.

### Shijin13 — 2012-10-22T17:35:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lyndra-rolls.4383/post-59643>

I made these today for Chaos and Havoc for lunch, as well as myself.

initially they we're like um I don't think so - so I let them just nosh the nori by itself they kept asking for more... so I made them tuna & sardine salad w/ celery, some apple cubes, spread it out on the nori, added some cucumber, and some avocado - wrapped it up...they were excited for their bento boxes for lunch today! I didn't take a pic - but I will the next time I make them! looking fwd to lunch today!

### ealachan — 2012-10-18T20:22:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tinfoil-hat-chat-tonight-8pm-est-anyone-interested.4384/post-58979>

>
>
>
> I didn't tak DH's last name b/c of the hoops I would have needed to go through to change my name.

Oh gods, I lived that one when I got married. It didn't even occur to me that I'd have to re-do alllll my security stuff for all the federal billing systems I access. Ugh. And it wasn't just "oh, fax us a copy of your new driver's license and we're good". It was notarized copies of marriage license, new driver's license, new social security card, and in one case, I had to go and get a sworn affidavit from not only my employer but also the town official that issued my marriage license, verifying that I had in fact gotten married but was still the same person.

Our tax dollars at work, ladies and gentlemen.

### ealachan — 2012-10-19T16:40:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tinfoil-hat-chat-tonight-8pm-est-anyone-interested.4384/post-59160>

The tin foil hats were epic. There was lots of me being social awkward in the beginning - I don't do the talking so good. Dr. K showed up with wine and chocolate (the food, not the forum member). Cavemam's husband did a drive-by. My mic was too loud a lot of the time. I couldn't shut my camera off, so everyone had to look at me the whole time. Dr. K made a mini tinfoil yarmulke. We're all supposed to live like polar bears and eat like Great Whites. Everyone got to meet my dog.

I had a good time...hopefully others did, too. Again, I apologize for my lack of conversation skills. I'm a typer, not a talker...LOL

### Lyndra — 2012-10-19T18:58:41+0300

# Thread: What can a 100-Year Old Irish Grandmother Teach Us About School Lunches?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-can-a-100-year-old-irish-grandmother-teach-us-about-school-lunches.4386/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 12
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Michael — 2012-10-18T20:02:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-can-a-100-year-old-irish-grandmother-teach-us-about-school-lunches.4386/post-58970>

> In a recent issue of the journal Nature, researchers studying the microbiota, or gut bacteria, of 178 older Irish citizens found they could group individuals based on the composition of their gut bacteria, which correlated with whether they lived at home, in a day-hospital, or in a rehabilitation or long-term residence care. Seems diet was the driver.
>
> According to the researchers, people living in their own homes had diets that were rich in â€œfiber, fruits, vegetables, grains, poultry and fish.â€￾ When they measured the composition of their gut bacteria, they found a greater diversity when compared to folks residing in assisted living facilities consuming the more institutionalized meals dominated by staples of mashed potatoes and porridge, puddings, sweetened beverages (several times a day), high-sugar and energy dense foods (several times a day), and less diversity of fruits and vegetables. Sound familiar?

Once you get in an institution, you get fed cheap food, which means processed carbs (those "dense acellular carbohydrates"). One might be forgiven for wondering whether it's all downhill from there.

[http://humanfoodproject.com/what-can-a-100-year-old-irish-grandmother-teach-us-about-school-lunches/](http://http://humanfoodproject.com/what-can-a-100-year-old-irish-grandmother-teach-us-about-school-lunches/)

And, of course this is what children are fed, too. Low "unit cost", because that's what's really important isn't it? That's what the Harvard Business School teaches.

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-10-18T21:13:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-can-a-100-year-old-irish-grandmother-teach-us-about-school-lunches.4386/post-58993>

>

>

> Honestly, if I'm going to be stuck in my old years living in a group home and being treated like cattle, I would rather just eat crap and get it over with.
>
>
>
> My plan, rather than go to a 'home', is to, in no particular order:
>
>
>
> 1\. Drink good wine.
>
> 2\. Skydive and see if I can will myself not to open the chute.
>
> 3\. eat very lovely and fancy french pastries and chocolates to my hearts content (irony here is that if I stay on this path, my hearts content will be none).
>
> 4\. Try cocaine
>
> 5\. Try to pull off an epic "heist" so I can flee the country and use my ill gotten booty to live out my last few years travelling in luxury.
>
> 6\. If the above doesn't get me, I'll become a mercenary/spy and go take out evil dictator of the day with my home made poison darts.

# Thread: Wow.. there are wrinkles gone from my face..!
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/wow-there-are-wrinkles-gone-from-my-face.4388/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Inger — 2012-10-18T21:54:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/wow-there-are-wrinkles-gone-from-my-face.4388/post-59004>

I need to share this!

I have checked my face the last days and recogniced some strange wrinkles I have had on the both outer sides down from the outer side of my eyes to cheek are totally gone..?! I had them for quite a time already, my younger sister have the same, they makes one look kind of tired. Weird wrinkles like you have slept on your face somehow..

I have done nothing else than adding quite much seaweeds to my diet, and seafood too (but I ate some before too regularely, just never seaweeds). I do have smeared some Buckthorn berries from the garden on my face...lol and some reindeer marrow leftovers from my breakfast once in a while.. because I run out of my argan oil and had no money to buy new..

Then I am quite diziplined with having only candels at night, triple protection when on the computer evenings and sunglasses when out in shops etc. after sunset.

Now I do not know which of these components have really helped but gone they are..!!! How cool!

### nonchalant — 2012-10-18T22:08:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/wow-there-are-wrinkles-gone-from-my-face.4388/post-59010>

Inger, that's great! I have expected some improvements in wrinkles, with all the other skin improvements we are seeing.

My skin has never felt like this, even back in my teens or 20's. It's like baby skin.

# Thread: chest is sore and minor cough after CT
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/chest-is-sore-and-minor-cough-after-ct.4391/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### growltgc — 2012-10-18T23:43:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/chest-is-sore-and-minor-cough-after-ct.4391/post-59038>

So, Yesterday I went swimming in water I would measure to be between 50 to 52 degrees. It's the coldest water I swam in yet. I did two 20 minute sessions in the period of 4 hours. The first time after coming out, I noticed a little pain in my chest that I quickly forgot about. I thought at the time it could be related to the cold exposure. The second time coming out I noticed the same pain. But I didn't notice it again after 5 minutes.

This morning, I have a minor cough and my chest hurts a bit when I cough.

I'm lead to believe the cough / pain is due to the CT I did and perhaps other factors.

# Thread: Flu killing healthier faster?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/flu-killing-healthier-faster.4393/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 5
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-10-19T06:14:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/flu-killing-healthier-faster.4393/post-59115>

[http://www.msnbc.msn.com/id/49465633/ns/health-cold\_and\_flu/#.UIDETGt5mK0](http://http://www.msnbc.msn.com/id/49465633/ns/health-cold_and_flu/#.UIDETGt5mK0)

They dont mention the mrsa till the end. Probably if he hadnt gone to hospital, he may have been able to survive the flu. What do you think about this?

### AndrÃ©s — 2012-10-19T11:45:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/flu-killing-healthier-faster.4393/post-59133>

> Given how unpredictable flu can be and how fast it can kill, says the CDCâ€™s Dr. Karen Wong, vaccination is the best protection for kids and adults alike.

That's a humorless joke:

-   [http://summaries.cochrane.org/CD004879/vaccines-for-preventing-influenza-in-healthy-children](http://http://summaries.cochrane.org/CD004879/vaccines-for-preventing-influenza-in-healthy-children)
-   [http://summaries.cochrane.org/CD001269/vaccines-to-prevent-influenza-in-healthy-adults](http://http://summaries.cochrane.org/CD001269/vaccines-to-prevent-influenza-in-healthy-adults)
-   [http://summaries.cochrane.org/CD004876/vaccines-for-preventing-seasonal-influenza-and-its-complications-in-people-aged-65-or-older](http://http://summaries.cochrane.org/CD004876/vaccines-for-preventing-seasonal-influenza-and-its-complications-in-people-aged-65-or-older)

    See also [Dr Brownstein's](http://http://drdavidbrownstein.blogspot.com.es/2012/09/mandated-flu-vaccines-whole-lotta.html) and [Dr. Briffa's](http://http://www.drbriffa.com/2012/03/30/does-flu-vaccination-prevent-death-in-the-elderly-apparently-not/) take on flu vaccination. Summing it up: it is worthless.

    Best protection?:

    Good level of vitamin D in blood: see [here](http://http://ajcn.nutrition.org/content/early/2010/03/10/ajcn.2009.29094.abstract) or [here](http://http://ebm.rsmjournals.com/content/235/12/1395.full#ref-2).
    High doses of vitamin C (here I go again ): see [here](http://http://perfecthealthdiet.com/2010/08/nz-man-left-for-dead-by-doctors-cured-by-vitamin-c/) or [here](http://http://www.orthomed.com/flu.htm).

### AndrÃ©s — 2012-10-19T11:56:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/flu-killing-healthier-faster.4393/post-59134>

> Thereâ€™s an antiviral drug called Tamiflu that can help prevent the worst effects of influenza if patients get it within a day or two of being infected.
> Another humorless joke:
>
>
> -   [http://summaries.cochrane.org/CD008965/a-review-of-unpublished-regulatory-information-from-trials-of-neuraminidase-inhibitors-tamiflu-oseltamivir-and-relenza-zanamivir-for-influenza](http://http://summaries.cochrane.org/CD008965/a-review-of-unpublished-regulatory-information-from-trials-of-neuraminidase-inhibitors-tamiflu-oseltamivir-and-relenza-zanamivir-for-influenza)
> -   [http://www.drbriffa.com/2009/12/11/does-tamiflu-actually-work-and-is-it-safe/](http://http://www.drbriffa.com/2009/12/11/does-tamiflu-actually-work-and-is-it-safe/)
>     [http://articles.latimes.com/2012/jan/15/opinion/la-oe-finkelstein-tamiflu-20120115](http://http://articles.latimes.com/2012/jan/15/opinion/la-oe-finkelstein-tamiflu-20120115)
>
>
>
> It turned out he had both influenza B and an infection with a â€œsuperbugâ€￾ called methicillin resistant Staphylococcus aureus, or MRSA.

After reading Dr. Cannell on [Blood Irradiation](http://http://www.vitamindcouncil.org/news-archive/2006/is-vitamin-d-an-antibiotic/) and on [Common Cold](http://http://www.vitamindcouncil.org/news-archive/2010/self-inflicted-vitamin-d-toxicity/), I get the impression that vitamin D is not going to be effective against staphilococcus aureus, rhinoviruses and influenza B at least, so if the moment comes [vitamin C megadoses must be](http://http://www.orthomed.com/bacteria.htm).

### Stavity — 2012-10-19T15:44:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/flu-killing-healthier-faster.4393/post-59149>

I just went through a bad flu. Didn't do anything different and now I feel better then before the flu. I am pretty sure I just helped my immune system.

My guess is what you say Cavemam.

### maggie — 2012-10-19T17:45:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/flu-killing-healthier-faster.4393/post-59177>

The article mentions that often healthy children get sicker: _The other theory is that for some reason the immune system goes into overdrive in some healthy children. “The doctors called it a perfect storm,” said Regina Booth, Austin's mother._

I think this is why the Spanish Flu during WWI was so much more lethal to young adults, who had more robust immune reactions, than elderly or young people. I think I remember Dr. Cannell saying in his article about that flu epidemic that Vitamin D helps because it moderates the immune reaction and helps prevent it from going into hyperdrive and killing you.

# Thread: Lethally Hot Earth Was Devoid of Life (Triassic)
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lethally-hot-earth-was-devoid-of-life-triassic.4396/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Michael — 2012-10-19T08:21:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lethally-hot-earth-was-devoid-of-life-triassic.4396/post-59125>

National Geographic:

> Extinctions during the early Triassic period left Earth a virtual wasteland, largely because life literally couldn't take the heat, a new study suggests.
>
>
> [http://news.nationalgeographic.com/news/2012/10/121018-triassic-extinctions-hot-global-warming-science-environment/](http://http://news.nationalgeographic.com/news/2012/10/121018-triassic-extinctions-hot-global-warming-science-environment/)
>
>
>
> Ars Terchnica:
>
>
>
>
> > Based on their findings, the Great Dying was accompanied by a huge increase in water temperatures in the area, which rose from 21Â° to 36Â°C over the course of 800,000 years. After a period of decline, they surged again, this time reaching at least 38Â°C, and possibly exceeding 40Â°Câ€”that's over 100Â° Fahrenheit. And this was ocean water; temperatures on land were likely to have been even more extreme.
>
>
>
> [http://arstechnica.com/science/2012/10/too-hot-to-handle-triassic-temps-may-have-killed-tropical-species/](http://http://arstechnica.com/science/2012/10/too-hot-to-handle-triassic-temps-may-have-killed-tropical-species/)
>
>
>
> Eurekalert:
>
>
>
> The dead zone would have been a strange world â€“ very wet in the tropics but with almost nothing growing. No forests grew, only shrubs and ferns. No fish or marine reptiles were to be found in the tropics, only shellfish, and virtually no land animals existed because their high metabolic rate made it impossible to deal with the extreme temperatures. Only the polar regions provided a refuge from the baking heat.

[http://www.eurekalert.org/pub\_releases/2012-10/uol-tcc101712.php](http://http://www.eurekalert.org/pub_releases/2012-10/uol-tcc101712.php)

Graphic:

[https://docs.google.com/file/d/0B691WmQZab8LZVdsYXRBR1pqOVU/edit?pli=1](http://https://docs.google.com/file/d/0B691WmQZab8LZVdsYXRBR1pqOVU/edit?pli=1)

### Jack Kruse — 2012-10-19T13:56:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lethally-hot-earth-was-devoid-of-life-triassic.4396/post-59139>

Good stuff here. Loved the paleo graphic in the google docs.

> People across the country are today joining forces to raise awareness of human trafficking as part of Anti-Slavery Day, an event initiated by former Totnes MP Anthony Steen.
>
>
>
> Taking place each year on October 18, Anti-Slavery Day sees workers, students and charities draw attention to forced labour, human trafficking and debt bondage.
>
>
>
> United Nations figures suggest 800,000 people are trafficked every year.
>
>
>
> Some 76 per cent of victims are trafficked for sexual exploitation, Eurostat finds, and 70 per cent of victims are women.
>
>
>
> Some 17 per cent are men, 11 per cent girls and two per cent boys.
>
>
>
> The day was created by an Act of Parliament following the passage of the Anti-Slavery Day Bill in 2010.
>
>
>
> The Bill was introduced in Parliament as a Private Membersâ€™ Bill by Anthony Steen, former Conservative MP for Totnes. It passed through both Houses unopposed, although amended. ...

# Thread: First Nations Fishing Village in Vancouver, BC
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/first-nations-fishing-village-in-vancouver-bc.4400/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 14
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Michael — 2012-10-19T15:16:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/first-nations-fishing-village-in-vancouver-bc.4400/post-59146>

Good stuff:

> ... "Radio carbon-dating from artifacts pulled up during their 2010 dig found the settlement was occupied more than 1,300 years ago.
>
>
>
> Rahemtulla says major trails lead into and out of the village and could have been used for trade exchanges at the village long before Europeans arrived.
>
>
>
> The village was abandoned about 200 years ago.
>
>
>
>
>
> The Babine Lake First Nation historical timeline on its website says cannery operators down river complained that the aboriginal fishing weirs threatened their supply. ...
>
>
>
> The economy of the Babine band later shifted from **relying on salmon** to trapping and the fur trade."

[http://www.vancouversun.com/technology/Ancient+village+along+river+reveals+1300+years+First+Nations/7414250/story.html](http://http://www.vancouversun.com/technology/Ancient+village+along+river+reveals+1300+years+First+Nations/7414250/story.html)

Here's a shorted URL if that rather long URL breaks:

[http://bit.ly/TBjLYp](http://http://bit.ly/TBjLYp)

I think people have hugely underestimated the extent to which some aboriginal peoples made use of, or even relied on, fish.

Note also the "major trails in and out of the village" - which recalls the point about people in the past being able to trade over distance that Maggie made here before.

### nonchalant — 2012-10-19T17:09:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/first-nations-fishing-village-in-vancouver-bc.4400/post-59169>

So, putting it in chronological order:

> After First Nations were prohibited from putting up the weirs, the village faced starvation, fish wardens sent reports that the Babine people were starting an uprising and a 100-strong militia was sent into the community.
>
>
>
> > The economy of the Babine band later shifted from relying on salmon to trapping and the fur trade.
>
>
>
> The village was abandoned about 200 years ago.

So it takes a while, after a change of diet, for a population to dwindle enough to abandon a site.

### finnite@dccnet.com — 2012-10-19T18:16:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/first-nations-fishing-village-in-vancouver-bc.4400/post-59179>

> **Michael;60686 wrote:** Good stuff:
>
>
>
>
>
>
>
> [http://www.vancouversun.com/technology/Ancient+village+along+river+reveals+1300+years+First+Nations/7414250/story.html](http://http://www.vancouversun.com/technology/Ancient+village+along+river+reveals+1300+years+First+Nations/7414250/story.html)
>
>
>
> Here's a shorted URL if that rather long URL breaks:
>
>
>
> [http://bit.ly/TBjLYp](http://http://bit.ly/TBjLYp)
>
>
>
> I think people have hugely underestimated the extent to which some aboriginal peoples made use of, or even relied on, fish.
>
>
>
> Note also the "major trails in and out of the village" - which recalls the point about people in the past being able to trade over distance that Maggie made here before.

you're not from around here are you

Coastal BC First Nations relied heavily on salmon and oolichan runs on a seasonal basis to stock winter food supplies. Spring and summer were predominantly shell fish and land based animals along with local vegetation, if you go on a dig to any of the coastal habitats the depth of the shell midden in many places is over 15ft deep. we have a berry here called a salmon berry that is the first edible berry of the year, it ripens Mid-May to Mid June depending on the weather. even today when you take you kids walking in the woods salmon berries abound and it's fun to watch the kids run from bush to bush picking them (they are called salmon berries because the colour is akin to the colour of salmon flesh)

for the record though the Babine Nation is a mid-northern inland nation around Terrace and Smithers BC (I was actually at the southern end of the Babine river last week), not Vancouver. in the geographic Vancouver area are the Tsawwassen, Musqueam, Chehalis and Squamish nations (apologies I might have missed a FN group) who are distantly related to the Babine nation but form part of the Coast Salish, Babine falls under the Carrier Nation (as far as I can tell). Each fall when the salmon run the fish migrate literally halfway through the province to find their spawning grounds.

@ Nonchalant, to understand why many of the villages were abandoned and why the militia was sent in look up the tradition of potlatch.

there was a concerted effort by Her Majesties troops to destroy the potlatch tradition and one way to do that was to prevent the ability to stockpile salmon which would have been given away.

### SeaHorse — 2012-10-19T18:32:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/first-nations-fishing-village-in-vancouver-bc.4400/post-59184>

The whole NW coast was a fish based economy for eons. Salmon was king. Whales, seals and shellfish were plentiful and the development of give aways or potlatches came about because there was more than enough for survival. Wealth was acquired and then distributed among and between the villages in massive gift giving. It was a sign of power to give away what you owned. There was also a significant class structure and distinctions existed between commoners, slaves and high ranking people.

These were not hunter/gatherers...interesting to look at how cultures through time respond to wealth.

The trading routes were incredible throughout the Americas....the fur trade was the first time wealth just left the community with no equal exchange or benefit. It's still going on with the extraction of natural resources throughout Canada....no soapbox though

### Michael — 2012-10-19T18:46:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/first-nations-fishing-village-in-vancouver-bc.4400/post-59188>

> **Nonchalant;60709 wrote:** So it takes a while, after a change of diet, for a population to dwindle enough to abandon a site.

Well. It might actually expand with a change to a worse diet. It would just be less healthy. I agree the article jumps around. You'd have to ask a historian with local knowledge what was going on at any particuilar time - and what they cutrently think might be changed by what the excavation of the remains reveals.

The article reads as if the shift to trapping follows 1906, and of course it wouldn't. They can't have meant to imply that. After the fur trade became less important and lucrative (beavers replaced by silk top hats owing to changing fashions, etc. - possibly over-exploitation of fur-bearing animals, too ... don't know on that one) I would think that people would not have been able to return to relying on the salmon, because by then they'd have been over-exploited. They probably moved away to the towns and cities in search of work.

They probably moved away to the towns and cities in search of work.

the reduction in salmon runs is a relatively recent pheonomenon (ie last 20 years) on the West Coast and is linked to seasonality as well as issues with farmed salmon fry infecting wild salmon fry with lice

the switch to trapping and the reduction of salmon dependance had more to do with political suppression of First Nations than depeletion of the salmon.

and yes, I'm from Vancouver

### finnite@dccnet.com — 2012-10-19T21:16:05+0300

um the winky smiley indicated it was a joke, it's unfortunate that you didn't bother to read or as Cavemam mentioned you could learn something

things such as the cultural importance of potlatch tradition for trade and the sharing of food and wealth. you would have also learned that the killing of a slave was an unusual event. potlaches were usually held to celebrate a birth, marriage or mourn the dead.

the bulk of the attempt to destroy the First Nations in BC was done while Queen Victoria was on the throne therefore the reference to "her majesty" in fact the ban against potlatches occurred in 1884. again, no slag against the monarchy but that's how the troops were referred to historically. and to your own point things just didn't happen in 1906 there were significant public and political pressures in that area for over 50 years beginning with the Caribou gold rush and small pox epidemic brought by the rush that greatly affected the health and lives of First Nations people.

### nonchalant — 2012-10-19T23:53:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/first-nations-fishing-village-in-vancouver-bc.4400/post-59233>

I wonder if the word 'potlatch' is the origin of the phrase "potluck supper", where everyone brings a dish to the gathering.

Similar to the persecution of the tradition of potlatch, the invention of the Mexican pinata was an attempt to put native traditions down. Taking a bat to hit a symbol (like a star) of an old religion in order to get candy gives a powerful message.

ColleenM, interesting about the salmon berries. Sounds nice.

### finnite@dccnet.com — 2012-10-20T03:22:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/first-nations-fishing-village-in-vancouver-bc.4400/post-59271>

>
>
>
> ColleenM, interesting about the salmon berries. Sounds nice.

that was something that I wondered as well and did take a look and apparently it\`s of Irish origin rather than from First Nations

thanks for the information about the pinata, that was something I did not know. powerful symbols indeed. here in the northwest there is a claim that in an effort to limit the number of First Nations persons the local government took advantage of a cultural ceremony called the blanket ceremony and distributed blankets infected with small pox and TB

btw, I was curious as to what the other tradtional food were for the Coast Salish group outside of Salmon, Sturgeon and oolichan and came across this rather interesting study on the changing food habits of the Coast Salish over the past 150 years.

[http://bitsandbytes.ca/sites/default/files/turner06-315editedfinal.doc](http://http://bitsandbytes.ca/sites/default/files/turner06-315editedfinal.doc)

I was floored to see that prior to the introduction of the potato the Hyacinth bulb (!) was a major root crop! I have a boatload of these in my garden, I\`ve always known they were prized by squirrels but did not realize they were edible by humans.

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-10-20T04:09:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/first-nations-fishing-village-in-vancouver-bc.4400/post-59274>

We need courses in wild food at our optimal retreat!

# Thread: Vitex vs bromocriptine
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/vitex-vs-bromocriptine.4402/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 4
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### FarmersDaughter — 2012-10-19T19:20:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/vitex-vs-bromocriptine.4402/post-59199>

This was suggested as an interim step when I brought up cycloset to my PCP.

It is a dopamine receptor agonist as well, if I understand correctly, just "smaller caliber of the same gun".

Thoughts or BTDT?

### Destiny — 2012-10-19T19:33:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/vitex-vs-bromocriptine.4402/post-59200>

My only experience with Vitex was that it really helped with keeping my breast cysts at bay. It was also beneficial with PMS. I was taking it over 4 years. I stopped twice and a cyst appeared in no time. I did not know anything about progesterone at that time.

### Shijin13 — 2012-10-19T22:21:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/vitex-vs-bromocriptine.4402/post-59226>

I've had success taking vitex, as far as providing regularity to my cycle, and decreasing length...

### SueC — 2012-10-20T02:52:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/vitex-vs-bromocriptine.4402/post-59267>

Traditionally Vitex is used to treat estrogen dominance. Studies show it decreases FSH and increases secretion of LH and prolactin. And, yes, it has a dopaminergic effect (_Principles & Practice of Phytotherapy, Mills & Bone_)

As an herbalist, I've used it with countless women for menstrual irregularities/infertility with good success. Also helpful in PCOS. But I honestly don't know the strength of its dopaminergic effects. I once recommended a client with Parkinson's at least try it, but we saw no effect on tremors. Obviously, that was a tough test case.

Thank you Patty Cakes! I'll call them tomorrow since their website is confusing. Do you have a gliadin intolerance or other allergy that you were testing for (I'm not sure of the correct terminology here)? Dr. K. suggested I find out exactly what I'm allergic to since I tested positive, and make sure to remove it completely from my diet. He also suggested that the offending food might be found in my make up as well.

Gliadin is everywhere. I had been saving two boxes of jelly beans in my freezer in case I had a sugar attack. I'd always thought they would be "safe" since I suspected I had an intolerance to gluten. Yesterday, I checked the ingredients for the first time -- ingredients 2 and 3 "wheat syrup and modified corn starch!" In jelly beans no less! The biggest joke is that I might be allergic to everything BUT chocolate, and that the ONLY SWEET I DON'T like! That totally amuses me. I'm glad you get to keep your favorite

It does now; thank you very much. I had no idea. Crap, one in all reality should do this too when knowing they are gluten intolerant. I have a feeling when you body has been fighting it for so long that it probably makes a reaction to almost everything. I'm thinking Jack should do a blog on all this since a lot of us are affected. Patty, where can I find more info? Do they have a lot on their website that would be useful?

I really got to get a handle on all of this. Saw my doc yesterday and it's pretty clear I still have adrenal fatigue. Pretty bummed. Have to get that ASI test done to see what direction if any.

### curves64 — 2012-10-21T05:16:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/has-anyone-used-cyrex-labs-for-gliadin-testing.4403/post-59432>

> **Owl;60838 wrote: Here's a place for Cyrex Array 4 for $239 - [http://www.ovitaminpro.com/cyrex4.html](http://http://www.ovitaminpro.com/cyrex4.html)**

**

Great resource, Owl, thank you! They provide great descriptions of the two arrays as well.

**Cyrex Labs Array 3** is designed to help determine more about a person's sensitivity to different parts of the wheat kernel including several aspects of gliadin, glutenin and even the wheat germ. The test was designed because so many people found they were healthier on a gluten free diet but tested negative with current testing at that time. The doctors involved with the lab wanted to find a way of better predicting who could benefit from a gluten free diet. Even today most gluten aware doctors will still only order a tTG or EM test (tissue transglutaminase or endomysial antibodies) and if these come back negative declare that you are not gluten sensitive. Researchers have found that about 12 different parts of wheat can cause significant antibody reactions resulting in a 24 step test including IgG and IgA antibodies for each of these 12 components.

Cyrex Labs Array 4** is an adjunct to Array 3. Array 3 looks more specifically at wheat gluten sensitivity and Array 4 looks at other foods that gluten sensitive people often react to. Generally is a person is already gluten free, we don’t bother with Array 3 and go instead to Array 4. If a person isn’t sure about their gluten status, Array 3 along with a genetic test can be most convincing. Test results for Array 4 are source of excellent information but it isn’t perfect. A “yes” means “yes” but a “no” means “maybe”. Sometimes you test negative for barley even though barley is a problem. It could be because you have been gluten-free for more than 3 weeks (that’s all it takes to quiet an antibody blood test) or the barley isn’t causing a specific antibody of its own but the barley gluten might still be activating the wheat gluten. This is true of the Polish Wheat, Sorghum and Spelt. When you get your results we will review them with you so you understand the significance for you and your health.


══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

# Thread: Low SIga
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/low-siga.4408/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Labs
**Posts:** 12
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### SueC — 2012-10-20T02:20:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/low-siga.4408/post-59262>

Lab puts my SIga at less than 5 (25-60 mg/dl normal; 20-25 mg/dl borderline).

Cortisol

6-8 am: 15 (13-24 nM)

11-1 pm: 3 (5-10 nM)

4-5 pm: 2 (3-8 nM)

10-midn: 3 (1-4nM)

So how do I best approach this? Long before this test I was already taking S. Boulardi & EpiCor, both of which are said to raise IgA. Been working on leaky gut for a long time: HDL was 55 at last lab, up from previous.

I've had chronic fatigue diagnosis years ago and I'm definitely not there now in terms of symptoms, but obviously cortisol is not good. I've started with blue blockers and low lights since reading of it here. I've long done a little licorice in my all-day herbal tea. I'm shifting toward Epi-Paleo, tho I don't quite have a majority seafood yet.

Are there any other ways to improve the mid-day cortisol slump besides observing the light cycle? Anything to raise IgA besides basic leaky gut blog protocol?

I'd appreciate any suggestions.

### Patty Cakes — 2012-10-20T21:11:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/low-siga.4408/post-59377>

Your cortisol really is bad all day long. You should be in the top of the range first thing in the morning, near the top on the second reading, mid range on the third reading and low end on the midnight reading. You are starting the right way w/ orange glasses and lights. Go back and read the blogs and comments on adrenals for more tidbits of great info. Also have you had your thyroid checked? They go hand in hand and the only way to know is to have it checked.

Do you know if there is a relationship between SIga and adrenals?

### SueC — 2012-10-20T22:32:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/low-siga.4408/post-59383>

Don't know about relationship between SIga & andrenals. Had thyroid checked recently & thought doc understood I wanted full panel, but unfortunately she just did TSH, which was high. Hence she upped my T4 (I take T4 7 T3). I can get tested again in Dec. and will insist on full workup then, along with AB testing, as I've never had that. (Still new to this site and learning a lot).

And THANKS much for the info. I can't believe how generous this community is and what a wealth of info is here.

### Patty Cakes — 2012-10-21T01:04:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/low-siga.4408/post-59395>

Oh here I went an just assumed you were not on thyroid meds. A full panel which really is just FT3, FT4, TSH, rT3, and ABs (TPO and TgAB) are all you need. There is a bunch more but so far it seems useless info unless something presented itself in these labs. Most important being the FT3 and rT3. More inportant than the FT4 that the doc probably likes.

This makes sense now. Your thyroid is probably not getting enough from either iodine in foods/supps or meds so the TSH is high. Over time this has made your adrenals take over for energy output but now your adrenals are exhausted from picking up the slack of the thyroid. Hopefully your doc can help you with that too. Many docs don't treat adrenals. If the doc won't help you with that I'd find another doc. It looks to me (not a doc just a person w/similar history) like you are on your way to complete adrenal exhaustion. I was almost there too. Read more info on thyroid and adrenal connection: stopthethyroidmadness.com.

Good luck Sue!

### SueC — 2012-10-21T01:33:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/low-siga.4408/post-59406>

Yes, after seeing this result I guessed my T3 is low given that I'd been eating VLC since January, but had just started adding seafood/seaweed. I doubt this doc will TX adrenal, we'll see. I've been doing Rhodiola, Eleuthro for some time but my hubby lost his job recently and the stress has definitely kicked up.

As for low SIga, lab report sez it can be caused by excessive cortisol, short imbalance in sympathetic to parasympathetic, chronic deficits in cortisol (duh, I missed that on 1st read) and/or DHEA. It's beginning to make more sense now.

Thanks again!

### Patty Cakes — 2012-10-21T02:18:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/low-siga.4408/post-59411>

Defnitely test your DHEA and pregnenolone if you can afford it. You probably also have pregnenolone steal going on with bad adrenals. The will shunt the preg to make cortisol and then there is nothing left for sex hormone production. Or you can try taking a bit of preg since it's over the counter. SOme people with adrenals issues can't tolerate much but even a little may help.

Personally I know each little piece will start to make sense after a bit of time (and reading it 5X!!!). Sounds like you are doing that already!

And of course, CT will help thyroid function. Good luck! I've got a very similar pattern to you and it's a lot to work on. Hang in there.

### SueC — 2012-10-21T20:56:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/low-siga.4408/post-59502>

Thanks, all! This is helping me from being terrified. Chronic fatigue was NOT fun. I did have my DHEA, pregesterone, etc. checked in the cortisol test (tho not pregnenolone); all saliva. The DHEA was high and my testosterone was too high to be measured. Lab said that happens a lot with women who take 7-keto DHEA and I was taking 100 mg daily for about 2 months. I've been doing the a.m. sunshine with BAB, but I'd forgotten the oxytocin. Thanks for that, too! (And I hate to be ignorant here, but what's BOB?)

# Thread: The tapeworms tell a story about the ecological linkage
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-tapeworms-tell-a-story-about-the-ecological-linkage.4411/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### ruby — 2012-10-20T14:11:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-tapeworms-tell-a-story-about-the-ecological-linkage.4411/post-59312>

between hominids and large carnivores that shared prey on the savannas of Africa."

Ancient hominids became infected with tapeworms after they shared food with hyenas or large cats, such as lions.

The researchers now think that T. saginata and T. asiatica diverged from a single ancestor species between 780,000 and 1.7 million years ago and that the ancestor tapeworm was already infecting humans.

T. solium seems to have an even earlier relationship with humans, having been infecting us for perhaps 2.5 million years.

[http://news.bbc.co.uk/2/hi/science/nature/2040756.stm](http://http://news.bbc.co.uk/2/hi/science/nature/2040756.stm)

### ruby — 2012-10-20T14:45:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-tapeworms-tell-a-story-about-the-ecological-linkage.4411/post-59314>

The tapeworms that live in humans feed on our food, despite the fact that they have neither a mouth nor a digestive tract. Their bodies are like a kind of inside-out intestine, rippling with finger-like projections that absorb nutrients. Once inside us, tapeworms can live for decades, deftly escaping the notice of the immune system. Some tapeworms have hooks or suckers on their front end (“head” is too generous a term), which they use to anchor themselves in place. They can also swim upstream to meet food coming out of the stomach and drift back down the intestines to feed, releasing chemicals to slow down their host’s peristalsis so that they don’t get swept away. Despite the legends of tapeworm-induced anorexia, most people have no symptoms if they are host to an **adult** tapeworm. Tapeworms are so unlike other animals, so exquisitely adapted to their own way of life, that it can be hard to imagine how they were anything other than tapeworms. The ancestors of tapeworms were free-living flatworms (platyhelminths). Many flatworms live in the ocean or in fresh water. The transition to parasitism was gradual. A group of flatworms called monogeneans are among the closest relatives of tapeworms, and instead of living inside their hosts, they mostly live on the outside. Monogeneans use suckers and hooks to clamp onto a fish. They then use their mouths to feed on the mucus or blood of their hosts. It appears the monogeneans move from fish to fish, each species of parasite living on a single species of fish host. The tree indicates that flukes and tapeworms descend from a flatworm that moved from the outside of its hosts to their inside. This happened a long time ago, judging from the fact that monogeneans, flukes, and tapeworms are found on all sorts of vertebrates except for the oldest lineages represented today by the jawless lampreys and hagfish. When jawed fish first emerged about 450 million years ago, flatworms began their invasion.

[http://scienceblogs.com/loom/2007/02/19/build-me-a-tapeworm/](http://http://scienceblogs.com/loom/2007/02/19/build-me-a-tapeworm/)


══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# Thread: blue toenails during CT
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/blue-toenails-during-ct.4415/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 6
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### ealachan — 2012-10-20T16:52:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/blue-toenails-during-ct.4415/post-59330>

So, I got back into the cold tub this morning for the first time since spring. I've been taking cold showers right along, and have been doing cold-air CT since it started getting chilly here, for the record. I actually found it quite easy to physically get back in the water (mentally was a different story...haha).

About 8 minutes into my 25-minute soak, my toenails started to turn blue, but...it was JUST the toenails on my big toes. None of my toes turned white or went numb (which I experienced a few times back when I first started CT...I suspect my o6 was quite high), and my fingernails didn't turn color at all either.

Does anyone else get ever get this? Does it mean anything (other than that you're cold)? I kept my toes out of the water and it still happened, so I don't think it's a submersion issue.

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-10-20T16:53:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/blue-toenails-during-ct.4415/post-59331>

> **ealachan;60873 wrote:** So, I got back into the cold tub this morning for the first time since spring. I've been taking cold showers right along, and have been doing cold-air CT since it started getting chilly here, for the record. I actually found it quite easy to physically get back in the water (mentally was a different story...haha).
>
>
>
> About 8 minutes into my 25-minute soak, my toenails started to turn blue, but...it was JUST the toenails on my big toes. None of my toes turned white or went numb (which I experienced a few times back when I first started CT...I suspect my o6 was quite high), and my fingernails didn't turn color at all either.
>
>
>
> Does anyone else get ever get this? Does it mean anything (other than that you're cold)? I kept my toes out of the water and it still happened, so I don't think it's a submersion issue.

Freak! omg take a pic next time. Seriously, that is strange. Makes no sense. My mermaid theory may be in play.

### ealachan — 2012-10-20T17:17:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/blue-toenails-during-ct.4415/post-59336>

I actually get the same thing, though to a much lesser degree, when my feet are just really cold. I don't have to wear shoes at work and I hardly ever wear socks, so it's not uncommon for me to get chilly feets under my desk (I work in a drafty old farmhouse). I'm just curious what the mechanism is and whether it indicates something ominous and horrible. Evil toe portents, etc.

### freesia — 2012-10-20T19:37:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/blue-toenails-during-ct.4415/post-59360>

[http://forum.jackkruse.com/showthread.php?1367-Blue-hands-when-showering-57F-and-other-problems-(-)](http://http://forum.jackkruse.com/showthread.php?1367-Blue-hands-when-showering-57F-and-other-problems-\(-\))

I had problems back when I was adapting. Reading that post I made in early May I honestly can NOT believe how far I've come over the course of a single winter. I wouldn't even describe myself as having severe ME/CFS anymore. (Still have ME but doing well with it.)

So anyway ealachan - blueness....possibly just an adaptive thing?

### ealachan — 2012-10-20T19:52:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/blue-toenails-during-ct.4415/post-59366>

Interesting, thanks! And so happy you've come such a long way!

### Patty Cakes — 2012-10-20T21:07:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/blue-toenails-during-ct.4415/post-59376>

I say just polish your toenails and you'll never see it again!!!!

Seriously isn't it just from lack of blood flow to the area. I agree that is is most likely an adaptation thing and will probably resolve itself in a short time.

# Thread: Need your best, cheapest, easiest recipes for company!
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/need-your-best-cheapest-easiest-recipes-for-company.4416/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 15
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### BJK77 — 2012-10-20T17:18:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/need-your-best-cheapest-easiest-recipes-for-company.4416/post-59337>

We're having friends over for dinner next week and I have no idea what to make. It's for a family of five and truth is they're probably going to complain no matter what I make. They are pretty picky and love to mock our eating habits. Obviously, I'd rather not spend a fortune feeding them or even waste a ton of my time just to have them goad us about our food choices.

On the flip side they desperately need a decent diet - the mother is obese (though she doesn't think so) and has had diabetes since she was 9. She doesn't take care of herself at all. All 3 kids suffer with illnesses - diabetes, asthma, severe allergies, ADD/ADHD etc, etc. I'd love to show them that eating healthy can be tasty and quite fun too.

Any suggestions?

### Melinda — 2012-10-20T17:47:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/need-your-best-cheapest-easiest-recipes-for-company.4416/post-59343>

Even though I don't think chicken is a great, when I have been in the situation of feeding a lot of ppl cheaply, I buy a large amount of chicken thighs, cut them in half-through the bone-season them and bake them until done. I lift them onto a platter and pour butter over them and let them rest. This can be finished before the guests arrive. I serve it with a salad and Trader Joe potato chips (olive oil). For dessert, I serve fruit with cream over the top. The butter on the chicken displaces the chix fat and tastes wonderful. Usually no leftovers.

### janagram — 2012-10-20T18:32:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/need-your-best-cheapest-easiest-recipes-for-company.4416/post-59350>

How about chocolate chili? (from melissa joulwan)

saute grassfed grnd beef...(however much you need-plenty!)

with onions, garlic, oregano, cumin, unsweetened chocolate (couple spoonfuls), allspice, salt, 1 (small) can tomato paste, can ch. tomotoes, 1 can beef broth, 1 c. water

simmer 2 hrs (the whole 2 hrs)

serve over rice (a lot better than what they would usually have) ...or spaghetti squash, or cauli-rice...or give a choice

### ealachan — 2012-10-20T18:47:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/need-your-best-cheapest-easiest-recipes-for-company.4416/post-59354>

If they're just going to give you crap about it anyway, I wouldn't bother wasting the money on serving them seafood that they won't appreciate. I'd do something like a beef stew, chili, or even a beef curry. There's a Rogan Josh recipe in Well Fed that is fabulous and not much work at all.

When we have guests, I usually make a big pot of beef stew in the crock pot. My dirty secret is that I use calf's heart for most of the meat in it, and I don't tell anyone. Well, except you guys, of course...

### hope2conceive — 2012-10-20T19:53:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/need-your-best-cheapest-easiest-recipes-for-company.4416/post-59367>

Eachalan, do you get your calf's heart from a farm? How long do you cook it?

### ealachan — 2012-10-20T20:00:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/need-your-best-cheapest-easiest-recipes-for-company.4416/post-59369>

> **hope2conceive;60912 wrote:** Eachalan, do you get your calf's heart from a farm? How long do you cook it?

I get it from a local farm that does grass-fed beef, yes. I can get a 3lb heart for like $4...it's a great deal! They do liver and kidneys as well, though I've been to chicken to try either of those yet.

Heart is so lean that it either needs to be barely cooked, or cooked for a REALLY long time, otherwise it gets super rubbery.

I use it in a stew application, so it cooks for a long time in the crockpot - usually 8-10 hours - and it's very tender by that time. Also, in terms of stew anyway, the heart lends it an extra beefy depth of flavor that's just unbeatable. It can be a little time-consuming to trim up, but after the first one I did, I got the hang of it.

### BJK77 — 2012-10-22T16:14:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/need-your-best-cheapest-easiest-recipes-for-company.4416/post-59613>

Thanks for the suggestions! I'm not going to waste too much time and energy since I know they'll b!@\*h about it not matter what, but I'd also like to do what I can to at least try to convince them to clean up their diet.

ealachan - I am so jealous of your 3 lb heart for $4!! At times I think I'm lucky to live in the middle of Amish country - grass-fed beef is super easy to come by, but on the flip side organ meats are highly coveted around here so I don't get the great price breaks everyone often talks about. I love, love, love beef heart and usually pay $8 lb! Now that I've been able to find shrimp and other seafood yummies for much less the heart is becoming a rare treat.

### Shijin13 — 2012-10-22T17:04:09+0300

Ok... I'm astounded at the prices of beef heart... I get mine from our Local CSA -for $1.99/lb I usually buy btwn 5-10lbs when they have it!

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/need-your-best-cheapest-easiest-recipes-for-company.4416/post-59631>

### nonchalant — 2012-10-22T18:00:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/need-your-best-cheapest-easiest-recipes-for-company.4416/post-59653>

> **Melinda;60886 wrote:** Even though I don't think chicken is a great, when I have been in the situation of feeding a lot of ppl cheaply, I buy a large amount of chicken thighs, cut them in half-through the bone-season them and bake them until done. I lift them onto a platter and pour butter over them and let them rest. This can be finished before the guests arrive. I serve it with a salad and Trader Joe potato chips (olive oil). For dessert, I serve fruit with cream over the top. The butter on the chicken displaces the chix fat and tastes wonderful. Usually no leftovers.

Melinda that sounds like a great recipe for this situation. Butter-infused chicken.

How do you cut the thighs in half? Have the butcher cut them?

### Shijin13 — 2012-10-22T18:31:51+0300

I think you can just go out and buy straight out of their freezers on Sat/Sun (they're open from 10-3 usually)

Chicama run is the name of the farm

### Melinda — 2012-10-23T02:40:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/need-your-best-cheapest-easiest-recipes-for-company.4416/post-59756>

I've got some kitchen scissors that have a cut out near the handle that will cut through the bone. I've done it with a sharp big knife: I cut down through the skin and meat and get it started in the bone then pick up the knife with the thigh hanging on and smash both down on my thick wooden cutting board. I usually get a good, clean cut. I picked up the idea from a Chinese kitchen. It's better with the bone in for flavor but our chix thights are just too big to cook efficiently and serve. Most ppl don't guess it's butter at first and it tastes wonderful.

# Thread: Epi-paleo Rx: saturated fats and DHA absorption. Let's question!
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/epi-paleo-rx-saturated-fats-and-dha-absorption-lets-question.4418/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 15
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Souldanzer — 2012-10-20T18:52:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/epi-paleo-rx-saturated-fats-and-dha-absorption-lets-question.4418/post-59357>

This is a discussion continued from Cavemam's journal:

> **Anna K.;60814 wrote: was thinking about this as well.
>
>
>
> It would be interesting to see the research that sat fat inhibit absorption... because it seems counterintuitive
>
>
> GF meat has a O6:03 ratio of roughly 2:1 (varies), apparently optimal ratio is around 4:1, seafood is way higher. Lots of sat fat in meat, not so much in fish. Lots of sat fat in breast milk (about 50% btw, our cells have about 50% sat fat depending on dietary intake).
>
>
>
> **Alternative hyperthesis: Saturated fat helps DHA absorption/utilization. Sat fat could potentially be a regulator of DHA absorption?**
>
>
>
> Humans need DHA, it's essential for brain health and development. Fish has lots and lots of DHA and little sat fat, there is plenty of DHA to be absorbed no matter the amount of sat fat present.
>
> GF meat has lesser DHA (yet still a higher than "optimal" ratio of O6:03) yet lots and lots of sat fat. Humans need DHA, it's essential for brain health and development. Therefore, saturated fat improves DHA absorption b/c the lesser the content in the food eaten, the better absorption should be, if evolution got it right.
>
>
>
>
>
> Ok, just for fun, somebody proof me wrong  I just made this up.
>
>
>
> There IS a confounding factor in meat and that is the 06 content. 06 can mess with 03 absorption b/c the body utilizes it though the same enzymatic mechanisms. So if you drench your meat or fish in 06 oil, most of those enzymes will probably be occupied with 06 and not 03 just b/c of amount present.
>
>
>
> http://www.nutritionj.com/content/9/1/10/figure/F1
>
> Omega-9 fatty acids
>
>
>
> What are they?
>
>
>
> Omega-9 fatty acids are from a family of unsaturated fats that commonly are found in vegetable and animal fats. This monounsaturated fat is described as omega-9 because the double bond is in the ninth position from the omega end. These fatty acids also are known as oleic acids or monounsaturated fats and can often be found in canola, sunflower, olive and nut oils. Unlike omega-3 and omega-6 fatty acids, omega-9 fatty acids are produced by the body, but also are beneficial when they are obtained in food.
>
>
>
> What are the types of omega-9 fatty acids?
>
>
>
> Oleic acid - Oleic acid is a main component of canola oil, sunflower oil, olive oil, and other monounsaturated fats, many of which are used as a solution for reducing bad fats in cooking oils.
>
> What are the sources of omega-9 fatty acids?
>
>
>
> Oleic acid â€“ Olive oil, canola oil, sunflower oil, peanuts, pistachios, almonds, and avocados**

Oppsie. Now olive oil interferes with absorption of DHA b/c it could potentially occupy the desaturase enzymes. Yet nature put a regulating mechanism in there..... omega 3 is preferred, then omega 6, then omega 9. Your body would still naturally seek out the omega 3's no matter what you drown your fish and meat in.

Hmmm. Dare I link this site? This issue has been discussed before. Nice ancient study in rats linked somewhere in the answers.

[http://paleohacks.com/questions/111820/saturated-fat-interferes-with-essential-fatty-acid-metabolism#axzz29mPcOD8o](http://http://paleohacks.com/questions/111820/saturated-fat-interferes-with-essential-fatty-acid-metabolism#axzz29mPcOD8o)

http:/http://www.westonaprice.org/know-your-fats/reply-to-ray-peat-on-efa-deficiency

> Finally, it should be stressed that certain components of the diet actually reduce (but do not eliminate) our requirements for EFAs. The main one is saturated fatty acids which help us conserve EFAs and put them in the tissues where they belong. Some studies indicate that vitamin B6 can ameliorate the problems caused by EFA deficiency, possibly by helping us use them more efficiently.

My earlier made-up hypothesis is coming to life a bit. I'm going to remove this from Cavemam's journal b/c I'm hijacking. What kind of enzymes are used to absorb sat fat? Same as omegas?

**Proposed new hypothesis: Our bodies have integrated regulator mechanisms for best absorption of proper fatty acid ratios. Those work unless we heavily focus on one particular fatty acid (as in SAD and too much O6), then they get messed up.**

So don't drown yourself in O6, don't drown yourself in O3, don't drown yourself in O9 (olive oil & avos), and don't drown yourself in coconut oil either. Maybe Enig got it right when she said "do not use one oil exclusively, instead, use a variety of oils for good health".

Now, that I've done an unreal amount of reading on this issue, I gotta say I'm not a fan of oils in general. I tend to think we should eat foods in their whole form. Avocado instead of avocado oil, olives instead of olive oil, coconuts instead of coconut oil etc. That really leaves very little options for "pure fat". You can find it in suet and pork belly and such. Back to using animal fats?

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/epi-paleo-rx-saturated-fats-and-dha-absorption-lets-question.4418/post-59378>

> Proposed new hypothesis: Our bodies have integrated regulator mechanisms for best absorption of proper fatty acid ratios. Those work unless we heavily focus on one particular fatty acid (as in SAD and too much O6), then they get messed up.
>

>
> So don't drown yourself in O6, don't drown yourself in O3, don't drown yourself in O9 (olive oil & avos), and don't drown yourself in coconut oil either. Maybe Enig got it right when she said "do not use one oil exclusively, instead, use a variety of oils for good health".
>

> Now, that I've done an unreal amount of reading on this issue, I gotta say I'm not a fan of oils in general. I tend to think we should eat foods in their whole form. Avocado instead of avocado oil, olives instead of olive oil, coconuts instead of coconut oil etc. That really leaves very little options for "pure fat". You can find it in suet and pork belly and such. Back to using animal fats?

Unfortunately, I also have the labs to back this up. After almost a year of focusing heavily on coconut oil for it's purported healing benefits, and a strong emphasis on seafood (I'm 3 months longer into the epi-paleo experiment then you all b/c I started earlier based on my own research of what I needed to eat for optimal health), Spectracell complained about my oleic acid level being low. CO only has 6% oleic acid, while animal fat has much more. Considering the absorption efficiency, our bodies are not build to absorb O9 efficiently. We are build to absorb O3 efficiently. Two reasons come to mind: 1. Omega 3 is super important. 2. Omega 3 might be available in limited quantities. Probably both. I think I recall a third safety mechanism: Humans burn the longest fatty acids last, which is why some people will experience fat loss when using CO instead of other oils. Nature is sparing O3 for other purposes?

Looking at fatty acid profiles of animal fats, they look rather attractive with a nice distribution of fatty acids.

### Zorica Vuletic — 2012-10-22T03:50:07+0300
