# Thread: Let's talk canned fish
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lets-talk-canned-fish.4449/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 86
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-10-22T17:30:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lets-talk-canned-fish.4449/post-59640>

So I just don't believe canned fish is worth the effort.

I know "jack said" etc, but everything I look at indicated the high temps needed for canning would effectively bastardize everything good about it, except protein molecules.

discuss?

QUESTION EVERYTHING!

### Michael — 2012-10-22T17:53:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lets-talk-canned-fish.4449/post-59651>

My feeling is that fresh would be best, but I don't eat that way. I don't always have the time to get it or cook it. (I get 30 minutes for lunch.)

Actually, I think best would be if one could pull it out the river and cook it on the bank rather than going to a fishmonger.

I think something of the same as regards vegetables. If I had a garden or allotment I'd grow high quality organic produce using heirloom varieties. I'd go out and pick them minutes before eating them. I'd have fruit in proper fruit cages, and I'd allow it to ripen on the plant before picking it. (For supermarkets, they pick 'em unripe and allow them to "ripen" in storage.)

The gap between what most of us can do and what would be ideal seems not negligible. The same for how every man, woman and child would have eaten in the best pre-modern environment.

I do the best I can. I shop almost every day as it is. I have to cut some corners. If people can do better than what I do, I'd say go for it.

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-10-22T18:31:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lets-talk-canned-fish.4449/post-59659>

> **Michael;61218 wrote:** My feeling is that fresh would be best, but I don't eat that way. I don't always have the time to get it or cook it. (I get 30 minutes for lunch.)
>
>
>
> Actually, I think best would be if one could pull it out the river and cook it on the bank rather than going to a fishmonger.
>
>

> I think something of the same as regards vegetables. If I had a garden or allotment I'd grow high quality organic produce using heirloom varieties. I'd go out and pick them minutes before eating them. I'd have fruit in proper fruit cages, and I'd allow it to ripen on the plant before picking it. (For supermarkets, they pick 'em unripe and allow them to "ripen" in storage.)
>
>
>
> The gap between what most of us can do and what would be ideal seems not negligible. The same for how every man, woman and child would have eaten in the best pre-modern environment.
>
>
>
> I do the best I can. I shop almost every day as it is. I have to cut some corners. If people can do better than what I do, I'd say go for it.

Ok, let me try again - perhaps up in Vancouver this read differently

Other than "Jack said", I see nothing but examples that all the fishy goodness is destroyed in the canning process and it's only contribution is it's a fatty carb-free protein food. People seem to be approaching it from the Jack-stated-view of "all fish is better than everything else"

Not to mention the high heat very well may oxidize and make the fat in the fish inflammatory. People take such care to avoid rancid and oxydized oils - what makes canned fish end up as a net positive?

I am questioning that. If all the fishy goodness is destroyed in canning, then why does Jack say it's better than all non seafood foodstuffs.

The more you know, the better you do!

### Michael — 2012-10-22T19:12:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lets-talk-canned-fish.4449/post-59663>

Well that's what I thought. I'm not saying anyome has to agree with any of it.

### Inger — 2012-10-22T19:52:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lets-talk-canned-fish.4449/post-59670>

I think Jack does not mean it like that...hmmm. You need to feel he's spirit, then you understand. It is important to not get stuck in phrases people say, this is just my own Nr.1.

Words.. they are just words.. they can be doing harm if people get stuck in them and does not see the spirit.. I am getting more and more aware of this issue.

I do not eat canned fish ever except in emergency situations. Why, when fresh is so much better? Jack says fresh and raw is best. Everyone has to choose for themselves how "good" one wants to get. I am not doing the best I could eather, I take it quite easy I know things I could still do better..but I never complain if I am not seeing so fast progress..lol cause then.. I also know too well why! There is always something to improve that is for sure.

We can go as slow as we want just no need to complain then..huh..

I am seeing it like that, Jack is not really recommending canned fish, but he tells it is much better then some other common Paleo foods!

What he really recommends.. is MHS and such..lol Cavemam, have you tried that? Since you kind of take Jacks recommandation to your heart it seems?

The most practical thing to take for lunch ever, is fresh raw salmon cut into pieces and then add fresh grated ginger, a little raw honey, applecider vinegar / lime juice / vinegar, seasalt, chopped raw onions and maybe cilantro or dill if desired. I often make it ready in the morning for lunch at work. I add chopped dried seaweeds too. I just put it into a glass jar and eat directly from the jar when lunchtime. Boah... it is totally delish...!!! And makes one so nicely satiated! I use to make it from one pound salmon and I usually eat all in one setting.

And hmmm.. one more thing.. I would not take the words of anyone without thinking of myself. I would not feel too great from most of the recipes in Jack cookbook..lol I just feel best from raw and quite plain foods. That is why I do not buy it (I might if I had money just to support him for sure. I am so very thankful for all his help!). I need fishhead smoothies and such to feel real good - that broken is this lady..huh! I would never go and eat canned fish because "Jack says so". He is no Guru and I am sure he want not to be eather. He want us to think ourselves.

I am rather thinking he does not say loud all strange foods he thinks is healthy because not to offend people. People get offended and escape when it goes too hard, like the MHS. That is sad but true. Jack is wise and does not do it that way because he knows he can help more with beeing a bit easier on the food. That is my guess.

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-10-22T20:22:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lets-talk-canned-fish.4449/post-59678>

Inger - good point, I think we agree actually!

BUT - sometimes the devil IS in the details (like with grains). I know many people on here (myself included) have ramped up our canned fish consumption - probably to the exclusion of some more nourishing options. I would like to explore if indeed canned fish has any fishy-goodness intact.

If it doesn't, then I would make different choices. . . .

so I ask the crowd - anybody got any info one way or the other on if the canning process destorys the nutrients and overheats the oils, thus oxidizing them and rendering them inflammatory?

Jack? What info did you have to make your conclusion? If someone was going to take things based only on "saw it in a blog post from Jack", they probably would never have made it this far in their journey (to be reading and interacting on these forums).

Here I go again, questions questions. . .

### kathylu — 2012-10-22T20:45:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lets-talk-canned-fish.4449/post-59681>

> **Cavemam;61246 wrote:** so I ask the crowd - anybody got any info one way or the other on if the canning process destorys the nutrients and overheats the oils, thus oxidizing them and rendering them inflammatory?
>
>
>
> Jack? What info did you have to make your conclusion? If someone was going to take things based only on "saw it in a blog post from Jack", they probably would never have made it this far in their journey (to be reading and interacting on these forums).
>
>
>
> Here I go again, questions questions. . .

I took a quick look for some data on this topic and can't find a whole lot. To summarize, "normal" cooking methods, i.e. poaching, grilling, steaming, baking do not appreciably alter the fatty acids or phospholipids in fresh fish. Frying is at such a high temp that damage can occur, so best to avoid that. Canning is a relatively low heat process, certainly less heat that baking, so I wouldn't worry too much. Oils become "rancid" not so much from heat but oxidation, which takes exposure to oxygen and time, though heat can certainly speed up the process. Some oils are quite resistant to oxidation, coconut for instance and it is true that fish oils (O3's) are subject to faster oxidation. However, there is very little oxygen in canned products, so again, I don't think it's a huge concern. My opinion is that the nutritional quality of canned fish would be quite similar to cooked fish.

However, I agree with you...canned fish certainly does not taste as good as freshly prepared fish! I do use it for convenience though.

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lets-talk-canned-fish.4449/post-59721>

Oh, how I love the internet! Here's a description of fish canning...most fish is processed at 121 degrees centigrade for about 10 minutes. That's rather gentle heat, less than steaming, baking or broiling.

Here's a general description of meat canning (which includes fish). Lots of pictures...knock yourself out!

[http://www.fao.org/docrep/010/ai407e/AI407E22.htm](http://http://www.fao.org/docrep/010/ai407e/AI407E22.htm)

### JanSz — 2012-10-23T00:19:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lets-talk-canned-fish.4449/post-59723>

On canned seafood.

I hope the can lining will do no harm.

Seafood with water is probably ok to eat.

Seafood with anything else than water needs study.

I am looking at my can of oysters in cottonseed oil.

Why do I eat oysters--- because of omega3, specially DHA + good incidentals, zinc, etc.

but

cottonseed oil is devoid of Omega3, it is pure Omega6 (how could they do that to me).

When I open the can, I will drain all oil, replace with olive or coconut oils.

\---------------

When I think seafood, I think predators and their place on a food chain.

Salmon is too high for my taste. Also other bigger fish.

Not really sure how this thinking would fare with bottom feeders, flounder, halibut.

Probably biggest would be a cod, sea bass, that would be ok to eat.

Yest to mackerel no to salmon.

Anyhow, saltwater fish is a secondary food, primarily we should concentrate on shelfish.

......................................................................

healthy ratios of n−6:n−3 range from 1:1 to 1:4 (an individual needs more n−3 than n−6)

Typical Western diets provide ratios of between 10:1 and 30:1 (i.e., dramatically higher levels of n−6 than n-3)

The ratios of n−6 to n−3 fatty acids in some common vegetable oils are:

**this three oils are acceptable

canola 2:1

olive 3-13:1

flax 1:3

this oils should not be eaten**

soybean 7:1

sunflower (no n−3)

cottonseed (almost no n−3)

peanut (no n−3)

grapeseed oil (almost no n−3)

corn oil 46:1 ratio of n−6 to n−3

.

### kathylu — 2012-10-23T04:16:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lets-talk-canned-fish.4449/post-59773>

Sorry I wasn't clear. Broiling, not boiling. Steam under pressure can get much hotter than 100 degrees C. Commercial canning is done in large autoclaves with steam under pressure. The temperature for commercial canning processes was chosen in order to kill the spores of clostridium species.

We old folk still use centigrade and celsius interchangeably.

### nonchalant — 2012-10-23T21:07:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lets-talk-canned-fish.4449/post-59887>

I think canned seafood is great as an introduction to eating seafood regularly. It was by eating canned sardines, salmon and tuna (with frozen shrimp and an occasional frozen fish fillet) that I learned to start liking seafood more than once a month or so. And I can send sardines in the mail.

I still eat canned sardines about once a month, and the other day I actually had tuna (but it was expensive tuna in a bpa free can and soy free.)

But now I'm starting to crave raw oysters once a week, though part of me still wonders why. I don't particularly love the taste. But they're ok.

I really like the frozen 1 pound bags of mixed squid, mussel, octopus, and shrimp at $3.99 each. For the last couple of months frozen 1/2 pound skin-on filets of wild sockeye salmon have been available at $9.99/lb. The cheapest I can find raw wild sockeye filets online from Alaska is 13.50/lb.

### Jude — 2012-10-29T03:26:20+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lets-talk-canned-fish.4449/post-60664>

My n=1 since Sept 1:

Daily has been 75% canned sardines/salmon, 20% fresh lightly cooked fish and 5% grass fed lamb. So, almost 2 months on tinned, wild Atlantic Canadian fish. Decided to go with Dr K's recommendation that it won't hurt you, and just did it. During that time I did one week exclusively on tinned sardines, the second on tinned and fresh cooked. I'm alive and well, in fact I completely stopped my ubiquitous palpitations with that week of tinned sardines! So for me, tinned fish has helped me have a fantastic win health wise.

Anna K: Only get fresh fish weekly here, no MH. all expensive..prawns and salmon 33-36$ kilo but I supplemented with these.

### Jonathin — 2012-10-30T19:59:50+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lets-talk-canned-fish.4449/post-60972>

Regarding cottonseed oil:

Cotton is not a food crop. This means that the chemicals used on cotton are not regulated as with a food crop. This means cottonseed meal (used for cow feed) and cottonseed oil can have residues not found in food crops. Also there is a natural component of cottonseed oil that is not healthful for humans. Sorry I don't have the info on the details.

BPA is not good but it is everywhere. Touched a cash register receipt lately? Non-BPA substitutes for cans are not necessarily better just because they are not BPA. BPA has been around since the '30s and just now a problem is perceived! There are a thousand things to be concerned about regarding the food processing industry but I am not sure that any of it is stopping us from getting healthier but probably optimally healthy.

### JackStapleton — 2012-10-31T00:58:31+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lets-talk-canned-fish.4449/post-61010>

Regarding intuition: everybody has it, in some it is underdeveloped. Because of the way I was brought up, mine was completely atrophied, but I trained myself to get it back and now I am considered by friends to be some kind of psychic genius. It just a matter of trusting that everything you need to know is instantly available and accepting the guidance when it comes. Also, be assured that if anyone else can do it, you can too. We are all the same at our core. Sometimes you can get more intuitive just by imagining that you are "borrowing" the powers of someone who has them. The answer will always be provided. The only impediment is trusting it when it comes. In most cases, people who try will get an answer but then immediately shut it out. Sometimes that is because if you open yourself to this kind of guidance, you will get messages you don't want to hear and a defensive reaction is to shut out all such info (as a way to maintain the illusion of control of your life).

Inger is correct in saying that someone with a history of a junk food diet could experience amazing benefits from canned fish if they cut out the bad stuff at the same time. So that would seem to indicate that canned fish might be great for them. But it is also clear that the fresh stuff is way better. It really doesn't matter what the researchers and scientists can prove. You just know, don't you?

If you don't trust that you know, hold some fresh fish in one hand a canned fish in the other. Your body will speak to you about the relative benefits of each. But you don't really need to hold the actual fish, you can just imagine doing so. Same results, unless you haven't learned to trust your intuition.

I would eat both. Of course. And the grassfed beef only raw and lots of different organs. Brain too. All raw. Additional canned fish. The best quality I could afford of course. And then I would go out and look for nettles etc. and wild berries for free and eat lots a lots of seaweeds too. Seaweeds raw if possible (avoid to heat it), to get some viruses in from the sea..  And then to get some dirt in like dirty raw carrots. I think I would feel great!

### Jonathin — 2012-10-31T17:32:53+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lets-talk-canned-fish.4449/post-61095>

> **Anna K.;62683 wrote:** Jonathin, how non-bpa substitues are worse then bpa? what do you mean? thanks.

I didn't say they were worse. The fact that they are not BPA does not mean that they are necessarily more benign. One would have to test and confirm the properties of any new manmade material that is in intimate contact with food or contained within (ie - ag chemicals, pollutants). Don't hold your breathe waiting for that to happen in a timely manner!

### Inger — 2012-10-31T19:18:16+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lets-talk-canned-fish.4449/post-61120>

> **Anna K.;62800 wrote:** thanks Inger, what do you do with nettles?

Anna, you can just crush them and eat them plain raw, or make a smoothie from nettles and water and drink it (that is what I like to do once in a while, I keep forgetting though..), or use as spinach! Fry in butter or whatever you'd like. One could make nettle soup with an cooked egg as condiment, we ate that as kids often. Just cook and puree nettles with water and onion, add spices you like and some cream or butter or other fat and enjoy!

You can use the "flowers" too, very healthy fats in there!

### JackStapleton — 2012-10-31T19:37:16+0200

Anna,

Sounds like Dr. Inger has some good advice for you. My intuition says that you have a very active mind, always asking very many questions. Quiet and meditation would help you to still your mind and take you to a place where you could hear messages from the silence. From the depths of your true self, you might find that fresh beef is good one day in the summer and something else for a different day in the winter.

I agree with you about eating the frozen thawed fish raw. I guess I am lucky to have fresh wild-caught fish available every day and sushi in every grocery store. If I didn't, I am sure my intuition would lead me to move to where I did!

Anna, I am also sending you a message that you will feel. It is not with words, so you will not be able to understand it with your mind. You will have doubts that you got it - that is your mind - but you will get it. When you feel it, even if your mind isn't sure you did, the feeling will take you to the place of your intuition. Enjoy it. It is a fun place. The more you visit, the more it will feel like home. It feels a bit like love.

JJack

> **Anna K.;62788 wrote:** JJack, Life is often not so simple. what is fresh fish? the fish that you caught yourself? sure, my intuition tells me it's the best!
>
>
>
> But the fish you buy in a supermarket is guaranteed not to be fresh but previously frozen (often for many months) before it it defrozen and put on display and it stays there for days slowly degrading. Now my intuition is telling me not to eat it raw.
>
>
>
> I'd say about 80% of people here on this forum don't have access to freshly caught fish or simply can't afford it all the time, if it is indeed fresh. So I'd say it again, we are trying to figure out if canned fish is better then fresh beef, for example.
>
>
>
> JJack, Should we eat canned fish or grass-fed beef? what's your intuition is telling you?

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-10-31T22:51:08+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lets-talk-canned-fish.4449/post-61157>

for the record, I don't think there has been anybody yet to disagree that Fresh fish is best.

What isn't clear is - is canned always the better choice. Let's take a forum favorite - canned sardines. The issues we have are:

1\. Heat- it's not superheated it seems, but it is cooked which in theory does degrade the epi goodness.

2\. Canning cooties, bpa and whatever else if it's in oil. If you can't find non bpa fish, might there be better choices?

3\. Profit. (hehehe internet joke for the cool kids

Jack put forth the idea that all seafood no matter what is always better than any other food. To me, that seems like a generalization to make a point rather than a hard/fast rule to live by.

Can we suss out how much fishy goodness we are gettin from a sardine? Same as 2 shrimp? Same as 1 shrimp etc. . .

### kathylu — 2012-10-31T22:58:48+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lets-talk-canned-fish.4449/post-61160>

I definitely nosh on shrimp more often than sardines. Shrimp taste better imo and rank #2 (crustaceans) on Jack's list of epi-paleo wonderfulness.

Lol.. sometimes I wonder what is the purpose with this speculations.. to me they would add only unneccesary stress and NOT contribute to my healing.. If I have a better choise, I do that. I I have NOT I do the best I can and do not speculate about that further. I just not have the time. Time is very high value to me. Very much. I do not want to waste it.

If I se oysters in the fish counter I buy them for sure! I leave other shopping like clothes etc. Money can always be spend on what we want, we can live without a lot of stuff we thought we could not.

What I know for sure is, if eating grassfed beef it is much more beneficial if eaten raw, and also more all kind of different organs, then cooking it and doing just muscle meats. I just know it by my body. But I am thinking many does not even want to consider that.. or trying high meat.. or wild edibles.. or lots of seaweeds.. but same time complain about having no access to fresh seafood... we need to broaden our minds much.. or that is what I feel at least.

And.. If one starts to look and ask for things.. it is amazing what one could find! This is my own Nr.1. It is just..wow!

There are many roads to Rome.

And then.. lol.. I wonder if I totally talk past you now Cavemam..or misunderstood what you intended to say. I apologise if it so.

Oh.. and one more thing come into my mind. I kind of have the feeling they are adding someting into the precooked/prefrozen shrimps here. I kind of swell a little up from them I have the feeling at least. I guess they add some weird salt or phosphate or some stuff. I have not access to fresh. I eat the shrimps seldom. But if, always with head and all. If they were fresh I would eat them more often I guess.. I need to investigate this issue further.

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-11-01T16:37:10+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lets-talk-canned-fish.4449/post-61281>

> **Inger;62994 wrote:** Lol.. sometimes I wonder what is the purpose with this speculations..
>
>
>
> There are many roads to Rome.
>
>
>
> And then.. lol.. I wonder if I totally talk past you now Cavemam..or misunderstood what you intended to say. I apologise if it so.

I think Inger, that your obvious and genuine happy levels are an inspiration - although I do not think everyone is built to have them the same way. Check what I posted over on the happy thread.

There are two things for consideration in understanding happy and how it relates to meditation. (and the incorrect assumption that everyone can/has the ability to meditate and respond spiritually).

1\. [http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Monoamine\_oxidase\_A](http://http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Monoamine_oxidase_A) This is ONE discovery, fairly recent, I'm sure there will be more.

2\. VMAT2 is a vesicular monoamine transporter that regulates the flow of mood-altering chemicals in the brain - commonly refered to as either the god gene or the meditation gene. See this interesting page and video: [http://www.thoughtsinperspective.com...c-warfare.html](http://http://www.thoughtsinperspective.com...c-warfare.html)

So the expression of these genes that one has are a very large influence on if you are religious, spiritual, meditative etc and recieve the positive brain reactions to those things. Some get it more, some get it less. Meditation only works on those that have a high expression of these.

In addition, It's likely many people have differing levels of damage to HC neurons.

I observe that nature has not made us all the same, and this surely applies to how we think and how our brains work. I bet there is a steady mix of homosapiens with both the bliss capability as well as the ones who's brains are wired to analyze and walk down the analyzing path.

Just like with an addict who goes over the edge to keep having their bliss, I'm sure there are some analyzers who go over the edge to their detriment. I think we've seen some of those on here, too.

I am at a place where this analysis is NOT debilitating - it's more information that my mind needs to keep moving forward on my health path. It's energizing, not draining.

### Stavity — 2012-11-01T16:58:25+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lets-talk-canned-fish.4449/post-61286>

Cavemam, are you saying that moving forward with the best information is energizing? I agree to a degree (hehehe!!) I have been studying nutrition for 20 years now and most of the information is garbage and I hit many walls.. What amazes me is the information I have gotten from this blog in a short time has been exhilarating, but there comes a point I need to just "do" instead of analyzing everything. Now is the time to use my intuition and feel with all my senses in order to move on. Someone once told me to "keep it simple stupid." Wow how that fits me. But, no joke, in order for me to move on I will have to take the time to meditate so that not all is lost and I am able to develop what I have learned so far. I feel disease runs skin, organs, mind/spiritual and in order to heal it's the reverse. If my mind heals/clears then I can work on the rest when the time comes.

I love your mind and read most of what you write with enthusiasm because of the person you are. I would take nothing away from your experience and only learn from having taken the time to read what you and others have to say. So personally, while information is power, I am kind of tired and just need to fully implement what I have learned thus far. I am probably way off base in my observation but I just wanted you to know you are appreciated here and I wish I had your mind at your age.

If I'm not learning new things all the time, I'm bored out of my gourd, which in turn leads me down the path to eventual depression. I can (and do) get a lot out of mindfulness meditation, observing silence, going with the flow, etc...but I also respect the part of my brain that is always saying "why? why? why?" It's who I am, and rather than try to shut it up for the sake of making life easier, I am learning to strike a good balance between "learning time" and "quiet time".

Also, having said that, I REALLY wish I'd have come to this realization about 20 years ago...LOL

But what I have recogniced is, if I eat just plain lamb roast and cooked vegetables with family.. yeah, it sounds very healthy, or? But for me, it makes my spirit a bit less light.. it is totally hard to describe but a certain heaviness appears that is not there had I had it raw. Lightness is like happiness for me too. The happier I feel the lighter I feel. IDK.. sometimes I feel like almost flying. When I feel real good and I have been strict with my food choises. Now.. I feel that way when in love too..lol But it also happends with right foods when not in love.

When eating certain cooked seafood the difference is small, I can not really notice any, with one cooked seafood meal/day. Also, if drinking HWC, or too much coffee.. or anything else that is not optimal for me like many not good quality almonds with butter (I love this combination..). I just feel less happy/vibrant. I know it because I have tried it many times you see. But I am not so willing to "try" it often anymore because I prefer to feel better.

Sometimes I think happiness feels like.. power too? It feels like, if the whole world fall into pieces around me it would not matter at all. It is kind of peace and power together. Hmm.

Why I think I am broken.. to me as long as something is not optimal it is broken. Easy...that is how I think. Maybe it is weird? IDK.. I am far from optimal, I know many things that I can improve on. My mind, my body.

There is so much to improve still! And I have the amazing days and I have the days when I feel a bit less good. The amazing days shows me where I am going. But not every day is like that yet.

Now.. I am wondering if the "crazy healthy foods" in a raw state makes my body/mind open up in a certain way, and that is what makes the joyful feeling? I am thinking it could very well be so? This happiness do feels very much like openness.. like sucking in life and beeing a part of everything. I just enjoy the people around me immensely, the nature too.. it is weird.. lol

Maybe it is all in my mind. Maybe I have made up my own rules about what makes me happy lol. But fact is, all raw stuff have lots of live viruses and bacterias in them still.. and more of certain vitamins.. and raw heart is the bomb for Q10. Q10 is sensible to heat...

All this just confirms what I am feeling.

### Souldanzer — 2012-11-01T20:02:03+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lets-talk-canned-fish.4449/post-61349>

Believing something makes you feel good will make you in fact feel good. Placebo effect.

Satisfaction is a very very nice feeling. You know how great I feel after cleaning the entire house spot-less? Accomplished. Satisfied. GOOD! Did the action of cleaning my house do anything particularly good for the health of my body? Probably not other than a bit of a workout maybe. And some sort of zen effect about having a spot-less environment.

BUT - I feel terrific when it's done. I do it just for that purpose. In fact, I love cleaning house for the sole purpose of feeling great. After. A lot of the time, the more undesirable the task itself, the more satisfaction after it has been completed. In my experience. I feel even more terrific if others admire my accomplishment of my super-clean house.

### ealachan — 2012-11-01T20:41:17+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lets-talk-canned-fish.4449/post-61371>

Inger, look at it this way - if what you do makes YOU happy, makes YOU feel good, and it's not hurting yourself or anyone else, then keep right on doing it. It doesn't matter what anyone else thinks about it, in the long run. Your life is yours, no one else's.

### Souldanzer — 2012-11-01T20:52:07+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lets-talk-canned-fish.4449/post-61374>

I'm saying that there technically could be an entirely different reason for you feeling great when you eat the way you say makes you feel great than the actual nutritional effect of the food.

The thing that doesn't feel good is that no one wants to have their bubble burst. Yet there is many many people who are entirely happy on a different than Inger's diet ("sub-optimal" diet). Which disproves your theory of your food choices being the sole/major factor in your happiness.

Equating food choices with happiness - that's the part that makes my gut act up. Look at the monks that Cavemam cited - they eat a veggie cooked diet and they live in bliss. B/c they don't equate their food choices with happiness. They equate it with time spent in mediation and prayer.

Cognitive dissonance is one of the most stressful internal states of humans. Remove it and you will be happier. Remove it and you will be healthier. You have removed the stress of knowing you eat junk by eating foods you believe healthier - you feel less stressed, you feel happier. Now if those foods actually made a difference or if it was your belief in those foods - that I yet need to see answered.

Not saying you should stop doing whatever you are doing - I believe every one person should do what makes them happy. Whether that is eating Inger's diet or cleaning house - who really cares?

Monks lived a very healthy life all their life Soul. You forget a lot of things here. And of course they are not stressed like normal people working long days and having destroyed their health/brains with modern luxuries like Jack said. They are in a totally different place.

I FOR SURE CAN TELL YOU, WITH COGNITIVE DISSONANCE NOT EVEN THE HEALTHIEST FOOD WILL HELP YOU!

You are absolutely right here. But to remove the dissonance, AND eat healthy foods.. that would be the most healing thing!

I am not seeing why these needs to be separated? Weird thinking to me. Why must we always separate everytning? Like.. this OR that.. When we could have it ALL...?

### Souldanzer — 2012-11-01T22:49:09+0200

### PrimalPam — 2012-11-02T00:22:58+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lets-talk-canned-fish.4449/post-61421>

It seems to me that Souldanzer, you get an oxytocin release from doing things that make you feel good, like getting your house clean. So what you're saying is, regardless of what food you eat, when you do certain things, they make you feel good? Again, I believe it's oxytocin.

And Inger, I believe you're saying that it IS the food you eat that makes you feel good. That by eating a SAD diet, no matter what you do, you do not feel good. But when you eat epi-Paleo, you feel good, which inspires you to do other things that make you feel good.

Maybe a chicken-egg thing?

For me, I do not feel good on a SAD diet. Period. Doesn't matter what I do in my life, how many friends I have, how well I'm doing professionally, personally. When I eat garbage, I feel like garbage. And literally, almost the moment it goes down my throat.

But if a person's BG axis is well, and their circadian rhythms are aligned, etc., then sure, they can eat some garbage and do some things that make them feel good, and they will be happy. Not for most of us who find these forums, and definitely not for me. I have to eat good, then I feel good, then I want to do good. But for each his own.

### JackStapleton — 2012-11-02T04:03:02+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lets-talk-canned-fish.4449/post-61478>

> **Souldanzer;63174 wrote:** maybe it would be a good idea to stick with "I" statements.......

Souldanzer,

I don't know you but I notice you seem to post a lot on this forum.

I would guess by your comments that you have a personal history of bullying, either you were bullied yourself, or maybe you are a bully, or maybe you come from a family or environment in which bullying is seen as normal and acceptable.

I come from an experience in which bullying is to be called out for what it is and addressed. This usually leads to attacks from the bullyer. In other words, more bullying. Still, it is important to do.

I read Jack Kruse's comment as affirming and encouraging. Very positive and beautiful. You chose to nitpick about a pronoun. This comes across as an attack on both Jack and Inger. I am guessing that you cannot see that. As far as accuracy goes, I am in complete agreement with the feeling that Jack expressed, so his use of the "we" is accurate since it includes more than Jack himself and therefore the "I" usage is not necessary. I assume many others may agree with Jack's sentiment. It would be helpful if you could try to feel what he means instead of criticizing insignificant details of his sentiment.

Your comment adds nothing to the discussion, but since you have made 3,764 posts since March, I am guessing that you do not censor your impulse to reply very much. It is likely that you do not care how your comments come across and will lash out at anyone who tells you what they feel about them, as I am trying to do, and as you just did in your reply to Jack's request that you not be so quick to add snarky comments to heartfelt ones.

This comment is meant to be helpful.

JJack

### KiwiLauren — 2012-11-02T04:36:40+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lets-talk-canned-fish.4449/post-61483>

I want to believe that there is room for everyone on this forum. That we don't have to agree... and that we are all entitled to our 'off' moments. I think it's vitally important that we treat each other with kindness. We are all different - and different versions of 'broken' to quote Quelsen's great description - and we know that this can be an uphill battle for many, myself included. I know I am capable of a post that I later regret. I really hope that someone would be kind enough to point that out to me; I also hope that there would not be shaming involved, nor outing of sensitive issues in a public way.

Whether or not I agree with Dr Kruse is not the point. I have learned a great deal from Inger and I adore her honest, open ways of discussing what she has discovered. She has inspired me. I have also learned a great deal from Soul and I adore her ability to persist when the going is tough, to keep questioning even when the entire world doubts her. She has also inspired me.

It was suggested by Dr Kruse to 'gloss over' things one does not like. That door swings both ways. Both JJack and Dr Kruse missed an opportunity to gloss over what they dislike. This feels very unkind, not helpful.

Optimal (and the road thereof) is not perfection... none of us attain that no matter how good our labs look. We all get it wrong sometimes. (I'm guessing someone is going to explain why I've got it wrong now.) I suppose what I'm really saying is that this community, comprised solely of people I don't know, is important to me. I'd like to find a way for us all to share and learn, question and challenge, fall down and get back up, and still treat each other well.

### Zorica Vuletic — 2012-11-05T15:02:46+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lets-talk-canned-fish.4449/post-62195>

> **Stavity;63018 wrote:** Cavemam, are you saying that moving forward with the best information is energizing? I agree to a degree (hehehe!!) I have been studying nutrition for 20 years now and most of the information is garbage and I hit many walls.. What amazes me is the information I have gotten from this blog in a short time has been exhilarating, but there comes a point I need to just "do" instead of analyzing everything. Now is the time to use my intuition and feel with all my senses in order to move on. Someone once told me to "keep it simple stupid." Wow how that fits me. But, no joke, in order for me to move on I will have to take the time to meditate so that not all is lost and I am able to develop what I have learned so far. I feel disease runs skin, organs, mind/spiritual and in order to heal it's the reverse. If my mind heals/clears then I can work on the rest when the time comes.
>
>
>
> I love your mind and read most of what you write with enthusiasm because of the person you are. I would take nothing away from your experience and only learn from having taken the time to read what you and others have to say. So personally, while information is power, I am kind of tired and just need to fully implement what I have learned thus far. I am probably way off base in my observation but I just wanted you to know you are appreciated here and I wish I had your mind at your age.

For me, I think epi-paleo is the most intuitive thing and is the best thing to work for me in all my life (whether I was looking at food as health or not). I know Jack was hinting at seafood for a while leading up to July...and I ate some seafood throughout for sure. But when July hit and I decided to eat seafood every day and also eat seaweeds, it felt like a miracle. I can't see myself eating any other way ever again. I don't like looking at hunter gatherers from paleolithic era...when they were deciding to branch out. Sure that works, but for me seeing how our brain evolved in the FIRST place then apply that to healing...it's as easy as 1 2 3. So if seafood 'built' a brain, then it can 'restructure'/heal a brain that might have been damaged in some ways by epi-genetics or even life events. I honestly think the nutrients provided in this plan (and the circadian rythms) allow for the brain to re-grow and/or set down new pathways so that you can override possible errors from before. You don't need to 'fix' the broken at all...you can just 'fill on top' of it. Make new neurons....

So this is why it's simple and easy for me and I don't get overwhelmed about canned or frozen or fresh. I eat mostly from frozen and even in expensive too. I eat canned if I want sardines and oysters. Easy. Seaweeds I've come up with many good ideas. In the Fall/Winter I decided to eat apples b/c I felt it's good for my digestion and for resveratrol, and the fact that they are harvested in Fall and keep so well over winter easily w/o modification. Just keep in cool dark room. So I put salt, curcumin and cinnamon on the apple slices (usually before breakfast). Very tasty. I eat carrots, onions and kale too. Of course I love my coffee and I put coconut oil in it b/c I want the ketones for my brain. For me it's important to get a decent amount of coconut oil in me despite it being from the tropics. I eat nuts and seeds but not like a million (and I feel OK on them) b/c I don't have AI. I eat eggs b/c of no AI. I don't eat dairy tough, b/c the way I feel on it. Goat butter is the only dairy I seem to tolerate. Cow butter...I don't know...I just don't like it anymore. For me my health is #1, so I use food to be prescriptive and within that context I overlay that with seasonality. So if I eat coconuts all year that are not normal for winter, well that's the prescriptive side of things. I mean, technically coffee is not from my region either, but I choose to drink it, and in this case mainly b/c I truly truly enjoy the complexity in each cup of coffee and the literature from where the coffee bean comes from. Just fascinating. (And b/c also it makes a great carrier for my coco oil lol).

I just feel like life is so much easier now b/c I know to go to bed early and wake up with the sun (well in winter it's before the sun due to going to bed early...but that's different). I obey the circadian cycles. Period. I don't worry about food or what to eat b/c I have my seafoods and I have the fats which I know are good. ie. bacon, duck fat, goat butter coconut oil, avacadoes. Easy. How can it get any easier?

That's MY n-1.

### EWO — 2022-08-07T23:18:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lets-talk-canned-fish.4449/post-314925>

> [caroline said:](/goto/post?id=314910)
>
> please tell us more ......how intriguing.
>
> I spend way too much time thinking....

There are lots of different formal practices (like "sealing of the five senses")...

with a similar intention...

of helping a person organize and "seal" up their energy field to avoid taking in undesired energies in from others/the world around them, or leaking personal energy reserves.

Doesn't have to be formal or complicated or esoteric though.

One thing I am practicing right now is "not having an opinion" rather than "being nonjudgemental." Being nonjudgemental is more active/requires more energy output, whereas having no opinion is more passive/requires less energy output. I've noticed, for example, I tend to go out and about seeking people and things that irritate me. Even just looking over at the driver next to me and "catching" them on their cell phone. Or noticing who is or is not wearing a mask. Or who is or is not plastering their cars with what bumper stickers. Then I go through being judgmental about those people and things. Then I pull my attention around to "trying to be non judgemental," telling myself things like "everyone is just doing the best they can." But I've already stressed out about stuff that doesn't really matter, for the tiny little bit of egoic boost that came from feeling momentarily self righteous, then the tiny boost that came from exercising the spiritual elitism of "being nonjudgmental."

Setting the intention to "have no opinion," I stop looking around for stuff to irritate me. If I happen to notice something that would otherwise irritate me, I catch myself and say "I have no opinion about that." I spend way less energy wrapped up in what other people are doing or not doing, and have way more energy left for whatever...

It's also making me more aware of all the things I waste my time having an opinion about when I can never really know why anyone is doing what they are doing.

Become your own best friend and guard your energy inputs and outputs, whether or not you follow any specific practices.

# Thread: Irivingia/fucoxanthin

**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 3

### FarmersDaughter — 2012-10-22T18:14:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/irivingia-fucoxanthin.4450/post-59655>

So once upon a time, I discovered LEF.org, and a couple of magic beans called fucoxanthin and irivingia, respectively.

Rediscovered them in my move.

Guess what they do....act on ucp-1 and BAT.

took one dose this am, and my temp hasn't dropped in the water once...started at 97.4 and one hour later, I'm at 99.86. 60 degree water. (I forgot to referee my blue ices)

I have been hesitant to spend $$$on bitter melon because I've spent so much already, was planning to wait, but as I already have these...

I wonder what the three together would accomplish.

### bigknitwit — 2012-10-22T19:44:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/irivingia-fucoxanthin.4450/post-59667>

I've been wondering what the word is on these supplements. A few years back i was researching, trying to figure out my unending appeitite, and came up with the idea that it was a "leptin" problem. So naturally I looke dinto how to solve leptin problems (finding JK around this point wound have been really, really useful!). I found info from Ron Rosedale, who recommended long spaces between very low carb meals (all seemed very impossible) to improve leptin signalling. Then I found information about fucoxanthin and thought that sounded much easier LOL! I tried it, but of course found absolutely zero result (no other diet/lifestyle changes in place. I thought I'd evaluate the "magic pill" idea first). But I do wonder about using it in combination with all I'm doing at the moment...

### FarmersDaughter — 2012-10-23T00:59:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/irivingia-fucoxanthin.4450/post-59735>

You and me both. I tried it, got nowhere, ad threw them in the back of my supplement drawer.

I think resurrection is imminent though...at least until I can tell whether they do or don't do anything.

Free experiment, can't pass that up!

# Thread: Lice
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lice.4454/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 9
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-10-23T00:36:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lice.4454/post-59729>

ok, my son's classroom has had several kiddo's with Lice. Any of you home remedy gods/esses have suggestions for PREVENTION?

I'll ask again for cure if we suffer that fate!

### Shijin13 — 2012-10-23T01:22:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lice.4454/post-59739>

> **Cavemam;61302 wrote:** ok, my son's classroom has had several kiddo's with Lice. Any of you home remedy gods/esses have suggestions for PREVENTION?
>
>
>
> I'll ask again for cure if we suffer that fate!

We went through this in March we just used an OTC thing from CVS. Treated the whole house. We think dd got it at ski school from the helmet she was using. No clue on prevention.

### kathylu — 2012-10-23T01:47:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lice.4454/post-59742>

Oh, ick! Here's an article about prevention...sounds like there is not a lot you can do!

[http://pediatrics.about.com/od/headlice/ht/08\_prevent\_lice.htm](http://http://pediatrics.about.com/od/headlice/ht/08_prevent_lice.htm)

### vkiernan — 2012-10-23T01:57:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lice.4454/post-59744>

My daugher was going thru it with her kiddos and the school wasn't publishing that they had a problem so it was on-going. Eventually after treating them several times and notifying the school district or whomever she had to, she used some shampoo that was preventative and she didn't have them anymore. You should be able to ask at Walgreens or some place like that. Mayonaisse on the head overnight with a shower cap works great at getting rid of them and you have no toxins. I believe it smothers them. You do have to comb thru it though to remove the nits I believe.

### chocolate — 2012-10-23T03:51:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lice.4454/post-59768>

I heard it was the egg white that nailed them in the mayo, along with the oil.

### Glen PDQ — 2012-10-23T19:30:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lice.4454/post-59844>

Lice on bedding, clothing, and furniture must feed on human blood or they die within 24 hours, so leaving the home empty of people for say 36 hours should eradicate the already hatched lice. Oily substances such as coconut oil or pomades suffocate them.

### Glen PDQ — 2012-10-23T19:37:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lice.4454/post-59849>

For prevention?

Learn to love the bald look. Keep family members' heads shaved.

### Owl — 2012-10-25T08:46:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lice.4454/post-60096>

Lice don't like tea tree oil, so a shampoo with that will keep them away.

### stacyj — 2012-10-25T14:42:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lice.4454/post-60119>

I know most here are avoiding chemical hair products......but, lice love clean hair. They do not like sticky, hard to move around in hair. Goop up his hair with gel before he goes to school.

# Thread: 100 years of wisdom from the oldest holocaust survivor
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/100-years-of-wisdom-from-the-oldest-holocaust-survivor.4456/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### TribalSpice — 2012-10-23T04:52:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/100-years-of-wisdom-from-the-oldest-holocaust-survivor.4456/post-59779>

[http://en.avaaz.org/986/100-years-of-wisdom-from-the-worlds-oldest-holocaust-survivor](http://http://en.avaaz.org/986/100-years-of-wisdom-from-the-worlds-oldest-holocaust-survivor)

She didn't just survive, she thrived. I love her message. I am pretty sure the cold must have helped as well.

### Inger — 2012-10-23T09:23:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/100-years-of-wisdom-from-the-oldest-holocaust-survivor.4456/post-59795>

Wow.

She says in one place,

I know about he bad, but I look at the good..

Just how I decided to live years ago. It makes life so beautiful. It gives the powers to go through bad times, anything.

Thank you TribalSpice for posting this it touched my heart.

# Thread: Leave the heating off and avoid the gym to lose weight
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/leave-the-heating-off-and-avoid-the-gym-to-lose-weight.4458/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 4
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### ruby — 2012-10-23T07:07:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/leave-the-heating-off-and-avoid-the-gym-to-lose-weight.4458/post-59789>

Professor Michael Symonds and scientists at the University of Nottinghamâ€™s Queenâ€™s Medical Centre are using thermal imaging techniques â€” and a bucket of cold water â€” to assess changes in brown fat in adult recruits, and see how it affects their weight.

Read more: [http://www.dailymail.co.uk/health/article-2221602/Why-leaving-heating-avoiding-gym-help-lose-weight.html#ixzz2A5kccAUK](http://http://www.dailymail.co.uk/health/article-2221602/Why-leaving-heating-avoiding-gym-help-lose-weight.html#ixzz2A5kccAUK)

### Michael — 2012-10-23T09:25:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/leave-the-heating-off-and-avoid-the-gym-to-lose-weight.4458/post-59796>

Top notch stuff.

I like the advice that if you can walk round in a shirt indoors the heating is too high. To be honest, I've thought _that for years. It's always seemed wasteful to me. And people talk airily about global warming!

These days I do wear just a shirt to the office even though_ it's not warm. That's what's changed. It's a small office and a colleague and I have access to the boiler room, and she wants the heating turned down low enough to virtually not trip-in, too. This is one of the troubles of bigger offices: one has no control over that kind of thing. I have little enough control over most things in my life; I'm glad I at least get not to sit in the normal working sauna (which is something I've never liked).

### bigknitwit — 2012-10-23T15:32:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/leave-the-heating-off-and-avoid-the-gym-to-lose-weight.4458/post-59807>

Excellent - thanks for the link! I sent it along to inquiring friends and family.

### Michael — 2012-10-23T15:59:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/leave-the-heating-off-and-avoid-the-gym-to-lose-weight.4458/post-59810>

Oh, dear. I arrived in late this morning, having been for a bllod test. A girly little man was working in the front office this morning and complained that he was cold..

Winter? Have you heard of it?

It's only about 17 C outdoors

anyway.

The heating is belting out now and is quite unpleasant.

The trouble is that people experience abnormality as normality, and situations that couldn't have existed 50 (even 30) years ago as matters of course.

# Thread: AI and I am searching for a breakfast staple substitute
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ai-and-i-am-searching-for-a-breakfast-staple-substitute.4459/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 23
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### ljbprrfmof — 2012-10-23T07:30:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ai-and-i-am-searching-for-a-breakfast-staple-substitute.4459/post-59790>

I am AI and am working at eliminating the foods that I commonly eat.

When I come to working on breakfast, I am at a loss as to what I should replace egg with. I normally eat 2 - 3 large or jumbo eggs in a scramble.

What do you eat for your BAB?

### Paradox — 2012-10-23T08:10:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ai-and-i-am-searching-for-a-breakfast-staple-substitute.4459/post-59791>

> **ljbprrfmof;61364 wrote:** I am AI and am working at eliminating the foods that I commonly eat.
>
>
>
> When I come to working on breakfast, I am at a loss as to what I should replace egg with. I normally eat 2 - 3 large or jumbo eggs in a scramble.
>
>
>
> What do you eat for your BAB?

I too have serious AI issue and have found several favorite breakfasts to be smoked salmon with half an avocado diced and finely diced red onion. I wrap the salmon around avocado & onion. Also I will have a big ass salad with half a can of lump crab meat with lots of different lettuces, cilantro, arugula, again the avocado & onion. Third favorite is sashimi it is amazing how much protein is in a small piece of fish. I try to have lots of veggies with my fishy breakfasts too. Wakame salad is really good if you make it yourself because the premade always has sesseme oil and seeds which is bad for AI issues.The least favorite is sausages because you have to eat so many to get the protein count. Although, liver, onions and bacon breakfast is my favorite non fishy one and you can quickly get the protein up there with that.

### fitness@home — 2012-10-23T15:28:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ai-and-i-am-searching-for-a-breakfast-staple-substitute.4459/post-59806>

I miss eggs!

If there are no leftovers in the fridge, my simplest "in a hurry" breakfast is shrimp. Boil water, add frozen shrimp, and within minutes it is done. I usually have a couple of slices of bacon with it.

### ljbprrfmof — 2012-10-23T16:01:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ai-and-i-am-searching-for-a-breakfast-staple-substitute.4459/post-59811>

I think I can stomach the shrimp with some bacon.

My old self would do a bowl of oatmeal or a toasted PB & J (make the toast first, otherwise it is a gooey mess) or an eggo waffle. But now I are edumacated and looking for more edumacation.

I am going to miss eggs also.

### ealachan — 2012-10-23T16:53:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ai-and-i-am-searching-for-a-breakfast-staple-substitute.4459/post-59812>

Couldn't you just cook up some ground beef, a steak, some sausage...something meaty? My go-to breakfast is canned salmon warmed up in a little bacon fat, with some crispy bacon crumbled into it. I do eat it with eggs, but it would still be good without them.

### MamaGrok — 2012-10-23T17:08:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ai-and-i-am-searching-for-a-breakfast-staple-substitute.4459/post-59819>

I really miss eggs, and hope to add them back, but I've totally de-brainwashed myself about breakfast. Now, just like all our ancestors, I can eat anything for breakfast, and do. It's just another meal. And a BIG one!

I usually eat some kind of seafood and some kind of landfood. Seafood might be canned sardines, sauteed salmon, shrimp, or ceviche. Landfood might be ground beef w/ some yummy spices, pastured pork sausage, pork chops, or any kind of leftover.

Lately, it's been a soup of bone broth, some non-starchy seasonal veggie, coconut oil, animal fat, seafood, landfood, and a side of raw liver.  It's wonderful to eat on the deck with my lenses off and chill on in the brisk fall morning, with a shaker of cinnamon by my side.

### FarmersDaughter — 2012-10-23T17:36:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ai-and-i-am-searching-for-a-breakfast-staple-substitute.4459/post-59822>

I have been struggling to find a food breakfast staple as well. I live in a food rut...I could easily eat the same thing for a week or more at a time. I don't care.

I decided that canned salmon is the answer-for me.

It's edible, and while its not GOOD it's not bad. It's relatively cheap, easy to fix, and after all, food is merely fuel. Hormonal information.

If it at tastes good, that is a bonus, but I got here by being concerned with flavor over function, and it's time to change course.

### MamaGrok — 2012-10-23T17:44:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ai-and-i-am-searching-for-a-breakfast-staple-substitute.4459/post-59825>

ITA. It's a cost-benefit analysis. Taste & variety are nice, but "will I do it every day" is much, much more important.

### Patty Cakes — 2012-10-23T19:30:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ai-and-i-am-searching-for-a-breakfast-staple-substitute.4459/post-59845>

I have a burger almost every morning and have been doing this since Jan. I prepare burgers three days worth at a time. Also in the evening I may prepare short ribs and cook them on 200 all night and have that for breakfast. Seafood for me is not filling enough for the morning. After breakfast I only eat fish but the morning needs to be beef. Simple, easy and can do them every day.

### Shijin13 — 2012-10-23T19:48:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ai-and-i-am-searching-for-a-breakfast-staple-substitute.4459/post-59852>

Not AI...but since the changing of the seasons I no longer want to eat eggs for breakfast. I make breakfast my big SAT FAT meal. So I do sausage, hamburger, left over soup, and I also add a 1/2 coconut milk and 1/2 HWC w/cacao and maca powder to keep my fat intake up and help deal w/the nausea I get w/taking the cycloset...

### ljbprrfmof — 2012-10-23T20:30:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ai-and-i-am-searching-for-a-breakfast-staple-substitute.4459/post-59868>

What is HWC? I guess I could switch to meats like sausage, bacon, and burgers. I had two burgers from last night for breakfast this morning.

I do not think I am ready, mentally to handle seafood first thing in the morning. That may change. I was able to get over no toast with my eggs, so seafood is another challenge.

### ealachan — 2012-10-23T20:38:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ai-and-i-am-searching-for-a-breakfast-staple-substitute.4459/post-59873>

HWC = heavy whipping cream.

Here's the thing about seafood in the morning - it tastes the same as it does at night. The first couple times I ate salmon for breakfast it kind of freaked me out, but I got over it really quickly. I can't honestly claim to be one of the enlightened few that now sees food as only fuel / hormonal information and not entertainment, because I love to cook and I love to make yummy stuff...but I DO seem to have a better grasp now of the concept that we should eat to live, not live to eat. The Breakfast Police is not going to come take you away if you have fish for breakfast, or soup, or last night's leftovers...know what I mean?

And I don't intend for that to sound snarky, btw. It's a mental hump that a lot of us are still working on getting over. You are by no means alone in this challenge!

### MamaGrok — 2012-10-23T20:57:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ai-and-i-am-searching-for-a-breakfast-staple-substitute.4459/post-59883>

It helped me a lot to go to a really fancy (like Ritz Carlton fancy) hotel buffet and see what was on their breakfast bar. Cereals, yeah, sure, toast if you're slack. Omelette bar, yes. Lox bar, YES! That's smoked salmon, cream cheese, capers, onions, and more toppings for the smoked fish. Sausage, fried potatoes, bacon, lots and lots of different breakfast meats. People have been eating lunch & dinner food for breakfast for far longer, and across far more of the world, than they've been having toast & even just eggs.

### FarmersDaughter — 2012-10-24T03:08:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ai-and-i-am-searching-for-a-breakfast-staple-substitute.4459/post-59923>

> **MamaGrok;61458 wrote:** It helped me a lot to go to a really fancy (like Ritz Carlton fancy) hotel buffet and see what was on their breakfast bar. Cereals, yeah, sure, toast if you're slack. Omelette bar, yes. Lox bar, YES! That's smoked salmon, cream cheese, capers, onions, and more toppings for the smoked fish. Sausage, fried potatoes, bacon, lots and lots of different breakfast meats. People have been eating lunch & dinner food for breakfast for far longer, and across far more of the world, than they've been having toast & even just eggs.

That is exactly how my "love affair" (artistic license, check) with savory stuff for breakfast got started...I went to a schmancy hotel in Portland for work, had a killer smoked salmon and horseradish cream somethingorother....drool worthy in every way.

And don't kid yourself...there are days I'd far prefer to have my beloved coffee and creamer for breakfast, or better yet, a big nasty plate of biscuits and sausage gravy, or not eat til noon....but I'm on the "fake it til you make it" plan re food as fuel vs. entertainment.and if I can talk myself into it, I'll stay on course. It's just like the cold bath...it doesn't sound good, you don't want it, but take a deep breath and do it anyway. (And curse a few times when you run into chunks of ice)

Smoked salmon and horseradish creamed somethingorother is a looooooooooooong way from canned salmon.

\[Sidebar\] At least you guys can stomach eggs. The texture of cooked eggs gives me the heebiejeebies. Raw is no problem, but cooked triggers a gag reflex like mad. \[/sidebar\]

### chocolate — 2012-10-24T04:05:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ai-and-i-am-searching-for-a-breakfast-staple-substitute.4459/post-59930>

> **FarmersDaughter;61510 wrote:** That is exactly how my "love affair" (artistic license, check) with savory stuff for breakfast got started...I went to a schmancy hotel in Portland for work, had a killer smoked salmon and horseradish cream somethingorother....drool worthy in every way.
>
>
>
> And don't kid yourself...there are days I'd far prefer to have my beloved coffee and creamer for breakfast, or better yet, a big nasty plate of biscuits and sausage gravy, or not eat til noon....but I'm on the "fake it til you make it" plan re food as fuel vs. entertainment.and if I can talk myself into it, I'll stay on course. It's just like the cold bath...it doesn't sound good, you don't want it, but take a deep breath and do it anyway. (And curse a few times when you run into chunks of ice)
>
>
>
> Smoked salmon and horseradish creamed somethingorother is a looooooooooooong way from canned salmon.
>
>
>
> \[Sidebar\] At least you guys can stomach eggs. The texture of cooked eggs gives me the heebiejeebies. Raw is no problem, but cooked triggers a gag reflex like mad. \[/sidebar\]

Raw egg whites cause biotin problems and lets candida grow.

### FarmersDaughter — 2012-10-24T04:29:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ai-and-i-am-searching-for-a-breakfast-staple-substitute.4459/post-59935>

> **chocolate;61519 wrote:** Raw egg whites cause biotin problems and lets candida grow.

(Ive only ever eaten the yolk)

whites=gag worthy in any form.

Shoulda specified but I forgot some peeps do eat raw white.

### Endless — 2012-10-25T04:41:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ai-and-i-am-searching-for-a-breakfast-staple-substitute.4459/post-60075>

I throw frozen large shrimp in a pan and cook them until they turn pink...a splash of lemon juice and garlic and some avocado on the side.

Some days I go for the MHS, or some scallops and bacon. I miss eggs too.

### kathylu — 2012-10-25T05:38:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ai-and-i-am-searching-for-a-breakfast-staple-substitute.4459/post-60082>

Shrimp (frozen, cooked salad sized, 50-100/lb.) and smoked oyster (canned) salad. Mix with paleo mayo (made with olive oil), lemon and lemon zest, finely diced onion and cilantro. Salt and pepper to taste. Yum.

### Endless — 2012-10-25T05:45:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ai-and-i-am-searching-for-a-breakfast-staple-substitute.4459/post-60084>

That sounds yummy....

### Glen PDQ — 2012-10-25T08:35:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ai-and-i-am-searching-for-a-breakfast-staple-substitute.4459/post-60094>

I think I found a decent drink to go with breakfast made with tea. I brewed a large cup of black tea, hot, with 2 teaspoons of honey, 1/4 stick of Kerrygold butter, 2 Tbls of coconut oil, and frothed in the blender. Very nice.

I don't eat any other sugar except for small portions of sauteed or poached sweet potato now and then and an 80% dark chocolate bar once in a blue moon.

### Glen PDQ — 2012-10-25T08:38:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ai-and-i-am-searching-for-a-breakfast-staple-substitute.4459/post-60095>

Would sweetbreads be a good replacement for eggs?

### SeaJane — 2012-10-25T17:08:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ai-and-i-am-searching-for-a-breakfast-staple-substitute.4459/post-60136>

> **Patty Cakes;61420 wrote:** I have a burger almost every morning and have been doing this since Jan. I prepare burgers three days worth at a time. Also in the evening I may prepare short ribs and cook them on 200 all night and have that for breakfast. Seafood for me is not filling enough for the morning. After breakfast I only eat fish but the morning needs to be beef. Simple, easy and can do them every day.

I found this interesting as my experience has been the opposite. I am much more satisfied eating seafood in the morning than landfood. The thing is it is even usually less calories. My typical breakfast is a seafood soup that includes a few dried shiratake mushrooms, some low carb veggies, a tablespoon of Olive oil, and then a mix of seafood that may include mussels, tiny wild oregon shrimp, squid, octopus, salmon, cod depending on what is around. I am in Seattle and have been getting fresh local mussels at Costco for $2.99/lb in 5 lb bags and cook them and shell them on the weekend. Then a have some everyday with breakfast. I also am able to get the tiny shrimp at Costco fresh for about $3.75/lb. These are my regulars and the others I add in. Overall, I eat about 50-55g of protein at breakfast.

### MamaGrok — 2012-10-25T17:45:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ai-and-i-am-searching-for-a-breakfast-staple-substitute.4459/post-60144>

I gotta eat landfood with my seafood or it will never fill me up. I have some of each, each morning.

# Thread: Thing on eyelid
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/thing-on-eyelid.4460/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 4
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Michael — 2012-10-23T09:18:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/thing-on-eyelid.4460/post-59794>

I thought I'd post this here, as it kind of recalled Inger's disappearing wrinkles. LOL

Anyway, I had this thing on my lower right eyelid. I don't know what it was. I was a bit like a wart only tiny: I was probably the only person that knew it was there. Strangely, it sort of dried up. I could tell that when I touched it with my finger, so I sort of gently prised it off with my fingernail and it came away very easily. That was a week ago, and it hasn't come back. This thing, whatever it was, clearing up could of course have been pure coincidence, but maybe it's related to increasing the percentage of seafood in my diet and adding seaweed a few weeks ago ...

### Huck — 2012-10-23T15:50:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/thing-on-eyelid.4460/post-59809>

Sounds like a skin tag.

### ealachan — 2012-10-23T16:54:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/thing-on-eyelid.4460/post-59813>

It was your body casting off the last vestiges of conventional wisdom. DEMONS OUT!

### Michael — 2012-10-23T17:03:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/thing-on-eyelid.4460/post-59815>

> **ealachan;61387 wrote:** It was your body casting off the last vestiges of conventional wisdom. DEMONS OUT!

It seems to fit the description of the thing Huck mentioned (except it was even smaller if anything than it says those tend to be):

[http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Acrochordon](http://http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Acrochordon)

I prefer your explanation, though. That's really funny.

# Thread: Maori Fishing
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/maori-fishing.4461/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Michael — 2012-10-23T09:37:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/maori-fishing.4461/post-59797>

[http://www.teara.govt.nz/en/te-hi-ika-maori-fishing](http://http://www.teara.govt.nz/en/te-hi-ika-maori-fishing)

Fishhooks:

[http://collections.tepapa.govt.nz/theme.aspx?irn=1467](http://http://collections.tepapa.govt.nz/theme.aspx?irn=1467)

I found that jewellery in the form of a stylized Maori fishhook made into a pendant is a thriving industry in New Zealand. Whether this really is a tradition from the Maoris or a modern invention of a tradition for purely commercial purposes I don't know. If it actually is something the Maoris wore, I guess it speaks volumes about the importance of the sea in Maori culture.

# Thread: Siberian Cat
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/siberian-cat.4462/>
**Subforum:** The Cave

# Thread: Mesolithic to Neolithic transition in Britain and Denmark
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mesolithic-to-neolithic-transition-in-britain-and-denmark.4463/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Michael — 2012-10-23T15:19:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mesolithic-to-neolithic-transition-in-britain-and-denmark.4463/post-59805>

Interestingly, it turns out that the Neolithic in Britain and Denmark is marked by a shift from fish to meat, from "maritime to terrestrial sources".

Not what we're usually told.

[http://eprints.whiterose.ac.uk/5606/](http://http://eprints.whiterose.ac.uk/5606/)

Full text unfortunately not available without subscription:

[http://antiquity.ac.uk/ant/078/Ant0780009b.htm](http://http://antiquity.ac.uk/ant/078/Ant0780009b.htm)

See also this earlier article

[http://www.archaeologyuk.org/ba/ba12/BA12FEAT.HTML](http://http://www.archaeologyuk.org/ba/ba12/BA12FEAT.HTML)

> The small-scale study - the first of its kind - of the bones of about 23 Neolithic people from ten sites in central and southern England, suggests that these \`first farmers' relied heavily on animal meat for food, or on animal by-products such as milk and cheese, and that plant foods in fact formed little importance in their diet. The bones date from throughout the Neolithic, c 4100BC - c 2000BC.

This latter study I was already aware of, and it has long interested me as showing that blanket claims about what people were eating in the past in different periods are quite erroneous. I still frequently hear "paleo" advocates speak as if around 10,000 years ago everyone stopped eating meat and vegetables and started eating grain. Not in North-West Europe they didn't. Neither did they to an appreciable extent, it seems, even into what is recognized as the Neolithic.

The hot topic weâ€™ll be taking on in Episode 31 is â€œKetogenic Diets & Cancerâ€ which is gaining a lot of traction amongst researchers looking for nutritional solutions to this horrible disease. ...

[http://www.askthelowcarbexperts.com/2012/10/31-dr-colin-champ-ketogenic-diets-and-cancer/](http://http://www.askthelowcarbexperts.com/2012/10/31-dr-colin-champ-ketogenic-diets-and-cancer/)

I am thinking.. or my logic is saying, a cancer diet should be ketogenic but then add in therapeutic carbs like wild berries because they are known to have anti cancerous properties. That is why I eat lots of wild picked berries as my only carb source really (except of some wild raw honey as a "spice") I can imagine the body and the cancer cell would then suck up the carb and with it the nutrients that kills it..huh clever, or? No idea if it is right but I have figured it sounds logic so I eat all the wild berries I want without any issue. I cannot eat apples from the garden or strawberries or domesticated raspberries without getting in troubles but wild grow berries do me only good! I figure then they must be great for me too..
