# Thread: European Union Public Service Review: Growing link between diet and brain function
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/european-union-public-service-review-growing-link-between-diet-and-brain-function.4466/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 5
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Michael — 2012-10-23T19:22:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/european-union-public-service-review-growing-link-between-diet-and-brain-function.4466/post-59843>

> Just as drugs can affect brain function, evidence shows that what we eat may influence not only our bodies but our brains and minds. Eating plenty of oily fish, olive oil, and fruit and vegetables has been linked to lower risks of age-related cognitive decline and mental illnesses such as depression, as well as conditions like heart disease and diabetes. These foods are rich in important micronutrients: vitamins, minerals and the unsaturated fatty acids EPA and DHA. ...

[http://www.publicservice.co.uk/article.asp?publication=European%20Union&id=590&content\_name=Research,%20Innovation%20and%20Science&article=21023](http://http://www.publicservice.co.uk/article.asp?publication=European%20Union&id=590&content_name=Research,%20Innovation%20and%20Science&article=21023)

### Jack Kruse — 2012-10-24T05:01:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/european-union-public-service-review-growing-link-between-diet-and-brain-function.4466/post-59943>

Europe is way ahead of us with respect to food and the industries around it.

### Michael — 2012-10-24T08:11:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/european-union-public-service-review-growing-link-between-diet-and-brain-function.4466/post-59949>

The public services in any country ought to be interested in this, because besides the problems and costs with regard to healthcare there's this:

> ... raises hopes of reducing levels of **educational failure via a relatively simple intervention. Secondly, evidence that violent behaviour** can be reduced by dietary supplements ...
>
>
> Education and also crime. If it were just people getting a little thicker around the waist; if it were even no more than people hitting a range of health problems as they entered old age ... But it's not. In the end, a denuded food supply is even a public order problem.
>
>
>
> The road seems rocky, though:
>
>
>
>
> > Government's role is essential, and here there is room for improvement. For example, a recent attempt to replicate the UK prison study was seriously disrupted when the prison service decided to move many enrolled participants elsewhere. Long-term science does not always fit well with short-term political and administrative priorities.
>
>
>
> BTW, this is coming from the IFBB. It says at the top:
>
>
>
> The Institute for Food, Brain and Behaviour's Professor John Stein and Dr Kathleen Taylor report on the growing link between diet and brain function…

Stein, along with Professor Michael Angus Crawford is one of the science advisors to the IFBB. Interestingly, so is someone from the NIH in America:

[http://www.ifbb.org.uk/science-advisory-council](http://http://www.ifbb.org.uk/science-advisory-council)

Gives a link here:

[http://www.blackdoginstitute.org.au/docs/JosephRHibbelnMDbiosketch.pdf](http://http://www.blackdoginstitute.org.au/docs/JosephRHibbelnMDbiosketch.pdf)

### Michael — 2012-10-24T09:23:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/european-union-public-service-review-growing-link-between-diet-and-brain-function.4466/post-59950>

Isn't that interesting? I've just noticed that the biography of the American expert is hosted at an Australian website:

[http://www.blackdoginstitute.org.au](http://http://www.blackdoginstitute.org.au)

Poking around there I found a news item on a lecture by him:

> Captain Joseph R Hibbeln, M.D. voiced this opinion when delivering the prestigious Black Dog Institute 2010 International Lecture on Friday, 12 November. Captain Hibbeln is a scientist and psychiatrist who was **the first to identify a link between reduced seafood consumption and depression** and has led an incredibly broad amount of research on the subject. ...
>
>
> [http://www.blackdoginstitute.org.au/docs/2010InternationalLecture.pdf](http://http://www.blackdoginstitute.org.au/docs/2010InternationalLecture.pdf)
>
>
>
> So he's the first person to note that particular link.
>
>
>
>
> > “Avoid junk, especially deep fried foods. Avoid mayonnaises, salad dressings and margarines. Avoid soy, corn and vegetable oil. Use olive and flax oils,” he said.
> >
> >
> > Thank you, Captain Hibbeln.
> >
> >
> >
> > He said that in the U.S. expectant mothers had been warned against eating fish because it contained small amounts of mercury. Captain Hibbeln questioned the wisdom of the advice ...
>
>
>
> The report finishes:
>
>
>
> “Seafood contains a host of balanced nutrients and if we have a seafood deficient diet it means the brain isn’t getting enough nutrition,” he said.

All this stuff is out there. And all these people I never heard of ... Hannah Russ in archaeology, Captain Hibbeln in depression ...

See also:

[http://www.blackdoginstitute.org.au/search/index.cfm?q=hibbeln](http://http://www.blackdoginstitute.org.au/search/index.cfm?q=hibbeln)

### Inger — 2012-10-24T10:27:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/european-union-public-service-review-growing-link-between-diet-and-brain-function.4466/post-59956>

You know, it is strange to think about the culture here. There is an Swedish culture in Finland too, they are a minority but here everyone must learn Swedish in scool and on every food and anything you can read the text in both languages. Now, the Swedish minority also is the richer and have better health and better education.. their culture have many different seafood sermonies like crab parties, lots of herring.. Seafood is a strong part of their culture. Also tanning and swimming in the sea, many have cottages on own small islands and are there a lot in summer. My best friend in scool, they had own an island too and her mother was the psychologist in town. They were the whole summer on their island and the were tanning and swimming naked! Yeah.. talk about paradise..

People are a bit more "wild" here. Now.. here are only around 5 millions in quite a large country. That might be a reason too.

That is why I love to live here quite a lot.

# Thread: what time of day do you like to do CT?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-time-of-day-do-you-like-to-do-ct.4469/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 14
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### ealachan — 2012-10-23T23:30:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-time-of-day-do-you-like-to-do-ct.4469/post-59896>

This may have already been covered previously in past threads, but I'm curious: what time of day works best for you with regards to CT (either cold-water or cold-air)? Do you have a set schedule, or just do it when the fancy strikes? Did you try it at one time of day and decide it was all wrong and switch it up until you found what worked best?

Inquiring minds want to know!

### Shijin13 — 2012-10-23T23:33:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-time-of-day-do-you-like-to-do-ct.4469/post-59897>

Mid afternoon works best for me...but w/working on site w/a client it isn't as feasible so I'm back to ice packs in the am while making breakfast... I'm trying to get my butt into the tub after the kids go to bed...may actually hit it this evening... definitely need it considering I've had a migraine for 3days...and the 2nd one in a week... effing hormones...

### Patty Cakes — 2012-10-24T00:40:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-time-of-day-do-you-like-to-do-ct.4469/post-59904>

Mid afternoon is best for me too. Did a 50 degree bath last night at 7:30 and it slayed me. I was so cold. I had the room dark w/a candle and was very relaxed but cold. It took me hours to feel warm again. Yah, I guess I should feel cold for a while. I don't know maybe the dark had something to do with it since most of my CTing has been during the day.

Mornings right after BAB are good too!

### Dali Dula — 2012-10-24T02:50:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-time-of-day-do-you-like-to-do-ct.4469/post-59919>

I CT mostly in the am after bab, but occasionally i do a sunset swim. This evening i swam in the rain at sunset. Very calming. I would CT before bed, but I am cold for hours and this freezes my wife so i don't. I like to CT in the am because I feel good all day.

### FarmersDaughter — 2012-10-24T03:22:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-time-of-day-do-you-like-to-do-ct.4469/post-59925>

I do it whenever I can...would love to be organized enough for a schedule, but alas...

Maybe in my next life.

I seem to fare better in the am, temp rises quicker and steadier, but the BAB is larger then dinner tends to be as well, so that may be why? With the advent of winter, I may start doing cold air CT in the am, we'll see.

### kathylu — 2012-10-24T03:32:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-time-of-day-do-you-like-to-do-ct.4469/post-59926>

AM is the only thing that works for me. I CAN'T fall asleep if I'm cold! I know the CT jocks here probably sleep naked on an ice slab, but I still use a blanket and wear socks to bed. I don't see that changing any time soon, so I need at least 6 hours minimum between the bath and a comfortable sleeping temperature.

### FarmersDaughter — 2012-10-24T03:51:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-time-of-day-do-you-like-to-do-ct.4469/post-59927>

> **kathylu;61514 wrote:** AM is the only thing that works for me. I CAN'T fall asleep if I'm cold! I know the CT jocks here probably sleep naked on an ice slab, but I still use a blanket and wear socks to bed. I don't see that changing any time soon, so I need at least 6 hours minimum between the bath and a comfortable sleeping temperature.

Yeah, we won't discuss bedclothes....cold water I can do...cold sleep equals no sleep.

Not there yet.

### nonchalant — 2012-10-24T03:52:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-time-of-day-do-you-like-to-do-ct.4469/post-59928>

Evening. I'm just fine afterwards, unless I dry off. And once I get dressed I start to tremble. When I touch the sheets the shivering begins. If I could stay up for a couple hours, I'd have no issues.

Lucky for me DH doesn't mind a little second-hand CT.

### furface — 2012-12-12T01:01:14+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-time-of-day-do-you-like-to-do-ct.4469/post-67180>

Since I can only CT using my shower, or face dunking...which I did for the first time today after lunch, I take my cold showers after my regular warmish shower in the morning, then again at night after dinner.

Â 

I'll probably face dunk before dinner.

### endless — 2012-12-12T03:02:06+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-time-of-day-do-you-like-to-do-ct.4469/post-67182>

Morning if I can, but anytime up until about 3-4pm (this time of year, anyway. After that I start to feel colder and have a lot of resistance. I can't sleep when I am cold either.

### Dali Dula — 2012-12-12T08:02:53+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-time-of-day-do-you-like-to-do-ct.4469/post-67190>

I love doing a sunset swim this time of year. The low light and the cold go nicely together.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-12-12T10:39:08+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-time-of-day-do-you-like-to-do-ct.4469/post-67202>

im doing it now.......22 degrees of frigid

### nonchalant — 2012-12-12T12:50:33+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-time-of-day-do-you-like-to-do-ct.4469/post-67219>

> **Jack Kruse said**
> im doing it now.......22 degrees of frigid

I'm guessing you don't mean 22C.

What are you doing?Â  Adding alcohol to the water?Â  Oh, it's probably a freezer, cold air.

### Awainer1 — 2012-12-12T16:40:29+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-time-of-day-do-you-like-to-do-ct.4469/post-67250>

I was gonna say 22 degrees wow that is cold water! Oh wait. I do it at night in a dark bathroom before bed. Sleep like a log. I understood it as that being the best time to do it.

# Thread: what type of seasoning do you use on your fish, beef, etc?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-type-of-seasoning-do-you-use-on-your-fish-beef-etc.4476/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 15
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### x_danny_x — 2012-10-24T15:23:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-type-of-seasoning-do-you-use-on-your-fish-beef-etc.4476/post-59964>

Im just starting this Epi Paleo and eating a bunch of fish and shellfish, mainly oysters. The taste is kind of "bleh"

since I am used to eating meat from cows, chicken, pork, etc.

I need to put some seasoning into my meat/fish so it will be more enjoyable to eat. The thing is, I don't know what type of seasoning I can use that would be acceptable to consume during the fall/winter.

What do you guys use?

### BJK77 — 2012-10-24T18:19:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-type-of-seasoning-do-you-use-on-your-fish-beef-etc.4476/post-59990>

I'm using tons of garlic lately, much to my husband's dismay  I have a small herb garden that's still going strong, so I'm also using lots of fresh rosemary, oregano, thyme and tarragon.

Those are my favorites.

### ealachan — 2012-10-24T18:30:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-type-of-seasoning-do-you-use-on-your-fish-beef-etc.4476/post-59994>

chimichurri sauce is really awesome on salmon. There are a lot of ways to make it, but mine is usually lots of fresh parsley and cilantro, olive oil, raw garlic, salt, pepper and a splash of vinegar.

### Shijin13 — 2012-10-24T19:01:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-type-of-seasoning-do-you-use-on-your-fish-beef-etc.4476/post-59999>

sea salt, black pepper, turmeric, cumin, garlic, ginger, cinnamon, nutmeg, thyme, rosemary, basil, oregano, sage, mint, cilantro.

it really depends on what I'm making - but the above are my go to seasonings and herbs.

### x_danny_x — 2012-10-24T19:14:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-type-of-seasoning-do-you-use-on-your-fish-beef-etc.4476/post-60000>

thanks guys, i will disregard this portion about eating seasonal and use some seasoning on the fish. got to get used to eating fish first.

### ealachan — 2012-10-24T19:41:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-type-of-seasoning-do-you-use-on-your-fish-beef-etc.4476/post-60002>

Eating seasonally isn't necessarily as black-and-white as you seem to want to make it. The over-arching intention, as I understand it, is that we should look around where we live and notice what kind of carb-containing stuff is growing at certain times of the year, and take our consumption cues from that. In other words, no bananas in January unless you live in Ecuador. Fresh and dried herbs aren't used in such vast quantities that you'd be consuming significant carbs from them, so they probably wouldn't fall under the "seasonality" rule, unless one was taking said rule to a fairly fanatical degree.

### diane — 2012-10-24T19:46:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-type-of-seasoning-do-you-use-on-your-fish-beef-etc.4476/post-60004>

Lemon or lime, cumin and cilantro. I like mine with a bit of a mexican twist.

### Glen PDQ — 2012-10-25T08:20:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-type-of-seasoning-do-you-use-on-your-fish-beef-etc.4476/post-60093>

I sometimes use Paul Prudhomme Seafood Magic or Meat Magic depending on what I'm cooking.

### freesia — 2012-10-25T10:09:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-type-of-seasoning-do-you-use-on-your-fish-beef-etc.4476/post-60101>

I often use use Maori "bush pepper" or horopito\* in fish dishes. Porcini salt on beef. Thyme on either. Rosemary when I cook a leg of lamb. And LOTS of garlic.

\*An anti candida herb, also known as Kolorex. Available here as a seasoning.

### x_danny_x — 2012-10-27T23:01:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-type-of-seasoning-do-you-use-on-your-fish-beef-etc.4476/post-60471>

> **ealachan;61595 wrote:** Eating seasonally isn't necessarily as black-and-white as you seem to want to make it. The over-arching intention, as I understand it, is that we should look around where we live and notice what kind of carb-containing stuff is growing at certain times of the year, and take our consumption cues from that. In other words, no bananas in January unless you live in Ecuador. Fresh and dried herbs aren't used in such vast quantities that you'd be consuming significant carbs from them, so they probably wouldn't fall under the "seasonality" rule, unless one was taking said rule to a fairly fanatical degree.

Im in new york, the fruits and veggies that I eat dont grow during the fall and winter. i kind of figure that that the amount of herbs would not be significant to make a difference, though wasn't 100% sure. thanks for that.

i have to say these responses are beautifull. now i have things to buy.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-10-27T23:31:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-type-of-seasoning-do-you-use-on-your-fish-beef-etc.4476/post-60478>

> **x\_danny\_x;61556 wrote:** Im just starting this Epi Paleo and eating a bunch of fish and shellfish, mainly oysters. The taste is kind of "bleh"
>
> since I am used to eating meat from cows, chicken, pork, etc.
>
>
>
> I need to put some seasoning into my meat/fish so it will be more enjoyable to eat. The thing is, I don't know what type of seasoning I can use that would be acceptable to consume during the fall/winter.
>
>
>
> What do you guys use?

Sirachi is a favorite

### Stavity — 2012-10-28T00:14:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-type-of-seasoning-do-you-use-on-your-fish-beef-etc.4476/post-60480>

Yummm!!! homemade mayo with Sirachi together. I often use ginger, cilantro, or dill in salmon tartare and ceviche. My Ana test was negative so lots of hot peppers it is. Mignonette Sauce is great on oysters, if you can handle vinegar (I can't). It's just shallots and some type of vinegar.

### Primal Dream — 2012-10-28T02:26:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-type-of-seasoning-do-you-use-on-your-fish-beef-etc.4476/post-60488>

One of my favorites for lightly cooked fish or scallops: I lightly saute each side in bacon fat. I add minced garlic, ginger, and rosemary, plus sea salt and black pepper. I add in some lemon juice and a tablespoon or so of water that my seaweed has soaked in. Then I let it reduce and pour it over the fish/scallops.

This is so good!

On raw oysters or other raw seafood: I squeeze lemon juice and add some tabasco. I love tamari (gluten free) and wasabi with raw sashimi fish (but I'll bet the tamari is not optimal!).

I (and my body) so love this way of eating.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-10-28T03:04:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-type-of-seasoning-do-you-use-on-your-fish-beef-etc.4476/post-60491>

Just made epipaleo stew with sirachi and grand padano stew........posted a FB pic. WOW was it good.

### Entelechy — 2012-10-28T21:58:59+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-type-of-seasoning-do-you-use-on-your-fish-beef-etc.4476/post-60632>

why not try what Michaelangelo's David would have used as a condiment?

Garum was a highly prized and precious(expensive) sauce of the ancient Greek and Roman empires.

Ancient Garum Recipe

Use fatty fish, for example, sardines, and a well-sealed (pitched) container with a 26-35 quart capacity. Add dried, aromatic herbs possessing a strong flavor, such as dill, coriander, fennel, celery, mint, oregano, and others, making a layer on the bottom of the container; then put down a layer of fish (if small, leave them whole, if large, use pieces) and over this, add a layer of salt two fingers high. Repeat these layers until the container is filled. Let it rest for seven days in the sun. Then mix the sauce daily for 20 days. After that, it becomes a liquid.

or just use Thai fish sauce if 35quarts of homemade garum is too much.

# Thread: A different take on calcium and oxalates
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/a-different-take-on-calcium-and-oxalates.4477/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 6
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### chocolate — 2012-10-24T15:39:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/a-different-take-on-calcium-and-oxalates.4477/post-59967>

[http://www.greatplainslaboratory.com/home/span/oxalates.asp](http://http://www.greatplainslaboratory.com/home/span/oxalates.asp)

> Oxalate and its acid form oxalic acid are organic acids that are primarily from three sources: the diet, from fungus such as Aspergillus and Penicillium and **possibly Candida (1-9), and also from human metabolism (10).
>
>
>
>
> High oxalate in the urine and plasma was first found in people who were susceptible to kidney stones. Many kidney stones are composed of calcium oxalate. Stones can range in size from the diameter of a grain of rice to the width of a golf ball. It is estimated that 10% of males may have kidney stones some time in their life. Because many kidney stones contain calcium, some people with kidney stones think they should avoid calcium supplements.
>
>
>
> However, the opposite is true. When calcium is taken with foods that are high in oxalates, oxalic acid in the intestine combines with calcium to form insoluble calcium oxalate crystals that are eliminated in the stool. This form of oxalate cannot be absorbed into the body. When calcium is low in the diet, oxalic acid is soluble in the liquid portion of the contents of the intestine (called chyme) and is readily absorbed from the intestine into the bloodstream. If oxalic acid is very high in the blood being filtered by the kidney, it may combine with calcium to form crystals that may block urine flow and cause severe pain**.

### SeaJane — 2012-10-24T16:24:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/a-different-take-on-calcium-and-oxalates.4477/post-59971>

The taking of Ca or Mag right before a meal is a pretty standard practice for folks on the Trying Low Oxalates Yahoo group. Most find this helpful and that it does not exacerbate their issues.

### villjamur_stevenson — 2012-10-24T16:24:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/a-different-take-on-calcium-and-oxalates.4477/post-59972>

> However, the opposite is true. When calcium is taken with foods that are high in oxalates, oxalic acid in the intestine combines with calcium to form insoluble calcium oxalate crystals that are eliminated in the stool. This form of oxalate cannot be absorbed into the body. When calcium is low in the diet, oxalic acid is soluble in the liquid portion of the contents of the intestine (called chyme) and is readily absorbed from the intestine into the bloodstream. If oxalic acid is very high in the blood being filtered by the kidney, it may combine with calcium to form crystals that may block urine flow and cause severe pain.

If this is the different take, then what is the normal take on it?

I've always thought calcium binding to oxalate in the gut was the normal view???

On a side note, sort of related - casein lowers uric acid levels.

### chocolate — 2012-10-24T16:57:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/a-different-take-on-calcium-and-oxalates.4477/post-59977>

Calcium causes kidney stones, so don't supp.

### chocolate — 2012-10-24T16:57:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/a-different-take-on-calcium-and-oxalates.4477/post-59978>

That's the other take.

### chocolate — 2012-10-25T03:29:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/a-different-take-on-calcium-and-oxalates.4477/post-60070>

> **PSJane;61563 wrote: The taking of Ca or Mag right before a meal is a pretty standard practice for folks on the Trying Low Oxalates Yahoo group. Most find this helpful and that it does not exacerbate their issues.
>
>
> I've been seriously asleep at the wheel on this one.
>
> [http://www.stopthethyroidmadness.com/2011/07/03/low-magnesium-uk-article-hypothyroid-spouse/](http://http://www.stopthethyroidmadness.com/2011/07/03/low-magnesium-uk-article-hypothyroid-spouse/)
>
>
>
> But it turns out if you consistently eat a LOT of food containing high levels of oxalates, on top of what your body naturally produces, your body will have excessive levels. This may be exactly what happened to me this year, also causing chronic hives (scratch~scratch~scratch). The latter pushed me to do research, and voila, I realized I had consumed far too many high-oxalate foods (which in turn produced excessive histamine). And my eyes popped out when I read this:
>
>
>
> “oxalates strongly bind to minerals and vice versa (e.g., calcium, magnesium, zinc and potassium**), and reduces the absorption of your minerals as they both come out via your urine.”
>
>
>
> It’s a negative love affair for your cellular mineral levels!!
>
>
>
> What are foods which are the highest in oxalates? In alphabetical order, they include:
>
>
>
> almonds, amaranth, black beans, brazil nuts, beets, blackberries, buckwheat, carob chips, carrots, cashew nuts, cannellini beans, celery, chocolate, corn meal, dried apricots, cooked tomatoes, great northern beans, green peppers, hazelnuts, marshmallow root, milk thistle, navy beans, oil of oregano, okra, peanuts, pecans, pine nuts, pink beans, pinto beans, potato chips, potato flour, rice bran, rhubarb, sesame seeds and tahini, slippery elm bark, all soy, spinach, star fruit, sweet potatoes, teff (flour and whole grain), quinoa (whole grain), white bean flour, and yucca powder.

# Thread: Project: Bone Broth
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/project-bone-broth.4478/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 12
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### ealachan — 2012-10-24T17:30:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/project-bone-broth.4478/post-59980>

So, I went in search of beef bones last night to make a batch of broth. Marrow bones at my local grocery store are $3.99 a friggin' pound!!! NOT EVEN FROM GRASSFED COWS. This seems SO expensive to me. Am I way off base, or does this sound like highway robbery to you, too?

I've started saving bones from roasts and steaks that I cook, but I wanted to supplement those with some bigger bones. Does anyone have any experience using something like knuckle bones (aka: "bones for dogs", per my local deli guy) as opposed to the pre-cut marrow bones? I'm wondering if having lots of little bones as opposed to just one or two BIG bones really matters that much? Also, a knuckle bone has a ton of connective tissue and cartiledge in it - wouldn't that make for a more gelatinous broth, theoretically? I don't have the means to split a big bone like a knuckle, if that matters any...

### Michael — 2012-10-24T17:54:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/project-bone-broth.4478/post-59987>

Depends how big your stockpot is I guess.

There's a video online of Cate Shanahan's husband, who's a chef, making stock, and that is exactly what he's using.

You could probably find it by googling. Cate demonstrates how the joint works, and Sean Croxton is assisting, too.

Maybe include some kombu, too?

### ealachan — 2012-10-24T18:00:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/project-bone-broth.4478/post-59988>

I want to use a pressure cooker, a la Nom Nom Paleo. I've done it in the crockpot before and it came out well, but it took forever and the smell became totally overwhelming after the first 12 hours or so.

Definitely want to try adding some Kombu, yes. I've been toasting sheets of kombu and eating them like chips and I really enjoy it.

If I can do some recon and figure out when the meat cutter is around at the local mom and pop grocery store, I might be able to get him to split some knuckle bones for me so that they fit into the pressure cooker more easily. It seems fitting that I'd be contemplating stalking the local meatcutter, honestly...LOL

### FarmersDaughter — 2012-10-24T18:14:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/project-bone-broth.4478/post-59989>

I use knuckles unsplit all the time, more than marrow bones, actually, and the gelatinous goodness oozes out after a couple hours. Looks yummy, and makes nice firm "jello". I used pork hocks a lot to make broth for split pea soup (or, well, I did, anyway) and they dissolve entirely, all that ligamenty and cartilaginous stuff makes for serious umami in the broth.

I don't have a pressure cooker, but I put my big stewpot into the oven at 250 and let it simmer all night after I've kept an eye on it all day.

If you let them sit for a while in the cooker with some vinegar, you can eventually dig a hole into the bone and let the marrow and other innards leach out. Takes a while with beef bones, veal knuckles are quicker but harder to find around here.

### ealachan — 2012-10-24T18:28:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/project-bone-broth.4478/post-59993>

mmm, pork hocks. One of my favorite things in the world is new potatoes and green beans cooked in the crock pot with smoked pork hocks. I haven't made it in forever, but damn it's good.

Sorry, mild pork-hock-related tangent there. Ahem.

### FarmersDaughter — 2012-10-24T19:00:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/project-bone-broth.4478/post-59997>

I was recently introduced to the delish that is nÃ©w potatoes this summer.

Omg. How did I not know about those?

Come summer, I'll definitely be indulging in that combo.

### diane — 2012-10-24T19:42:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/project-bone-broth.4478/post-60003>

I throw any kind of bones in to make bone broth. Neck bones, knuckles, marrow bones. whatever I buy for the dogs - well, I just take some out for me too. Then I sometimes put some bone broth in their dishes - they love it!

### Shijin13 — 2012-10-24T20:20:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/project-bone-broth.4478/post-60010>

Before we got into our meat CSA I'd ask the butcher at WFM if he had any bones left over from local beef. No particular cut. He gave them to me for $1.99/lb which is what we pay for them at the meat CSA now

### BJK77 — 2012-10-24T21:36:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/project-bone-broth.4478/post-60022>

I use giant knuckle bones when I can get them and they make an awesome, gelatinous broth. I've only been able to find them at one local farm and they're $1.99 lb here. If I can't get them I just use marrow bones, but my broth is never as thick or flavorful with them. The cheapest I can get them for is $4.99 lb, but I do love eating the marrow. Yum!

### ealachan — 2012-10-24T21:45:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/project-bone-broth.4478/post-60025>

> **BJK77;61616 wrote:** I use giant knuckle bones when I can get them and they make an awesome, gelatinous broth. I've only been able to find them at one local farm and they're $1.99 lb here. If I can't get them I just use marrow bones, but my broth is never as thick or flavorful with them. The cheapest I can get them for is $4.99 lb, but I do love eating the marrow. Yum!

Do you bother to have the knuckle bones split, or do you use them whole?

### Glamazon — 2012-10-24T21:51:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/project-bone-broth.4478/post-60026>

add some splashes of wine or apple cider vinegar. it helps leach the minerals out of the bones into the broth.

### BJK77 — 2012-10-24T23:37:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/project-bone-broth.4478/post-60035>

> **ealachan;61619 wrote:** Do you bother to have the knuckle bones split, or do you use them whole?

I use them whole.

# Thread: I'm not making this up
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/im-not-making-this-up.4480/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 6
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Michael — 2012-10-24T20:10:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/im-not-making-this-up.4480/post-60009>

I was listening to a podcast interview today ...

Anyway, the interviewee says that a biochemist at the NIH decided to qualify in medicine and pursue that instead. So this doctor, as a new doctor, is going the rounds with a more senior doctor - a registrar or something. The senior doctor says to him that he's got two men who are dying, because they've been amateur mushroom collecting and made an identification error. Go and sit with them till they die and make them comfortable, he says.

So the junior doctor does. But after a while he just can't stand it. He telephones old colleagues at the NIH and says, Have you got any suggestions? Is there anything you've found that may bear on this? An old colleague tells him that it looks like alpha-lipoic acid can help. The junior doctor gives that to these men, and both survive their ordeal. But get this.

The senior doctor tears him off a strip. He says, I already told the family they'd die and there's nothing we could do, and what you did wasn't sanctioned. _Don't ever do that again!_

In other words, a doctor was actually telling another doctor that he should have let people die rather than embarrass him.

Honestly, he said that:

[http://www.blogtalkradio.com/drloradio/2012/10/24/complicated-cases-naturally-with-dr-tim-gerstmar-1](http://http://www.blogtalkradio.com/drloradio/2012/10/24/complicated-cases-naturally-with-dr-tim-gerstmar-1)

### Optimalbound — 2012-10-24T21:21:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/im-not-making-this-up.4480/post-60020>

That's just incredible, Michael. When a doctor is more concerned about not losing face than about saving lives, something is horribly, horribly wrong.

That particular story is told at 70:05 in the podcast, by the way.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-10-24T23:41:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/im-not-making-this-up.4480/post-60036>

Sadly, I am not surprised one bit.

### nonchalant — 2012-10-25T00:55:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/im-not-making-this-up.4480/post-60047>

I am appalled. We really have to remember that we are ultimately responsible for our own health.

### Shijin13 — 2012-10-25T01:16:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/im-not-making-this-up.4480/post-60051>

Considering my own n=1 not surprised.

### Destiny — 2012-10-25T03:09:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/im-not-making-this-up.4480/post-60066>

I guess I am naive. I would never consider that something like this is possible. Good to know!

# Thread: Nausea while icing
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nausea-while-icing.4481/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 8
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### FarmersDaughter — 2012-10-24T20:34:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nausea-while-icing.4481/post-60012>

The last several times I have either ct or spot iced (belly or between shoulder blades) I have had nausea. Never enough to cause vomiting, but certainly enough to be decidedly unpleasant. Mostly like a carsickish feeling...motion sick.

My water is 55-60 out of the tap so no ice used yet, body temp is consistently 99+, and I maintain a pink to reddish skin color when icing ( no itching or urticaria). I should mention that it doesn't seem coincide with a general feeling of malaise or otherwise, in fact when I am feeling crummy it doesn't seem to happen, so in my mind, that shrinks the odds of a herx reaction, doesn't it? It disappears as soon as I'm out of the cold.

Any ideas what causes this? I'm pushing through, but I'm curious. If its covered in a post and I've missed it in the multiple re readings, please point in the right direction! I'm wondering if its related to ACTH/aMSH production....I had an ACTH stim done to check pit function some years ago, and it caused sudden intense nausea that last about 5 minutes, then vanished as quickly as it appeared.

### nonchalant — 2012-10-25T01:01:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nausea-while-icing.4481/post-60050>

Sorry, FD, I didn't have any nausea when first starting CT. Did you do the ramp-up? Start with face dunks for a couple weeks? It took me several weeks to get down to the 50-55F range for baths.

### Patty Cakes — 2012-10-25T03:07:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nausea-while-icing.4481/post-60065>

I had a little nausea for about the first four months. I think it was the detoxing from estrogen or other bad guys. Keep doing it and the nausea will subside sooner or later. I also started to itch on my back a lot. That went away too. I started in the 60s temps for the bath and worked my way down to 55.

### FarmersDaughter — 2012-10-25T21:05:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nausea-while-icing.4481/post-60183>

Hmm. Yep, ramped up, and actually have been doing the face dunks since April,stopped doing CT because of rapid weight gain...20 pounds in roughly two months. I didnt ramp up (down?) bathtemp, just dove in to +/- 60ish. It comes and goes, seemingly regardless of meals and am/pm.

I'll reread CT6 and see what I've missed there...like I said, not THAT big a deal, I'm just curious about the whys.

I've started to itch a little, which I'm taking as a good sign...and I have significantly reduced "fluff" around my waist, so I'm absolutely continuing. Heh...couple weeks down, couple months to go. DIM and altering estrogen metabolism might have something to do with it too,who knows.

One of the many mysteries I guess.

TY

### BJK77 — 2012-10-25T23:16:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nausea-while-icing.4481/post-60190>

I've had random, off and on episodes of nausea from CT. I'm sure there could be many causes for it, but Jack mentioned to me that in my case it could be a detox reaction, possibly from halide toxicity. Like I said, for me it's totally random. It will happen for a week straight and then not again for a month or two. Very bizarre!

### kcorcoran@comcast.net — 2012-10-26T04:36:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nausea-while-icing.4481/post-60257>

This may be tmi, but I had a major gut reaction clean out from ct last spring. I think it disturbs the gut biofilm and ramps up the immune system. Are you getting salivation with the nausea? Seems to be a vagal nerve stimulation thing is my guess. Maybe give your system a few days to recover between ct sessions. The body needs time to clear out toxins.

### FarmersDaughter — 2012-10-26T07:07:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nausea-while-icing.4481/post-60269>

Hmmm....halides, biofilms, and somewhere I ran across mycotoxins as well.

Truthfully, it's probably a many causes, single symptom thing.

I think I may start taking NAC again...it's good for helping the various hepatic pathways do their thing...glutathione, I think, among others?

You know, I had the random thought that it might be vagus nerve stuff, but I haven't wrapped my head around all that it does, so I may just been whistling Dixie too. I dunno. All I know is that it's affecting my sinuses and my schnozzle in addition to the whole queasiness business.

And THIRSTY. Dear heaven I haven't been this thirsty in years.

### PrimalPam — 2012-10-26T23:32:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nausea-while-icing.4481/post-60367>

I, too, get queasy sometimes. The strange thing for me is I lay on my back in the tub (50-52F) for 20-25 mins, then I turn over and lay on my stomach for 20-25 mins. I don't get the queasiness until I've turned over onto my stomach. I just lay there and deal with it, and it's never been bad enough to get out.

I do eat protein & fat just prior, and I rarely ever drink alcohol. I just think it's a bit of detox going on, so I figure it's a good thing.

# Thread: Look at this, Inger!
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/look-at-this-inger.4484/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 9
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Michael — 2012-10-24T21:43:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/look-at-this-inger.4484/post-60024>

Quote from Inger:

> I am wondering what is in Birch ash that is so good? When I put the ash leftovers from the sauna out for our sheeps to taste on they love it. If we have burned some threes or bushes on their pasture thei love to lie down in the ash at times...
>
>
>
> I also read about children in the second world war when people were so poor and the children ate ash too. I am thinking there must be some important minerals in it?
>
>
> I don't know whether it works or not, but it turns out that it is sold as a health food.
>
>
>
> It says it provides:
>
>
>
> Potassium (K), Copper (Cu), Magnesium (Mg) Manganese (Mn), calcium (Ca) Molybdenum (Mo), phosphorus (P), iron (Fe), Selenium (Se), Zinc (Zn), Chromium (Cr) Cobalt (Co)

[http://www.halsans.com/en/null/immuforsvarshojandemedel/birch-ash-powder/](http://http://www.halsans.com/en/null/immuforsvarshojandemedel/birch-ash-powder/)

### Inger — 2012-10-25T11:41:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/look-at-this-inger.4484/post-60109>

Michael, that is intresting, thank you! We can buy the birch ash extract here too, liquid. It cost 30 € for one bottle..huh. I thought about making it myself. Birch was the magic tree of my ancestors. I read somwhere it was the first tree that popped up after the ice melted in Scandinavia!

### Michael — 2012-10-25T11:57:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/look-at-this-inger.4484/post-60110>

It's sown by Vainamoinen in the Kalevala, I see:

[http://www.sacred-texts.com/neu/kveng/kvrune02.htm](http://http://www.sacred-texts.com/neu/kveng/kvrune02.htm)

### Inger — 2012-10-25T12:17:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/look-at-this-inger.4484/post-60112>

Michael..?! You are amazing! You kind of find all kind of weird things..! I wonder about you!

I have read this maybe once in school almost 30 years ago..lol I always found the old tales of Finland like Kalevala kind of sad back then... it might be because I am Norwegian and the culture was foreign to me. Sure differs a lot from the Norwegian and Schwedisch one!

### Michael — 2012-10-25T12:46:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/look-at-this-inger.4484/post-60114>

It would be a quite different cultural root, I guess. It's a Finno-Ugric language, isn't it? The language and mythology are akin to Lithuanian.

I don't know I like the _Kalevala that much. But that might be because I only know it in translation, where so much is lost.

The most well-known story seems to be that of Kullervo and his sister, which is a very tragic story. But there's knockabout humour in there, too - like the ladies' man Lemminkainen, seducing all the women on an island.

I like the Jean Sibelius settings of the Kalevala_ a lot. Shame he drank himself mad.

### Inger — 2012-10-25T13:41:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/look-at-this-inger.4484/post-60115>

Yes it is a Finno-Ugric language. Totally different to Norwegian or Swedish. My parents still cannot speak fluent even if they have lived here for almost 40 years..lol.

You know people drink here a lot. That is sad for sure, they drink so much, until very drunk. I lived in Hamburg (Germany) for 10 years and there was a totally different alcohol-behavior. I liked it much better! But yeah.. maybe people get depressed from long dark winters and need to drink.. if they have not too much love in their life..?

I figure I love the dark now first time in my life. What an amazing change. The street lamps are strarting to bothering me huge! They makes it so hard to see the beautiful stars and the heaven! People have cut themselves out from the most beautiful things with their crazy fake lights for sure! lol

I am laying naked on my balkony right now in the fresh air.. enjoying the sun and my day off.. wow Life cannot be better...!

### Michael — 2012-10-26T08:45:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/look-at-this-inger.4484/post-60271>

I dislike street lamps, too. I preferred it when I lived in the country and there were none. You couldn't see your hand in front of your face walking back from the pub.

Did you know the action of the greatest poem in Old English - _Beowulf_ - is set in Sweden? England lies sort of midway between Scandinavia and the European mainstream represented by France and her neighbours in geographical terms. The Norman Conquest moved us decisively out of the orbit of the first and into that of the second.

### x_danny_x — 2012-10-27T01:20:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/look-at-this-inger.4484/post-60379>

where can i buy this in the united states, more precisely online in the usa?

### Michael — 2012-10-27T11:03:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/look-at-this-inger.4484/post-60418>

> **x\_danny\_x;61995 wrote:** where can i buy this in the united states, more precisely online in the usa?

What the film they made of it with Ray Winstone? Haven't seen it and don't know what it's like but it seems to be around in the US:

[http://www.amazon.com/gp/aw/d/B0011NVC98/ref=mp\_s\_a\_5?pi=SL75&qid=1351324773&sr=8-5](http://http://www.amazon.com/gp/aw/d/B0011NVC98/ref=mp_s_a_5?pi=SL75&qid=1351324773&sr=8-5)

# Thread: Vast Welsh fish trap spotted on Google Earth
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/vast-welsh-fish-trap-spotted-on-google-earth.4490/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 4
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Michael — 2012-10-25T09:18:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/vast-welsh-fish-trap-spotted-on-google-earth.4490/post-60099>

The story's from 2009, but worth seeing anyway.

> Stretching more than 280 yards along the sea bed, the V-shaped structure was used to catch fish without the need for a boat or rod. Scientists believe it is one of the biggest of its kind.
>
>
>
> The trap close to Poppit Sands on the Teifi Estuary in Dyfed was discovered by archaeologists studying aerial photographs of the West Wales coast.

[http://www.dailymail.co.uk/sciencetech/article-1162395/Google-Earth-reveals-fish-trap-rocks-1-000-years-ago-British-coast.html](http://http://www.dailymail.co.uk/sciencetech/article-1162395/Google-Earth-reveals-fish-trap-rocks-1-000-years-ago-British-coast.html)

The sheer size of this thing shows how important fish must have been in the diet of the people at the time it was built. And yet "for a millennium it has lain undisturbed beneath the waves a stone's throw from one of Britain's best-loved beaches".

Interestingly, the article says that fish traps and weirs had become controversial at the time of the Norman Conquest, owing to their effect on fish stocks.

### nonchalant — 2012-10-30T00:59:18+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/vast-welsh-fish-trap-spotted-on-google-earth.4490/post-60826>

Michael, you dig up the most interesting cites...

Must have been aliens helping them. ;-)

### PrimalPam — 2012-11-01T01:26:51+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/vast-welsh-fish-trap-spotted-on-google-earth.4490/post-61186>

Interesting! And yes, it shows that seafood has been important for a long time. Thanks for posting it!

### shah78 — 2012-11-01T02:31:43+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/vast-welsh-fish-trap-spotted-on-google-earth.4490/post-61196>

I'm going to second nonchalant's praise and wonderment. What a gift you have Micheal for uncovering the most bizzare,interesting tidbits/angles of Epi-Paleo "lore". Well done and keep it coming.

# Thread: Hormone therapy may cut Alzheimer's risk in menopausal women
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hormone-therapy-may-cut-alzheimers-risk-in-menopausal-women.4493/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Shijin13 — 2012-10-25T15:36:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hormone-therapy-may-cut-alzheimers-risk-in-menopausal-women.4493/post-60127>

[http://vitals.nbcnews.com/\_news/2012/10/24/14678558-hormone-therapy-may-cut-alzheimers-risk-in-menopausal-women?lite](http://http://vitals.nbcnews.com/_news/2012/10/24/14678558-hormone-therapy-may-cut-alzheimers-risk-in-menopausal-women?lite)

good article - one thing they don't touch on is the type of hormones they looked at. BHRT vs crappy conventional synthetic hormones....

### kathylu — 2012-10-27T15:40:03+0300

# Thread: Glucose and fructose
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/glucose-and-fructose.4494/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 6
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Michael — 2012-10-25T15:52:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/glucose-and-fructose.4494/post-60128>

I may have the details in this wrong. I was very distracted while listening. But here goes.

So Jimmy Moore's guest on the latest podcast was a university researcher, name of Johnson, who says that he used to believe, like Dr. Lustig, that fructose was responsible for obesity. Then he wondered about all carbohydrates. Now he's somehere in between.

What he said is that they found it easy to produce obesity in rodents by feeding them fructose ("fruit sugar"). However, the animals tend not to get obese when given a high proportion of starch (which is, of course, a polysaccaride, and hence a source of glucose when metabolized) in their feed.

He also said that mice that were genetically-engineered not to be able to break down fructose did not get obese.

For both reasons they were persuaded fructose was the key.

But he says after talking to Jimmy before and finding out that Jimmy just could not tolerate any carbohydrate, he began to doubt whether he'd explored everything. So they tried other things, including putting a glucose solution in the animals water-bottles. _That, apparently, does tend to make animals obese. And they also revised their earlier opinion on starch, finding that even starch will _to a certain extent.

But here's the surprise. He says the special mice that couldn't be damaged by fructose also_ could not get fat on glucose or starch. Looking into this, they decided that fuctose actually is_ the key and that what can happen is that glucose can be converted into fructose by liver enzymes. In some people, he says, this gets uprated like mad. And a good way to do this is to drink soda.

They speculate that what makes beer so fattening is that something else in it - the proteins perhaps - help to uprate these enzymes. And it gets even more speculative after this point. ...

I'm not so sure on the beer. I know the SCD people warn people off it, because they say that the malt sugars in it are too complex to be easily broken down, so that they become food for the wrong sort of bacteria. I'd have thought something like that could be equally plausible.

But, at any rate, it is interesting to know that glucose can be converted to fructose by the liver, and that some people's livers do this to an uncontrolled extent.

### FarmersDaughter — 2012-10-25T16:58:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/glucose-and-fructose.4494/post-60132>

Very interesting! Thanks for posting...I enjoy reading your links.

### Michael — 2012-10-25T17:06:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/glucose-and-fructose.4494/post-60134>

Thanks.

Actually, maybe a link would be useful here:

[http://www.thelivinlowcarbshow.com/shownotes/6904/630-dr-richard-johnson-shifts-obesity-culprit-position-from-fructose-to-all-carbohydrates/](http://http://www.thelivinlowcarbshow.com/shownotes/6904/630-dr-richard-johnson-shifts-obesity-culprit-position-from-fructose-to-all-carbohydrates/)

### nonchalant — 2012-10-25T18:42:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/glucose-and-fructose.4494/post-60157>

Why would the liver convert glucose (a useful molecule) into fructose (a toxic molecule that the liver tries to keep out of the circulating blood)? Or do I have that wrong? I thought the liver will cause itself damage (from the fructose) in an attempt to keep fructose from damaging the rest of the body. Take one for the team, if you will.

### Michael — 2012-10-25T19:47:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/glucose-and-fructose.4494/post-60169>

I don't know. He didn't make any suggestions as to why - he just said it did. And darned if I could.

I guess there are actually two questions. Why would it make the conversion at all? (Presumably doing so at a low rate in some situations likely has its uses or we wouldn't do it at all.) And secondly, why does the conversion get up-regulated?

I guess the answer to the second one might be that it's something else to _do with glucose in a hurry (besides turning it into fat) before it does damage.

It seems whatever you do you train yourself to do better. Dr. Cate likes to say that by eating sugar continually (and pretty much continuously, too) most people are training themselves to become very good at burning sugar and very poor at burning fat - because they're down-regulatung their fat-burning enzymes. I'd guess that principle is everywhere in biochemistry - as in life.

I suppose the real takeaway is simply that eating out of the ancestral pattern is going to screw things up in one way or another. It's not that fructose is "bad" or something. It's that we're only likely to be able to deal with it in its "evolutionary package" (to use a Krusism) and at appropriate levels in the appropriate season.

The recent New York Times_ story impresses me. This guy isn't eating as the Paleo people would tell him to. But what he's doing works at least as well (if not better). It's whole foods; it's seasonal; it has the fish. He's not going to be eating anything out of its evolutionary package or out of season, because the island probably doesn't offer an infrastructure where he can.

[http://www.nytimes.com/2012/10/28/magazine/the-island-where-people-forget-to-die.html?pagewanted=all](http://http://www.nytimes.com/2012/10/28/magazine/the-island-where-people-forget-to-die.html?pagewanted=all)

The deeper irony here is that he went to find the American Dream and it nearly killed him. He went back home, and paradise was there all along.

I don't mean it was paradise because he had a long life, but because he had a pain-free and stress-free life running in natural rhythms close to nature, and a social life in cooperation and harmony with his neighbours, and because the food probably tasted of something and satisfied him.

### Destiny — 2012-10-27T13:26:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/glucose-and-fructose.4494/post-60420>

Just my practical observation. I had a really hard time losing weight on paleo and/or low carb diet with a bit of fruit (usually berries). It took me 10 months to lose 9 lbs. I also had a lot of times when I was going up and down 4 lbs for no reason. That was really frustrating.

Since cutting ANY fruit out, I am down additional 7 lbs. I am at 136 lbs and finally getting closer to my weight around 130 lbs that I was at most of my life.

This also goes with my FBG that was between 95-110. Now, my FBG is between 78-84.

### MamaGrok — 2012-10-25T17:25:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/need-accountability-stat.4496/post-60140>

It's been almost one year since my last binge. The last two months have been tough, though ... but only with one thing - fruit. And it must stop! I've got crazy cravings for fruit, crazy, and went crazy with dried fruit yesterday (nothin' but fruit).

Today I've allowed myself one apple to taper off (whole, not dried), and that's it. NO MORE FRUIT till like April.

Help me!

So far I've had a 70g protein/fat breakfast with radish greens, and the apple. I'm posting here every time I eat (should be just one more time) or feel compelled to eat for the rest of the day.

Pray for me! I sense this going downhill fast if I don't get these cravings in control now. Right now.

### Shijin13 — 2012-10-25T17:45:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/need-accountability-stat.4496/post-60143>

question for you MamaG. wondering if the Hashi's dx, might have triggered something that causing the craving for fruits, as a result of all the restrictions?

Can you increase your fat consumption, to help keep it at bay? Of recent I've had serious chocolate cravings... and for a while I just had chocolate every night until I ran out of my 100% dark chocolate.... then stopped... but suddenly the chocolate craving came back - so I did it again... no there's nothing in the house... and I don't want the chocolate... not saying its the same thing...

I'm having an apple or winter squash ever couple of days right now, b/c they're in season - I don't eat a lot of them...but I always pair it w/something FATTY. could you cook the apple in oodles of ghee (I forget if you were given the OK to eat Ghee - as some w/AI can and other can't) or Coconut Oil to address the craving.... hang in there... you'll make it you always do!

### MamaGrok — 2012-10-25T17:50:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/need-accountability-stat.4496/post-60146>

Interesting thought ... but I don't think it's the case here. I'll say why and you can poke holes in my logic, lol.

The cravings started in June. I was restricting almost anything but meat, fat, & well-cooked greens at the time. I ate some fruit here and there, occasionally too much, all summer. The Hashi's dx came in mid-September. At the same time, I decided to ignore the bloating & cramps & runs and just eat. Meat, fat, non-starchy veggies, but allowing FODMAPs again, here and there. The cravings continued. I think it's been worse the last two months, but it may just be that I'm really facing the end of warm weather and can't use the summer excuse to eat fruit anymore.

I always try to eat meat & fat before the fruit, but then I just keep coming back to the fruit again and again even after a full meal. Want to beat my head against a wall! I think it's just going to have to be will power that gets me through this one, but it's so frustrating bc I had NO fruit cravings at all for so long.

On a good note, I think my thyroid is LOVING the Naturethroid. Week 2. On a bad note, I woke up this morning after 8.5hrs feeling like I needed 9 more hrs of sleep and 3lb heavier than yesterday. !

### Shijin13 — 2012-10-25T19:17:36+0300

maybe its not the time to have 1. IDK. I'm inclined to think that there's something in the Hashi's driving this for you specifically. on the days you don't have the fruit - how do you feel, how do you feel the following day? what about on the days you have the fruit and spiral into the dried fruit? how do you feel, what about the next day? how's your sleep etc? additionally, could this be something your thyroid (figure low T3 possibly) is driving you towards the fruit?

my n=1, is when carbs decrease - my TSH kicks into high gear, but I'm not making enough T4, to convert to T3, I get HOT "flashes" but not the menopausal kind, I feel like my furnace is smoking, and I just can't cool down, yet I'm having problems getting myself in to the ice bath b/c of the Low T3. go back and re-read BG11... you might find something here... I think your body is trying to carb up to replace the fact you're not making enough T3, so its looking for the natural circadian solution, only you're moving into winter and its not right - but your body doesn't know it. have you run thyroid labs since you started NDT? might be time for another set to see if you need to up the dose? if you fix the supply issues for the T4 ->T3 your cravings might just go away....

# Thread: This breakfast gives crazy energy!
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/this-breakfast-gives-crazy-energy.4497/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 40
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Inger — 2012-10-25T17:48:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/this-breakfast-gives-crazy-energy.4497/post-60145>

So, that was my breakfast today, a breakfast that starts to get more and more often on my menu..

Small raw and fresh Baltic herring heads, a few had roe so I throw that in too, spaghetti algae, water. Made a big cup voll. Nose closed it "tastes" totally like baby porrige I used to love! Smooth, velvet like and a little salty, the saltiness I can taste strangely, from the seaweeds, and it makes it nice! After drinking the cup I rinse my mouth still nose closed and put a spoonful of wild lingonberries in my mouth and chew on them before breathing again. Easy as anything. No bad tastes or feelings at all.

After, I cooked the rest of the fish after removing the stomach and intestines and rinsing. I cook it just for a few minutes in water, add seaweeds, curcumin / turmeric, seasalt and then precooked and prefrozen mussels. I eat a big bowl of this too. Boah it is totally delish!! I never get tired of it. For dessert I eat wild berries as much as I want.

Now, I can tell you one feels just great after this meal..!

Even this groggy girl. Spontaneous pic from today soon after waking up,

with my moms old pullover on because here is coool.. and fuzzy hair.. sorry lol

And yeah.. do not forget to look at the sun rising.. This is from my balcony.

### Inger — 2012-10-25T19:46:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/this-breakfast-gives-crazy-energy.4497/post-60168>

Yes Janagram. Sunrises are so beautiful always! Now, after I have started to look at them, and into the sunset too, and avoiding fake lights as much as possible, I have gone so sensible about having to be in artificial lights after dark. It feels like my whole beeing is against it! I even use dark sunglasses when out and on my way home from work in the dark because of the streetlights. They are annoying to me too. People do stare.. what the heck it is so dark outside and just a few streetlamps on and she has sunglasses on...? lol

I cannot believe I just started to look at the sunrise and sunset now, because of Jack. I never did regularely before really! Now I want not to miss it! I don't know how to say it but it has almost something very spiritual to me...

That is how it is in always in my appartement in the evenings these days, with candels only, pic from the other day. I use sunglasses of course reading my computer.

### Inger — 2012-10-25T20:00:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/this-breakfast-gives-crazy-energy.4497/post-60171>

> **caroline;61759 wrote:** You look amazing Inger! That sounds like a big breakfast. When do you eat next? Do you have lunch and dinner? Are you still eating high meat? I would like to eat high meat sometimes but don't know how to start. Should I just get some grass fed beef at the grocery store?

Caroline.. I eat really much. That is a normal breakfast for me..

I eat 3 times a day. The next time around 1 pm or so. I never have very rigid eating schedules, it can vary.

Next meal was today 1,2 pund of raw salmon, made into tartar with onion, seaweeds, ginger, cilantro...

Then for dinner 1 pound of raw grassfed beef as carpaccio with raw beef fat slices, raw onion, raw dried wild mushrooms, olive oil and balsamic vinegar. Wild berries for dessert. I ate a lot! I craved it. I always eat as much as my heart desires. I counted my calories one day just out of curiosity and they were between 2500 and 3000. Yeah I know quite a lot because I do not exersice at all. But I am not gaining eather, ok I have maybe gained a tiny bit since summer but it is okay. I bet it is the huge amount of wild berries I eat..lol Bears gain on wild berries too so I figured I will allow myself the same. Ha.

I am going to make a batch of high meat these days, I just got some fresh grassfed beefheart! I had no high meat for months!

Yes, just get some good quality meat that is not prefrozen, and cut into pieces and put in a glass jar. Only around 50% full, there needs to be oxygen. Then close, and put in the fridge. Air it every other day or so for a minute. After a few weeks it is ready.

>

>
>
>
> women typically consumed between 3,000 and 3,500 calories per day while men consumed 4,000 - 5,000 calories,

[http://www.ifbb.org.uk/changing-diet](http://http://www.ifbb.org.uk/changing-diet)

It's really interesting. You're eating half a kilogram or more of fish or meat at a sitting sometimes. And the results speak for themselves: you feel great and look amazing.

Yet someone like Jimmy Moore says he has to eat no more than 12% of his calories as protein or he balloons out. You must be eating far more protein than he is, and yet he's a foot taller than you and perhaps twice your weight.

I dunno - maybe your metabolism is just normal and his is just totally screwed from the years of eating nothing but processed carbs.

You don't currently eat much in the way of vegetables, Inger? It's mainly fish/meat and the berries? And do you use olive oil, ghee, coconut oil, etc.? Or is your fat percentage probably lower than people might expect?

EDIT

Do you eat any dairy at all? Or is it all out for you even goat's/ewe's milk cheese, yoghurt etc.?

I sometimes feel weird eating so much, because you can read so much around about woman eating 1200-1500 kalories and counting and all that and I think OMG I eat way too much. Sure I am not skinny but I feel great. I am thinking as for now I am fine with beeing not skinny.
>

> I do think I have a quite healthy metabolism, I am so warm always.
>
>

>
>
>
> You don't currently eat much in the way of vegetables, Inger? It's mainly fish/meat and the berries? And do you use olive oil, ghee, coconut oil, etc.? Or is your fat percentage probably lower than people might expect?

That is strange about Jimmy Moore for sure! I did not know..

I eat very littlevegetables. Once in a while one cucumber, I like their juicyness a lot. And once in a while a dirty carrot from the garden. But there can be days in between with no vegetables. I often eat a few wild herbs when outside, just a few leaves or so. Sometimes I eat a green salad but very seldom. I would say seaweeds are my vege of choise these days after reading Epi Paleo Rx.

When it comes to fat.. I want to quit coconut oil for the winter but I have done it a bit halfheartedly..lol. Because I use it for my coffee and sometimes I still make it. I use handwarm organic coffee with cardamom, 1 eggyolk and 1-2 large spoons of coconutoil and mix it into the most delish foamy drink.. I some days had only black coffee but when I feel like having something special in the morning I still sometimes do the bulletproof version.. And then one day I made bark from organic cocoa powder and coconut oil and a tiny bit raw wild honey and ate a lot. hmmm. But my goal is to quit it for the winter for sure!

Then, I use olive oil too. I use many spoons a day if eating carpaccio. And sometimes I do it on my cooked fish after cooking if the fish is not fatty. I love to eat slices of raw grassfed beef fat if I can get it. It is my favorit fat. I can eat a lot of it just plain!

Dairy.. I sometimes cheat at work and have some HWC in coffee. It does me no good and I should not. From that I gain for sure!

But I love raw organic goats milk if I can get it. Sometimes I buy a bottle or two and drink it plain, it is so delish. It does me no bad AFAIK. Only the regular HWC at work is real bad. I eat no dairy normally. I never buy cream, but I seldom have it when beeing invated for dinner and there is wild berries with HWC. Very seldom some good raw French goat or sheep cheese.

I eat nuts too at times. My favorit are Siberian Pine nuts, they are wild grown and so healthy! I just ordered some.. I would eat only these for nuts if I could. I feel great from them. Not so from the regular almonds I sometimes eat in my mothers kitchen..lol Sometimes I eat raw organic macadamias. But nuts I eat not regularely at all. Avocad also seldom, one here and there thats it.

In summer I like coconut! I eat the dried coconutflakes too.

Oh.. and the eggyolk foam.. it is my weakness..lol. I whisk up 4 organic eggyolks with a half teaspoon raw wild honey into thick foam, and eat with wild berries. Now.. I think it makes me a little swollen.. but it is my comfort food. I am going to quit it for the winter though, or eat it very seldom! Yes.. lots of improvement to do for me for sure!

What I _don't_ have though is the sense of vibrant energy you seem to have. ### Inger — 2012-10-26T08:59:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/this-breakfast-gives-crazy-energy.4497/post-60273>

Michael, are you eating much of your food raw?

I am quite sure that eating a big part raw is a secret for my vibrant energy. I got a big AHA experience as I read Jacks BG series.. especially that about bacterias and viruses. OMG when we cook something we kill the bacterias and viruses too for sure! Our poor guts!

All of a sudden it was clear to me why the tiredness disappeared as I started to eat raw. And it never come back. I eat most of my food raw you see. It makes me feel like flying..lol I have nothing against some gently cooked seafood here and there though. But raw is certainly more healing for me at least. I could never eat cooked eggs, sausage, meatballs, fried bacon... as many do for breakfast, and feel great! I guess it would make me fat too. IDK. I always was fatter eating cooked. And less energy. Less happy.

If I ate like Jimmy Moore, I would feel terrible.

I need the high meat once in the while, I need the moose organs, raw, once in a while, the raw lambshearts, the raw seafoods.. wild berries.. wild herbs.. these all makes me feel jut GREAT! I can feel very well what my body appreciate, and my mind too. I cannot help it, but it is how it is. I am addicted to feel good so I follow my bodys needs. I have figured delish recipes too that are raw and please my palate if I feel like it. Recipes that makes me feel just great. Sometimes we need something very enjoyable not only fishheads..lol

> To be honest I think modern diets and one too many beers and late nights probably do things you don't bounce back from quickly.

You know what I think Michael? I think we could bounce back awfully fast if we want to! It depends what are we willing to do. If drinking raw fishheads, raw fish, seaweeds, high meat, raw hearts and meat and fat.. raw fresh oysters.. any powerfood out there from the wilderness, I bet one would feel amazing fast!

### Michael — 2012-10-27T16:51:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/this-breakfast-gives-crazy-energy.4497/post-60440>

> **x\_danny\_x;61999 wrote:** a question about eating raw ... what ... made you say "poor guts"?

I guess she's thinking that bacteria on and in the fish would include some that would be beneficial to the stomach, and that cooking the fish would destroy those. This would be similar to one of the reasons that some people prefer unpasteurized milk.

However, high heat can damage proteins and promote rancidity in fats, so there could be other reasons why the food makes her feel good when she doesn't cook it. When she does cook the herrings she does it gently and briefly in simmering water.

Depends on the food really. Some vegetable foods have to be cooked. Cassava or kidney beans could kill you if you ate them raw. "Animal foods" - as long as they're from a clean source - seem readily assimilable with little or no cooking. Few people would cook oysters. The French have steak tartare; the Japanese, sushi.

Richard Wrangham, as someone comments in another thread, has a whole book on the anthropology and prehistory of this.

### Inger — 2012-10-31T10:00:16+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/this-breakfast-gives-crazy-energy.4497/post-61046>

> **fitness@home;61928 wrote:** Ok, that was fun. I freaked out the contractor installing tile in our home with the smoothie. He watched as I put smelt, the juice of a lemon, and some seaweed into the Vitamix. By the way, the seaweed does add a slightly salty flavor to the concoction and is a nice addition.
>
>
>
> The look on his face as I gulped it down was priceless .

LOL... Fitness@home, I can tell you from personal experiences, one gets lots of additional funny moments into life without even trying, eating Epi Paleo.. which is adding to the positives for sure

Like this one.. It was in Germany, in the train and I was hungry on my way home from work. Some guys in their 20ies sat down close to me and started to chat with me. They asked if I'll come to a local party in a city close by. I said.. nooo I am tired I need to go home and eat my food I just buyed. They asked me what that was then. I said..hmm.. calf liver. They asked how would I prepair it. I said I eat it just plain raw. They could not believe it and said they want to see it. I said, no not here, this train is full of people! But they insisted. So I then grabbed my bloody liver from my bag and just munched it down..mmm it was so good! It was thin sliced and delish and tender. Now.. everyone around was STARING at me. A old lady said then.. hmm how does that taste? I said - great, little sweet like chocolate! She said, hmm that must be healthy for sure and smiled. But they stared in a nice way.

I then said to the guys, you want to taste the liver too? I have enough for you too! But they said no thanks..lol. I said, okay I have some raw organic carrots here, maybe you want them instead? They looked happy and said, yes! So I gave them each one organic carrot and they munched on it happily with a smile on their face. It was a totally funny trip! I will never forget it for sure.

### Inger — 2012-10-31T10:13:33+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/this-breakfast-gives-crazy-energy.4497/post-61047>

> **x\_danny\_x;61999 wrote:** a question about eating raw, i really dont have the time to read everything on the BG series. what was mentioned about the viruses and bacteria being killed that made you say "poor guts"?

Danny, you need to read BG 1 and 2 and all the others too. Now.. Jack does not said it directly you need to eat your food raw but that was what additionally got from the whole thing. I just thought it must be logic! He told the story about the centarians in Okinava and that they got the "long life" mutation from a virus from raw fish.. and then I checked that viruses and bakterias almost all get killed by heat and then I figured I need this mutations too.. lol.. and then.. when one gets lots of viruses and bakterias it could help my body in many ways! So this summer when eating strawberries from the garden I always looked out for the one already eaten by some birds or snails.. to hopefully get some more viruses in..lol They taste the best anyways, that I can tell for sure!

I do not recommend this to anyone else but this is how I do it.

Now, look. Our guts needs a lot of different, good bacterias! So I eat the carrots dirty too. And I let my meat rot in a glass jar(must be aired once in a while!), or just sit in the fridge, open on a wooden plate. It dries a little on the surface and get a nice taste. I then just cut nice thin slices when I want some, I eat it raw. It gets a taste almost like salami.

Danny.. sorry I have not time to look it all out for you and copy and paste. If I have more time one day I could do it but these days I am just so busy! You could maybe print the BG series out and read in the train or something?

### Inger — 2012-10-31T10:17:55+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/this-breakfast-gives-crazy-energy.4497/post-61048>

> **Marccus;62580 wrote:** it looks like very awesome breakfast to me! just want to ask as i am unable to understand the exact recipe of it can tyou give me a detailed procedure for preparing this fish i would be very thankful to you

1\. Put soft fishheads (mackerel, herring, sardines...) in a blender. Cut into smaller pieces with a scissor if bigger heads.

2\. Add water and seaweeds (I add soaked dried seaweeds and their water because I have only dried seaweeds here).

3\. Blend until smooth. Strain if still bony pieces in it, that nothing gets stuck in your throut. Add water if too thick.

4\. Enjoy (with nose closed if pussy like me..)
