I never get tired of it. For dessert I eat wild berries as much as I want.
>
> Now, I can tell you one feels just great after this meal..!
>
>
>
> Even this groggy girl. Spontaneous pic from today soon after waking up,
>
> with my moms old pullover on because here is coool.. and fuzzy hair.. sorry lol
>
>
>
>
>
>
>
>
> And yeah.. do not forget to look at the sun rising.. This is from my balcony.
>
>
>
>
> n

never tried to do MHS with algae...WHY? This is my breakfast as soon as I am lay my hands on some fresh fishhhhh.

### Inger — 2015-01-23T17:26:16+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/this-breakfast-gives-crazy-energy.4497/post-153704>

> [Hope2Learn said:](/goto/post?id=153694)
>
> Please tell me I'm not the only person who spent five minutes staring at these trying to decide if those are bubbles or eyeballs.

They are bubbles.... LOL

### Inger — 2015-01-23T17:28:12+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/this-breakfast-gives-crazy-energy.4497/post-153705>

I often do it like this too, Kovita - I first do the fish head smoothie with heads, roe and fish milk, then i rinse the mixer and make me a smoothie with seaweed only. And enjoy it  Because i do not like the taste of the fish head smoothie at all... but I love the taste of the seaweedsmoothie! So the seaweedsmoothie is my treat after the bad stuff is down

### Hope2Learn — 2015-01-23T22:01:01+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/this-breakfast-gives-crazy-energy.4497/post-153716>

> [Inger said:](/goto/post?id=153704)
>
> They are bubbles.... LOL

Oh good, I feel much better now, LOL! Today at the grocery store my husband and I stopped and looked at some jarred herring for a moment and then I looked at my husband and said, "you know, there's a woman online who takes fish heads and blends them into smoothies and swears by them". He just turned his head slowly to look at me with this blank look on his face and his eyebrows slightly raised.

I took advantage of his silence and said "she also takes organic, grass-fed beef hearts and livers and eats them raw on occasion". He said "okay, that's enough" and laughed a little to himself while trying to wrap his head around it. I just happened to stroll past the hearts and livers at that moment and just turned and looked at him with one eyebrow up and he made a show of ignoring me and looking the other way.  It doesn't read as funny as it felt, trust me!

Hope you don't mind me talking about you like that, but as others have said - you're inspiring! I'm not to the point of making any of your recipes yet, but they certainly have made an impression anyway!

### Inger — 2015-01-24T11:58:13+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/this-breakfast-gives-crazy-energy.4497/post-153751>

I do not mind at all Hope... in fact your story made ma laugh.....  juts do not tell him about the high meat and the raw ram balls
now... to eat raw balls really makes one horny so that could be some thing to consider for you  your husband might be all in

### Hope2Learn — 2015-01-24T15:37:46+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/this-breakfast-gives-crazy-energy.4497/post-153763>

Thta sounds like a breakfast to end all breakfasts there. Most people have eggs, I prefer to go straight for the balls.

### Inger — 2015-01-24T20:32:15+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/this-breakfast-gives-crazy-energy.4497/post-153775>

In German language balls = eggs... so i can say I will have eggs for breakfast and no one will know if i mean eggs or balls...lol pretty cool!

Have you gotten your hands on some ram balls already, Hope? Those are not easy to come by! Never seen any here in the shop ever... maybe i should ask the sheep butcher - I can tell her i feed them my dog?

# Thread: What would eat while watching theatre in Shakespeare's time?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-would-eat-while-watching-theatre-in-shakespeares-time.4499/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Michael — 2012-10-25T20:02:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-would-eat-while-watching-theatre-in-shakespeares-time.4499/post-60172>

Not popcorn, ice-cream, Coca-Cola, or the like, of course.

It's all real food - and it includes shellfish:

> There are a number of contemporary accounts saying that you could buy food and drink within these theatres. Amongst the groundlings standing in front of the stage, people would have sold their wares, rather like the old cinema usherettes with a tray on a strap around their neck. The theatres didnâ€™t have any room for a bar or foyer in a modern sense so things like nuts, apples, fruit, beer, or wine, or water could all be bought inside.
>
>
>
> **We know that people were eating shellfish; oysters, muscles, periwinkles, whelks, we even found a cuttlefish.** The groundlings would have just dropped the shells on the ground, although they would have been swept up at the end of the day â€“ most of the shells weâ€™ve found are in the dumps at the back of the building.

[http://blog.britishmuseum.org/2012/04/18/archaeological-findings-at-shakespearean-playhouses/](http://http://blog.britishmuseum.org/2012/04/18/archaeological-findings-at-shakespearean-playhouses/)

### kathylu — 2012-10-26T02:58:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-would-eat-while-watching-theatre-in-shakespeares-time.4499/post-60246>

Michael, I love your posts! Please keep finding obscure but fascinating information.

# Thread: Artificial light - 1924?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/artificial-light-1924.4506/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 16
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Kiera2 — 2012-10-26T08:53:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/artificial-light-1924.4506/post-60272>

Jack has mentioned a few times now, and again in the October webinar, that the first time humans were faced with artificial light at night was Paris streetlighting in 1924.

I can't figure this out. This picture shows Paris streetlighting in 1878 [http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Yablochkov\_candles\_illuminating\_Avenue\_de\_l%27Opera\_ca1878.jpg](http://http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Yablochkov_candles_illuminating_Avenue_de_l%27Opera_ca1878.jpg)

Inside a Paris music hall in 1880 [http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Yablochkov\_candles\_illuminating\_Music\_hall\_on\_la\_Place\_du\_Chateau\_d%27eau\_ca\_1880.jpg](http://http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Yablochkov_candles_illuminating_Music_hall_on_la_Place_du_Chateau_d%27eau_ca_1880.jpg)

And here in London, again in 1878 [http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Jablochkoff\_Candles\_on\_the\_Victoria\_Embankment,\_December\_1878.jpg](http://http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Jablochkoff_Candles_on_the_Victoria_Embankment,_December_1878.jpg)

Wikipedia, Street light:

> The first street in the UK to be lit by electric light was Mosley Street, in Newcastle upon Tyne. The street was lit by Joseph Swan's incandescent lamp on the 3rd February, 1879.\[3\]\[4\] The first in the United States, and second overall, was the Public Square road system in Cleveland, Ohio, on April 29, 1879.\[5\] Wabash, Indiana holds the title of being the third electrically lit city in the world, which took place on February 2, 1880. Four 3,000 candlepower Brush arc lamps suspended over the courthouse rendered the town square "as light as midday."\[6\] Kimberley, South Africa, was the first city in the Southern Hemisphere and in Africa to have electric street lights - first lit on 1 September 1882 .\[7\] In Latin America, San Jose, Costa Rica was the first city, the system was launched on August 9, 1884, with 25 lamps powered by a hydroelectric plant.\[8\] Timişoara, in present-day Romania, was the first city in mainland Europe to have electric public lighting on the 12 of November 1884. 731 lamps were used. On 9th December 1882, Brisbane, Queensland, Australia was introduced to electricity by having a demonstration of using eight arc lights, erected along Queen Street. The power to supply these arc lights was taken from a 10 hp Crompton DC generator driven by a Robey steam engine in a small foundry in Adelaide Street and occupied by J. W. Sutton & Co. The lamps were erected on cast iron standards, 20 ft in height. In 1888 Tamworth, New South Wales, Australia became the first location in Australia to have electric street lighting, giving the city the title of "First City of Light".\[9\]

So, what was different/what changed in 1924?

### Michael — 2012-10-26T11:42:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/artificial-light-1924.4506/post-60280>

I expect it was a slip of the tongue - 1924 for 1824.

The first major lighting with gas would have been around that time:

> By the use of Argand burners in 1821, the lighting was improved. The first gas lamps were placed in the Place du Carrousel in 1829.

[http://www.nature.com/nature/journal/v132/n3345/abs/132888c0.html](http://http://www.nature.com/nature/journal/v132/n3345/abs/132888c0.html)

Lighting doesn't get really prolific or really bright anywhere till you get into the 20th century, though. And it couldn't except on the back of the incandescent lamp:

[http://americanhistory.si.edu/lighting/history/patents/ed\_inc.htm](http://http://americanhistory.si.edu/lighting/history/patents/ed_inc.htm)

### Kiera2 — 2012-10-26T12:25:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/artificial-light-1924.4506/post-60281>

The Westminster bridge was lit in 1813.

If you're right, that it was a slip of the tongue, that would add 100 years of artificial lighting to flip epigenetic switches.

### Kiera2 — 2012-10-26T12:34:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/artificial-light-1924.4506/post-60282>

From your second link:

> However, he departed from this experimental path even before the patent was granted. His demonstration lamps of late December used bristol-board filaments cut in a single arch, horse-shoe shape. The bamboo filaments used in commercial lamps from 1880 to 1893 also featured a single arch. Filaments with a tight spiral did not become common in commercial lamps until Irving Langmuir developed the gas-filled tungsten lamp in 1913.

So perhaps it's the filament that Jack was referencing, rather than gas or electricity as I was thinking.

### Kiera2 — 2012-10-26T12:44:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/artificial-light-1924.4506/post-60283>

This is interesting: [http://www.simoncornwell.com/lighting/timeline/index.htm](http://http://www.simoncornwell.com/lighting/timeline/index.htm)

> 1882 The City Of London is the first to light streets with the Edison incandescent lamp when Holborn Viaduct and neighbouring streets are lit.

### Michael — 2012-10-26T13:14:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/artificial-light-1924.4506/post-60284>

> **Coriander;61894 wrote:** This is interesting: [http://www.simoncornwell.com/lighting/timeline/index.htm](http://http://www.simoncornwell.com/lighting/timeline/index.htm)

Thanks.

That figures. I think London would have been the first to do something really stupid with technology by the 1880s.

By that time Napoleon had damaged France pretty badly - something like a million men lost in the failed invasion of Russia alone. And then there was the Franco-Prussian war:

[http://francoprussianwar.com/](http://http://francoprussianwar.com/)

With France exhaused, the baton for doing stupid things with techology would have been passed to Britain.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-10-26T13:57:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/artificial-light-1924.4506/post-60285>

I was referring to the entire city being simultaneous electrified during all night time hours. That first happened in 1924. Light made an appearance in spots earlier.......for sure. I should have been clearer about it.......but what I am pointing out to you was when modern night time became infected with fake light and became the primordial condition that biology would begin to battle against

### Kiera2 — 2012-10-26T14:56:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/artificial-light-1924.4506/post-60299>

Ah, thankyou. The can't-get-away-from-it kind of light which became the new way of life.

### Patty Cakes — 2012-10-26T21:26:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/artificial-light-1924.4506/post-60350>

I'm glad you asked this Corriander. I was a bit puzzled myself while watching Downton Abby on PBS set in the early 1900s (season 1) and they had lighting in the house.

### Kiera2 — 2012-10-27T02:08:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/artificial-light-1924.4506/post-60385>

Now I've found some more confounding info.

[http://www.jstor.org/stable/2141397](http://http://www.jstor.org/stable/2141397)

1888

> ...Paris was behind her sister capitals in Europe and very far behind the larger cities of America in the public use of electric light.

The first contract to supply electricity for public lighting and power in Paris covered 1888 to 1907. There were six power companies, plus the municipal supplier, covering the city. Then in 1907 there was a new contract, in effect until 1940, during which the municipality may purchase the private infrastructure of the other six companies any time after June 30 1924.

The only significance I can ascribe to Paris/street lighting/1924 is the fact that the local govt could have owned all of it after that time.

### Primal Dream — 2012-10-27T02:30:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/artificial-light-1924.4506/post-60389>

I live in Schenectady, NY, where Thomas Edison founded General Electric -- in the 1890s. But my guess is that the more widespread use of electrical lighting happened after WW I, which would be much closer to Dr. K's dates. think of what it took to do the infrastructure for street lights, wiring for homes, etc. I know that early street lights in many places were gas or lantern.

My house was built in 1926: the electrical system has been upgraded, but I still have a couple of rooms with just one outlet each. Which is now out of code.

But the fluorescent light did not come into widespread use until the 1930s. I think this is a more destructive form of lighting than the incandescent lightbulb.

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-11-01T19:01:41+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/artificial-light-1924.4506/post-61319>

> **Primal Dream;62005 wrote:** I live in Schenectady, NY, where Thomas Edison founded General Electric -- in the 1890s. But my guess is that the more widespread use of electrical lighting happened after WW I, which would be much closer to Dr. K's dates. think of what it took to do the infrastructure for street lights, wiring for homes, etc. I know that early street lights in many places were gas or lantern.
>
>
>
> My house was built in 1926: the electrical system has been upgraded, but I still have a couple of rooms with just one outlet each. Which is now out of code.
>
>
>
> But the fluorescent light did not come into widespread use until the 1930s. I think this is a more destructive form of lighting than the incandescent lightbulb.

For all intents, isn't an incandescent bulb the same as a candle? It's light is from burning?

### Entelechy — 2012-11-01T21:16:00+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/artificial-light-1924.4506/post-61382>

> **Cavemam;63053 wrote:** For all intents, isn't an incandescent bulb the same as a candle? It's light is from burning?

not burning, but you are right both incandescent and candle light are "black-body radiatiors" and are a continous spectrum.

candle color temperature is around 1900 degrees Kelvin though, and incandescents are over 3000K, meaning the have more blue.

[Colour Temp](http://http://www.3drender.com/glossary/colortemp.jpg)

you can put an incandescent on a dimmer switch and until the bulb is the same as candle light though. at 20watts, you are at approx. 2400K, about the same temperature as sunrise/sunset.

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-11-02T01:38:18+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/artificial-light-1924.4506/post-61449>

> **Entelechy;63116 wrote:** not burning, but you are right both incandescent and candle light are "black-body radiatiors" and are a continous spectrum.
>
> candle color temperature is around 1900 degrees Kelvin though, and incandescents are over 3000K, meaning the have more blue.
>
> [Colour Temp](http://http://www.3drender.com/glossary/colortemp.jpg)
>
> you can put an incandescent on a dimmer switch and until the bulb is the same as candle light though. at 20watts, you are at approx. 2400K, about the same temperature as sunrise/sunset.

ooooh, you seem to know some things. I've been pondering the effects of being in big windowed naturally lit room (by sun only) during day. Do the windows filter out anything beneficial you think? I wonder if the same melatonin effects are being had.

### Entelechy — 2012-11-02T02:14:37+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/artificial-light-1924.4506/post-61458>

don't we all?

I can't say about the health benefits, just the facts as we know them.

Different coatings on glass affect transmittance, but you are safe to say plate glass blocks out all light below 350nm

UVB is 315 – 280 nm, and this is what is required for VitD production. Your window would block it.

as for melatonin.. the SCN is reset by UV at approx 470nm (or any visible light of sufficient intensity), and this frequency passes freely through glass.

I'm personally of the belief that the UV benefits us in other ways too, such as preferential microbial control, and molecular effects such as ["enzymatic photorepair"](http://http://forum.jackkruse.com/showthread.php?43-What-the-H-is-Factor-X-Post-your-guesses&p=22496&viewfull=1#post22496) ....longer wavelengths, can counteract damage by repair of DNA.

### Adria — 2012-11-02T16:53:01+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/artificial-light-1924.4506/post-61544>

Our town had electric street lights installed in 1895. Photos from that time show electric lighting indoors also.

### Inger — 2012-10-26T09:27:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-hope-jack-has-not-gotten-burned-out.4507/post-60275>

I wonder how Jack is.

He usually post every day. He have given some hints he has rough times. I hope he is well and taking some time for breathing. He have done so much this last year. I sometimes have been silently thinking I hope it is not too much for him all this. Sometimes... people are not easy to help when they are acting like you are a God too. Taking every sentence into investigation.. forgetting that Jack is also a human, asking same questions over and over again.. not bothering to look for the answer themselves in the blog etc. Demanding more and more without really listening to his core message. I think this cannot be easy. I think it must be real hard.

I wonder why people want to fight. I wonder what will it bring? I wonder if we sometimes forget we are all human and we all fail at times, fail at doing things perfect. It does not matter. In the end we are all one big family. Today I fail, tomorrow maybe you. But we could always encourage each other and love our selves and each other anyways. And learn, all the way long.

IDK I just needed to get this out of my mind.. might be a bit confusing.

# Thread: Armour question for the Thyroid experts
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/armour-question-for-the-thyroid-experts.4509/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 7
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Shijin13 — 2012-10-26T15:10:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/armour-question-for-the-thyroid-experts.4509/post-60301>

So for those of you on Armour (I'm taking a compounded version) when you increased doses, did you just add to your am dose or did you split it across the day?

right now I'm taking it in the am, and again in the afternoon around 4pm. I'm increasing from 1/2grain (30mg) to 1 grain (60mg), when I get my new rx, it will be 60mg in one dose. Should I be splitting it or should it be taken all together..

how long will it take to see improvement... I'm still getting the HOT surges/flashes through out the day from my thyroid trying to turn on....

intuitively I think I need to be on a higher dose... but we'll see if I can talk pcp in to upping it to 90mg

Love to hear the experts thoughts

### FarmersDaughter — 2012-10-26T17:38:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/armour-question-for-the-thyroid-experts.4509/post-60317>

I would split, and take a half grain am and noon. If (when) you get bumped up, add that to a pre-bed dose. A bedtime bump of t3 makes for much more refreshing sleep.

Once you know it won't cause any weirdness, you can combine them all into one if the t3 will last long enough. If you convert poorly and feel like you "run out", consider keeping a split schedule.

Have you tried taking it sublingually? That can yield more oomph while you wait for the doc to catch up.

### Patty Cakes — 2012-10-26T18:12:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/armour-question-for-the-thyroid-experts.4509/post-60321>

The great thing about Armour is that you can pretty much do anything you want and your body will tell you whether it likes it or not. Personally, if it were me I would split the dose. I could not have gone all day with just one dose. If you get up to 90 mg you may want to split 45/45 or 30/60 or 60/30. The usual 2-3pm dip hypo people get can sometimes be relieved with a higher afternoon dose. I do a small evening dose with my T3 but never did it with Armour. Some people have trouble sleeping but I never do with the T3 or Armour. You'll know what to do once you start upping. When I first started upping I just used my intuition while taking symptoms into account to decide how much when.

### bigknitwit — 2012-10-26T18:24:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/armour-question-for-the-thyroid-experts.4509/post-60323>

In my kids, I notice a behavior difference even within 48 hours of increasing their dose. However, I think for full effect, it takes several weeks, as the cells see more T3 and think about doing stuff with it (making mitochondria, and expressing different genes). For me personally, I would start by splitting the dose for a more even level of T3 throughout the day. Of course symptoms are the ultimate navigating tool..

### Shijin13 — 2012-10-26T18:51:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/armour-question-for-the-thyroid-experts.4509/post-60331>

That's what I'm inclined to do - but since Its compounded...and in capsules I'm not sure how to split the dose... right now I've got 1/2grain capsules...so I can split it... I see my doctor on Monday - and may ask her if she can adjust the Rx so that its split... 30mg in the morning and 30mg in the afternoon...

I definitely feel better w/my 2nd dose around 4pm. I guess we'll see.

### Patty Cakes — 2012-10-26T19:16:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/armour-question-for-the-thyroid-experts.4509/post-60337>

I would think your doc will be fine with keeping the pills at 30mg and just taking two doses. It may cost a little more but if that's what works it's worth it!

### Shijin13 — 2012-10-26T19:28:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/armour-question-for-the-thyroid-experts.4509/post-60341>

> **Patty Cakes;61953 wrote:** I would think your doc will be fine with keeping the pills at 30mg and just taking two doses. It may cost a little more but if that's what works it's worth it!

That's what I'm hoping... guess we'll see if she's willing to do that...

# Thread: Q fever fatigue syndrome?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/q-fever-fatigue-syndrome.4510/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 7
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### caroline — 2012-10-26T15:27:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/q-fever-fatigue-syndrome.4510/post-60302>

Does anyone know anything about this? I am travelling with a lovely gentleman who has had this for ten years. It started out as Q fever with high temperatures (up to 43C) and horrible headaches and aching limbs and lethargy) and lasted for about seven months. It has now morphed into Q fever fatigue syndrome with terrible migraine headaches that are continual and extreme lethargy and hot sweats. There is a doctor -Sir Macfarlane Burnett who has researched and isolated the bacteria from which CSR labs developed a vaccine to inoculate vets and farmers and abattoir workers.

Q fever comes from urine spores in the dust.

### FarmersDaughter — 2012-10-26T17:32:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/q-fever-fatigue-syndrome.4510/post-60316>

I am not familiar with this, at all, so please forgive if this is dumb question.

Is the Q Fever Fatigue syndrome substantially different from ME or Chronic Fatigue Syndrome? I wonder if a treatment protocol similar to what helps with CFS would be of any benefit.

### Jude — 2012-10-27T00:55:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/q-fever-fatigue-syndrome.4510/post-60378>

> **caroline;61918 wrote:** Does anyone know anything about this? I am travelling with a lovely gentleman who has had this for ten years. It started out as Q fever with high temperatures (up to 43C) and horrible headaches and aching limbs and lethargy) and lasted for about seven months. It has now morphed into Q fever fatigue syndrome with terrible migraine headaches that are continual and extreme lethargy and hot sweats. There is a doctor -Sir Macfarlane Burnett who has researched and isolated the bacteria from which CSR labs developed a vaccine to inoculate vets and farmers and abattoir workers.
>
> Q fever comes from urine spores in the dust.

All I know comes from my son contracting it at 12yrs. He was acutely ill with roaring temps, and eventually diagnosed clinically (before blood work proved it) . Had IV antibitics for a couple days and has had no reoccurring symptoms in 18yrs. Did your friend have ABX with his initial diagnosis?

### Remo — 2017-04-14T16:48:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/q-fever-fatigue-syndrome.4510/post-212321>

I need to get this tested myself as I live in the epi-centra of the exacty breakout + I ate a ton of goat cheese during the 2007-2010 era..

The only thing that has helped me regain some energy is sunlight.

### caroline — 2017-04-15T05:34:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/q-fever-fatigue-syndrome.4510/post-212338>

I am pretty sure this was an "ask Jack" question a few years ago.......

### JanSz — 2017-04-15T17:11:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/q-fever-fatigue-syndrome.4510/post-212352>

> [caroline said:](/goto/post?id=212338)
>
> I am pretty sure this was an "ask Jack" question a few years ago.......

**Q fever**
Discussion in '[Ask Jack](https://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?forums/ask-jack.18/)' started by
[caroline](https://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?members/caroline.888/), [Oct 26, 2012](https://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/q-fever.4512/).
[https://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/q-fever.4512/#post-60403](https://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/q-fever.4512/#post-60403)

> [caroline said:](/goto/post?id=60304)
>
> Dr Kruse - have you heard of Q fever? If so - could you point us to some research we could look at. Thanks for any help.

> [Jack Kruse said:](/goto/post?id=60403)
>
> Sheep, goats and cattle are the main source of Q fever. It is spread from contact with excretions such as milk, urine, feces and the afterbirth of infected animals. Q fever is caused by a microbe called "Coxiella burnetii." This microbe can survive for months and even years in dust or soil. **The antibiotic tetra doxycycline is often used to treat Q fever.** Patients usually recover promptly when treatment is started without delay. **For chronic Q fever, both doxycycline and hydroxychloroquine may be used for several months.** Q fever is rarely fatal. Untreated chronic Q fever may be fatal and mainly in patients with existing heart disease.
>
> [http://www.cfsph.iastate.edu/FastFacts/pdfs/qfever\_F.pdf](http://http://www.cfsph.iastate.edu/FastFacts/pdfs/qfever_F.pdf)

### Remo — 2017-04-15T17:36:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/q-fever-fatigue-syndrome.4510/post-212355>

I would be very reluctant to do antibiotics even if I found out I had Q-fever.

# Thread: Cooked vs Raw in relation to brain growth study for big brains
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cooked-vs-raw-in-relation-to-brain-growth-study-for-big-brains.4513/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 5
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Glamazon — 2012-10-26T16:28:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cooked-vs-raw-in-relation-to-brain-growth-study-for-big-brains.4513/post-60308>

[http://www.latimes.com/news/science/sciencenow/la-sci-sn-homo-erectus-cooked-raw-food-brain-size-20121022,0,2243513.story](http://http://www.latimes.com/news/science/sciencenow/la-sci-sn-homo-erectus-cooked-raw-food-brain-size-20121022,0,2243513.story)

### Michael — 2012-10-26T17:01:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cooked-vs-raw-in-relation-to-brain-growth-study-for-big-brains.4513/post-60314>

I have, shamefacedly, to admit that I was given Wrangham's book last Christmas (by an anthropology student, as it happens) but have still not read it.

I think the _LA Times must be misunderstanding what he's saying by quite a long way. After all, gorillas don't eat the same things as humans, and Wrangham would hardly not be aware of that.

It's not as if Inger, who eats a high percentage of raw food has to spend all day chewing. On the Times_ writer's understanding, she would have to. The same goes for the Eskimos in the 1949 video I found and posted here recently.

### Dali Dula — 2012-10-26T19:19:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cooked-vs-raw-in-relation-to-brain-growth-study-for-big-brains.4513/post-60338>

No mention of seafood,shellfish, coastal dwelling and the fact that our brains are actually shrinking lately. Raw shellfish would provide enough nutrition without having to chew 8 hours a day. Hmmm.

### Michael — 2012-10-26T20:06:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cooked-vs-raw-in-relation-to-brain-growth-study-for-big-brains.4513/post-60343>

Nail on the head, Dali.

Meat from land-animals or marine mammals isn't necessarily far behind. Stefansson remarks somewhere, when disputing the point that chewing is the reason for tooth-health among arctic peoples, that much of what they ate was soft and slipped down with a minimum of chewing. How much would you need to chew liver?

I think Wrangham probably has some interesting things to say, and the LA Times has misunderstood him.

I mean gorillas even eat twigs, don't they? I shan't be tucking into twigs tonight, raw or cooked.

Maybe he's talking about the energetic advantage of being able to make use of starchy tubers, which you HAVE to cook or something ...

### x_danny_x — 2012-10-27T23:05:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cooked-vs-raw-in-relation-to-brain-growth-study-for-big-brains.4513/post-60473>

From my understanding, our bodies have no problems digesting raw meat. Some folks get a boost in eating things raw, such as eggs.

### hope2conceive — 2012-10-27T00:15:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hack-my-labs.4519/post-60375>

I would love it if any of you might give me your input on these labs. Not done yet, still waiting for chemistry panel to come back, and have to GO back because they FORGOT to do my RT3! After I finally got the MD to get me that test. Ugh. Thanks so much in advance...here we go:

**ASI â€“ done at Diagnostechs, no Melatonin tested

**Cortisol

### Patty Cakes — 2012-10-27T00:43:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hack-my-labs.4519/post-60377>

Oh boy you do have a lot going on here. First you ASI is not OK. You should be in the top of the range in the morning when you need it the most. The second reading should still be near the top of its range. The third reading should be mid range and the last should be near the bottom. You have a reverse cortisol pattern. This actually is probably worse since you are on Cortef too. This could have been made worse by not taking the thyroid meds. Having a low FT3 constantly, stresses the adrenals because they have to pick up the slack. My guess is with the low amt of Naurethroid you are on is still not enough but you probably cannot handle more because of the poorly functioning adrenals. NDT will suppress the TSH way below 1.0. However, you want to pay attn to the FT3 and FT4 levels, not TSH so much. The FT4 is also low, should be near 3.0 or above. So you are looking metabolically inefficient to me.

DHEAs is low and should be in the top of the range. Pregnenolone is also low. No surprise as you probably have preg steal going on to help support the adrenals. Ferritin which is a storage iron hormone is also too low. Check your other iron labs to see what is going on there. Vitamin D could be better but in spite of all going on not bad.

I'm not a hormone expert but.....I think the estrogen should be a little higher which means the Pg should be way higher.

Good: hs-CRP and no Hashis apparent. You have some thigns to work on (like all of us!).

### JanSz — 2012-10-27T02:57:56+0300

> **hope2conceive;62003 wrote:** Thanks so much, Jan! I have some DHEA but not the Pregnenolone....would you take it in addition to Progesterone? That is what my home MD said to do...luteal phase. I would prefer the Pregnenolone though....

Make sure that you use the pregnenolone that I specified. Other types, pills or creams are rather useless.

Personally I use DHEA from lef.org but plan on switching to DHEA from Nutricology.

Nutricology & Allergy Research is same company, same products, different colors of labels.

That pregnenolone is expensive, and, you will need lots of it.

I buy mine from HerbsPro.com

When you buy dozen bottles they will give you break.

It is going to take a while to figure out proper dose.

It was simple for me (I am a man), you need to check at least two days within monthly cycle.

days 2-4 (minimums)

days 19-21 (maximums)

if you are rich ($$) add ovulation

It would be a good idea to add 100-200mg/day 7ketoDhea to this set,

but I have seen ladies complaining of excessive testosterone.

Usual thinking is that DHEA may increase testosterone

7ketoDHEA is thermogenic, thyroid should work better.

\=========

When using pregnenolone, I suggest that you do not use progesterone.

If your blood progesterone is low, increase pregnenolone pills.

but

hopefully

pregnenolone will increase your morning cortisol.

You may use little extra pregnenolone if you need to raise cortisol.

Do all dr Kruse's advices on circadian rhythm (light, sleep, BAB) that helps moving cortisol.

Note, when I first started pregnenolone and DHEA supplementation I had very low blood levels of them.

At first I was using 300mg/day DHEA

and

900mg/day pregnenolone

that lasted few month, then I filled up voids and my levels started to raise over top levels.

I use now 150mg/day DHEA

and

300mg/day pregnenolone

and

200mg/day 7ketoDHEA

I am a men, that is equivalent to women's needs on day 2-4

latter

women needs more progesterone

first

try to get some order on first half of your cycle.

Latter you will see if you will need to increase pregnenolone dose (or possibly for few days you may need 100mg/day Microgest or prometrium (natural, micronized progesterone), to give you the required higher progesterone levels in luteal phase.

///

When you use pregnenolone and all works as planned, you should get higher cortisol.

Do not use hydrocortisone, that may mess up proper flow.

Also,

watch out for interaction with thyroid hormones.

They (thyroid hormones) may start working,

you may get (temporary) hot flashes and higher heart rate.

Stop pregnenolone use for little bit, untill all settles.

Eventually, after the Steroid Hormones are at better level,

you will be able to work on your high TSH.

I use Thyroid-S

You can buy it now from amazon.com

for future use

When you will have good steroid hormone panel, max 3grains/day should be enough to keep

TSH(0.5-1.5)

Do not be preoccupied watching T3/RT3 ratio and forget about manipulating it with extra T3.

That is a dead end road.

RT3 and T3/RT3 are indicators, you watch them but do not manipulate them directly.

///

# Thread: Yellow teeth

**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread

### BJK77 — 2012-10-27T01:24:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/yellow-teeth.4520/post-60380>

Any thoughts on what causes teeth yellowing other than the typical plaque build up, coffee/tea, smoking, etc? My 12-year old daughter has never had problems with her teeth. They're strong and she has yet to have a cavity (unlike me and her brother). She's great about brushing, eats a good diet - no grains or refined sugar at all. She does have some fruits here and there and a bit of raw honey once in a while, but her diet is primarily meats, seafood, eggs, fats and veggies.

We use a homemade toothpaste made from coconut oil, olive oil, baking soda and some essential oils. I was hoping that would help, but it's been over a year that we've been using it and I see no difference.

### Patty Cakes — 2012-10-27T02:07:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/yellow-teeth.4520/post-60384>

I was told that my teeth had a yellow cast to them (before whitening) that it was from antibiotics as a child. May not be your daughter's issue but I thought I would post it here in case it might ring a bell with anyone else.

### Destiny — 2012-10-27T02:41:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/yellow-teeth.4520/post-60390>

You are right, Patty, Tetracycline antibiotic can stain teeth in children.

### BJK77 — 2012-10-27T13:53:21+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/yellow-teeth.4520/post-60421>

She has only been on antibiotics (amoxycillin) twice in her life, still more than I would have liked, but not too bad considering society's gross overuse of them.

### Glamazon — 2012-10-27T15:27:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/yellow-teeth.4520/post-60427>

fluorosis? too much fluoride in the tap water, etc. irreversible I think. It is interesting to note that the color of your teeth sometimes is a reflection of the color of all your bones which would indicate a systemic problem.

### BJK77 — 2012-10-27T15:54:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/yellow-teeth.4520/post-60430>

> **Glamazon;62046 wrote:** fluorosis? too much fluoride in the tap water, etc. irreversible I think. It is interesting to note that the color of your teeth sometimes is a reflection of the color of all your bones which would indicate a systemic problem.

I appreciate everyone's responses and hate to keep knocking them down, but I'm pretty sure it's not fluorosis. I've never given her the fluoride drops from the dentist and we've had our well water tested and it was fine.

You do have me a bit concerned about the relation to the color of bones and a systemic problem though. I did some googling and can't find much about this. Do you happen to have any links to info so I can research some more?

12 year olds are so critical of their appearances. I really wish I could figure out what was causing this and fix it for her, especially if there's some underlying cause that I need to address.

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/yellow-teeth.4520/post-60432>

Not everybody has white white pearls in their mouth. Could it be that it is her natural teeth color especially when her teeth are healthy?

It sure isn't because I've been brushing or flossing more often. My coffee/tea consumption hasn't changed.

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/yellow-teeth.4520/post-89078>

I have been using a two step process. First 1 tablespoon of hydrogen peroxide and 1 tablespoon of non fluoridated water and swish for a minute. Then mix equal parts Tom's non-fluoridated toothpaste and baking soda and brush for two minutes...AM and PM. Whitens up the teeth quickly. Not all Tom's toothpaste is non-fluoridated.

### fitness@home — 2013-06-28T02:33:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/yellow-teeth.4520/post-89099>

> [Dextery said:](/goto/post?id=89078)
>
> I have been using a two step process. First 1 tablespoon of hydrogen peroxide and 1 tablespoon of non fluoridated water and swish for a minute. Then mix equal parts Tom's non-fluoridated toothpaste and baking soda and brush for two minutes...AM and PM. Whitens up the teeth quickly. Not all Tom's toothpaste is non-fluoridated.

This is _exactly_ what I just finished doing! I keep a shot glass on the counter and combine my hydrogen peroxide & water in it. Tom's non-fluoridated is my choice too.

### Dextery — 2013-06-28T03:39:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/yellow-teeth.4520/post-89104>

**Yello Teeth**

One thing I would like to point out is that Tom's Toothpaste as well as practically all other brands contain sodium laural sulfate or Sodium Laureth Sulfate. Both seem to be poisons in quantity. And they seem to mimic estrogen.

"A report published in the Journal of The American College of Toxicology in 1983 showed that concentrations as low as 0.5% could cause irritation and concentrations of 10-30% caused skin corrosion and severe irritation. National Institutes of Health "Household Products Directory" of chemical ingredients lists over 80 products that contain sodium lauryl sulfate. Some soaps have concentrations of up to 30%, which the ACT report called "highly irritating and dangerous"."

I was looking at amazon and there are toothpastes that have Neem in them that do not have SLS and that is a selling point the manufacturers like to tout.

I also understand that SLS being a poison, must have a poison emblem on the tube. I have my Toms in front of me. There are two emblems but who knows what they mean. I plan on calling Tom's tomorrow.

Look on your shampoo bottles. Most contain SLS.

This is why we use no shampoos or soap or conditioners as there are unpronounceable ingreients in those products. Baking soda works fine....even for under arm.

# Thread: The future of education is going to meet epigenetics this way:
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-future-of-education-is-going-to-meet-epigenetics-this-way.4527/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Kids
**Posts:** 78
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Jack Kruse — 2012-10-27T22:53:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-future-of-education-is-going-to-meet-epigenetics-this-way.4527/post-60469>

Teenagers learn best by doing things, they learn best in teams and they learn best by doing things for real â€” all the opposite of what mainstream schooling actually does. Education needs to work by pull, not push. Education should be a self-organizing system, where learning is an emergent phenomenon. The teachers union does not want us to know that. Education is great, to a degree â€¦ but it's really my creativity that's taught me that I can be much more than what my education told me I am. Creativity now is as important in education as literacy, and we should treat it with the same status. We don't because of the system of education in this country. Very many people go through their whole lives having no real sense of what their talents may be, or if they have any to speak of. We are educating people out of their creative capacities. We need to stop it and I think the internet will do just that soon enough. I know my website is going that route in healthcare.

[http://bigthink.com/collective-intelligence/higher-ed?utm\_source=Big+Think+Weekly+Newsletter+Subscribers&utm\_campaign=83ba574a7b-Sat\_10\_26\_Nantucket\_Project10\_26\_2012&utm\_medium=email](http://http://bigthink.com/collective-intelligence/higher-ed?utm_source=Big+Think+Weekly+Newsletter+Subscribers&utm_campaign=83ba574a7b-Sat_10_26_Nantucket_Project10_26_2012&utm_medium=email)

### DarleenMB — 2012-10-27T23:27:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-future-of-education-is-going-to-meet-epigenetics-this-way.4527/post-60476>

I don't think I'd be so quick to blame the teachers' unions for this. Our educational system has been formed over the last couple of hundred years by trial and error. There's no real blueprint for anything at all in this life. We just make it up as we go along.

While the very basics need structure so that as the child grows and matures they have a solid foundation on which to build the rest of their education, I think you are on the right track with adolescents. By the time they reach 9th grade their brains have matured enough that what you're talking about ... allowing their own creativity to build their education ... seems like it might be a viable addition to regular "book learning."

I'm not sure how this next would fit into your scenario but on the surface I find it more than appealing. The editorial in the latest Mensa Bulletin mentions "chaordic organization" which is a "combination word melding of 'chaos' and 'order.'" Rudolph says it is a phrase that "... I believe describes our Mensa organization and a concept that, once embraced and implemented, is the path to success." She goes on to state that "Our wild diversities (opinions, backgrounds, styles) and the need for some scaffolding for those wild ideas to take form are what make Mensa a chaord."

Now that resonates with me somehow and I can see applying it to your scenario. But I need to really think on this some more. It's intriguing anyway.

### Entelechy — 2012-10-28T00:31:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-future-of-education-is-going-to-meet-epigenetics-this-way.4527/post-60481>

Take at hazard one hundred children of several educated generations and one hundred uneducated children of the people and compare them in anything you please; in strength, in agility, in mind, in the ability to acquire knowledge, even in moralityâ€”and in all respects you are startled by the vast superiority on the side of the children of the uneducated.

â€” Count Leo Tolstoy, "Education and Children" (1862)

A government run public education system is incompatible with the values of a free society, as it's interest by natural emergence is not education but in social change and cultural conformity.

### DarleenMB — 2012-10-28T01:37:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-future-of-education-is-going-to-meet-epigenetics-this-way.4527/post-60484>

Sorry, but I don't agree.

### Huck — 2012-10-28T02:04:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-future-of-education-is-going-to-meet-epigenetics-this-way.4527/post-60486>

I owned a training company for about 10 years and my classroom strategy was controlled chaos. The more senses that a student uses, the better they learn, the better they retain what they learn, and it also gives them more "hooks" to pull the information out when they need it. There is a time for a quiet and controlled environment, but it should be as minimal as possible.

One comment that I got over and over again from students was that they didn't know learning could be so much fun.

### DarleenMB — 2012-10-28T02:16:43+0300

>

But that's just my opinion and worth exactly what you paid for it.

>
> what is the natural evolution in a culture where those who are democratically voted to power control education?
>
>
>
> and this quote, should resonate with many people here who have struggled trying to fit the 'average' mold.

>

>
> George Bernard Shaw (1856 - 1950)
>
>
>
> DarleenMB;62131 wrote:** "Our educational system has been formed over the last couple of hundred years by trial and error".

The history of it's evolution is fascinating, and on it's face somewhat disturbing... It is not random, and was not designed with education as it's primary goal,

Learning is natural, rewarding, exciting "play"! not just effortless, but invigorating and leading to even more curiosity.

Feeding the mind in schools today is done by forcing food up the wrong end... until it hurts..

sure you might get a bit of nutrition that way, but at the cost of fearing your 'food'

so we disagree, and isn't that wonderful?

### Entelechy — 2012-10-28T03:52:18+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-future-of-education-is-going-to-meet-epigenetics-this-way.4527/post-60503>

> One comment that I got over and over again from students was that they didn't know learning could be so much fun.

I'll bet you didn't do much lecturing. Maybe some 'story telling', and the rest was up to them?

I worked as an instructor for 7 years, and my mentor gave me one bit of advice that summed things up nicely for me.

"you can't teach anyone anything... they teach themselves. You present your point of view, and let them come to their own conclusions.

That classroom was the most fun work I've ever done, and I had similar responses as you. This was what is considered some pretty dry and technical subject matter too,,, nonetheless, it's amazing how engaged people are when they want to be there, because it's so much fun.

let me know if you are hiring. ;-)

"What makes people smart, curious, alert, observant, competent, confident, resourceful, persistent - in the broadest and best sense, intelligent- is not having access to more and more learning places, resources, and specialists, but being able in their lives to do a wide variety of interesting things that matter, things that challenge their ingenuity, skill, and judgement, and that make an obvious difference in their lives and the lives of people around them." -John Holt, Teach Your Own

### Jack Kruse — 2012-10-28T05:41:07+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-future-of-education-is-going-to-meet-epigenetics-this-way.4527/post-60517>

The whole world loves a maverick and wonders why? Why is easy. The world wants this person to achieve something more noble than a simple healthcare or an education rebellion. Maverick is a symbol that appeals to me more than a rebel because a maverick is active in achieving change while a rebel is a misfit without a cause. Maverick has a negative connotation to some, but for me, it has a special meaning. "Maverick thinking" is necessary to survive especially in times of crisis, when the rules of engagement in industry change and when you set big goals to achieve big things that transform people and ideas.

A human being should be able to change a diaper, plan an invasion, butcher a hog, conn a ship, design a building, write a sonnet, balance accounts, build a wall, set a bone, comfort the dying, take orders, give orders, cooperate, act alone, solve equations, analyze a new problem, pitch manure, program a computer, cook a tasty meal, fight efficiently, die gallantly. Kids are so creative when they are encouraged to explore their world

What you are doing, Shijin, is great. My parents did the same. They never missed an opportunity to give us another perspective on things including what is a democracy and freedom. I credit them that I had a drive to escape the communism and experience the taste of freedom myself.

### nonchalant — 2012-10-29T03:16:46+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-future-of-education-is-going-to-meet-epigenetics-this-way.4527/post-60662>

### RiverNeighbor — 2012-10-29T05:37:17+0200

### Huck — 2012-10-29T14:18:35+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-future-of-education-is-going-to-meet-epigenetics-this-way.4527/post-60688>

> **Nonchalant;62326 wrote:** Way back when, slaves were not sent to school. The people in power found it easier to control those who don't know much.
>
> Currently, the people in power have taken another tack. Send them all to school, but water down the curriculum. Same result.

Let me piggyback off this to give an example.

When I was teaching at my own company, I would tell students that there job was to learn and mine was to present the information in a way that they could understand it. If they didn't understand something I said, their responsibility was to ask me to explain it again and my job was to explain it in as many different ways as necessary for them to understand. If they didn't understand my 2nd explanation, then tell me so and ask if I can give an example, an analogy, tell a war story, etc.

So at 55, I'm in grad school working on a Ph D. I have a newly minted Ph D instructor that was a lousy teacher. I didn't understand something and asked her to explain it a different way. She immediately challenged my prerequisite skills and said to see her outside of class if I needed special assistance. The rest of the class was also having trouble with her, but they just shut up and played the game.

I decided that getting a Ph D wasn't for me as I didn't want to work in an environment where learning was not the highest priority. In universities, teaching is a low priority, at least in my experience. Meetings, publishing, government contracts, consulting, all seemed to be a higher priority.

I was taking 3 classes, and 2 out of 3 instructors were new Ph D's with little to no practical experience. They offered nothing I couldn't get out of a book. So that is what I did. The head of the department agreed to allow me to withdraw with no penalty even though it was after the drop date.

Huck you're correct. Publish or perish.......much like healthcare, education is on life support in the USA.

### nonchalant — 2012-10-29T16:59:26+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-future-of-education-is-going-to-meet-epigenetics-this-way.4527/post-60711>

I just had to add that I have a sister who is a teacher, and a very good one. She gets all the "problem" kids and she really makes a difference in their lives. She expects them to learn much more than the regular curriculum. They all end up loving her, and learning that they can do anything with their lives that they work for.

That said, she constantly has to battle the principal, other teachers, and parents. I think it is a losing battle for her, but I admire her.

### DarleenMB — 2012-10-29T18:18:48+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-future-of-education-is-going-to-meet-epigenetics-this-way.4527/post-60733>

I have been mulling this over for 2 days trying to clarify what I wanted to say in my first post.

The point I was trying to make was that before you can allow children to frame their own educational/creative experiences they have to have a solid foundation (the scaffolding) on which to build it. The educational process provides that basis with the "basics" of reading, writing (grammar) and arithmetic. Add to that world geography, history and art and you have a nicely rounded BASIC education.

200 years ago a 6th grade education was more than sufficient for children to make it or break it out in the world. Today it's a different story. I would also like to point out that there is nothing inherently wrong with specialization. Let's face it, neurosurgery, for example, is, of necessity, a highly specialized field.

Now this is just my personal POV and you can take it for what it's worth. School's are not a free, public day care system. I am OF THE OPINION too many parents just dump their kids at school and forget about them until 3PM. They forget, or simply don't realize, they are part of that educational process. If that person thinks their child's school is falling short or stuffing their child into a (non-creative) box they have the option to put that child into a more creative-friendly environment or to GET INVOLVED in the schooling process.

Most children and adolescents need structure. And most of them will never have the necessary creativity to just dump it all and head out into the world and make a fortune. Which is why I disagree heartily with the article linked to in Jack's original post. When I went to CSU there was something like 17,000 of us crowding the campus. Out of that number I'd bet money that less than 1% would have had what it takes to follow the suggestion to quit college and just go for it and to be a success. And I don't think it has anything to do with creativity or ability. I think that's just the way people are. (side note ... I don't watch "reality" TV but I do know who Snookie is. Need I say more?)

Now about the crack about teachers unions. That one really stuck in my craw. One, my husband belonged to a union and is still a member although he's retired. So color me biased from the get-go. And he'd have been a union member even if he hadn't gone to work for the railroad because after he graduated from CSU he was offered a position as ... wait for it ... a school teacher until he found out what the pay was.

Secondly, one of my dearest friends is a school teacher. I've known her for about 25 years going back to when she just had a BA and was teaching out at Clearmont (which is so small it can't even be compared to a pimple on a flea's backside). There were times she had to go on HORSEBACK to get to some of the small schools out there. THAT'S dedication.

She decided to go back to school and went to CSU for her Master's and CU for her Ph.D. It was a proud moment and one we got to share it with her. She came back to Sheridan and COULD NOT GET A JOB even working at the local bagel store because now she was over-educated. She finally got lucky and landed a teaching position out at Big Horn which she still holds. I get to see her MAYBE for 20 minutes after Christmas and MAYBE for 20 minutes right after school let's out in the Spring. Why? Because the rest of the time she is busting her backside working at her teaching job. She travels all over the state with her students, she is so creative she takes my breath away and her students love her and several have gone on to attend major universities at her urging to follow their passion. What does she teach? MUSIC. She lives it, breathes it, eats it and shares that passion with her students.

And I bet there is not ONE person here who would work the hours she works for the pay she gets.

Blaming the teachers is like blaming the victim. And blaming the teachers' unions for your children's failings is even worse.

We pay athletes and movie actors and celebrities millions of dollars to entertain us. But we balk at paying our teachers a barely living salary. It infuriates me.

The median income in this country is around $50,000 and teachers most places make way less than that.

We expect our teachers to train and teach our children so they can make their way in the world but we don't want to pay them anything. and then some people have the nerve to blame the teachers because a federal directive aka EVERY child left behind, has added a huge burden to their already heavy workload.

I have read posts on other forums from teachers who are buying school supplies for their classrooms out of their own pockets.

But hey, let's blame the teachers. They all have such an easy life. Who wouldn't want to get paid all that money and get their summers free.

Sorry if this is not real coherent. My thought processes are not what they were before 30 years of poor nutrition, insomnia and hormonal misfires derailed them.

But that's my story and I'm stickin' to it.

### Lyndra — 2012-10-29T18:35:38+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-future-of-education-is-going-to-meet-epigenetics-this-way.4527/post-60738>

Right on, Darleen. Public schooling is only a fraction of the education children need to be getting. Too many get next to no learning assistance at home (can you say TV and video games instead of math & spelling help) and then society railes about "the schools are failing." A teacher friend recently earned her master's and would you like to guess how much more she is earning every month? Yes, hold your breath - a whopping $50. Starting salaries for teachers here are around $33K. Yeah, maybe that sounds like a lot if you work in retail but it's a pittance for a professional we expect to help us produce the next generation of workers. Teachers can't bear the full responsibility of educating children but somehow that seems to be what many people expect these days.

### RiverNeighbor — 2012-10-29T20:58:30+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-future-of-education-is-going-to-meet-epigenetics-this-way.4527/post-60771>

> before you can allow children to frame their own educational/creative experiences they have to have a solid foundation (the scaffolding) on which to build it
> You mean define the box that they are allowed to live within? Make sure they're good at standing in lines, only allow them to socialize with other children their age, set arbitrary time limits for doing work, shame them if they are uninterested in the subject at hand, and drill and assess them on facts that they may or may not be able to see in the context of their lives at that moment?
>
>
> > the "basics" of reading, writing (grammar) and arithmetic. Add to that world geography, history and art and you have a nicely rounded BASIC education
> > Who decides which "basics" are the important ones? Who decides what perspective the story of geography, history, and art are told from? How is it decided that all kids should be ready to read at a certain level and process mathematics at a certain level at a certain age?
> >
> > I see my children learning the "basics" every day through living their lives. Working on reading when they see signs they are interested in or when they are navigating games. Learning math by playing with tape measures and measuring cups. Learning geography by looking at maps of the zoo or our town. And they're not even school age yet.
> >
> >
> > > nothing inherently wrong with specialization. Let's face it, neurosurgery, for example, is, of necessity, a highly specialized field.
> > > Ahhh, yes it is a specialized field. The problem is if one loses competence in solving problems by integrating information that lies outside their specialization. How many neurosurgeons are thinking about the gut? Or hormones? Or evolutionary biology? When specialization means adhering to a closed set of ideas or rules that serve some institution rather than the individuals involved then you're losing, rather than gaining, from specialization.
> > >
> > > Most children and adolescents need structure. And most of them will never have the necessary creativity to just dump it all and head out into the world and make a fortune.
> >
> > Do they? Or, Do adults feel the need to create structure for children and adolescents because it makes their lives easier? Less questions to answer, less time helping them get access to things that interest them, less discomfort when they challenge "authority". I think children need parental guidance from the aspect of being a partner... someone to help them understand behavior within a social context, and someone to act as a docent for the world both by helping them get access to things they're interested in and introducing them to new and interesting things. Some people will grow up to choose to go after high salaries, some will have passions that leave them with empty bank accounts but full hearts, and some will create in imaginative ways the world has never seen before, and unfortunately.... some will be told by a teacher, or counselor, or principal, or parent, that they don't have the necessary smarts or creativity to make anything of themselves in the world and they'll believe it, and give up.
> >
> > Now about the crack about teachers unions. That one really stuck in my craw.
>
> There is nothing inherently wrong with workers joining together to use a common voice. The problem is that many unions have their own political, monetary, and institutional objectives that have nothing to do with the teachers, students, or families.
>
>
>
> And I bet there is not ONE person here who would work the hours she works for the pay she gets.

I left a six-figure job to stay home with my children. I work harder now than when I had a full time job. So what. If money is important to her I'm sure she could make the decision to find a trade or career that the market would offer her more money to perform.

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-10-29T21:19:33+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-future-of-education-is-going-to-meet-epigenetics-this-way.4527/post-60775>

> **DarleenMB;62428 wrote:**
>
>
>
> Now this is just my personal POV and you can take it for what it's worth. School's are not a free, public day care system. I am OF THE OPINION too many parents just dump their kids at school and forget about them until 3PM. They forget, or simply don't realize, they are part of that educational process. If that person thinks their child's school is falling short or stuffing their child into a (non-creative) box they have the option to put that child into a more creative-friendly environment or to GET INVOLVED in the schooling process.

My tax bill to pay for the public school (that we don't use as I think the whole system is rubbish!) says differently. I'm fortunate that I can choose to be involved and go an alternate route. I would totally homeschool if my son were not such a social butterfly. So I get to pay for school twice.

I do think the vast check we pay the school district SHOULD give me a say and it certainly gives me an opinion on the matter. The teachers are a single symptom in the raging inflammation cascade that is public school. I think a daycare would probably do abetter job with the little ones, as they wouldn't interfere with their natural play as much.

This is one area where I think some old school anarchy might actually work. I think we should absolutely be able to put our $ somewhere else if we so choose. FREE MARKET BABY! Let the public school collapse!

The current system is never going to treat/pay teachers as if they are the professionals we want them to be. Unions are a zit that need popping. I want vouchers and I want teachers salaries cut, I want riots in the streets and a collapse of the system. Nothing short of that is going to see a material change.

AND, I think the opposite about who is serving who. We are paying a lot of $ to the school - the school should be serving the children and familes that created it, NOT the other way around. Policy, procedure, required homework, and all that institutional blather and requirements put on the families is ass backwards.

RISE UP FAMILIES!!! RISE UP CHILDREN!!! Now is the time.

(hmmm, my testosterone may be high still).

Darleen, i see you are very passionate about this topic, and you give some personal reasons for why you think as you do.

I have not attacked you, your ideas, nor people you know. I too have carefully and thoughtfully derived opinions on this subject, and wish to share them as well. The point in doing so is to question them, to test them, to evolve them to a more refined state, or to identify weaknesses in their accuracy and effectiveness.

I can only respond to bits and pieces of your post, if there is point made from the sum of the whole I am afraid I've missed it.

> **DarleenMB;62428 wrote: The educational process provides that basis with the "basics" of reading, writing (grammar) and arithmetic. Add to that world geography, history and art and you have a nicely rounded BASIC education. .
> there are numerous examples of systems where basic education is achieved without a system of classroom conformity. One very common one here is Montessori. According to many studies (J.Piaget, et al. and supported by fMRI scans) would indicate that teaching 'reading/writing/arithmetic as early as we do, is a net detriment to overall brain development. (over develops some networks) all children are of course different.. but on average the most appropriate age seems to be 11 or 12.
>
>
>
>
> > **DarleenMB;62428 wrote: there is nothing inherently wrong with specialization. Let's face it, neurosurgery, for example, is, of necessity, a highly specialized field..
> > this is specious. It is quite obvious that being a neurosurgeon requires an incredible breadth of knowledge, from a wide and disparate range of subjects. to "specialize" in medicine, one must first 'generalize'. Specialization only would be an accountant, who calls an electrician to change a light bulb, or a fry cook who can't run the range.
> >
> >
> >
> >
> > > **DarleenMB;62428 wrote:** School's are not a free, public day care system. .................they have the option to put that child into a more creative-friendly environment or to GET INVOLVED in the schooling process..
> >
> > no, it's also a programming system. Allegiance to the flag, sit up straight and don't question authority. It's getting young minds to buy into the idea of a Social Contract, before they even know what one is. As most parents don't either, because they were never really "educated", it's difficult for most to even know there is a limitation in the first place. They never question that there might be another, even superior way.
> >
> > This was my fundamental point, and the subsequent quotes by myself and others. It's self fulfilling. Those that question are mavericks.
> >
> >
> >
> > Dare to question....
> >
> >
> >
> > DarleenMB;62428 wrote:** Most children and adolescents need structure. And most of them will never have the necessary creativity to just dump it all and head out into the world and make a fortune. .........
>
> 1\. All children without severe brain damage, possess unfathomable creativity, that is hampered by structure, and slows down synaptogenesis.
>
> spaghetti and marshmallow towers is a commonly used simple example.
>
>
>
> a fortune is not required. That's something else that's taught as a necessity for a fulfilling life. The under story is "be as economically productive as possible"
>
>
>
> DarleenMB;62428 wrote:** Now about the crack about teachers unions. That one really stuck in my craw.

Should comments on the internet that you don't agree with, affect you that way?

The truth is that trade unions ARE modelled after communism. In the US, it was the Communist party between 1937–1950 that drove the Labor Movement.

Right, wrong, ethical, moral, or other subjective opinions are not the point. The question is whether they are beneficial or do they stifle?

> **DarleenMB;62428 wrote: One, my husband belonged to a union and is still a member although he's retired. So color me biased from the get-go. And he'd have been a union member even if he hadn't gone to work for the railroad because after he graduated from CSU he was offered a position as ... wait for it ... a school teacher until he found out what the pay was.
> I'm was also a union member for over 20 years, but this is irrelevant. One should still question whether it's optimal or even effective. In fact, when one is in a position of benefit the need to question becomes greatest....and hardest. Of course the Ministry of Buggy Whip manufacturing is going to support it's own interests, to the end.
>
> The wages of a teacher in a free market would be what someone thought they were worth to them. Great teachers would receive great pay, (relative) and the poorest teachers would not have their incompetency protected by tenure. The damage caused by an incompetent teacher, multiplied by 30 or more students, is quite substantial.
>
>
>
>
> > **DarleenMB;62428 wrote: Secondly, one of my dearest friends is a school teacher.......................And I bet there is not ONE person here who would work the hours she works for the pay she gets..
> > You're friend sounds like a wonderful person!

> >

I've been a guest presenter there numerous times on specialized courses for free. numerous people volunteer. I don't think we can or should speak for others on the forum.
> >

> >
> > DarleenMB;62428 wrote:** Blaming the teachers is like blaming the victim. And blaming the teachers' unions for your children's failings is even worse..
>
> while no one did either of these things.. a straw man argument. .. the first is like saying that we blame the farmers for the grain based food system we have. secondly, while it wasn't said, nonetheless how is it WORSE to blame the teachers union for the state of education of the failings of our children? the one is individual... the other institutional. I think the first is worse. The second is like blaming agricultural policy on the epidemic of obesity. It's at least partially to blame.
>
>
>
> DarleenMB;62428 wrote:** We pay athletes and movie actors and celebrities millions of dollars to entertain us. But we balk at paying our teachers a barely living salary. It infuriates me.

on it's face, there seems to be limited sense in what we value in our culture. Teachers at one time were venerated... is it possible that the "levelling of the field" via unions might be part of that change in attitude? A teacher is a teacher is a teacher. Just a thought.

I don't understand why people are enthralled with Sports or Jersey shore, but as it's not enforced upon anyone to support without, what's the concern?

Personally I believe it's our scientists that are the most undervalued in our society. What's a Nobel prize worth?

No one is "blaming" the teachers.. no one is blaming anyone, I don't think. Question everything..

quote by Epictetus..

To accuse others for one's own misfortunes is a sign of want of education. To accuse oneself shows that one's education has begun. To accuse neither oneself nor others shows that one's education is complete.

### Entelechy — 2012-10-29T22:44:19+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-future-of-education-is-going-to-meet-epigenetics-this-way.4527/post-60789>

> **Cavemam;62471 wrote:** RISE UP FAMILIES!!! RISE UP CHILDREN!!! Now is the time.

this is why we need public schooling! to nip this type of thinking in the bud before it starts....

if we start early enough, it will take even the strongest minds 20 or 30 years to break free of the delusion, and by then... they will be wage slaves with no choice but to comply. lol

here in Canada, we have 2 public school systems our taxes pay for. One secular public, the other Roman Catholic.

I believe it puts Canada on the list of human rights violations with United Nations.

after 20 or 30 years, it's not really a delusion anymore. All those people have made it their very own reality, to their own detriment.

I recognize that your lot in life is highly influenced by luck and happenstance, and that not everyone can earn a \*\*\*\*load. What everyone can do is realize not all is equal nor just - and $$$ isn't the only measure of a life. Some people get health, some people get wealth, and some people get \*\*\*\* on. . .but rarely does anybody get it all, and some people really do get screwed. I want my son to reaaaallllyyyy believe that he can actually and in reality go out in the world and not stop himself with imaginary limitations. He can influence is life, dependent on what he's willing to do FOR it.

I figure he will learn to stand in line or drink out of the water fountain in an orderly fashion should that be a requirement on the way to getting something he wants. Hell, look at all the biology and crap we are learning here and now? I would bet most here know more about how the body works than almost any public school graduate. . and we are old and sick and slow.

Imagine what things our kids can learn when we let them. I figure there are going to be millions of other school children who know the core curriculum to testing standards, and my son can hire them when that is needed.

No one said multinational corporations are OK, but they are the present reality we live with at the moment. Why so argumentative Darleen?

The Corporations are a result of the very systems that are in place. Obviously, they exist in the environment that has created them. Throughout history, every type of political organization has eventually migrated towards accumulation of capital. This is just the form that the concentration takes in a Capitalist one. Again, this in not an assessment of right or wrong. Just a description of what is. Organization of the 'common man' to 'protect themselves', via trade unions is just another part of that same beurocracy. We are a long way from 'shoveling 16 tonnes to get deeper in debt', at which time the trade unions were necessary. now, thy no longer protect but rather offer another level of protection to the very corporation itself. (much like supposed individual privacy legislation is for corporate protection, not yours... but that's another issue)

I would imagine RiverNeighbor must have worked smarter in addition to harder for >100k. I'll bet she didn't 'fit the average mould, learn her R's, get a college diploma, and join a trade union"

### KiwiLauren — 2012-10-30T05:53:30+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-future-of-education-is-going-to-meet-epigenetics-this-way.4527/post-60870>

This is a very interesting and important discussion. I've worked connected to school systems in 3 different countries. My personal take is that the key to authentic education is authentic relationship. When control becomes the overriding principle, learners have two choices: rebel or comply. When authentic relationship is cultivated, the adult takes responsibility for the child but not for the learning. In other words, the teacher meets the child where s/he is and works in unison with that child to help create opportunities for understanding themselves and the world. All schooling has some sort of structure, but structure can be fluid and responsive or it can be oppressive. The Steiner/Waldorf system frames structure as rhythm. Montessori frames it as an internal order that then allows children to work as they wish. Structure, when done with the child's best interest at heart, is about creating a safe platform in which to jump (as well as a place where you can rest when you don't want to jump). Where you jump and how your rest are driven by who you are and who you find yourself unfolding to be, not be a pre-determined idea of what you should be doing. It is also a function of how good the relationship is.

I have seen 'mainstream' schools allow children a great deal of personal freedom, expression and assumed creativity. I have also seen mainstream schools stifle every ounce of creative humanity. I believe it is possible to have a curriculum while also allowing freedom, but this requires conscious attention. I want my children to learn freedom and to know freedom: intellectually, emotionally, interpersonally, spiritually. I want my children to know real connection and to realise that real connection supports freedom... and that those who seek to shut you down or make you like everyone else are not working toward real freedom and connection. And, like the serenity prayer famously says, I want them to have the wisdom to know the difference.
