The public school system I see is an indoctrination institution that also teaches kids information they need to pass a test. Short of breaking it down and it collapsing the system, what do you think will change it in a non destructive way? Me? I want to edit the process so I don't pay taxes to something so ridiculous.
>

He can be prepared to make a happyness level income and also dance unrelated to that.

> Paradox;62669 wrote:** find peaceful ways of change and growth and ways to build their community.

What I observe is widespread change to a system like a corporation or public school system just does not come without destruction of the original system. I am not trolling here, I would love to consider/study examples of large systems changed without the destruction of the original. I see that peaceful change can evolve - but it's with such little swiftness, that for my lifetime and the world I leave my child in, it's theory not practicality.

I'm not saying take a sword and destroy all the teachers in a bloodbath. Many systems have been destroyed and the cogs can move onward and upward or sideways or whatever they want.

My assumption is that the world at large and abroad isn't a peaceful place, and never has been, so to expect it to behave different in the future isn't practical. I personally can not stand real conflict, but I do my best to teach my son there will be conflict - probably destructive conflict - and he's going to have to navigate that, too.

The peace we can have is from building our own world that suits us.

### Entelechy — 2012-10-30T23:05:20+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-future-of-education-is-going-to-meet-epigenetics-this-way.4527/post-60997>

> **Cavemam;62679 wrote:** Many systems have been destroyed and the cogs can move onward and upward or sideways or whatever they want.
>
>
>
> The peace we can have is from building our own world that suits us.

By design, we are cogs in a machine that moves on without us. In this machine, the cogs can choose to move on without the machine being destroyed. It will attempt to recruit more cogs, it will attempt to force cogs into position where they will not function.. but cogs that can reason and think can opt out of turning. No wooden shoes required, the system will evolve to suit it's new environment. It already has. I've had similar discussions to this for decades, and never before have I seen so many with common questions, normally I've been attacked or looked at like I have two heads. Even called silly and ridiculous. The internet has allowed us to educate freely, without limits, chaotically. without someone guiding what we should think, and hoarding the information as being "too complicated/technical/over your head" This alone will spawn new ideas that have never been considered before. Before now, to access this information one would need to have spent years.... decades... following a curriculum.. being taught... even forced to think in a certain way. (isn't that what grading is?) DaVinci and company weren't famous because they thought the same way everyone else did. That should be the norm, not the exception.

### KiwiLauren — 2012-10-30T23:10:56+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-future-of-education-is-going-to-meet-epigenetics-this-way.4527/post-61000>

> **Entelechy;62707 wrote:** Which 3 countries? it would be invaluable to get a perspective from people who have not been biased... or more accurately biased differently.
>
> most public educations systems come from common roots.

the USA, Germany and New Zealand. In Germany, I worked most closely with the US military primary & secondary school system (DODDS). I've found New Zealand to be the most open-minded, flexible and workable... it is also where my children have gone to school. We are currently facing the institution of 'national standards' for the first time in the country's history (following the US model, sadly). However, almost every single principal has come out against this change and eloquently spoken about it. It's going to happen (cogs in the machine that move without us....) but it will not be swallowed whole as the government wants. We also still have parent-run preschools that are nationwide and government funded like all other early childhood options. Lots of food for thought (most of it not epi-paleo).

Love it. The biggest issue I have with the current state of public education is that not only are the kids being taught how to take a test bit they're also being taught to the lowest denominator I imagine that many good teachers are frustrated by their inability to step out side "No Child Left Behind" to help those that need help but don't qualify b/c they don't have a learning disorder or b/c they're not quite smart enough to qualify for accelerated learning. ### diane — 2012-10-30T23:36:36+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-future-of-education-is-going-to-meet-epigenetics-this-way.4527/post-61003>

> **Shijin13;62715 wrote:** These days I see kids who when confronted with a real life challenge/problem can't manage to figure out how to even consider what to do and immediately seek adult guidance We got to reengage active thinking.

While I don't have children (and therefore am not familiar with the current state of the education system), I do work with and teach adults. And I see the exact same pattern in adults. They expect to be handed the answer, and don't like when the answer is "figure it out."

I have changed careers a couple of times, and one required me to take a course outside of a school or university. This was taught by a person who wasn't university educated - but by far, taught me the most. The biggest thing I learned was how to learn - and how to figure out things by myself. How to observe and use observations and informations to design action plans. And how to communicate to clients.

I wish I would have learned this more as a child - but am glad that have done this as an adult as it's never too late.

But public education system or not - I think that parents can have a huge influence on whether a child is curious and will be out of the box thinkers. Mine valued following the rules, getting good grades, and following the accepted path (and my mom was a school teacher - but definitely one that went above and beyond for the kids). So it's not just an institutional change, but cultural change as well.

### RiverNeighbor — 2012-11-01T18:36:17+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-future-of-education-is-going-to-meet-epigenetics-this-way.4527/post-61311>

Regarding Jack's link above.....

Yes, the internet is a wonderful tool. But, it's only a tool and just because someone can translate a classroom course into an online course that is available to anyone, not just those capable of attending a university, just means that the information is more accessible. Don't get me wrong, accessible is good, but the writer of the article seems to miss something that I think is important - that the value in having the information accessible is what is done with it. It's mentioned that when snail mail correspondence courses were first offered that there was a low completion rate and this is a problem to be grappled with. Who cares about completion rate? Isn't it more important whether people found something useful in the material? What if I only need a portion of the course? Sure, there will be folks who are able to improve their income or job situation because they are able to access training that an employer seeks. Yes, that is a good thing, but I'm thinking bigger. The real explosion of thoughts and ideas without constraint. Where we don't think of learning and knowledge in the context of courses, and degrees we've completed, but where we seek to maximize our cognitive potential (ugh, I'm going to have to eat fish, huh?). Where things like this are more common: [http://cosmiclog.nbcnews.com/\_news/2011/09/18/7802623-gamers-solve-molecular-puzzle-that-baffled-scientists?lite](http://http://cosmiclog.nbcnews.com/_news/2011/09/18/7802623-gamers-solve-molecular-puzzle-that-baffled-scientists?lite)

I think that the type of learning experience that one can participate in here is where a tool like the internet really shows its value. Jack isn't a teacher because someone hired him into a job to perform that function, he's a teacher because he has thoughts, and information, and experiences that people are interested in. And lots of folks aren't just interested in what he says, they're interested in the style of thinking he uses. We are all in a position to choose whether we view him as a teacher. No one is here under any form of compulsion. We have the liberty of asking ourselves, "Is there anything I can gain by studying the information this person is offering?" We don't have to play any games so that we can obtain a piece of paper that will allow us to practice a profession. This site isn't important because there is a course or coursework to complete, it is important because it moves beyond formulas and procedures. There's no need to have quizzes or tests to see if one can regurgitate answers or facts, the "test" is life. The information isn't important because someone else thinks it is, it's important because it fits within the context of a need in our real life.

### Entelechy — 2012-11-01T20:04:25+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-future-of-education-is-going-to-meet-epigenetics-this-way.4527/post-61351>

> **KiwiLauren;62713 wrote:** the USA, Germany and New Zealand....

USA and Germany have a similar roots based on Prussian ideals of centralized schooling and national uniformity. I'm unfamiliar with NZ, but google shows that the details of NZ Education Act 1877 follows a similar path and timeline. This is to be expected, as a natural outcome of 'democracy' by majority as it has been implemented throughout the world in parliamentary and republican systems.

What I found telling in the original accusation of vilification, was that I was attacking the US system although I am from Canada, and was quoting a Russian. The US was formed at a time and by a people who where escaping the very systemic control that is being discussed. Early colonial America was the first time in the "civilized world" that a persons character and capacities could determine their social standing, rather than lineage and wealth.

They had shaken off thousands of years of subservience and created a nation of free men, whose worth was not based on a caste system of privilege. Individuality and independence were core principles or of these early Americans, and look at the great innovative and practial thinkers this period inspired..... Franklin, Paine, Jefferson, Thoreau... all at a time when there was no compulsory education. That's fine for them, but what about the lazy masses? the 1840 census figures show that there was a never before seen revolution in reading and self directed education, people wanted to, had the freedom to learn, and did so.

It was later that compulsory govt. directed public "education" was enforced, based on systems in place in Prussia and India, for very specific purposes that created what is in place today.

Villify the US system? not at all. I think you had it right... you just messed it up by copying someone else.

and of course, I must leave it with a quote.

"only here in America were common folk given a chance to show what they could do on their own without a master to push and order them about" Eric Hoffer.

### Entelechy — 2012-11-01T20:07:32+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-future-of-education-is-going-to-meet-epigenetics-this-way.4527/post-61354>

> **diane;62716 wrote:** ............ I think that parents can have a huge influence on whether a child is curious and will be out of the box thinkers. .

the biggest influence is to stay outa their way... kids are curious by nature, and approach learning like breathing... until we put them on artificial respiration.

### Entelechy — 2012-11-01T20:26:42+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-future-of-education-is-going-to-meet-epigenetics-this-way.4527/post-61364>

> **RiverNeighbor;62834 wrote:** What is specious about quoting an anarchist?......................

Not wanting to speak for you Darleen.. correct me if I'm wrong in my interpretation. I believe you thought that the quote was specious. If my intent was to use it to bolster my argument it could be interpreted that way... as it is merely a man's opinion without any supporting evidence, and would merely be an appeal to Authority due to his fame as a philosopher.

Referencing the fact that he anarchist, was I believe an ad hominem attack, as most people have been taught that anarchism a purely negative philosophy, ergo anyone that subscribes to it, then all their opinions must be wrong. The fallacy of this argument is evident as the conclusion would also have to be that "War and Peace" sucked.

(full disclosure) I referenced another famous American anarchist above. Thoreau..

### Entelechy — 2012-11-01T20:32:10+0200

### Entelechy — 2012-11-02T01:41:43+0200

The goal: to see if illiterate kids with no previous exposure to written words can learn how to read all by themselves,

“I thought the kids would play with the boxes. Within four minutes, one kid not only opened the box, found the on-off switch … powered it up. Within five days, they were using 47 apps per child, per day. Within two weeks, they were singing ABC songs in the village, and within five months, they had hacked Android,”

### Huck — 2012-11-02T03:57:27+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-future-of-education-is-going-to-meet-epigenetics-this-way.4527/post-61477>

Finally read the article. The biggest problem I see is, to a large degree, they are simply computerizing what colleges already do. The only one in the article that is doing anything really new is Sal Khan ([Khan Academy](http://http://www.khanacademy.org/)). Schools that use his classes have the students use Khan Academy outside of class as homework. This flips the typical education around by having the lecture done as homework and labs and exercises does as classwork. The teacher has a dashboard which shows her which students are having trouble and what they are having trouble with so she can give individual help.

What I dislike about college structure is that you have to take course 1 before course 2, when only a small amount of the information in course 1 is actually required to do course 2. Courses need to be structured so that I can jump right into the course I want to take with the prereq's built into the course as options if needed. With online courses there is no reason this can't be done.

The other thing I don't like about the direction is the reliance on statistics. In my former career, I had to maintain several computer certifications that were earned through computerized testing. Over the years, companies such as Microsoft, Novell, and Prosoft began hiring Ph D's in Education and Statistics to develop courses and tests. Prior to that time subject matter experts developed the courses and tests. The courses and tests developed by subject matter experts were far superior to those done by the Ph D's, but the Ph D's had statistics to show a high correlation between passing the test and learning the material. The problem was that the tests often tested on material that wasn't covered in the courses. So the high correlation was based on the prior knowledge and experience of the tester as much as the actual mastery of course material.

The other problem I see being ignored is Instructional Design. Having a fancy delivery system doesn't make up for poor design.

I earned my M Ed through an online university. The course design was pretty bad, the instructors were average at best, but I learned a tremendous amount. I was already sitting at the computer, so if I didn't understand something, I would just look it up online and then go back to the course. I started a doctorate program where the courses were broadcast from a live classroom and it was terrible. There is minimal interaction, so it would have made more sense to video tape the lectures and put them online. This was from a major state university in VA.

Some questions I have.

I earned my BA degree in early 70's. That means I had to physically go to library and type papers on a typewriter. Today you look everything up online and use word processing software with spell check and a thesaurus. If you have a question you email a classmate or look it up online. The point is you don't have to wait for help. So why are courses the same amount of time they were 40 years ago?

Why are courses 1 hour, 3 days a week or 3 hours 1 day a week? Why not do it like a real job and take 1 class for 2 weeks straight?

If you did 1 course in 2 weeks, you could take 10 courses straight (20 weeks) and then 4 weeks off, then another 10 courses followed by 8 weeks off. That would be 20 courses or 60 credit units in 1 year or a Bachelor's degree in 2 years.

You could also do this with classroom courses. You could reduce tuition costs by about 1/3rd plus you wouldn't have to build new buildings since students would only be on campus for 2 or 3 years, not 4 or 5.

Why not find the best 2 or 3 English speaking instructors on a given subject and build an online course based on their lectures? Videotape them and build the rest of the support structure. Hire some graduate students to answer online questions.

### RiverNeighbor — 2012-11-04T05:33:18+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-future-of-education-is-going-to-meet-epigenetics-this-way.4527/post-61924>

> **Huck;63225 wrote:**
>
> Why not find the best 2 or 3 English speaking instructors on a given subject and build an online course based on their lectures? Videotape them and build the rest of the support structure. Hire some graduate students to answer online questions.

This was the idea of the guy who started that company Great Courses (I think that's the name) - to hire the best instructors he could on a subject, video / record it and publish it. Anyone can get the course, it's inexpensive, and you can do it on your own time table. I don't think there is support to answer any questions though.

### Huck — 2012-11-04T16:43:04+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-future-of-education-is-going-to-meet-epigenetics-this-way.4527/post-61988>

> **RiverNeighbor;63698 wrote:** This was the idea of the guy who started that company Great Courses (I think that's the name) - to hire the best instructors he could on a subject, video / record it and publish it. Anyone can get the course, it's inexpensive, and you can do it on your own time table. I don't think there is support to answer any questions though.

If universities adopted this model. they could get rid of a large number of their faculty and replace them with lower cost educational assistants who answer questions and grade tests.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-11-04T16:49:04+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-future-of-education-is-going-to-meet-epigenetics-this-way.4527/post-61990>

# Thread: Anyone making progress with thyroid issues doing CT?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anyone-making-progress-with-thyroid-issues-doing-ct.4528/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 11
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Claudia — 2012-10-28T00:45:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anyone-making-progress-with-thyroid-issues-doing-ct.4528/post-60482>

I am diagnosed hypothyroid and on Cytomel only. I have a conversion problem, my t4 is turning into rt3 instead of t3. I have lots of hypo symptoms, but my numbers are still in range, but t3 barely in range. I don't have Hashi's according to doc's but autoimmune runs in my family (mom had rheumatoid arthritis).

Just treating with Cytomel, but only 10 mcg once in the morning. I haven't been doing my CT, but I swam a lot every day all summer and into fall. I'm in NY. I plan on continuing to swim when I can. I have maintained my cold adapting from the spring, though. I'm never cold (interesting for hypothyroid), and I often still wear sandals and don't use a jacket when others clearly are very cold. That's why I think I didn't lose where I got to when I was doing really cold baths before summer.

I am gaining weight, probably due to low thyroid, and it's upsetting. I know it's not my diet because I eat paleo. I'm wondering if anyone has been able to lose weight or reverse thryoid doing CT. I was hoping not to be on Cytomel long term. I normally don't take any medications, not even tylenol. If I have a headache, I just deal.

I'm only about 5-10 lbs overweight right now and would like to keep it there and not keep gaining .

### barattafam@gmail.com — 2012-10-28T17:04:10+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anyone-making-progress-with-thyroid-issues-doing-ct.4528/post-60566>

I have Hashi's, and I'm interested in the replies as well.

### Patty Cakes — 2012-10-28T19:40:56+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anyone-making-progress-with-thyroid-issues-doing-ct.4528/post-60606>

One's inability to convert FT4 to FT3 is often caused by low iron or adrenal dysfunction. Have you checked your adrenals? Issues there often cause weight gain and inability to lose. If you are barely in range for FT3 you are operating on too little FT3. 10 mcg is a very low amount and may not be enough for you. The FT3 range should be near the top.

Once I cleaned up my adrenal problems I was able to lose the last 7#. CT also helped with that and the depressed appetite so I ended up eating less.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-10-28T19:55:24+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anyone-making-progress-with-thyroid-issues-doing-ct.4528/post-60609>

When you eat Epi paleo you are never missing any brain or thyroid specific nutrients. Conversion becomes natural.

### barattafam@gmail.com — 2012-10-28T21:01:27+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anyone-making-progress-with-thyroid-issues-doing-ct.4528/post-60621>

> **Jack Kruse;62268 wrote:** When you eat Epi paleo you are never missing any brain or thyroid specific nutrients. Conversion becomes natural.

Does this apply to someone with Hashi's as well? I take 6 mg Cytomel along with my synthroid each day. 6 mg is the max or else my T3 shoots over range, and I get hyper symptoms. Skipping the Cyomel makes me feel fatigued and sluggish.

### Claudia — 2012-10-28T22:06:23+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anyone-making-progress-with-thyroid-issues-doing-ct.4528/post-60633>

My ferritin is up high so I think my iron is good. I cook with cast iron pots every day, for years now. Not sure if that matters. I haven't tested adrenals yet but I'm on adrenal support anyway. I didn't have the money for the saliva test that is not covered by insurance. It is a priority for me to test. I have a depressed appetite anyway from doing high fat low carb and from CT I think so I'm already eating less but I'm gaining weight (slowly gained 5 lbs in the last few months).

Patty Cakes, how did you clear up your adrenal problems? You make it sound easy .

### Patty Cakes — 2012-10-29T01:34:00+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anyone-making-progress-with-thyroid-issues-doing-ct.4528/post-60652>

Claudia I worked on my adrenals for 18 mo with some supplementation, enough thyroid meds, no exercise except walking, etc. I felt a little better but still had fatigue especially in the morning. Along came CT and watching light cycles and looking into the sun in the morning. At this same time I switched from NDT to T3 only and started the Circadian Method of taking T3. Six months later I tested and my adrenals were good and actually a little high in the morn which I have fixed. I am continuing the CT, lights out at night and looking at the sun in the morning, epi-paleo, etc to keep things right so I don't go back to where I was before. These are the things that helped me, YMMV. Sounds like your adrenals are your Achilles heel and once you get that healed you will be able to tolerate more T3 and hopefully feel less fatigued and just better all around!

### Claudia — 2012-11-08T00:47:07+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anyone-making-progress-with-thyroid-issues-doing-ct.4528/post-62724>

Sorry I lost power during the recent hurricane.I'm in NY. We didn't have power for 7 days, it was so cold. I wonder how that helped my cold adapting. It was 50 degrees in the house.

Anyway, I do tolerate the 10 mcg of Cytomel I take very well and I'm sure I could take more. My doc thought I perhaps don't have adrenal issues since I tolerate it fine. That's what she said. I am taking the adrenal support anyway, she said ashwanga and rhodiola are good for thyroid, too, anyway, and can't hurt me.

She won't give me more Cytomel and says I should do 5 in the morning and 5 later in the day. I am still doing 10 in the morning. I originally started with 5 in the morning for a couple of months and that became too little so I increased it to 10. I have heard of Circadian Method of t3 (Paul Robinson????). I am scared to do it on my own. My doc said it was dangerous for the heart but she is open to hearing more about this method and clearing the rt3, which in my case is high. This doc also tested me for the MTHFR gene mutation and I am homozygous for c677T. So, now I am getting b12 shots and have to take a Rx called Metanx, which is methylfolate, for life. She also said baby aspirin. I have a high risk for stroke, heart attack. Patty Cake, I'm glad things are going better for you.

### DarleenMB — 2012-11-09T03:46:15+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anyone-making-progress-with-thyroid-issues-doing-ct.4528/post-63059>

Claudia, I have just started struggling with this myself. I'm finding that I can only tolerate about 6mcg of the cytomel (1/4 of a 25mcg tab) daily. After about a week or so I then start getting hyper and have to drop it for a day or two. I don't really feel all that much better when I do take it. My energy level still is in the basement and if it does come up a bit it disappears just as quckly.

My cortisol tested low both A.M. and P.M. Did you read Jack's latest post on adrenal fatigue? As usual it's chockablock with great info. You might also want to read the iodine thread (over in the monster thread forum). I've been upping my iodine slowly, very slowly. So far no detox symptoms.

### BenG — 2012-11-09T14:04:28+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anyone-making-progress-with-thyroid-issues-doing-ct.4528/post-63105>

My TSH was 7.87 last December (PCP said I had subclinical hypo). Started LRx in January and CT a few weeks later. TSH dropped to 4.23 by March (PCP was amazed). I stopped CT over the summer months (and ate post-leptin reset foods (continuing LRx with BAB/no snack/no night eating)), and TSH was in the 2's in September. (Started Doing CT again.) I still have about 1/2 the weight to lose, but my internal labs (including fatty liver) all normalized except for Adrenal fatigue/DHEA didn't improve from March to Sept. (as it did from the CT) As soon as I got back in the ice, sleep improved, and I'd bet DHEA is improving. Detox took 2-2.5 weeks after the 6 month lapse (as opposed to 6 weeks last winter). I only get labs every 6 months, but things are improving again.

### MartiD — 2012-11-09T19:22:03+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anyone-making-progress-with-thyroid-issues-doing-ct.4528/post-63157>

# Thread: Palpitations- finally gone.
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/palpitations-finally-gone.4534/>
**Subforum:** Success Stories
**Posts:** 25
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Jude — 2012-10-28T08:57:37+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/palpitations-finally-gone.4534/post-60523>

I thought I would like to share my success at finally being rid of dreadful palpitations. Noticed them 5 yrs ago and they gradually increased, especially with strenuous activity. Started Leptin reset last Nov and they hit me with vengeance. Started to ruin my life, little walking & climbing stairs for a couple of mths...disaster for me as I'm very active.

Dr K assured me it was a lack of DHA/iodine causing them and to eat more seafood.(this is before the epi paleo release). I started adding 2 to 3 meals a week and I think I noticed some improvement, enough to want to keep going. Then epi paleo was released, and I finally decided to try it and go all out on Sept 1.

I really hit the tinned fish twice daily,sardines and salmon mainly, went through heaps of lemons! We can buy fresh fish once a week here. Prawns are 33-36$ kg, salmon 36$ ! Lamb consumption halved instantly-hubby watched in awe. We are both going to try prawn smoothies soon!...Discovered I could eat the fresh salmon very lightly cooked, but haven't been game to try Inger's raw recipe yet.

Once I noticed that the palpitations really were decreasing I did 2 weeks solid- fish only. This was intentional/accidental sort of, as I was away with sick friend in city hospital-hopeless cafeteria and no local shops close by, so canned sardines was my fare for a week!. I noticed at the end of the week that I could climb the stairs quite well- surprise, surprise. So, back home, kept up the fish only for another week. That was the big kicker for me as there was an amazing downturn and complete disappearance by the end of the second week. I kept testing myself with exercise to prove to myself that I was improving, running up and down stairwells when I could find them.

Long story short-in ER late Aug with severe palps -now late Oct, with epi paleo and basically they're gone. Yes!.

So the question is... am I fully tanked with DHA/iodine or just out of the woods? I'm not sure- I note that I do need to keep to 2 lots of fish daily because on days away and only having 1 fish meal(breakfast) I can notice I'm inclined to develop a faster pulse the following day.

I put in 3 huge, strenuous days in my garden this week and worked like I could 20yrs ago, with no palpitations-WOW..I haven't been able to do that for 5 yrs........wonderful.

Anyhow, it's great that such a 'simple' food change can bring about such an amazing difference in ones' life. What a great result and a big thanks to Dr K and his protocol.

### Kiera2 — 2012-10-28T09:15:40+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/palpitations-finally-gone.4534/post-60524>

That's a brilliant outcome Jude! Good on you for sticking with the sardines for that first week; what a breakthrough!

### Jude — 2012-10-28T13:22:38+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/palpitations-finally-gone.4534/post-60528>

Thanks Coriander, was really looking for a change in seafood by the end of the week. Fellow residents at hostel I stayed at, watched me eat like this for the week and wanted to know why. one of the supervisors heard all about epi P and the Leptin reset and started it while I was there!!!!

### Destiny — 2012-10-28T14:13:57+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/palpitations-finally-gone.4534/post-60530>

Jude, I've been having palpitations for years like you. Mine are not completely gone but they improved tremendously. Congratulations on your success!

### Jack Kruse — 2012-10-28T14:33:45+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/palpitations-finally-gone.4534/post-60531>

Jude you just gave proof of concept to what I wrote in BG 14. Once your brain is filled with its brain specific nutrients it wont have to usurp nutrients from the heart to function well and cause its dysfunction. You must have the brain injury first to get the disease. Moreover, lots of things cause the injury to begin with.” The injury must exist in the brain before the illness appears. This is a foundational concept in my theory

**For those who dont understand how brain function can cause heart rhythm issues here is the playbook again for you to review:**

The brain allows us to sense the epigenetics alterations in our environment well. I want you to now channel what I said about the heart and brain in Brain Gut 13 with respect to how the Na/K ATPase is affected in metabolic terms in all tissues. Then, I want you to think about the number one killer of all humans today: Heart disease. How does heart disease tie into this theory of whole body physiology?

If you begin to alter your blood glucose because of dietary or any other epigenetics signal (light or temp) and cause inflammation at a cellular level by eating badly or not being mindful of circadian cycles, you start secreting large amounts of insulin from beta cells to deal with it. This alters your Magnesium levels which activate the metabolically efficient Na/K ratio because of DHA as laid out in BG 13. When we have an altered Na/K (low) ratio, this in turn, simultaneously cause brain metabolism to ratched down because its function is directly coupled to the efficiency of the Na/K ATPase for our species. As the brain’s metabolism falters, it begins to shunt energy to itself at the determent of other organs. It robs Peter to pay Paul. This physiologic bio energetic “stealing” of energy alters functions everywhere in the body to satisfy the brains needs. As other organs fail slowly under this physiologic directive this further alters brain function from the excessive 60% energy boon created by the DHA/ Na/K ATPase, which results in your feeling less well with less energy and you have a chronic cognitive haze. Is this sounding familiar to anyone?

It is the modern human condition that most of us exist in. As this goes on chronically, brain function is eroded (the real source of adrenal fatigue in the PVN) because there is an excess of Na ions, which alter our homeostatic brain controls of the renin angiotensin system and this alters our Blood Pressure. The renin angiotensin system target organ is the kidneys. The kidneys control the electrolyte balance of the serum and ultimate of the CSF (ultrafiltrate of the serum) to further alter the function of our brain.

This slow erosion of our kidney function goes on undetected and slowly undermines our electrical systems balance. This implies that our declining kidney function, directly efffects our electrical system of our heart (conduction system AV node and sinus pacemaker) to become less optimal or “foggy” in its function too. The relays in the hearts electrical system is sticky and this cause all kinds of unusual heart rhythms. This shows you why declining brain function always correlates with declining heart function. Modern medicine has never made this link but they know it exists. These organs are chemically and electrically tied together by the action of the Na/K ATPase, DHA and iodine at a foundational level physiologically in humans in our cell membranes. This all results in the many types of cardiac dysrythmias we see clinically. This is an early sign of major bio-energenic shifts in cells all tied to oxidation.

When this goes on further brain function erodes and we can not sense it well. We, in essence, lose our primal sense of well being and eventually we develop an inability to sense vascular trouble before it happens clinically. This leads to a heart attack/palpatations/or heart failure.

### kathylu — 2012-10-29T21:36:36+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/palpitations-finally-gone.4534/post-60780>

Thanks for this thread everyone! My husband recently (six months now) has been noticing a heart rhythm irregularity. The palpitations occurred mostly at night and would wake him from a sound sleep. Went to the "best" cardiologist in DC, who told him he was having premature atrial beats, and that no one knows what causes them. The doc suggested that he give up all caffeine. Since my hubby infrequently drinks anything with caffeine, he wasn't too hopeful there would be a positive effect and there wasn't. Nothing changed. I suggested that he start taking magnesium, Lugol's iodine and eat more seafood. All based on Dr. K's ideas of course. About 3 weeks later he mentioned to me that he hadn't had any night time palpitations for a few days and was sleeping straight through the night. The problem seems to be completely gone now. Hallelujah!

### Michael — 2012-10-28T10:27:50+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/john-jewitt.4535/post-60526>

_The Adventures of John Jewitt_. This short narrative is available in several formats from Project Gutenberg.

It's interesting from the anthropological point of view, but among other things there are some descriptions of food. Basically, in this area it seems to have been almost all seafood, including a lot of roe, and everything - even the berries (and by the sound of it even the blubber!) - was dipped in train oil.

[http://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/38010](http://http://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/38010)

### LinD — 2012-10-28T19:39:15+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/zrt-sunday-saliva-test.4541/post-60605>

I hope I didn't take my collection too soon -- today. Sunday. UPS doesn't pick up until tomorrow. Should I freeze it and drop it off tomorrow?

### Patty Cakes — 2012-10-28T19:42:37+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/zrt-sunday-saliva-test.4541/post-60607>

I would check online and through the paperwork they sent just to make sure freezing doesn't destroy anything in the saliva. I just refrigerated mine then dropped off the next day to be mailed.

### Lyndra — 2012-10-28T20:23:17+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/zrt-sunday-saliva-test.4541/post-60613>

From their instructions:

> NOTE: Freezing your sample prior to mailing is recommended but not required. Saliva samples are stable for up to 30 days at normal room temperature.

### LinD — 2012-10-28T23:08:26+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/zrt-sunday-saliva-test.4541/post-60638>

Awesome. Thanks. I found other info that says it's ok to freeze and that it's stable for 30 days at room temp. (ew!) But who knows what temp it's exposed to en route.

[http://www.pinestreetfoundation.org/avenues/avenues8/cortisolqa8.html](http://http://www.pinestreetfoundation.org/avenues/avenues8/cortisolqa8.html)

### Destiny — 2012-10-28T23:47:04+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/zrt-sunday-saliva-test.4541/post-60643>

I did my test on Sunday as well (convenient) and had it in the freezer until Monday before shipping it.

# Thread: Optimal Canines
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-canines.4543/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 16
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### diane — 2012-10-28T20:08:01+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-canines.4543/post-60612>

Instead of hijacking the "ask Jack" thread, loved the suggestion of starting an Optimal Canine thread here! I've been thinking a lot about the dogs and optimal as I read the blogs. Sometimes I'm more interested in how it helps them lol, but am determined to help myself too.

I've been feeding my 2 dogs raw for almost 3 years (beef, turkey, chicken and some pork. Sardines a couple of times a week). In the summer, as I upped my seafood consumption, I also increased theirs. I noticed their body comp changed and they were more muscular. Their coat is so shiny, they've never looked better. I also noticed mood changes - they're happier (which could also be linked to the fact that I feel happier too lol).

But in conjunction with the seafood, I also started turning the lights out earlier, not watching as much TV. So these changes can be linked as well. Also, changing the order of eating and going on our walks too.

What I love about this is that I am in some ways using the dogs as markers - watching how the changes are impacting them, and validating that I'm doing the right thing. I definitely see changes in myself, sometimes it's easier to see them in others. I'm also watching them follow their instincts to learn from them - my older dog goes to bed when it gets dark, and now that it's fall, she will go at 7:30 or 8:00. They are eating dirt - which I'm sure is a natural instinct as well (but one I'm not likely to start lol. I hate dirt!).

I love that having dogs means I get up and into the air and sunshine first thing in the morning - every day. I have to get out and walk them, so we're out over an hour every day.

What I haven't cracked yet is the CT. I'm guessing that getting them into a lake to swim is probably the best way. Has anyone else done CT? My older dog has had 2 ACL surgeries in 2 years, so I'm interested in helping the healing process and keeping her joints healthy.

### FarmersDaughter — 2012-10-28T21:51:36+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-canines.4543/post-60629>

My Aussie is an outside dog. He gets his CT the old fashioned way

He chooses to lay on cement when it's below freezing, and if you coax him inside, he wants back out immediately "I said hi, now please open the door like a good human"

I no longer feed raw, as the truth is I just can't afford it. He was raised raw, and is far larger than he should be...he is a papered Mini Aussie, that tips the scales at 58 pounds in fighting trim. His cannon bones are thicker than my wrist. Both sire and dam were less than 30 pounds.

Im interested to see how the CT helps the ACL healing process for the old girl!

### diane — 2012-10-28T22:30:06+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-canines.4543/post-60634>

Ha - that's awesome! Mine love the snow - but we didn't have much last year, and it's still been pretty warm here.

She's actually only 6 - so not that old. But once she did one leg, apparently it's high risk for the other. Which went about 8 months later. She probably was a bit heavy when she tore the first one, and I really worked to get her weight down so we weren't stressing the joints. But we still tore the other lol.

### JoeBranca — 2012-10-29T23:53:32+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-canines.4543/post-60805>

I've brought our dogs into epi-paleo with me (minus the sea veggies except some dried sprinkled Dulse here and there). I get cheap eats like 5lb boxes of wild frozen whole sardines, frozen whole mackerels, shrimp, etc at the Asian markets.

Sometimes I'm running a little late or low on the raw stuff, so I still resort to canned salmon / sardines here and there, but I try to minimize that. Often their seafood comes with freshly heated bacon drippings.

For non seafood meals I still have lots of of bags of dry trimmings in the chest freezer from our side of grass fed beef purchase earlier this year. The problem is there's tons more fat to them than meat, which raises a question - what is a good rule of thumb in protein/fat ratios for canines?

### shah78 — 2012-10-30T00:22:12+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-canines.4543/post-60817>

Here's a question. How much DHA does a dog or cat really need.?They may be offspring of cold water mammals like we are, but they did not undergo the huge DHA induced brain growth spurt that we humans did. Underdog and Top Cat are fictional, aren't they? They really don't speak and make sarcastic coments, do they?

### FarmersDaughter — 2012-10-30T01:07:34+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-canines.4543/post-60833>

> **shah78;62513 wrote:** Here's a question. How much DHA does a dog or cat really need.?They may be offspring of cold water mammals like we are, but they did not undergo the huge DHA induced brain growth spurt that we humans did. Underdog and Top Cat are fictional, aren't they? They really don't speak and make sarcastic coments, do they?

You've not spent much time around heelers then, I'll wager..I think they have the mentality of most six year old children, just without thumbs and/or the benefit of the english language.  They have an uncanny way of getting their point across, however, and most can certainly use sarcasm.

### KiwiLauren — 2012-10-30T05:59:38+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-canines.4543/post-60871>

Our dog has eaten raw meat from birth. We buy most of his food from K9 Natural. They are solely free-range, grass-fed, without any grains or hormones/antibiotics, no preservatives or anything artificial (in other words, better than what many would buy in a US supermarket for human consumption!). And they are based literally up the road from where I live. I recently learned their business has become so successful that they are now throughout the USA and Canada, as well as Europe. For those of you with dogs and cats, it could be worth checking out... [http://www.k9natural.com/](http://http://www.k9natural.com/)

### Shijin13 — 2012-10-30T14:30:44+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-canines.4543/post-60896>

our Lab started out on a grain fed diet when we got him from the breeder - we switched him to evo - however, after he was neutered at 2, he began to have problems digesting the high protein content... so we moved to pure vita salmonhttp://www.petflow.com/product/pure-vita/pure-vita-grain-free-salmon-formula-dry-dog-food . he loves it!

recently we've added a 1/4 of freeze dried raw to his afternoon meal - this is the brand we get [http://www.wyliewagg.com/index.cfm?action=store&sub=product&prod=1118&fromCat=177#.UI\_Hk2-HIRo](http://http://www.wyliewagg.com/index.cfm?action=store&sub=product&prod=1118&fromCat=177#.UI_Hk2-HIRo)

while not ideal - this is what works for us...

### diane — 2012-10-30T15:43:13+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-canines.4543/post-60907>

> **JoeBranca;62501 wrote:** For non seafood meals I still have lots of of bags of dry trimmings in the chest freezer from our side of grass fed beef purchase earlier this year. The problem is there's tons more fat to them than meat, which raises a question - what is a good rule of thumb in protein/fat ratios for canines?

I think it's similar to humans - you have to find our own dog's n=1 to see what works in terms of fat ratios. Mine tend to gain weight with too much fat, but then again, could be the kind of fat that I was giving them. I feed my dogs the same thing, but one is leaner than the other. I also give them CO once a day which they love.

In terms of DHA, I give them as much as I can. Although I haven't switched to mainly seafood yet. And mine won't eat fish raw lol. But I do give them canned salmon, cooked fish and fish skin, canned mussels and oysters. Plus, i put dried kelp on their meals, about 1/4 tsp each day.

### Marie — 2012-10-30T17:05:22+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-canines.4543/post-60936>

Here are some great sites for those wanting to feed raw:

[http://www.rawmeatybones.com/](http://http://www.rawmeatybones.com/) (Australian veterinarians Drs Tom Lonsdale)

[http://dogtorj.com/](http://http://dogtorj.com/) (Dr J is a member of this forum and just as wordy as Dr K. LOL)

[http://www.rawlearning.com/](http://http://www.rawlearning.com/)

What Really is in Pet Food? [http://www.bornfreeusa.org/facts.php?p=359&more=1](http://http://www.bornfreeusa.org/facts.php?p=359&more=1)

I watched this recently and loved it. [http://animal.discovery.com/video-topics/wild-animals/eating-giants.htm](http://http://animal.discovery.com/video-topics/wild-animals/eating-giants.htm)

(Now I know why Green Tripe has been called Doggie Crack. (Get your fresh frozen GT here: [http://www.greentripe.com/](http://http://www.greentripe.com/))

Anyway, it really gives you a good view of how a body is consumed by predators.

**

It is not for the squeamish.**

Oh, and for those who don't have a large freezer, yet, I just sourced some pastured beef for $0.08/lb. Purchasing a freezer on CL has paid for itself many times over and it costs me about $30/month to feed a 65lb dog all organic, pastured meats + Salmon oil + GT + Oysters + canned or fresh fish. Anyone who says Raw is expensive hasn't done their research. (Just don't put your ice too close to the GT. It doesn't make for a pleasant CT experience.)

### diane — 2012-10-30T18:06:44+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-canines.4543/post-60950>

Thanks for the links Marie! I've seen some of them, but will definitely check out the discovery link. I switched after reading about what's in most kibble - although it took me some time to get my head around it. Now - no second thought!

My guys love tripe! And after a few years, I'm actually getting used to the smell. There is a canned version, which I will get if I can't find frozen, but I find it smells much worse! And yes, it really is doggie crack!

I too got a freezer from Kjijiji and we have a fairly small condo - yet found room for this darn freezer lol. Of course, it also came in handy when I got a 1/4 grass fed beef - that the dogs have to share lol.

Does anyone feed any veggies? I had done a "nutritional consult" and she recommended sweet potato (but also got me on the sardines for them, kelp - long before I found this blog lol), which I did for a few months. But then realized that if I don't need them, they probably don't either. And will occasionally include some pumpkin or squash. There are lots of people who feed their dogs raw who pile in the fruits and veggies.

### Marie — 2012-10-30T18:43:13+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-canines.4543/post-60963>

Sweet potato was the only veggie I feed, and I only feed that with tripe to firm up the poo. I use two back-to-back tripe meals as a "mini fast" due to the low protein and fat content. It gives his body about 36 hours of a break.

Regarding veggies...unless they are extremely cooked, very little is absorbed. I began feeding a BARF style plan and spent many, many, many hours cooking. This was when I was healing foster dogs' issues about 10 years ago. Now I feel Prey Model Raw is the way to go. _With pasture fed Green Tripe and Oysters, you're covered w/r/t veggie content.

{Look for a Raw Feeders co-op to get started, find out where they source their meats and start meeting and greeting in your neighborhood. This is how you get the phone calls saying "I need to unload 1,800 lb of pork loin at $0.03/lb can you call your friends?", or "Do you need any mackerel heads, I've got 10lbs that it is either you or the dump." This is also a great way to get REALLY good meat for yourself at 10% of the retail price.}

I suggest that you be HIGHLY suspicious of anyone suggesting supplementation is necessary, except at the most extreme of dog sports. Specific dogs may need some supplementation for their specific gene issues, but a broad diet plan should not need supplementation for the average healthy dog.

_Also, I would also recommend doing a lot of breed related research to pinpoint exactly what issues you may be facing._ I had a Boxer mix rescue who just died recently at the age of ~15. His diet was No Carb due to Boxer breed issues with cancer. His vet was shocked to discover how old he was as most boxers don't make it past 10. She is now feeding her dog a no carb diet (and is, despite 30 years as a DVM and dog sport competitor, struggling with the "lack of science" behind Raw diets. She has a lot of mythology to combat. I told her "Mother nature wrote the science, so just follow her direction." and she smiled, saying "Yes, it is as simple as that, isn't it?"

Yes, at the end of the day....it should_ be simple.

### diane — 2012-10-31T04:17:04+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-canines.4543/post-61032>

Thanks Marie. Great idea about the co-op. I have been feeding raw for 3 years, but have been going mostly to the grocery store near me, as it has a butcher in it and really great cuts (along with heart, tongue, etc). But I'm sure there's much better ways for me to get it!

In terms of supplementation - I would suspect that it's much the same as humans. The ideal is to get it from food, but some things are harder given the quality of the food now. This to me is more the issue for supplementation than those that perform in dog sports. After reading Jack's blog, I was actually pretty impressed that she recommended kelp - and I've started to use the dried kelp I bought for them lol. So they just have to share!  I also started making bone broth from their bones. So I've started to eat more like them!

### gizmeaux@pacbell.net — 2012-10-31T16:51:37+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-canines.4543/post-61085>

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-canines.4543/post-61111>

### maggie — 2012-11-17T21:58:51+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-canines.4543/post-64996>

Has anyone here had experience with treating a dog with a systemic yeast infection? I'm almost certain now that this is what is ailing my small mixed breed (possible beagle/chiahuaha mix). She's tested allergic to just about everything that grows and has a constantly recurring yeast-y ear infection, along with itchy skin that makes her bite, and runny stools.

In the past the vet has put her on prednisone when things got really bad, but I've just read that that causes even greater yeast overgrowth. Right now she's got an ear infection that isn't responding well to Zymox (usually that works).

I mentioned in the earlier thread that she has apparently recovered from a seizure disorder (almost three months off the phenobarb now, and no seizures) after going epi-paleo with me. Before that we were both eating paleo. And before paleo she was on one of those "ancestral diet" types of kibble. Only the fish has worked for the seizures. I imagine thirteen years of phenobarb has done a lot of damage, but I hope I can make it better.

Anyway, I'd really welcome hearing about any of your experiences or knowledge about handling systemic yeast.

Thanks.

# Thread: Courtesans  Fishcakes
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/courtesans-fishcakes.4545/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Michael — 2012-10-28T21:00:12+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/courtesans-fishcakes.4545/post-60619>

I've mentioned this book before - _Courtesans & Fishcakes: The Consuming Passions of Classical Athens.

It turns out that the _New York Times_ has a chapter online.

I don't know that the book tells us anything about fish, but it's certainly part of the interesting cultural history of fish-eating. In Classical Athens fish was the_ luxury food - much as it is Japan nowadays. (I recall Anthony Bourdain saying that the best meal he'd ever had in a life which must have included quite a few well-cooked meals was in Japan and mostly of fish and other seafood prepared with fanatical carefullness.)

Anyway, here's a taste:

> \`But if you go to the prosperous land of Ambracia and happen to see the boar-fish, buy it! Even if it costs its weight in gold, don't leave without it, lest the dread vengeance of the deathless ones breathe down on you; for this fish is the flower of nectar.' The Greeks were fond of fish. Fondness, on second thoughts, is rather too moderate a word for such a passion. What the literature of pleasure manifests, time and time again, is something rather more intense, a craving, a maddening addiction, an indecent obsession. ...
>
>
>
> What should be noted is not so much the extravagance of the language used to describe the fish, as the fact that in a work about the pleasures of eating in general, reference is made to almost nothing else. ...
>
>
>
> ... fish are sometimes found used as love-gifts in Attic vase-painting. One depicts a young man and his attendant approaching a hetaera \[courtesan\] spinning wool, with gifts of an octopus and two birds. Another vase, once in Leningrad, now lost, had a boy seated and wrapped in a cloak being offered a hoop and a large fish by a winged Cupid.
>
>
>
> It is not just their tastiness that connects fish to seduction, but also the way they look. The two sisters popularly known as the \`anchovies' mentioned in a speech of Hyperides were apparently so named because of their \`pale complexions, slender figures and large eyes'. ...
>
>
>
> ... the practice of comparing women to mouth-watering fish and fish to women seems to have been rather more general in Athenian society. Apart from the anchovy sisters mentioned above, we find flute-girls and hetaeras given nicknames like \`Sand-smelt', \`Red Mullet' and \`Cuttlefish', a practice exploited to full comic effect by the poet Antiphanes in his play She Goes Fishing, where he plays on this double-meaning of the names of fish, so that it is hard to know at any one time whether he is satirizing his victims for their love of fish or for their excessive devotion to hetaeras ...

[http://www.nytimes.com/books/first/d/davidson-courtesans.html](http://http://www.nytimes.com/books/first/d/davidson-courtesans.html)


══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# Thread: Let's talk beef liver . . .

**Posts:** 2

### BJK77 — 2012-10-28T21:21:03+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lets-talk-beef-liver.4546/post-60622>

How do you know if it's 'bad?' I'm sure many would say it's always 'bad,' but I'm talking spoiled here  I absolutely love liver and generally eat it every day. I just got some ground liver from a new farm and the smell is just about killing me. It's stronger than any I've ever had before. I'm almost afraid to eat it.

I honestly have never had spoiled meat before so I don't know what it would smell like. This somewhat reminds me of dog food. It's just awful and soooo strong. I don't know if I can even stomach it if y'all say it's probably ok to eat. The infamous google seems to say that truly bad liver would have an ammonia smell and I definitely don't smell ammonia, but OMG!!

### Jude — 2012-10-29T03:34:51+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lets-talk-beef-liver.4546/post-60666>

> **BJK77;62283 wrote:** How do you know if it's 'bad?' I'm sure many would say it's always 'bad,' but I'm talking spoiled here  I absolutely love liver and generally eat it every day. I just got some ground liver from a new farm and the smell is just about killing me. It's stronger than any I've ever had before. I'm almost afraid to eat it.
>
>
>
> I honestly have never had spoiled meat before so I don't know what it would smell like. This somewhat reminds me of dog food. It's just awful and soooo strong. I don't know if I can even stomach it if y'all say it's probably ok to eat. The infamous google seems to say that truly bad liver would have an ammonia smell and I definitely don't smell ammonia, but OMG!!

Perhaps it to do with what they have been fed? Turnips ? -they can give meat and milk an awful smell. We can't eat our sheep if they have been eating wild turnips--absolutely stinks- and what a waste of meat, of course the dogs think it's Christmas.....

Freshly killed sheep's liver doesn't smell. I can't stand the smell when it's being cooked -just don't like offal.

# Thread: Scientists: New GMO wheat may 'silence' vital human genes
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/scientists-new-gmo-wheat-may-silence-vital-human-genes.4548/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Jude — 2012-10-29T03:56:43+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/scientists-new-gmo-wheat-may-silence-vital-human-genes.4548/post-60667>

Australian scientists are expressing grave concerns over a new type of genetically engineered wheat that may cause major health problems for people that consume it.

[http://www.digitaljournal.com/article/332822#ixzz296GZsBOg](http://http://www.digitaljournal.com/article/332822#ixzz296GZsBOg)

"What we found is that the molecules created in this wheat, intended to silence wheat genes, can match human genes,

and through ingestion, these molecules can enter human beings and potentially silence our genes," Heinemann stated. "The findings are absolutely assured. There is no doubt that these matches exist."

Flinders University Professor Judy Carman and Safe Food Foundation Director Scott Kinnear concurred with Heinemann's analysis.

"If this silences the same gene in us that it silences in the wheat -- well, **children who are born with this enzyme not working tend to die by the age of about five,"** Carman said.

### FarmersDaughter — 2012-10-29T15:32:43+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/scientists-new-gmo-wheat-may-silence-vital-human-genes.4548/post-60693>

Fools rush in where angels fear to tread.

And then, when you factor in genetic drift...Once these genes are out there, eg RoundupReady everything, they cant be called back.

The human race will engineer their own demise.

# Thread: Hands and feet always very cold except for after 7-9pm?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hands-and-feet-always-very-cold-except-for-after-7-9pm.4551/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 8

### seanb4 — 2012-10-29T11:33:12+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hands-and-feet-always-very-cold-except-for-after-7-9pm.4551/post-60684>

Hi guys,

Like the title says may hands and feet are always really cold throughout the day (I have to wear gloves all the time) except when it gets to around 8pm they seem to warm up somewhat. I have noticed this more so since CT.

What is causing this phenomenom? I suspect thyroid/ T3 issues but I dont understand why my thyroid would suddenly switch on at night.

Could gut inflammation during the day play a role on this (from constant bloating after eating?)? Maybe blocking T4 conversion or something?

What do you think?

I'm not sure that one thing 'causes' the other, but that they are all interconnected.

# Thread: First round ever of HCG started today
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/first-round-ever-of-hcg-started-today.4554/>
**Subforum:** HCG Protocol
**Posts:** 71
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### natural_girl — 2012-10-29T17:36:52+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/first-round-ever-of-hcg-started-today.4554/post-60718>

So, I have been waiting on this stuff forever! First shipment got lost, second held in Customs and so I start today and I pray that I did this right! I am still in shock I stuck myself with a needle! LOL

So I followed the directions on the site Colleen moderates. I have 2,000 iu vials and added a total of 4 cc's bacteriostatic water to the powder in the mixing vial and did my first dose of .30 or 30 on the syringe. It just doesn't look like there is much left in that vial! I really hope I did it right! Plan on having me some Chipotle steak salad for lunch today & tomorrow since I won't be eating it for awhile.

A lot of people have commented they can't believe I am doing this through the Holiday's but what the hell. This is my busy time of year for work, crazy busy! I don't cook much because I am so busy so throwing some shrimp, chicken or steak on the George Foreman sounds perfect. I know exactly what I can eat don't have to think about it etc. I am not home much so the family can eat whatever they want. I have a big business trip in February to NYC and know that I will want some cocktails so I just figured why not. I am so tired of the weight. Yes, I want to be Optimal but to me that means a healthy weight also. I did not weigh this morning and I am loading today & tomorrow so I will weight tomorrow morning to see where I am at. Hoping to only have to do this one round but not sure cause I probably have anywhere from 30-40 pounds to lose. Although I care more about the size I am in than what the scale says. Today I am a size 12 and would love to be a 6 again so we will see!

Would love any suggestions on meals & sides from those of you who have done this before. I am making sauerkraut tonight so I can use that as a side often and I do plan on eating shrimp often. I have organic, pastured grass fed beef as well.

Will check in here often to keep me accountable & for support. Thanks Colleen & Cavemam for answering all my PM's really appreciate it!

### colleencoble — 2012-10-29T23:07:44+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/first-round-ever-of-hcg-started-today.4554/post-60793>

You'll be shocked how great it works.

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-10-29T23:15:20+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/first-round-ever-of-hcg-started-today.4554/post-60798>

Keep us posted! Being busy sounds perfect!!

### natural_girl — 2012-10-30T21:28:14+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/first-round-ever-of-hcg-started-today.4554/post-60983>

Ok am I allowed to save all my protein to eat at dinner or no? Also, Cavemam I plan on doing 300g of protein like you did to substitute for the breadstick. You didn't have any problem with this right? What kind of sides were you guys eating?

### colleencoble — 2012-10-30T22:03:06+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/first-round-ever-of-hcg-started-today.4554/post-60990>

No. You need to eat the food as laid out. An extra 100 grams of protein is too much to sub in for those measly breadsticks. LOL Cavemam may have subbed a fruit out for that. I don't really recommend doing that long term though. It seems to cause problems late with thyroid suppression in some if the carbs fall too low.

### natural_girl — 2012-10-30T22:20:24+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/first-round-ever-of-hcg-started-today.4554/post-60992>

> **colleencoble;62702 wrote:** No. You need to eat the food as laid out. An extra 100 grams of protein is too much to sub in for those measly breadsticks. LOL Cavemam may have subbed a fruit out for that. I don't really recommend doing that long term though. It seems to cause problems late with thyroid suppression in some if the carbs fall too low.

Colleen,

I plan on doing shrimp most of the time & 4 oz of shrimp has 70 calories & 16g protein (this was for 26/30 shrimp). I am on compounded T3. I was actually planning on having 2 apples most days. I haven't eaten fruit in years! May have an orange here and there but I love apples! So I have to split the protein up?

### colleencoble — 2012-10-31T02:22:54+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/first-round-ever-of-hcg-started-today.4554/post-61015>

Caveman, did you take it to up your testosterone? She's not doing that. LOL It's perfectly safe and an absolute miracle for weight loss.

natural girl, yes you need to split it up into 2 meals. You can do more but losses tend to be better if you do only 2 meals.

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-10-31T04:04:57+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/first-round-ever-of-hcg-started-today.4554/post-61029>

SO HCG Ladies,

Here are a couple of things I found on my round. If it conflicts with what Colleen says, go with her! She's seen more success and failure and will not guide you astray.

I had a 25 lbd net loss and so far have kept it below my LIW (last injection weight). I think my round ended mid august, so clearly I don't have much long term success to guide one.

I found:

1\. Fish just didn't cut it for me. I was way too hungry after meals and I never got that full feeling. When combined with strawberries it just wasn't enough food for me volume wise. I found I really needed the chicken/beef. I did mostly chicken with beef every couple of days and I kept TRYING fish, and would go back to chicken or beef. I would combine a chicken and beef day.

2\. I didn't have same veggie more than one time a day, and used fermented/pickled veggies quite a bit. It really helps the dry chicken! I did not mix veggies at a meal.

3\. I did not eat off protocol or fancy things up. I seasoned with salt and pepper and the allotted daily lemon and that was it. No recipes etc. I have a theory that its probably for the best if the food is bland and unappealing. Much less likely to take too many bites!

4\. I would weigh out several portions raw all at once.

5\. Dose issues aside, I found those times when hunger would creep in - a larger apple with my protein did the trick.

6\. I did not eat off protocol AT ALL.

7\. I did eat fruit every day. I'm for it. I mostly ate it with both meals. I did strawberries and apples.

8\. I cut melba and after adding up my calories for the day I made up the difference with veggie and protein. I did no more than 500 calories.

9\. Don't panic if you don't poop. PM me

10\. I did my round over the 4th of July family vacation. I pre cooked and took my food with me. It sucked so hard. It SUCKED MAJORLY. So, if my hungry fat arse can do that - you can make it 40 days through the holiday. I know you can! Go team!

### colleencoble — 2012-10-31T15:45:00+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/first-round-ever-of-hcg-started-today.4554/post-61073>

I could not eat beef on the protocol without major stalls. I ate almost exclusively fish and shrimp with some chicken. If you had hunger on fish, cavemam, your dose likely needed adjusted. On the right dose, it doesn't matter what you eat--you just aren't hungry.  But you did great! 25 pounds is super!

### natural_girl — 2012-10-31T16:56:44+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/first-round-ever-of-hcg-started-today.4554/post-61086>

Cavemam was the 25 pounds you lost including what you gained loading? I gained 6 pounds loading! Not surprising since I really never eat carbs and ate a ton of potatoes! I feel the water I gained funny thing is when I used to eat carbs I would bloat like crazy but lately that doesn't really happen maybe just a tiny bit but I feel like it's normal. I think that might mean my hormones are getting back on track.

Colleen when I read Pounds & Inches it said not to take thyroid meds while on protocol but you said you continued on yours right? It said people who have been on them long term lose less weight while on hcg? I have only been on my T3 since March so hoping that won't apply as much to me.

I plan on having shrimp as much as possible to stay Epi Paleo. I am not a fan of fish and love lobster & crab but not without ghee so shrimp is it! I will have pastured, organic chicken and occasionally beef but not often since I am low on beef and my cow won't be in & cut until end of November and most cuts I have left I think might be to fatty. I adore beef but I really want to lose as much as I can. I am hoping whatever I have left to lose after this I will be able to lose through IF or the Bulletproof RFL protocol

So today is my first restriction day. I brought 6 grilled shrimp, about a 1/4 head cabbage & apple to work for lunch. Did my 3rd injection of .30 cc and will continue with that dose. I am a bit hungry but thinking it might be my mind and I am sure the first few days are the worse? Can I chew a piece of sugarless gum? And I know we can have vinegar but any kind? What about balsamic?

Thanks girls! I am praying I lose a ton!

I am a bit hungry but thinking it might be my mind and I am sure the first few days are the worse? Can I chew a piece of sugarless gum? And I know we can have vinegar but any kind? > Thanks girls! I am praying I lose a ton!

Watch the balsamic, they almost always have calories/carbs. Lemon is a nice thing, too. Look at is as a chance to cleanse out everything. If the hunger is sated - might as well get the benefits of resting the liver etc.

My 25 lbds was net. I gained a lot at load in. I was much bigger than you though, so you will most likely have smaller losses! I went up to 240 after load in, and ended at 208.8

I keep seeing more success in others with the bigger fatter gainier load ins. SO that was probably a helper.

Also, get out of your mind that you are "over" when the round is over. You really have to stay vigilant and do restrictions and slow introductions and steak days after the round is over during P3. I thought I was NEVER going to stabalize. . and was doing some back to back steak days to keep within the 2 lbd limit. Then boom, hit week 4 after and it all clicked and it was easy to maintain.

### colleencoble — 2012-10-31T17:52:59+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/first-round-ever-of-hcg-started-today.4554/post-61098>

Most of us stay on our thyroid meds. Some have stellar losses. I lost about 20 pounds per round but my metabolism was shot. And 20 pounds was fabulous as far as I was concerned. Most people count the load weight because it has to be lost too. Dr. Simeon's counted load weight. Your dose might be a bit high. If you're still hungry in the morning, do down to 25 units.

SO, weighing in week 1 is fun. Weighing in week 2 makes baby jesus cry!

I would use strawberries to increase the calories of my lunch when needed. Fruit is NOT just a carbs/fructose/glucose story. Just like with fish, there is a lot more to it than just the triad macro nutrients.

I would err on the side of working with your hunger + going as close to protocol as possible. When I said earlier I ate beef, I should have clarified - I only ate it maybe 1-2X a week and usually on a fish day as fish never seemed to work for me.

### colleencoble — 2012-11-05T16:12:18+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/first-round-ever-of-hcg-started-today.4554/post-62202>

Your body will slow for a bit. It's normal to stall out at about 10 pounds. Now things will slow down to .5-.75 per day average. Some days you won't lose anything, then will lose a pound the next day. That kind of thing. It won't be consistent. The first week's losses are mostly water weight. Then as your body starts adding back in the water and continues to lose fat, the scale will be inconsistent.

Today I got me a whirlpool for outdoors which I am putting up on my patio. I can keep this at 50-55Â°F and since ice in Germany is hard to come by, especially in the winter, I bought two (hot)water bottles from rubber which I will fill up, put in the freezer and put on my chest. Afterwards I can roll around in the snow. And if I feel like it I can put the temperature up and have a hot bath. So far, I did cold showers. Now temperature in Germany is down to 40Â°F already. Wonder, how this goes with hCG. Eating is not a problem, on hCG I did well on steak, fish and vegetable. I almost always skipped the apples and never did the bread stick due to gluten allergy. But I nevertheless had stalls, but after six weeks I was rid of the first 15 kg. So I think, stalls are normal. My diabetes vanished completely and I do not take any medication anymore. The body is amazing in knowing when to lose and when to stall. So I wish you that your weight loss continues to make progress and you do not lose motivation.

### tyodonnell@roadrunne — 2012-11-25T09:46:59+0200

Colleen,

Â 

Need some help! Ok so weighed in Thanksgiving morning and I was down a total of 17.2 pounds and I guess I got to confident or what. Went to Thanksgiving dinner and I had planned on having turkey breast but I also ate one half of a deviled egg, 3 bites of sweet potato, a few brussel sprouts & about 4 bites of gluten free dressing! Next day I was up .2 lbs, yesterday up .6 and today .8!!!! I don't know why it keeps increasing even though I have followed protocol since. Also, I never ate lunch Thanksgiving day I had another half of the deviled egg for lunch. I did switch to a new gum also and I wonder if that has something to do with it? It is sugar free though still. I usually chew one or two pieces at most a day but just in case I am giving it to my daughter. My dose is at .20 and I am not hungry. I did lower it some since I was starving on .25 and went down to .22 but was still hungry a lot. I feel the weight or bloat in my stomach. I so should have avoided that darn GF stuffing! I am so mad at myself but at the same time it just stinks that I can't have a few bites of what most people would consider healthy food without blowing up! I guess it will be like this forever! My body is so sensitive! Funny thing is though too I wonder if something had gluten or dairy or was because since then I have had a slightly scratchy sore throat which I usually get if I eat either one of those foods.Â  Now what should I do to get back to losing again? I can't even begin to tell you how bad I hate myself right now!

### colleencoble — 2012-11-25T14:22:38+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/first-round-ever-of-hcg-started-today.4554/post-65568>

That is very normal. When you eat off protocol, the gains can go on for a couple of days. There's nothing you can do to avoid the consequences though. { "lightbox\_close": "Close", "lightbox\_next": "Next", "lightbox\_previous": "Previous", "lightbox\_error": "The requested content cannot be loaded. Please try again later.", "lightbox\_start\_slideshow": "Start slideshow", "lightbox\_stop\_slideshow": "Stop slideshow", "lightbox\_full\_screen": "Full screen", "lightbox\_thumbnails": "Thumbnails", "lightbox\_download": "Download", "lightbox\_share": "Share", "lightbox\_zoom": "Zoom", "lightbox\_new\_window": "New window", "lightbox\_toggle\_sidebar": "Toggle sidebar" }

Just stick to protocol and it will start falling off again. You can't have a few bites of ANYTHING on hcg. It's not a diet. It's a hormonal protocol and anything off the list can disrupt the hormonal response. That's the first thing people have to realize. And just because something is gluten free, doesn't mean it's healthy. Gluten free dressing still has grains and gluten free stuff is often higher in carbs than wheat products. So I avoid them the majority of the time.Â 

When you get to your ideal weight, if you stick to Doc's leptin timing, your insulin resistance will get better and better and you won't react to badly to the occasional ingestion of higher carbs than normal. Good luck!
