# Thread: Progesterone is like an ssri?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/progesterone-is-like-an-ssri.4559/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 9
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### ColdBren — 2012-10-29T22:56:39+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/progesterone-is-like-an-ssri.4559/post-60791>

I just started taking prometrium and it seems to be lifting my mood and obliterating my anxiety, just like zoloft did, but without the side effects. Does progesterone actually increase seratonin? If so, why doesn't anyone talk about this when discussing ways to raise seratonin. It seems so easy.

### shah78 — 2012-10-29T23:08:30+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/progesterone-is-like-an-ssri.4559/post-60794>

dear bren, please remember the seratonin-depression connection is a unproven fantasy concocted by Big Pharma in order to convince allopathic physicans to hand out ssris like candy. My depression went away when I started eating 60% of my calories from animal fat. It took less than one week.

### nonchalant — 2012-10-29T23:25:18+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/progesterone-is-like-an-ssri.4559/post-60800>

I think that when the brain gets what it has been lacking, you will get a 'high'. I sure had one when starting paleo, and I had it again when I started eating seafood daily. In fact, I get a charge out of CT also, just in a different way. Glad you are seeing some relief!

### ColdBren — 2012-10-29T23:45:23+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/progesterone-is-like-an-ssri.4559/post-60801>

My issue has never really been depression, but as for anxiety, there is no doubt ssri's have always relieved the symptoms.....though I don't know the mechanism and I know from the info on here it is not a good drug. I just can't believe how fast prometrium worked. BTW, the creams have never had this kind of effect. So again I am just wondering, does progesterone increase seratonin? Because we can all agree seratonin does influence feelings of well being correct?

### Jack Kruse — 2012-10-29T23:49:35+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/progesterone-is-like-an-ssri.4559/post-60802>

I think you're issue might have always been an upside PG/E2. The webinar on cation ion transporters hit this a bit. Glad to hear you feel better.

### shah78 — 2012-10-29T23:57:51+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/progesterone-is-like-an-ssri.4559/post-60809>

Taking (ZOLOFT)ssri does,, did influence feelings of well being. but so does TCH.But they both just mask some other issue that is causing a low seratonin issue.Low Vit d, low cortisol, low thyroid, low "anything". Sort of like the statin- cholestoral issue. High cholestorol is a sympton of something else. In Paleoland it would be inflamation.

### RiverNeighbor — 2012-10-30T00:00:02+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/progesterone-is-like-an-ssri.4559/post-60811>

I got the same relief from progesterone! Only my anxiety was often mixed with feeling kinda angry.

### ColdBren — 2012-10-30T00:05:13+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/progesterone-is-like-an-ssri.4559/post-60812>

Dr K- I think you are right about my issue always being my pg/e2 ratio, at least in terms of chronic anxiety....shocker, you are right again  But a side note to anyone who may see this with nocturnal panic attacks, after testing sistergroks theory for many months, salicylates are the cause of these nocturnal central sleep apnea episodes.....turns out they weren't really panic attacks at all.

shah- makes total sense.

River- I had the anger too!!

### janagram — 2012-11-01T19:02:19+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/progesterone-is-like-an-ssri.4559/post-61320>

wow...you found this out, Bren? What powerful innformation! Great to hear.

### Dali Dula — 2012-10-30T01:14:12+0200

### Shijin13 — 2012-10-30T01:44:26+0200

### Lyndra — 2012-10-30T01:44:52+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/preparing-for-optimal-hurricane.4561/post-60837>

Geeze, I guess I am too practical. We were without power 2 days last fall and I was worried about losing all the fish & meat in the freezer and pipes freezing. I was glad we didn't have tropical fish - I went through a power failure back in my fish-keeping days and lost a lot of them.

### Jude — 2012-10-30T02:40:13+0200

### kathylu — 2012-10-30T03:01:05+0200

Ok, the 5 year old is in bed, asleep. Now I have no worries at all! I was afraid the worst would hit and he would be frightened and not able to sleep. Once he is asleep, a hurricane could be raging outside and he wouldn't notice...

### Shijin13 — 2012-10-30T13:49:16+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/preparing-for-optimal-hurricane.4561/post-60886>

Well we survived. No loss of power (disappointed) and no snow.

### Melinda — 2012-10-30T15:15:08+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/preparing-for-optimal-hurricane.4561/post-60900>

> **Shijin13;62593 wrote:** Well we survived. No loss of power (disappointed) and no snow.

Thanks for the real time update. It's hard to picture what it's like to be in the way of such a massive storm.

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-10-30T15:31:35+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/preparing-for-optimal-hurricane.4561/post-60906>

> **Shijin13;62593 wrote:** Well we survived. No loss of power (disappointed) and no snow.

My people! I am always really sad after preparing for disaster and it doesn't happen. I am always completely energized when disaster hits - I go into superhero mode and am never happier and more full of life than when I can serve people who REALLY need it.

### Shijin13 — 2012-10-30T16:11:02+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/preparing-for-optimal-hurricane.4561/post-60920>

> **Cavemam;62613 wrote: My people! I am always really sad after preparing for disaster and it doesn't happen. I am always completely energized when disaster hits - I go into superhero mode and am never happier and more full of life than when I can serve people who REALLY need it.
>
>
> you crave chaos don't you! why aren't you doing something in law enforcement or emergency management - you could put that extra T to work!
>
>
>
>
>
> Melinda;62607 wrote:** Thanks for the real time update. It's hard to picture what it's like to be in the way of such a massive storm.

having been through it several times before - you can't imagine it unless you've lived through it. the difference is when I lived in Guam and we had 11 Super typhoon (Cat 5 hurricanes) in one season, the island was always prepared. the houses were built in such away that the winds weren't an issue, and while we may have lost power for several weeks, we didn't loose water. they knew how to deal with it on a regular basis b/c they were a part of daily life for 9mths out of a year, you knew you were gonna get hit. In NJ and NY they've never seen anything like this in the past 60yrs (no one alive remembers the last time a hurricane - blew threw and brought the city to its knees) the infrastructure isn't built to withstand a direct hit... add a Cat1, plus high tide and a full moon - and that's a lot of water going into a city that's surrounded by water on 3 sides...

the best you can do is prepare for the worst, and hope for the best, and get out of the way if you're in low lying areas if you can. That's whats different about a hurricane hitting the mainland of the US - you can get out of the way if you choose too. when you live on an island that's 9 miles long, and btwn 3-6miles wide at given points - you can't go anywhere - you can't afford a $3K plane ticket to the Philippines, or Korea, or Japan - and most like the typhoon you're trying to get away from will just end up their any way - so you ride it out...

This is mother nature's way of reminding us we don't control the environment to the extent we think we do...

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-10-30T16:59:37+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/preparing-for-optimal-hurricane.4561/post-60933>

> **Shijin13;62627 wrote:** you crave chaos don't you! why aren't you doing something in law enforcement or emergency management - you could put that extra T to work!

I really do crave it! Good way to put it. I always fantasized about doing something like FBI/Emergency work - the problem was I was pushing 350 lbds when I graduated high school and there wasn't much of a calling for fat trombone players in the FBI back then!!!!

Onto now, I keep batting about the idea of becoming an EMT - but all the people in the know says the "system" is so broke and does not allow for casual epi-paleo circadian minding people. Of course, that's what they told me about real estate and I dominated in the worst year ever for real estate agents when I was brand new by helping the people screwed by the falling market who were totally in personal crisis. So who knows.

### Lyndra — 2012-10-30T18:00:19+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/preparing-for-optimal-hurricane.4561/post-60948>

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-10-30T18:11:31+0200

hehe I'm OK with animals, some I like, some I don't - I think the bigger problem for me is that kind of operation sounds like a lot of volunteers all in a group. I don't function well in groups or volunteer situations. I'm one-on-one all the way. Man, I'm a pain in the arse. I figure now that I'm older and wiser I get to engineer my world to suit me. When I was young and innocent I had to try to fit into the world. Who knew the world was just an imaginary and flexible construct. I hope I can impart that early to caveboy!

# Thread: Celiac and seasonality
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/celiac-and-seasonality.4562/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Lyndra — 2012-10-30T00:59:35+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/celiac-and-seasonality.4562/post-60827>

Just saw this at NYT - [http://well.blogs.nytimes.com/2012/10/29/really-celiac-disease-is-influenced-by-season-of-birth/?smid=tw-nytimeshealth&seid=auto](http://http://well.blogs.nytimes.com/2012/10/29/really-celiac-disease-is-influenced-by-season-of-birth/?smid=tw-nytimeshealth&seid=auto).

Reportedly, babies born in spring/summer are more likely to develop celiac disease. They theorize it is because when babies are typically weaned at 6 months and start eating gluten, it will be wintertime when colds & flu are more common, thus depressing the babies' immune systems. No mention about what the mother is exposed to when she's pregnant during fall, winter & early spring when she is probably not eating based on seasonal food availability.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-10-30T01:01:44+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/celiac-and-seasonality.4562/post-60830>

I tweeted this story this afternoon..........

### Lyndra — 2012-10-30T01:46:33+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/celiac-and-seasonality.4562/post-60838>

Oops, maybe that's why I saw it!!!

# Thread: Water well casing question
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/water-well-casing-question.4564/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 8
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### nonchalant — 2012-10-30T02:22:49+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/water-well-casing-question.4564/post-60842>

If you were going to put in a new water well, about 300' down, what kind of casing would you use? The water is acidic.

PVC seems to be everywhere, but who wants the BPA?

Copper would be extremely expensive. Galvanized?

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-10-30T02:55:27+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/water-well-casing-question.4564/post-60855>

I run into well issues around here on our mountain properties - sadly, there are only a few options from well providers that provide permit quality (thus saleable in the future and documented properly) . I would get your bids from the well companies and see if any of them provide options on that.

That is a great thing to think about - and it would be worth shopping around.

Also I would find out if your installer just meets required levels, or exceeds the epa standards. I know here we have one more "eco" friendly option - but it costs about twice as much.

My point is, you don't really have many choices - unless of course you are going off grid and unpermitted - in which case I would say SHHHHHHH! don't let your insurance or neighbors find out. Also hide it if you ever want to sell!!!

### nate.reik@yahoo.com — 2012-10-30T15:06:12+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/water-well-casing-question.4564/post-60898>

BPA usually is only used in flexible PVC, I would think well casing would be stiff?

### nonchalant — 2012-10-30T15:15:17+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/water-well-casing-question.4564/post-60901>

> **Cavemam;62554 wrote:**
>
> My point is, you don't really have many choices - unless of course you are going off grid and unpermitted - in which case I would say SHHHHHHH! don't let your insurance or neighbors find out. Also hide it if you ever want to sell!!!

Actually where this property is, there are few regulations. Thanks Nate, it's good to know rigid PVC pipe doesn't have bpa, though who knows what other endocrine-disrupting chemicals it has. It definitely would be the less expensive option.

### Dali Dula — 2012-10-30T17:04:33+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/water-well-casing-question.4564/post-60934>

There are two elements here. the well casing is usually 8" iron and usually does not go all the way to the bottom. The other issue is the pipe coming from the submersible pump which can be pvc or galvanized. Most use schedule 40 pvc. The iron casing does not typically contact your water it just holds the shape of the well through top soil layers. The supply pipe would be 1-1/2" pvc. I don't know if pvc leaches chemicals into drinking water. I hope not cuz that's what I've got. Pvc is polyvinylchloride.

### freshveggies — 2012-10-30T17:13:20+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/water-well-casing-question.4564/post-60940>

What kind of pipes do you all recommend in a house. Post are using the flexible PVC now. Copper has been phased out.

### villjamur_stevenson — 2012-10-30T17:20:59+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/water-well-casing-question.4564/post-60943>

i think we used a steel casing on my well 6 years ago. However, we are only required to put the casing in down to the bedrock. I'm assuming you don't have bedrock where you live. 300' would be very pricey!

### nonchalant — 2012-10-30T17:43:51+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/water-well-casing-question.4564/post-60946>

> **freshveggies;62647 wrote:** What kind of pipes do you all recommend in a house. Post are using the flexible PVC now. Copper has been phased out.

Good question, Veggies. I'm leaning towards copper pipes.

Villjamur, we don't have rock, just sand and clay.

# Thread: What is the main and backup fuel sources for gum tissue?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-is-the-main-and-backup-fuel-sources-for-gum-tissue.4565/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 8
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Glen PDQ — 2012-10-30T02:27:47+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-is-the-main-and-backup-fuel-sources-for-gum-tissue.4565/post-60844>

Anyone know for a fact the main fuel source of gum tissue when the body is NOT in ketosis and liver glycogen stores are still sufficiently available and carb intake is not low?

Anyone know what the backup fuel source for gum tissue is when the body is chugging along during ketosis assuming the body is getting sufficient protein and fat but liver glycogen stores are low/depleted?

The answer may seem obvious, but keep in mind that during (ketosis/ketogenic diet) the brain gets the majority of the ketones, and the muscles, as an example, switch to burning fat instead of glucose, sparing the ketones for the brain.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-10-30T02:31:07+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-is-the-main-and-backup-fuel-sources-for-gum-tissue.4565/post-60847>

Periodontal disease is correlated with very low Co Enz Q10 levels........

### Glen PDQ — 2012-10-30T02:42:49+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-is-the-main-and-backup-fuel-sources-for-gum-tissue.4565/post-60853>

To detour just a bit, and this question is open to all, what is a sufficient daily intake in peoples' opinions of the reduced form of CoQ10?

### Jack Kruse — 2012-10-30T03:14:39+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-is-the-main-and-backup-fuel-sources-for-gum-tissue.4565/post-60860>

min 400 mgs a day of oil based reduced form..........I did Nov webinar today and mentioned this in the webinar.......

### Glen PDQ — 2012-10-30T03:50:00+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-is-the-main-and-backup-fuel-sources-for-gum-tissue.4565/post-60865>

Thanks for the heads up!

### John Ottman — 2026-04-16T18:50:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-is-the-main-and-backup-fuel-sources-for-gum-tissue.4565/post-371230>

After 10 yrs of very routine dentist visits for cleaning and checkups, and repairs, I left my dentist in search of a new one. Found one about a year later. I was not in a rush as my teeth had been good for a couple years now.

This Dentist did a full exam lots of pictures, couple x-rays and during the the gum line check found 3 deep pockets around 7mm on my molars - the side where it doesn't have a tooth next to it. My wisdom teeth were removed 25 years. The rest of my pockets are 2's and 3's. Hopefully I'm explaining the location of deep pockets in in a way that makes sense. Boy did it hurt when they stuck that tool in those molar gums.

This was pretty shocking to me, and disappointing to say the least. I felt I had everything under control and especially so after the past 2 years of living the way Jack teaches.

I have no doubt that before coming across Jack's work my previous life style had impact on this. Is see up above where Jack said periodontal disease is due to low Co Enz Q10 levels... is 400 mg of CoQ10 still recommended along side everything he teaches or has there been more data to suggest other protocols?

I see the sunrise daily, ground, get mid day light an evening light. All in all about 2-3 hrs a day, and weekends much more. t. I do CT and eat clean. Limit RF and other tech and light position best I can living in San Francisco, CA

Thank you

### Jack Kruse — 2026-04-19T03:38:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-is-the-main-and-backup-fuel-sources-for-gum-tissue.4565/post-371473>

Chewing mastic gum also is a hack for perio diseases. But you need the hard resin.

### John Ottman — 2026-04-21T07:16:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-is-the-main-and-backup-fuel-sources-for-gum-tissue.4565/post-371674>

> [Jack Kruse said:](/goto/post?id=371473)
>
> Chewing mastic gum also is a hack for perio diseases. But you need the hard resin.

Thanks Jack! I appreciate the reply. Been chewing it since the podcast with Alexis dropped. That was a fantastic podcast.

These three deep pockets really threw me for a surprise. My teeth have gotten so much better(whiter) in the past two years since following the science you teach. Guessing the deep pockets starting a while back and wasn't caught by my previous provider

# Thread: 'At Home' testers for things other than blood sugar levels
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/at-home-testers-for-things-other-than-blood-sugar-levels.4567/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Labs
**Posts:** 5
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Glen PDQ — 2012-10-30T02:38:10+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/at-home-testers-for-things-other-than-blood-sugar-levels.4567/post-60851>

Are there relatively inexpensive 'at home' testers for other things besides blood sugar that would replace some doctor visits to get one or more panels? For example, are testers available that can test saliva or blood samples at home for a omega 6/omega 3 ratio? The smart chips in testers that sample blood ought to be able to do lots of other things or is that just wishful thinking?

### Glen PDQ — 2012-10-30T23:46:12+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/at-home-testers-for-things-other-than-blood-sugar-levels.4567/post-61004>

That A1c tester sounds helpful. I might spring for the one at Walgreens.

### JanSz — 2012-11-04T05:32:05+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/at-home-testers-for-things-other-than-blood-sugar-levels.4567/post-61923>

> **Glen PDQ;62549 wrote: Are there relatively inexpensive 'at home' testers for other things besides blood sugar that would replace some doctor visits to get one or more panels? For example, are testers available that can test saliva or blood samples at home for a omega 6/omega 3 ratio**? The smart chips in testers that sample blood ought to be able to do lots of other things or is that just wishful thinking?

I am afraid that rather than be helpfull, testing only omega 6/omega 3 ratio

would create even more misinformation.

Similarly to HS-Omega3 index

or similar tests.

Many people eat lots (or even little) fish oils or krill oils.

They have way way too much EPA(omega3).

Introduction of EPA creates havoc.

EPA suppresses AA (omega6) which fools you twice

first it suppresses AA (the most valuable Omega6)

second it makes you think that your totalOmega6 is low (because it took out the most valuable part)

High EPA makes you think that you have highTotalOmega3

by raising it (mathematically) with garbage=EPA

//////////

.

### RiverNeighbor — 2012-11-04T05:52:22+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/at-home-testers-for-things-other-than-blood-sugar-levels.4567/post-61925>

Jan, OMG, now I get this.... and my test from last winter. Thank you!

# Thread: Foraged food
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/foraged-food.4569/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Michael — 2012-10-30T07:56:48+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/foraged-food.4569/post-60878>

> Luigi Ballerini revisits \[the\] distant past in "A Feast of Weeds: A Literary Guide to Foraging and Cooking Wild Edible Plants" (University of California Press), which celebrates the foraged foods that are currently enjoying a renaissance in Italy and elsewhere. ...
>
>
>
> "Once you taste greens that you plucked from the field, you'll never go back â€” or do so with great regret," he said. "The taste of what you get at the market is nothing compared to what wild weeds really taste like."

[http://newsroom.ucla.edu/portal/ucla/ucla-professor-author-champions-237441.aspx](http://http://newsroom.ucla.edu/portal/ucla/ucla-professor-author-champions-237441.aspx)

### Jonathin — 2012-10-30T20:16:46+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/foraged-food.4569/post-60975>

Most foraged foods are better than cultivated counterparts. It is not because a human did not grow them deliberately. It is that the biological and chemical nature of farm and garden soil is usually poor to mediocre. Chemical or organic techniques are not necessarily a determining factor of quality. It varies widely.

One easy way to tell if a vegetable is of very good quality is: does it rot, turn brown or dehydrate easily? Eating junk vegetables no matter how they are labeled is not benefiting your body.

I already posted part one of this series. This second part has a lot of the archaeology of this fishy area:

> "That's probably 90 per cent of the reason why this place has been occupied for so long," McLaren continues, gesturing to encompass more than the spot we stand on. "Because one of the major resources here is shellfish, people are harvesting shellfish. You've got beautiful clam beds nearby, you have all these rocks and there are these great mussel beds. So you know you can go out there and if you're hungry here's a quick and easy food source. And a reliable one. It's there all winter when people are waiting for the salmon or whatever to come in." ...
>
>
>
> Over 250 stone fish traps dot the Central Coast. In his study, White focused on 40 traps, and interviewed community elders about the salmon harvest.
>
>
> [http://thetyee.ca/News/2012/10/30/Archaeology-on-BC-Coast/](http://http://thetyee.ca/News/2012/10/30/Archaeology-on-BC-Coast/)

>

# Thread: Shellfish Conference in Oban
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/shellfish-conference-in-oban.4571/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Michael — 2012-10-30T09:11:54+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/shellfish-conference-in-oban.4571/post-60880>

Shellfish conference footage, including a demonstration of how to shuck an oyster:

\[video\]\[video=youtube\_share;48y3lzB6LW0\]http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=48y3lzB6LW0\[/video\]\[/video\]

# Thread: Richard Wrangham again
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/richard-wrangham-again.4572/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Michael — 2012-10-30T09:22:20+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/richard-wrangham-again.4572/post-60881>

Another article on Richard Wrangham's theories:

[http://www.slate.com/articles/health\_and\_science/human\_evolution/2012/10/who\_invented\_fire\_when\_did\_people\_start\_cooking\_.single.html](http://http://www.slate.com/articles/health_and_science/human_evolution/2012/10/who_invented_fire_when_did_people_start_cooking_.single.html)

He does sound to be saying some stuff that really isn't supported by the evidence:

> \[Wrangham's\] hypothesis stems from a few modern observations. When you eat cooked food, you have access to many more calories than if you eat the same food raw. There are two reasons: Our digestive systems can extract more calories from a cooked steak (for instance) than a raw steak, and it takes much less energy to cook and eat a steak than to gnaw on a raw one for hours. Access to cooked food means a hominid no longer needs enormous teeth to break down all that raw meat and roughage into swallowable hunks, nor does it need as robust a digestive system to process it all. The combination of more calories and less complicated intestines means more energy can be devote to cogitatingâ€”hence H. erectusâ€™ relatively big brains, which suck up a lot of calories.
>
>
> This implies the brain's needs are all about calories. It sounds like he needs to talk to the brain scientists.
>
>
>
> As evidence for his theory, Wrangham likes to point to the fact that modern-day humans canâ€™t thrive on an all-raw dietâ€”raw foodists tend to stop menstruating, precluding reproduction.

Pretty poor evidence, isn't it? These women are vegans - _that's_ why they stop menstruating. It hardly amounts to a controlled experiment showing what happens when someone eats, say, a raw shellfish diet. I don't believe it is in line with the ethnographic evidence either - which is strange since Wrangham is an anthropologist.

This is a report about what Wrangham is saying rather than his own words, but it does sound like he's coming out with some shaky stuff.


══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# Thread: The Wonders of Docasahexaenoic Acid
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-wonders-of-docasahexaenoic-acid.4573/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Michael — 2012-10-30T11:28:05+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-wonders-of-docasahexaenoic-acid.4573/post-60882>

A report from the _Economist from an international conference on DHA held in May 2010 (with references to Michael Crawford and Joseph Hibbeln):

> ... its displacement from modern diets by the omega-6 acids in cooking oils such as soya, maize and rape is a cause of worry. Many researchers think this shiftâ€”and the change in brain chemistry that it causesâ€”explains the growth in recent times of depression, manic-depression, memory loss, schizophrenia and attention-deficit disorder. It may also be responsible for rising levels of obesity and thus ... heart disease ...
>
>
>
> In this experiment \[Dr. Hibbeln\] fed rats diets that were identical except that in one case 8% of the calories came from linoleic acid (an omega-6 fatty acid) while in the other that value was 1%. These percentages reflect the shift in the proportion of omega-6s in the American diet between 1909 and the early 21st century.
>
>
>
> In the 8% diet, levels of rat obesity doubled. It turns out that in rats (and also in humans) linoleic acid is converted into molecules called endocannabinoids that trigger appetite. Those who eat omega-6s, in other words, want to eat more food. ...

The Economist_'s own solution to this set of appalling problems is to suggest genetically engineered soya! Good grief.

[http://www.economist.com/node/16214142](http://http://www.economist.com/node/16214142)

### Michael — 2012-10-30T11:31:21+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-wonders-of-docasahexaenoic-acid.4573/post-60883>

# Thread: I wanted my life back
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-wanted-my-life-back.4574/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 12
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Michael — 2012-10-30T13:50:37+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-wanted-my-life-back.4574/post-60887>

I'm just listening to Abel James' "Fat Burning Man" podcast. This week he has a lady on as a guest who was pretty much wrecking her life with a paleo eating plan till she woke up to what she was doing and backed off.

The plan, which I hadn't heard of before, is called Lean Gains and involves deliberate fasting and bingeing. It sounds very unhealthy - particularly for women whose hormones seem so much more complex than men's. I know "intermittent fasting" is something of a fetish on the fringe of the paleo world. I don't see anything wrong with skipping the occasional meal when one's not hungry, but these plans of forced starving and eating sound foolish to me.

Be that as it may, it was disastrous for this woman. Her sleep went first. Then she broke out in acne. Then she found her sense of satiety was destroyed, so that the binges, which she at first had to force down, eventually only stoked her hunger. Finally, she seems to have developed a range of psychological problems, including secretive behaviour, what sounds like a mild form of paranoia, and a range of obsessive behaviours, such as weighing herself multiple times a day.

She must have totally screwed her biochemistry.

She says that her having owned up publicly has antagonized some women who do this. She's says they have a website on which they exchange photos of themselves with distended sromachs, when in the bingeing phase. They're now posting hate material about her - which she sensibly doesn't read. It all sounds very creepy. Maybe Abel should've posted it on Halloween.

The lady said: "I just wanted my life back". I hope she's getting that.

### Shijin13 — 2012-10-30T14:06:38+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-wanted-my-life-back.4574/post-60890>

I never got the IF fanatics, nor the followers of lean gains... intuitively it never made sense... when I tried to IF 1x/month before starting the LR - I was always hungry and never satisfied... I tried it 2x and decided it wasn't for me.... I seriously believe that there are ppl so desperate to loose weight NOW - that they'll do anything to do it... even sacrificing their health.

While I loved MDA - what bugged me was how every one pushed IFing, and Carb cycling or counting calories on me - b/c it would fix my weight struggles... only 1 person said - I know how to fix you - I fixed myself, I've been where you are - and that was JAck... pretty compelling... what's crazy is that b/c it wasn't fast sudden success that some ppl see in IFing, or Carb cycling - they say its bunk... especially the young xfit guys... but there were women who derided anything that didn't give them license to eat how every they wanted - IMO lean gains/carb cycling is just an excuse to eat foods that isn't good for you...

### Jack Kruse — 2012-10-30T14:18:23+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-wanted-my-life-back.4574/post-60892>

That is why the people at MDA, PH, and Wolf's site had issues........not everyone is easily fixable and this mixed message hurt lots of people. What happens every friday on those sites are not everyone's results.......contrary to what they say in their books and podcasts. That is clinical medicine 101. Patients want explanations why.........not BS.

### shah78 — 2012-10-30T15:16:42+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-wanted-my-life-back.4574/post-60902>

The woman admits to eating bread, oreos and bagels to do her carbo loads and having/using a SCALE many times a day.Leangains is not /never been,Paleo. She blames Mark Sisson for touting 16 hour fasts. She,(and then YOU ) refer to this as "starvation". Hardly. To their credit these" fasting mavens" say women should do 14 hour fasts instead.......The woman has an eating disorder. She had one before she started Leangains, or what she thought was leangains. .....Personally I think Leangains is "way too Rube Goldberg" for my tastes. However I do 16 hour fasts whenever it feels comfortable. (Maybe 15 days a month). When I want to eat at nine am I eat my Salmon/carbs. When I'm not hungry I wait till eleven or twelve..... This is "reality internet". She is the "SNOOKY" of the Pleosphere. I smell Theatre.

### Michael — 2012-10-30T15:27:40+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-wanted-my-life-back.4574/post-60905>

> **Shijin13;62597 wrote:** While I loved MDA - what bugged me was how every one pushed IFing, and Carb cycling or counting calories on me - b/c it would fix my weight struggles...

Yeah, Mark himself is very sane and rather middle of the road, but some curious and extreme fashions wash across those forums. I think the IF tide has gone out again now - probably because enough people have tried it and found it didn't work.

But I can remember when people were regularly posting things like "eating breakfast is conventional wisdom". AFAIK "conventional wisdom" is a dietary term coined by Taubes to represent what people who were too quick to believe their own hypotheses considered was wise. Mark Sisson obviously liked the term and bandied it around quite often. By the time it had reached the forum posters it meant "whatever dietary practice I dislike or don't currently believe in". It all gets a bit precious ([sense 2](http://http://oxforddictionaries.com/definition/english/precious)) in the end. I mean most people believe it's wise to look before crossing the road. There's nothing wrong with many things most people believe.

The other two strategies are still in full swing. Calorie-counting is a hard creature to kill - it's like the many-headed hydra in the myth. My understanding is that there's data going as far back as the 1950s (Keckwick and Pawan) and extending into the present to show that things are not as simple as that, but it's simply impossible to get many people to discard the idea.

### bigknitwit — 2012-10-30T20:38:32+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-wanted-my-life-back.4574/post-60978>

I tried intermittent fasting last fall for a few months, as a last ditch effort to have my cake and eat it too and not blow up. I tried not eating until 4:30 pm everyday. But then by 4:30 I was ready to eat everything in the house, starting with all the junk food that I had "earned." Then, if I needed to make real headway, I would fast longer. 24 hours without food would drop the scale 3.5 pounds. 48 hours would take off another 3, and then if I was really really dedicated, 72 hours would take off another 2. That's 8.5 pounds in 3 days. Hard to beat!! Except that the weight is mostly water, and comes back on with a vengeance, and then there was the feeling of being perpetually cold no matter what I wore (thanks to thyroid hormones being shut down to RT3), the exhaustion, the unending dry mouth/unquensable thirst, and then the shame of being unable to control oneself. Actually, having written all that, I'd say it's coming close to the description of an eating disorder.

### Michael — 2012-10-30T20:50:38+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-wanted-my-life-back.4574/post-60979>

> **bigknitwit;62689 wrote:** Actually, having written all that, I'd say it's coming close to the description of an eating disorder.

Yeah, what the woman on the podcast felt, too - although she couldn't see that when she was swept up into the mindset surrounding it. She was very lucky to have Abel and George as friends to help her out again.

### shah78 — 2012-10-30T22:10:29+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-wanted-my-life-back.4574/post-60991>

I say the same thing on every subject it seems. Your CT should be challenging and pleasant, with little to no suffering. Your weight lifting should be challenging and pleasant, with little to no suffering. Your IF should be even less challenging and pleasant, with little to no suffering. What is with all this suffering? Have I missed the "Thou shall suffer when curing your ailments" eleventh commandment? Way too Neolithically Judeo-Christian for a "devout" evolutionist like myself.

### AKMan — 2012-10-30T22:49:01+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-wanted-my-life-back.4574/post-60995>

If you could find and afford enough seafood and seaweed to eat that, and only that, every day...there would be no overeating, undereating or a need to count calories or IF. It would be very, very hard to overeat on fish and seaweed.

However, I doubt many are doing that. Most of us also eat eggs, cheese, veggies, butter, pork, beef, chicken, fruit, starch, and other things. It's all this stuff that makes a need for IF, calorie counting, crossfitting, cyclical ketogenic diets--all that.

I love IF. For me, IF means skip breakfast. It's very natural for me. I get up at 6am, drink a little coffee, go to work, take an exercise break, and eat a nearly perfect epi-paleo lunch of raw salmon or oysters, canned oysters, or cooked fish. Then don't eat again til supper, which is paleo, but not epi-paleo most days.

I don't recommend this for everyone, but for me it works great!

### TribalSpice — 2012-10-31T03:01:25+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-wanted-my-life-back.4574/post-61020>

Let me start with a quote out of "Good Health in the 21st Century" by Dr Carole Hungarford, which incidently if you don't own a copy, seriously think about it next time you are on booko.

\*

"The biochemistry of breakfast

Insulin resistance is the end result of of continuously high levels of circulating insulin. \*In the West, we rarely fast. \*Breakfast is meant to be the breaking of a fast. During that fast, our insulin levels should have fallen, stayed low until all the food is absorbed from the stomach, has entered and left the circulation, and has been distributed to \*the cells of the liver, fat and muscles. \*During this time, the cells of those organs have minimal exposure to insulin and thus maintain insulin sensitivity.

\*

Suppose two individuals eat the same high-calorie diet. One is a grazer and spreads the meal across the waking hours of the day; the other eats in two sittings, with a long gap between the last meal of the day and the first meal of the following day. \*The first individual develops insulin resistance and a fatty liver, but the second stands a good chance of avoiding these complications. Why?

\*

Through out human evolution, hormonal influences developed to tie in with various circadian and diurnal cycles. Hormonal patterns and the impact of various activities can now be studied by laboratory measurement. One of the hormones of interest is DHEA or dehydroepiandrosterone.

\*

DHEA is made of cholesterol, and it is a precursor to all the eostrogens and testosterones we produce. Taken as a medication, it is a banned drug for Olympic athletes because it increases their performance and is deemed to give them an unfair advantage. \*It is also known as a longevity hormone. \*Pathological states aside, DHEA levels are an indicator of the health of the individual. \*According to Ray Kearny, an immunologist from the University of Sydney (and a member of the delayed-breakfast brigade), contracting our eating hours by having a late start to the day improves DHEA output and appears to reduce cancer risk.

\*

We produce DHEA in opposition to growth hormone and the glucocorticoids. High levels are reached in the early hours of the morning, as these other hormone levels fall. \*The rise in DHEA production is stimulated by bright sunlight, so it makes sense to be out of doors then. \*With our first meal, further glucocorticosteroids are produced and the DHEA surge is suppressed. \*The longer breakfast is delayed, the longer the DHEA peak lasts and the greater the overall output.

\*

Early morning urine has a high concentration of DHEA. It is probably for this reason that some monks drink a glass of their own urine first thing in the morning. (Should we suggest this to the Olympians?)

\*

The health benefits of the overnight fast include both the restoration of insulin sensitivity and the optimisation of DHEA production. \*Less quirky than consuming odd drinks and easier than under-going long fasts is simply delaying breakfast until hunger asserts itself. \*Take a morning jog to complete the picture.

\*

Further support for 'no breakfast' came from a report in The Lancet that normal children were unaffected or performed better without breakfast.

\*

This all happened as a matter of course for our primitive ancestors. They ate at dusk, and finishing the meal around the campfire. \*Without artificial lighting they were finished with food relatively early in the evening. \*Although they arose with the sun, there is good reason to believe that some time elapsed before they ate. \*Stored food would be eaten by small animals, or would attract large animals to which humans were also prey. \*Almost all food was fresh (with all the attendant health benefits), whole and acquired work. \*They had to find, even hunt, kill and cook breakfast before they could eat. \*Maybe they snacked on a few nuts and berries as they went. \*You work first, you ate second. \*You burnt energy, and then you replaced it. \*We do the reverse. \*We eat first, preemptively refueling in case our supplies run low. \*The price is called metabolic syndrome.

So in short, we really aren't giving our body enough "down-time" from insulin or enough time with peak DHEA. \* DHEA as mentioned above is the building block for all our sex hormones thus playing a huge role in fertility, but it is much more than that. \*DHEA helps keep our cells "young", it plays a part in cognition, muscle strength, your immune system, sex drive, cardiovascular health.. the list goes on."

I started officially doing IF about 14 months ago, my weekly debilitating headaches disappeared within 2 weeks. I wasn't doing it to lose weight I am 57kg and 169cm (126lb, 5ft 7) and have been for a long time. The transition to IF wasn't hard, actually it was better because it really is how my circadian rhythms told me to eat. Now I get hungry at 11am and again at 6pm. I eat until I am comfortably satiated, I have never counted calories and always preferred fats and protein over carbs anyway. It sounds like this woman's metabolism was totally stuffed to start with and all the sugar and carbs just stuffed it even more.

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-10-30T15:56:09+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sleep-worse-during-full-moons.4576/post-60910>

We didn't want to say, but since the person suffering usually has no idea. . . A rare and old world condition called Lycanthrope. If you had done the 23andme, it would tell you - I think it's a snip on the MTHRF 215467.

Or, you are doing very well - and you SHOULD be sleeping less as it's much brighter out at night. You should be out foraging for shrooms and all that kind of stuff.

Happy Halloweeeeen!

It does beg the question - why do we think we should sleep the same amount everynight, when the amount of "night" varies by season and phase of the moon.

Just as eating has evolved in congruency with what nature provides - so has sleep. I think all of our "levels" of whatnot should probably be variable as well based on all our sensory input, including sound. I bet in the natural world the night is also LOUDER during a full moon.

I bet we have NFI what optimal levels of anything are as we are still not taking into account some mofo variables such as:

Time of year

Where subject is

Sex

Weather

Solar patterns

Position in the tribe

and probably a boatload more of material items. We are STILL in the reductionist mode of isolating (even the most erudite are thinking in terms of carbs/fats/protien) because we simply don't have the abilities nor language to quantify and evaluate the material variables.

So, what is one to do? Look to nature to guide you. Or Inger, that will work too

Yes, I think there is something to nature and the full moon. I am not too stressed out about it. It is just that I have been sleep deprived for 6 years and want to heal my body. I do not feel nappy ever. It has been hard for me to sleep since day one. My mom would have to take my crib with if she went to stay at Grandma's. I had issues all the way back then. ### Jack Kruse — 2014-02-05T06:39:40+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sleep-worse-during-full-moons.4576/post-108404>

Remember the moon controls our tilt at 23.5 degrees. This tilt does two things........give us seasons and circadian cycles.....but its gravity also pulls on the surface water and gives us a tidal bulge.............but it also gives us a magna bulge..........and this alters the native EMF in places where the mantle is high to the crust. If you happen to be sensitive to it you wont sleep as well. This is why the moon has always been associated with bad mojo.........it is a magnetoreception perception. I think women should track their cycles along with the moon, simply because it's fascinating, and often enlightening. When I was 19 I met this amazing woman who called her periods her moon cycles, and from the moment I heard that, I always regarded my blood moon with honor, respect, and awe. So many women find their periods to be a hassle or an interruption, but they are missing out on the intuitive wisdom of our fascinating female body. I am a cancer, and the moon has always been a remarkable influence in my life. Tides are easy to see, but if you live away from large bodies of water, they are not obvious and tend to be forgotten.

# Thread: Study in Ireland on Suicidal Ideation
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/study-in-ireland-on-suicidal-ideation.4577/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 7
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Michael — 2012-10-30T15:47:52+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/study-in-ireland-on-suicidal-ideation.4577/post-60909>

There's been a new crop of stories referring to the omega 3 smoothies for soldiers trial. These ones refer back to a study carried out in Ireland that aimed to see whether omega 3 supplementation could reduce suicidal ideation.

I think I've found it:

[http://m.bjp.rcpsych.org/content/190/2/118.short](http://http://m.bjp.rcpsych.org/content/190/2/118.short)

### nonchalant — 2012-10-30T17:07:57+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/study-in-ireland-on-suicidal-ideation.4577/post-60937>

Thanks for this, Michael. It is helpful.

### Shijin13 — 2012-10-30T20:20:49+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/study-in-ireland-on-suicidal-ideation.4577/post-60976>

during the stand-down Suicide prevention Day the DoD had several weeks back, one of the things the Medical folks were pushing was Fish oils, and the consumption of more fish/seafood. the interesting thing is - unless deployed fish is usually breaded fish. when deployed - Steak and Lobster are served once/week, as is shrimp in the chow hall.

### ealachan — 2012-10-30T20:58:12+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/study-in-ireland-on-suicidal-ideation.4577/post-60980>

Omega-3 supplementation absolutely helped drag me out of clinical depression and allowed me to go through last winter unmedicated. For a bit of perspective: I'd spent every winter (and the majority of the rest of the year for that matter) since 2005 on a combination of antidepressants and anti-anxiety meds. For the majority of 2005-2007, I was all but house-bound by my depression and anxiety. I don't think it's a stretch to say that heavy Omega-3 supplementation saved my life.

### ColdBren — 2012-10-31T00:20:19+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/study-in-ireland-on-suicidal-ideation.4577/post-61006>

Wow Ealachan, I too have noticed significant benefits to my emotional issues from omega 3, for sure.

Must have been invented by Al Qeida.

Does anyone remember that or care to share where they're getting them?...

I have been using _**HEX fermented Sea Kraut**_ from _Whole Foods_. A 'processed' product from Baltimore, MD (somewhat regional to me).
[https://www.hexferments.com/products/seaweed-kraut](https://www.hexferments.com/products/seaweed-kraut)
Version says _Sea Kraut_ but it still has _Green Cabbage_ and _Gold Beets_ as first two ingredients, then, unrefined _sea salt_, Ginger, _**Arame**_, _Burdock Roo_t and lastly **_Red Dulse_, _Black Laver_, _Green Sea Lettuce_**. Would not likely be too high in Sea Veggies but the label shown online has a sticker that reads, "More Seaweed Power" so hopefully they are increasing the 'sea' amount. My clear jars label is black and gold and does not have the, "More Seaweed Power" Here is their PRODUCTS page. [https://www.hexferments.com/collections/all](https://www.hexferments.com/collections/all)

**_Farmstead Ferments_** is a local Ferments company I use... but they do not have sea veggies [https://farmsteadferments.com/](https://farmsteadferments.com/)

Was a little concerned about eating raw cruciferous veggies or gritrogens due to my hypothyroidism.
Many allopatic doctors believe this Dr. Izabella Wentz quote. "**Cruciferous vegetables** in particular are considered to be “**goitrogenic**” because they have the potential to block iodine absorption. They contain glucosinolates, which are responsible for this action. **Glucosinolates can prevent the absorption of iodine into the thyroid gland when consumed in large quantities."** I never ate large quantities watched my level of these foods and except for some slaw I ate them steamed or cooked. But I did notice when I ate more flaxseed my hair loss became very noticeable when I cut flaxseed out my hair loss lessened.
This _ThyroMate_ article covers Flaxseed and Thyroid.
[https://www.thyromate.com/blog/can-flaxseed-help-thyroid-problems](https://www.thyromate.com/blog/can-flaxseed-help-thyroid-problems).

I've been hypothyroid not believed to be Hashimoto's for 14 years and I'd had low iodine for over 22 years. Tested urine and blood with but no one neither GP or Endo Doctors cared to treat my low iodine. I was extremely low almost nil and then worked up to less than 30 eating more fish and then I worked up to <40 serum trying several things--kelp flakes, _Wellness Resources_ iodine drops, Kelp supplements etc.
But using small doses of these two products took me to 108.6 last year. I was so used to not getting any results I was using both of these when my level went up that hight last year.
DR. COWAN'S _**GARDEN SEA-VEGETABLES POWDER**_ -Ingredients: Wild-harvested **dulse, wakame, kelp.** Nothing else.
[https://www.drcowansgarden.com/coll...oducts/dr-cowans-garden-sea-vegetables-powder](https://www.drcowansgarden.com/collections/available-now-2/products/dr-cowans-garden-sea-vegetables-powder)
**and**
_Activation Products_ **Oceans Alive Raw Phytoplankton**
[https://www.activationproducts.com/wellness-mama/oceans-alive/](https://www.activationproducts.com/wellness-mama/oceans-alive/)

I also use some of Dr. Cowan's low Oxalate products.

All this was before Dr. Jack Kruse influence and now I go to learn about _**Iodine Jack Kruse** and will post this on the [The iodine Thread!!!](http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/the-iodine-thread.943/)_
_and maybe a hypothyroid thread if I find one._

# Thread: Power postures raise Testosterone and lower cortisol
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/power-postures-raise-testosterone-and-lower-cortisol.4584/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Glamazon — 2012-10-30T21:59:20+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/power-postures-raise-testosterone-and-lower-cortisol.4584/post-60988>

[http://youtu.be/Ks-\_Mh1QhMc\[/video\]\]\[video=youtube\_share;Ks-\_Mh1QhMc\]http://youtu.be/Ks-\_Mh1QhMc\[/video\]](http://[video=youtube_share;Ks-_Mh1QhMc)

Body language affects how others see us, but it may also change how we see ourselves. Social psychologist Amy Cuddy shows how "power posing" -- standing in a posture of confidence, even when we don't feel confident -- can affect testosterone and cortisol levels in the brain, and might even have an impact on our chances for success.

### Lyndra — 2012-10-31T01:18:22+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/power-postures-raise-testosterone-and-lower-cortisol.4584/post-61011>

Wow, great video, thanks for posting. What a powerful story.

> Our bodies change our minds...and our minds change our behavior...and our behavior changes our outcomes.
> Hmm, where have we heard something like that?
>
>
>
>
> > Don't fake it until you make it...fake it until you BECOME it
>
>
>
> Tiny tweaks ---> BIG CHANGES

So many different applications with her theories. If a two minutes power pose can lower your cortisol, I'll be giving it a try before bed.

### nonchalant — 2012-10-31T16:30:34+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/power-postures-raise-testosterone-and-lower-cortisol.4584/post-61083>

I like that "Tiny tweaks ---> BIG CHANGES".

> So many different applications with her theories. If a two minutes power pose can lower your cortisol, I'll be giving it a try before bed.

Great idea, Lyndra.

At one of my kids schools, on a Wednesday morning one of the local church groups holds "Healthy Breakfast". You are going to love this menu:

Nutrigrain, fruit loops and coco pops

White bread toasted with jam or Vegemite

And a fruit salad (which at least has some nutritional value)

One tells me all the time her mom says donuts are ok (the small ones covered in powdered sugar) because she dunks them in milk so at least she gets calcium then!!! WHAT?!?! And these people are actually raising the next generation . . . it's like a horror movie, only worse cause it's real!!

### nonchalant — 2012-10-31T23:50:04+0200

This church means well, but they are teaching a bad lesson, as well as giving poor nutrition (malnutrition).

### Entelechy — 2012-11-01T21:46:42+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/school-healthy-breakfast.4585/post-61389>

> **Michael;63110 wrote:** Samuel Johnson. He meant that while he might have good intentions they were worth nothing if he didn't make the effort to carry them out.

Johnson often gets credited with saying "The road to hell is paved with good intentions," but never actually said it.

In Boswell's Life of Johnson, he is quoted as saying, (in 1775) "Hell is paved with good intentions." A footnote in the book indicates it to be a 'proverbial sentence,' quoting an earlier 1651 source. Two others are

John Ray, 1670, "Hell is paved with good intentions."

Saint Bernard of Clairvaux (1091-1153), "Hell is full of good intentions or desires."

whomever added "the road" is not known,

the aphorism is most commonly meant to infer that individuals may do bad things even though they intend the results to be good.

### FarmersDaughter — 2012-11-01T22:17:56+0200

That pic of Johnson...sure is scary looking. Those people were all unhealthy looking like that. Sooooo bloated. If I can recall any picture from that era; man or woman, it's bloated. Yuck!

# Thread: Freezing your eyeballs for better vision
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/freezing-your-eyeballs-for-better-vision.4586/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 13
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-10-31T04:13:41+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/freezing-your-eyeballs-for-better-vision.4586/post-61031>

I have noticed the last few weeks some vision problems. Like I was squinting like an oldie, and tonight I had to drive and I noticed the nightime glare etc was back.

Early this year, I had noted how notably my vision had improved. Night vision had done a 180, and I kept braggin to everyone how my magic paleo diet (and cutting diet soda) had improved my eyes.

As I was driving and trying not to wreck tonight - I realized one factor that has changed.

For many months I was doing 20 min daily face dunks in ice water with a snorkel. I was able to open my eyes in the 29-35 degree icy waters. I stopped over the summer, and since I've picked up CT this fall, I've just been doing baths. No face stuff.

Coincidence or evidence of the supernatural. Could icing my eyeballs effect my vision? I wonder if I could freeze half my head/ears and improve my hearing.

### Marie — 2012-10-31T04:55:51+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/freezing-your-eyeballs-for-better-vision.4586/post-61037>

I've definitely noticed an AMAZING change in my vision after a quick dip in the Pacific Ocean, so I'd be very interested in whether I should be using those frozen eye masks and a little mg to fix my old eyeballs...

### Melinda — 2012-10-31T05:46:41+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/freezing-your-eyeballs-for-better-vision.4586/post-61040>

Very interesting theory. I had really bad headlight dazzle a few years ago and now I'm OK and I attributed it to getting rid of sunglasses during the daytime...I had auto-darkening lenses in my Rx glasses. I embraced the sunlight so now I can take the headlights. But I also did lots of face dunking which I haven't done for some time. I'll start tomorrow and see if my vision gets better.

### KiwiLauren — 2012-10-31T06:20:48+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/freezing-your-eyeballs-for-better-vision.4586/post-61041>

From one of JK's blogs....

For men or women, you might notice your own free testosterone level is really low on a high Vitamin D3 supplemented level, and your libido is lagging badly or your body comp is not coming around. Or you might try reading in low light conditions and see how you do. Many will notice that they can’t do it well any longer. If you listened to my recent Nov 2011 podcast on [JoanneUnleashed.com](http://http://joanneunleashed.com/) you might have wondered why I read in low light and red light levels during my own reset. I was checking myself for signs that I was pushing my Vitamin D3 status too high. Affected individuals are unable to distinguish images in low levels of illumination. Vitamin A deficiency affects vision by inhibiting the production of rhodopsin, the eye pigment responsible for sensing low light situations. Rhodopsin is found in the retina and is composed of retinal, which is an active form of vitamin A, and opsin a protein made by the retina. Basically, if you “over do” the use of Vitamin D3, you will not make enough rhodopsin (from a relative Vitamin A deficiency), and you will suffer from night blindness. I pick this up in patients who notice night time visual driving issues. Most people won’t read in low light levels like I do to assess themselves for this issue. However, you can use this simple test too. Their doctors usually send them to the ophthalmologist, but most are cured once they tweak their diets and add back some vitamin A containing foods.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-10-31T14:48:47+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/freezing-your-eyeballs-for-better-vision.4586/post-61062>

CT augments the rhodopsin effect.........

### nonchalant — 2012-10-31T14:56:51+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/freezing-your-eyeballs-for-better-vision.4586/post-61064>

Cavemam you opened your eyes during the face dunks?!? I never considered that.

Currently in my ice water baths when I turn over on my tummy I also dunk my face a few times. And I also immerse my neck and (closed) mouth for a good long time, to tighten up the neck even more. And I'm sure to get my underarms in for the same reason.

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-10-31T15:07:45+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/freezing-your-eyeballs-for-better-vision.4586/post-61065>

> **KiwiLauren;62755 wrote:** From one of JK's blogs....
>
> For men or women, you might notice your own free testosterone level is really low on a high Vitamin D3 supplemented level, and your libido is lagging badly or your body comp is not coming around. Or you might try reading in low light conditions and see how you do. Many will notice that they canâ€™t do it well any longer. If you listened to my recent Nov 2011 podcast on [JoanneUnleashed.com](http://http://joanneunleashed.com/) you might have wondered why I read in low light and red light levels during my own reset. I was checking myself for signs that I was pushing my Vitamin D3 status too high. Affected individuals are unable to distinguish images in low levels of illumination. Vitamin A deficiency affects vision by inhibiting the production of rhodopsin, the eye pigment responsible for sensing low light situations. Rhodopsin is found in the retina and is composed of retinal, which is an active form of vitamin A, and opsin a protein made by the retina. Basically, if you â€œover doâ€￾ the use of Vitamin D3, you will not make enough rhodopsin (from a relative Vitamin A deficiency), and you will suffer from night blindness. I pick this up in patients who notice night time visual driving issues. Most people wonâ€™t read in low light levels like I do to assess themselves for this issue. However, you can use this simple test too. Their doctors usually send them to the ophthalmologist, but most are cured once they tweak their diets and add back some vitamin A containing foods.

Great Find KL! I love your posts. Come on over for coffee after you drop the kids off at school!

So, guess what I've been doing for about 3 weeks now? Super Vitamin D3 Dosing! My levels have been tanked and unbudging since I started checking back in April. All summer I did major sun + oral and sublingual dosing. My level didn't move even one point.

So after doing the best google research and consulting with random people on the internet, I started an experiment of dosing 30-50K iu a day by lady-parts.

So, looks like it might be time to check and see if Vit-D went up. Probably my serum levels are stagnant, and all I'm doing is sucking out all the Vit-A in my foods to process the D overload, and causing more harm.

I'm 99% sure that type of supplementation is at worse useless, and at best bad and sets something out of balance - I really think this, but yet I keep trying different things. I guess if anything, each FAIL teaches me lots. I'm tempted to start in on Vit-A, but then there we go farther down that path of unbalancing things and unintended consequense.

I will start face dunking again to test on that (no downsides to that!)

Unfortunately it's going into winter now and my veggie consumption is dropping rapido!

SO - I think I will keep going on the D3 Vag experiement until I can get my blood drawn (it's test T time again, too) and perhaps supplement the K&A until then.

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-10-31T15:15:24+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/freezing-your-eyeballs-for-better-vision.4586/post-61068>

> **Nonchalant;62780 wrote:** Cavemam you opened your eyes during the face dunks?!? I never considered that.
>
> Currently in my ice water baths when I turn over on my tummy I also dunk my face a few times. And I also immerse my neck and (closed) mouth for a good long time, to tighten up the neck even more. And I'm sure to get my underarms in for the same reason.

I really was! My method for face is big bowl of ice water at around 32 degrees and head in with snorkel so I could just sit there and veg and listen to a book or podcast. I found it super duper relaxing. I quit over summer - but I have been thinking I should do it again for several reasons. . .

65 degree baths seem to be doing nothing by way of cold adaptation. I don't think they are really preparing me for "deep ct". I was hoping not to drag ice all around the world this year and just go with what nature provides. . .but right now it's just an obnoxious chore and I'm not finding time for it as much as I know I should.

I am a big fan of CT, don't get me wrong - but it is NOT easier and less stressful to get accomplished than the old school chronic cardio stuff. At least right now that is what my experience is. I think I could probably head to the elliptical at the gym easier than go buy ice and sit my ass down for 45 mins and do nothing but freeze.

### Patty Cakes — 2012-10-31T18:01:34+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/freezing-your-eyeballs-for-better-vision.4586/post-61100>

> **Cavemam;62784 wrote:** I think I could probably head to the elliptical at the gym easier than go buy ice and sit my ass down for 45 mins and do nothing but freeze.

I'm feeling the same thing here!

### nonchalant — 2012-10-31T18:12:28+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/freezing-your-eyeballs-for-better-vision.4586/post-61101>

> **Cavemam;62784 wrote:**
>
> I think I could probably head to the elliptical at the gym easier than go buy ice and sit my ass down for 45 mins and do nothing but freeze.

But you don't freeze while you're doing CT. When you were dunking your face, you were vegging out, relaxing. Not freezing. I don't think I could stand CT if I felt I was freezing for more than the first 3-4 minutes. I often get sleepy, laying in the tub. Very relaxing.

You could wait until your tap water gets down to 55F or so. Probably won't be long, where you are.

### Lyndra — 2012-11-02T01:25:07+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/freezing-your-eyeballs-for-better-vision.4586/post-61442>

My vision got much better last spring/summer. I was doing a lot of new things, including BHRT and 20-30 minutes of CT per day. I stopped the CT for the summer and my vision has declined. Since I'm still on the BHRT, I can only attribute the changes to CT. For me, it wasn't necessary to submerge my face as I was just soaking in the tub.

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-11-02T01:27:11+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/freezing-your-eyeballs-for-better-vision.4586/post-61444>

>
> You could wait until your tap water gets down to 55F or so. Probably won't be long, where you are.

Welllllll, to clarify - didn't mean I was literally freezing as in frozen body and uncomfortable. . .but contained and bored while freezing, and not feeling like it's accomplishing as much as that time could by me in other ways. I think. Mebby. Dunno!

### ljbprrfmof — 2012-11-04T02:03:45+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/freezing-your-eyeballs-for-better-vision.4586/post-61885>

> **Cavemam;63192 wrote:** Welllllll, to clarify - didn't mean I was literally freezing as in frozen body and uncomfortable. . .but contained and bored while freezing, and not feeling like it's accomplishing as much as that time could by me in other ways. I think. Mebby. Dunno!

I noticed my CT showers seeming to get shorter and shorter. I attribute part of it to boredom and another part to a newer showerhead and diverter valve. In my old shower, I was able to focus water on my back and chest at the same time because the diverter valve was nicely worn. Not so in my new shower. It is working perfectly. New landlord is a plumber.

# Thread: Couple of weekly portions of oily fish can help ward off stroke
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/couple-of-weekly-portions-of-oily-fish-can-help-ward-off-stroke.4589/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Michael — 2012-10-31T08:48:47+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/couple-of-weekly-portions-of-oily-fish-can-help-ward-off-stroke.4589/post-61044>

Here's the press release:

> Eating at least two servings of oily fish a week is moderately but significantly associated with a reduced risk of stroke, finds a study published on bmj.com today.
>
>
>
> **But taking fish oil supplements doesnâ€™t seem to have the same effect**, say the researchers.

[http://www.bmj.com/press-releases/2012/10/30/couple-weekly-portions-oily-fish-can-help-ward-stroke](http://http://www.bmj.com/press-releases/2012/10/30/couple-weekly-portions-oily-fish-can-help-ward-stroke)

The top press hit is from the relatively high circulation _Daily Mail_:

[http://www.dailymail.co.uk/health/article-2225408/Eating-portions-oily-fish-week-help-ward-stroke.html](http://http://www.dailymail.co.uk/health/article-2225408/Eating-portions-oily-fish-week-help-ward-stroke.html)

# Thread: I just made the weirdest drinking chocolate..
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-just-made-the-weirdest-drinking-chocolate.4590/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 15
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Inger — 2012-10-31T13:11:52+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-just-made-the-weirdest-drinking-chocolate.4590/post-61051>

I have made smoothies from soaked seaweeds only, lately a few times.. and I was about to make some for dessert today after eating my raw moose burgers. Then it all of a sudden popped into my head, I could try to make a chocolate from it?

I added organic 100% cocoa powder and a teaspoon of wild raw honey, the soaked kombu and fresh water into my mixer. Let it run for a while, and it turned into a nice smoothie. Hmmm it tasted great! Really great! I drank it all... I figured if I add a raw eggyolk and a spoonful of coconuoil it might get even better.. so I made one more smoothie with kombu, raw honey, cacao, a yolk, coconutoil. OMG that was even better! Now my stomach is sooo full of seaweed..lol But I feel great so far.

Now, I could never believe it will turn out so delicious. Something to try if one is not yet so into seaweeds. Ok this would be more of a summer drink maybe because there are a few carbs for sure.

### Michael — 2012-10-31T14:02:14+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-just-made-the-weirdest-drinking-chocolate.4590/post-61053>

You surprised me, Inger. I thought there was going to be at least one raw fishhead in there for sure.

### nonchalant — 2012-10-31T15:11:02+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-just-made-the-weirdest-drinking-chocolate.4590/post-61067>

### Inger — 2012-10-31T15:26:21+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-just-made-the-weirdest-drinking-chocolate.4590/post-61070>

@ Michael..lol No way! I do not like the taste of fish heads at all, that is why I always hold my nose drinking them..

But this I wanted to be delicious you know, and a fishhead would have ruined the taste I am quite sure.. Feel free to try though..lol

Do not forget to report how it turned out..

@ Nonchalant.. I am not sure anyone would want a cookbook with fishhead smoothies, raw lambshearts and seaweed chocolate..lol

But hey.. you got me to think... one could never know!

### Michael — 2012-10-31T15:46:51+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-just-made-the-weirdest-drinking-chocolate.4590/post-61075>

Some of the cans of coconut milk are thickened with carragheen - so why not seaweed?

Maybe one could do something like 8 fl oz coconut milk, 1 tbs cocoa powder, 1 tsp raw honey, and some soaked seaweed.

How briny does it taste? I don't know how other flavourings would go with that seawater taste. Vanilla escence probably wouldn't work. Maybe some spices would. Cinnamon? Chilli?

### Inger — 2012-10-31T16:05:34+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-just-made-the-weirdest-drinking-chocolate.4590/post-61078>

You know Michael, it tasted not briny at all! IDK if it was that I had soaked and so removed the saltiest taste, because I used new water, not the saltier soaking water wich I love to drink as it is. It still was a tiny touch of saltiness there but in a nice way, I used to add salt to cookies too! You can buy chocolate here with salt in it too..

To use coconut milk..mmm.. good idea!

Cinnamon and chilli.. sounds cool! I need to try that! Wow!

Tummy still feeling very happy after the all the kombu...  I guess my tummy starts to get used to digesting seaweeds. I had the feeling I got some bloating in the beginning... for sure I cannot know if it was the seaweeds but I think so.

### FarmersDaughter — 2012-10-31T19:11:46+0200

### Inger — 2012-10-31T19:22:15+0200

### chocolate — 2012-11-13T15:32:03+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-just-made-the-weirdest-drinking-chocolate.4590/post-64008>

[http://www.greenmedinfo.com/blog/seaweed-extracts-inhibit-gum-disease-bacteria](http://http://www.greenmedinfo.com/blog/seaweed-extracts-inhibit-gum-disease-bacteria)

> Researchers from Korea's Silla University have determined that many seaweed extracts inhibit bacteria known to cause gingivitis and gum disease.
>
>
>
> The researchers tested 57 different species of seaweeds harvested from the coast of South Korea. The research screened each of the extract of these seaweeds against the gum disease-causing bacteria Prevotella intermedia and Porphyromonas gingivalis. They found that nearly a third of the seaweed extracts - 17 different species - inhibited the growth and colonization of these pathogenic bacteria.
>
>
>
> Extracts from two forms of Sea Lettuce, Enteromorpha linza and Ulva pertusa, along with Sargassum (Sargassum sagamianum) were found to significantly block the growth of both P. intermedia and P. gingivalis.
>
>
>
> They determined that one of the major mechanisms of this inhibitory effect against oral bacteria was their ability to block collagenase enzymes that allow these bacteria to expand and grow.
>
>
>
> The researchers determined that the phenolic content of these seaweeds was the primary component inducing their antibacterial effects. Isolated phenols were then tested directly against the oral bacteria and these proved lethal to the bacteria.
>
>
>
> Oral bacteria are the cause of gum disease. Gingivitis and periodontal disease take place as these bacteria build up in the gumline. As their populations increase, pockets form in the gums. The waste products of these bacteria are also extremely acidic, and they erode the enamel and produce tooth decay.
>
>
>
> **Worse, these bacteria also produce waste streams that can leak through the gums and pollute our blood vessels. This can produce artery disease and significant damage to the heart.**
>
>
>
> This study confirms 2011 research from Korea's CHA University in Kyonggi. In this study, Enteromorpha linza was found to significantly inhibit plaque, gingivitis and bleeding on probing. A mouthwash using the seaweed extract was found to significantly reduce the P. intermedia and P.
